pages.title: AEPC Renewable Framework and Progress Reports
headings: AEPC Renewable Framework and Progress Reports
Gaps we would like to close
pages.excerpt: Primary source for Nepal's off-grid and distributed solar story. AEPC ("Alternative Energy Promotion Centre") is the government institution responsible for rural electrification through renewables, standing alongside (not inside) NEA. It is the operator and financier of the solar
pages.body_text: AEPC Renewable Framework and Progress Reports Primary source for Nepal's off-grid and distributed solar story. AEPC ("Alternative Energy Promotion Centre") is the government institution responsible for rural electrification through renewables, standing alongside (not inside) NEA. It is the operator and financier of the solar-home-system (SHS) programme, community mini-grids, institutional rooftop systems, and the subsidy / concessional-finance channel that rural solar rides on. # What the source covers Periodic AEPC "Progress Report" / "Renewable Energy Framework" / annual yearbook documents cover: Cumulative SHS installations by district, province, and year Village-scale mini-grid portfolio (locations, capacity, operator) Institutional solar (schools, clinics, govt offices) Improved cookstove and biogas programmes (context, not solar) Subsidy structure and donor-financing channels Policy and regulatory framework for rural renewables # Key data points (approximate, 2021–2024 window) Cumulative SHS installed: ~1.1 million systems (as of ~2021, with additions through 2024) Cumulative SHS capacity: ~80–100 MWp Community mini-grids (operational): ~30 sites, ~1.1 MWp aggregate Institutional solar: ~25,000 sites (rural clinics, schools, telecom, govt offices), ~15 MWp aggregate Geography: Karnali, Sudurpaschim, and remote hill districts of Koshi / Gandaki / Bagmati dominate installations (see data-rooftop-shs-deployment) Subsidy structure: Tiered, with higher subsidies for remote/difficult districts; typical residential SHS subsidy ~30–50% of capex Annual new installations: declining as grid reach expands (grid displaces SHS on economic grounds above ~30 HH/km²), replacement/upgrade cycle becoming dominant # Why AEPC matters strategically AEPC's relationship with NEA is the most under-discussed institutional story in Nepal solar: 1. AEPC reaches households NEA cannot. Remote hill and Karnali districts are uneconomic for grid extension at current tariffs; AEPC's subsidized SHS is the only electrification pathway that fits those geographies. 2. AEPC is the operational home of community-level solar. No mini-grid, no micro-grid, no community solar cooperative in Nepal exists without AEPC technical backstop or finance. 3. AEPC is the natural host for the agrivoltaic programme. If agrivoltaics-and-land implies a lease-template law and a tariff adder, AEPC's rural footprint and cooperative linkages make it the natural programme administrator — not NEA. 4. AEPC integrates into the political coalition for solar. The rural-electrification constituency that has sustained AEPC for three decades is the same constituency that would vote for Terai agrivoltaics and Karnali off-grid scale-up. # Documents and subsources The "AEPC renewable framework" source is a composite of: AEPC Yearbook / Annual Progress Report (annual, typically one-year lag in publication) National Renewable Energy Framework (NREF) 2017 and revisions Renewable Energy Subsidy Policy 2016 and updates AEPC-published feasibility studies for specific mini-grid and institutional projects Donor project completion reports (ADB, WB, EIB, UNDP) that flow through AEPC programmes # Relevance to project This is the anchor source for: aepc — the institution entity rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — the off-grid / mini-grid track data-rooftop-shs-deployment — district-level SHS counts agrivoltaics-and-land — AEPC as natural programme host claim-solar-political-coalition-is-rural — the rural-electrification politics And it is the first place to look when the question is "how much solar is actually deployed in Nepal total — utility + rooftop + off-grid?", because NEA's 141.94 MWp number captures only the first track. # Data-quality notes AEPC yearbooks lag by ~1–2 years in publication. District-level data fidelity is high (AEPC subsidizes per-system; each install has a record) but aggregation into the public yearbook is lagged and sometimes incomplete. The 1.1 M cumulative SHS count includes decommissioned and replaced systems; the operational fleet is likely 700,000–900,000 at any time. Mini-grid reliability / continuous-operation data is uneven; some grids have been dormant between donor-project cycles. # Gaps we would like to close A proper 2024 district-level registry — ideal source for the map layer in data-rooftop-shs-deployment. Operational-status snapshot of the ~30 mini-grids (how many are actually running as of 2024?). AEPC–NEA integration agreement — the grid-edge protocol for when grid reaches an off-grid site. # Related aepc rooftop-minigrid-offgrid data-rooftop-shs-deployment eib-rural-solar-phase2 — donor channel running through AEPC wb-grid-solar-ee-project — adjacent donor channel (NEA-side) nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 — the complementary utility-side primary source

pages.title: Arun-3 Project Status & PDA Summary (2025)
headings: What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 900 MW Arun-3 Hydroelectric Project — combining the 2014 Project Development Agreement (PDA) between Nepal and SJVN with 2024–2025 construction progress reports.
pages.body_text: Arun-3 Project Status & PDA Summary (2025) # Summary Reference compilation for the 900 MW Arun-3 Hydroelectric Project — combining the 2014 Project Development Agreement (PDA) between Nepal and SJVN with 2024–2025 construction progress reports. # Key Data Points Project basics Installed capacity: 900 MW (4 × 225 MW Francis turbines) Location: Phaksinda (dam) to Pukhuwa (powerhouse), Sankhuwasabha District Design: peaking run-of-river, 3.4 hours/day at full rated output Estimated cost: USD 1.6+ billion Annual energy: 3,924–4,019 GWh (design) Concession: 30-year Build-Own-Operate-Transfer (BOOT) Commercial structure (PDA, 2014) Developer: SJVN (Satluj Jal Vidyut Nigam), via subsidiary SAPDC Nepal's free-power share: 21.9% (~197 MW equivalent, royalty-free) Additional 10% offered to Nepal on commercial terms Remaining electricity exported to India via a new 400 kV DC line to Sitamarhi, Bihar Schedule and progress (December 2025) Construction start: 2018/19 Original COD target: March 2025 — not met SJVN applied for COD extension to September 2026; Project Review Panel formed to decide Physical progress: 74.61% (power generation side) Financial progress: 74.6% 400 kV cross-border transmission line: 58.3% physical progress # Relevance to Project Primary source for Arun-3's capacity, schedule, cost, and the Nepal–India free-power / export structure that makes Arun-3 a flagship example of India-developed, India-dispatched hydropower inside Nepal. # What This Source Does Not Cover Independent cost audit (SJVN figures are self-reported) Social / resettlement impact assessment outside SAPDC's own materials Seasonal generation shape beyond the 3.4-hour peaking design number Grid-side absorption constraints on the Indian side # See also arun-3 hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation

pages.title: Dudhkoshi Storage — NEA Proposal & Feasibility Record
headings: Dudhkoshi Storage — NEA Proposal & Feasibility Record
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project, covering the original 1980s ADB-supported feasibility, the 2021 restart with land acquisition, the 2024 NEA construction proposal to government, and the 2024–2025 financing conversations with AIIB.
pages.body_text: Dudhkoshi Storage — NEA Proposal & Feasibility Record # Summary Reference compilation for the Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project, covering the original 1980s ADB-supported feasibility, the 2021 restart with land acquisition, the 2024 NEA construction proposal to government, and the 2024–2025 financing conversations with AIIB. # Key Data Points Capacity: 635 MW (NEA / Investopaper) or 670 MW (ELC / Power Technology) — design basis varies between the two references Main powerhouse: 600 MW underground + 70 MW dam-toe ecological flow unit Annual energy (design): 3,377–3,444 GWh/year Dam: 200–220 m height at Lamidanda / Rabhuwaghat Headrace tunnel: 13.3 km, reservoir to Dhitung powerhouse Active reservoir volume: 1.581 MCM (per Power Technology) Estimated total cost: ~USD 2.2 billion (~NPR 308 billion) Districts: Okhaldhunga, Khotang, Solukhumbu Developer: Nepal Electricity Authority Financing track: AIIB sovereign funding proposed Schedule: construction from ~2027, commercial operation ~2031 (ELC / Power Technology estimate) Land: ~20,000 ropani private, ~162 households fully displaced, ~988 partially affected # Relevance to Project Primary source for Dudhkoshi's technical envelope, financing posture, social footprint and timeline — the three axes on which its buildability will actually be judged. Note: two self-consistent but divergent capacity numbers (635 MW vs 670 MW) are present in respected secondary sources; this page records both. # What This Source Does Not Cover Detailed seismic design basis (the project sits adjacent to Everest- region fault systems) Reservoir-induced seismicity modelling GLOF integration into the dam's design flood (the 2024 Thame GLOF in the same river system is directly relevant) # See also dudhkoshi-storage glof-risk storage-deficit

pages.title: EIB Promotion of Solar Energy in Rural and Semi-Urban Regions II
headings: EIB Promotion of Solar Energy in Rural and Semi-Urban Regions II (Phase II)
pages.excerpt: European Investment Bank line of up to EUR 8 million for the development of ground-mounted, grid-connected solar PV installations at four locations in Nepal. Implemented through the Government of Nepal with AEPC-facilitated site selection and NEA-grid interconnection.
pages.body_text: EIB Promotion of Solar Energy in Rural and Semi-Urban Regions II (Phase II) European Investment Bank line of up to EUR 8 million for the development of ground-mounted, grid-connected solar PV installations at four locations in Nepal. Implemented through the Government of Nepal with AEPC-facilitated site selection and NEA-grid interconnection. The second phase of a two-phase EIB solar programme; Phase I (earlier in the decade) financed smaller distributed and rural installations. Phase II is notable for being among the first EU-financing instruments for utility-scale grid-connected solar in Nepal, parallel to the World Bank Grid Solar & EE Project. # What the programme covers Credit size: up to EUR 8 million Instrument: EIB concessional loan to Government of Nepal Implementation partners: AEPC (site selection, community engagement), NEA (grid interconnection, off-take), Ministry of Energy (oversight) Target: four ground-mounted grid-connected solar PV sites Geographic focus: rural and semi-urban locations, likely in the Middle Hills region (site names to be verified from EIB project documentation and AEPC implementation reports) Time horizon: implementation expected 2023–2027 window # Key data points Specific figures require verification from the EIB project portal and AEPC implementation progress reports; the public-domain anchor facts: EUR 8 million credit ceiling Four ground-mounted sites Grid-connected (distinguishing it from Phase I distributed installations) Rural / semi-urban focus AEPC-implemented — this is critical: the programme runs on AEPC's cooperative-facilitation model rather than a pure-NEA tender, which makes it a test case for the AEPC-anchored solar-scaling thesis argued in rooftop-minigrid-offgrid and agrivoltaics-and-land # Why this source matters Three institutional-frame reasons: 1. It is a bridge between off-grid AEPC and utility-scale NEA tracks. Most financing either funds SHS/mini-grids via AEPC (track 3) or funds utility-IPP solar via NEA (track 1). EIB Phase II finances utility-scale grid-connected PV through AEPC — a rare institutional bridge. If it works well, it is a template for future donor programming. 2. Scale matches the agrivoltaic test case. EUR 8M / ~4 sites is ~EUR 2M per site ≈ NPR 280M per site ≈ 3–5 MWp. This is exactly the right size for early agrivoltaic pilot projects — big enough to matter, small enough that community co-operation can be worked out site-by-site. 3. Geographic focus on rural / semi-urban Middle Hills tests Zone C of the solar-resource-geography-nepal classification, where most Nepali household solar investment actually lives. # Parallel / adjacent programmes Programme | Donor | Channel | Track | ---|---|---|---| Grid Solar & EE Project (wb-grid-solar-ee-project) | World Bank | NEA | Utility | EIB Phase II (this page) | EIB | AEPC → NEA interconnect | Utility, AEPC-delivered | 960 MW Tender (nea-960mw-solar-tender) | Private IPP + debt | NEA | Utility | SREP Nepal | CIF / ADB / WB | AEPC | Off-grid | Karnali Off-grid Solar + BESS | WB | AEPC / NEA hybrid | Off-grid + grid edge | Biogas / improved cookstove programmes | Multi-donor | AEPC | Non-solar, non-electricity | # Documents and subsources EIB Project Page — eib.org/en/projects (search: Promotion of Solar Energy in Rural and Semi-Urban Regions II, Nepal) AEPC implementation monitoring reports — annual, AEPC internal with public summaries NEA Annual Report — references the programme in solar-projects rollup Ministry of Energy press releases — at site identification / inauguration milestones # Data-quality notes Site-specific details (exact locations, MWp per site, operator selection) have limited public-domain disclosure until AEPC / NEA implementation progress reports are published. The EUR 8M / four-site summary in the NEA FY 2024/25 annual report is the cleanest public reference anchor. Timeline ambitions and actual delivery pace may diverge — worth re-checking in FY 2025/26 NEA annual report. # Relevance to project Supporting source for: rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — AEPC as an institutional anchor beyond off-grid agrivoltaics-and-land — test-scale candidate for template pilots hybrid-siting-logic — mid-hill zone siting experiment aepc — institutional expansion of AEPC mandate claim-solar-political-coalition-is-rural — the rural-cooperative politics this programme rides on # Gaps we would like Publication of the four specific site locations and their MWp, district, developer, and COD target. AEPC implementation status reports at project-mid-point (likely 2026). Cross-reference with any agrivoltaic pilot element — if the EIB-AEPC line funds an agrivoltaic demonstration, it is the single most important test for the agrivoltaics-and-land thesis. # Related wb-grid-solar-ee-project — parallel donor programme (NEA-track) aepc-renewable-framework — institutional home nea-960mw-solar-tender — private-finance counterpart rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — the track this programme belongs to nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 — the public-disclosure anchor

pages.title: Global Solar Atlas (Nepal coverage)
headings: Why this source is the map-layer anchor
pages.excerpt: The primary satellite-derived solar resource dataset for Nepal's explorer map layer and the reference cross-check for every GHI / DNI number used elsewhere in the wiki. Provided as a free-to-use web-tile and downloadable-data service by Solargis under the World Bank Group ESMAP p
pages.body_text: Global Solar Atlas (Nepal Coverage) The primary satellite-derived solar resource dataset for Nepal's explorer map layer and the reference cross-check for every GHI / DNI number used elsewhere in the wiki. Provided as a free-to-use web-tile and downloadable-data service by Solargis under the World Bank Group ESMAP programme. Cross-validated against the in-situ wb-esmap-solar-resource-assessment pyranometer network (14 stations, 2015–2019), so the derived spatial maps carry ~±5% accuracy at station locations and somewhat wider error bands in untested high-mountain terrain. # What the source provides Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) — annual and monthly means at ~9 arc-second (~250 m) spatial resolution Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) — same spatial detail Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance (DIF) — for tilted-array optimization Photovoltaic Electricity Output (PVOUT) — modelled output for a reference PV system at each location Optimal tilt angle maps Air temperature 2-m height climatology Tile-ready map service (XYZ / WMS endpoints for integration into web maps) # Nepal-specific findings GHI range across Nepal: ~3.8 to ~6.5 kWh/m²/day Highest GHI zones: trans-Himalayan rain-shadow (Mustang, Dolpa, upper Humla, upper Manang) — 5.8–6.5 kWh/m²/day Lowest GHI zones: Kathmandu Valley and east-facing mid-hill slopes — 4.2–4.6 kWh/m²/day Terai plains: 4.8–5.3 kWh/m²/day — broadly uniform with the north Indian plains PVOUT (specific yield): 1,500–1,800 kWh/kWp/year in trans-Himalayan, 1,350–1,500 in Terai, 1,150–1,350 in mid-hill — the yield hierarchy that translates GHI into MWh/MWp # Why this source is the map-layer anchor The wiki explorer's proposed layer (see data-map-inventory, data-nepal-solar-resource-zones) is built from Global Solar Atlas classifications, specifically: 1. Raw satellite GHI grid is classified into five zone bands (A, B, C, D, E) by threshold 2. Zone polygons are intersected with district boundaries for display 3. Map tiles are served directly from the Global Solar Atlas XYZ endpoint as an overlay basemap option in the explorer The attribution required by the Global Solar Atlas licence (CC BY 4.0) is included in the layer legend and the map credits. # Licence and data access Licence: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Attribution: "Solargis, The World Bank Group" in any derived product Tile service: XYZ / WMS endpoints (see Global Solar Atlas documentation) Data download: National-level GeoTIFF layers available directly from the Global Solar Atlas download section (annual GHI, DNI, PVOUT) API: Solargis commercial API for higher-resolution or bespoke time-series (not needed for map display) # Cross-validation against ESMAP station data Global Solar Atlas vs measured (from wb-esmap-solar-resource-assessment): Station | GSA GHI | Measured GHI | Bias | ---|---:|---:|---:| Kathmandu | 4.4 | 4.3 | +2% | Pokhara | 4.5 | 4.5 | 0% | Nepalgunj | 5.2 | 5.1 | +2% | Simara | 4.9 | 4.9 | 0% | Biratnagar | 5.0 | 4.9 | +2% | Dhankuta | 4.5 | 4.4 | +2% | Jumla | 5.3 | 5.2 | +2% | Surkhet | 4.9 | 4.8 | +2% | Jomsom | 5.5 | 6.0 | −8% | Trans-Himalayan is the one zone where satellite systematically under-estimates GHI (Jomsom 8% bias). This is the most important caveat: for Mustang / Dolpa / upper Manang planning, field measurement should be used where available, and the "Zone A 5.8–6.5" range cited in the wiki is calibrated to measurement rather than to the satellite layer. # Related wb-esmap-solar-resource-assessment — the calibration source solar-resource-geography-nepal — the concept-page interpretation data-nepal-solar-resource-zones — the classification used in the map layer data-map-inventory — where this appears in the explorer irena-remap-nepal — independent scenario use of the same data

pages.title: Green Hydrogen Roadmap for Nepal
pages.excerpt: Strategic roadmap for producing green hydrogen from Nepal's surplus monsoon hydropower via electrolysis. Envisions an entirely new domestic industrial ecosystem absorbing stranded wet-season power.
pages.body_text: Green Hydrogen Roadmap for Nepal # Summary Strategic roadmap for producing green hydrogen from Nepal's surplus monsoon hydropower via electrolysis. Envisions an entirely new domestic industrial ecosystem absorbing stranded wet-season power. # Key Data Points Primary target: domestic green ammonia/fertilizer production (Nepal imports 100% of chemical fertilizers) A domestic green fertilizer plant could absorb up to 500 MW of continuous baseload power Replacing coal in Nepal's cement clinker production would require 227,743 tonnes of green hydrogen annually, needing 2,002 MW of dedicated hydropower Projected viable production cost: $2.15/kg by 2035 Government adopted Green Hydrogen Policy in 2024 with tax exemptions and regulatory framework Commercial pilots targeted for 2025–2027, industrial scale by 2035 # Relevance to Project Central to green-hydrogen-nepal and the demand-creation argument in domestic-led-hydro-strategy. Addresses stranded-generation by creating domestic demand for surplus monsoon power.

pages.title: ICIMOD HKH Glacier Change Summary
pages.excerpt: Regional glacier change assessment for the Hindu Kush Himalaya. Documents the scale and pace of glacier retreat affecting Nepal's river systems and long-term hydropower viability.
pages.body_text: ICIMOD HKH Glacier Change Summary # Summary Regional glacier change assessment for the Hindu Kush Himalaya. Documents the scale and pace of glacier retreat affecting Nepal's river systems and long-term hydropower viability. # Key Data Points Glacier area across HKH fell by ~12% between 1990 and 2020, with faster recent ice loss Nepal-specific inventories show substantial long-run glacier retreat Glacier retreat rate: ~0.4–0.5m water equivalent per year Under high-emission scenarios, 65% glacier volume reduction projected by 2100 # Relevance to Project Underpins peak-water concept and glof-risk. Relevant to koshi-basin (278 glaciers, 51 km³ ice). The 12% area loss and 65% volume projection are key numbers for the climate dimension of the public energy-system story, including the peak-water framing for the 2040–2070 build window.

pages.title: ICIMOD Koshi Basin Programme
pages.excerpt: ICIMOD's research programme on the koshi-basin, covering downstream impacts, agricultural dependency, and flood dynamics. Supports the geopolitical significance of Nepal's eastern river system.
pages.body_text: ICIMOD Koshi Basin Programme # Summary ICIMOD's research programme on the koshi-basin, covering downstream impacts, agricultural dependency, and flood dynamics. Supports the geopolitical significance of Nepal's eastern river system. # Key Data Points Koshi basin supports >40 million people Contributes disproportionately high sediment loads to the Ganges system Koshi irrigates ~500,000–600,000 ha in Bihar The Koshi is known as the "River of Sorrow" in Bihar due to shifting course and catastrophic flooding # Relevance to Project Central to hydro-geopolitics and ganges-contribution. Also relevant to sediment-as-design-constraint given extreme sediment loads. .

pages.title: ICIMOD Threats to the Koshi River Basin
headings: ICIMOD Threats to the Koshi River Basin
pages.excerpt: Detailed assessment of sediment dynamics, erosion, and environmental threats in the koshi-basin. Provides the evidence base for treating sediment as a central engineering and planning constraint.
pages.body_text: ICIMOD Threats to the Koshi River Basin # Summary Detailed assessment of sediment dynamics, erosion, and environmental threats in the koshi-basin. Provides the evidence base for treating sediment as a central engineering and planning constraint. # Key Data Points Koshi carries extreme sediment loads into the Ganges system Sediment, flood, and erosion risks are interconnected Climate change is intensifying GLOF, landslide, and sediment-pulse risks # Relevance to Project Supports sediment-as-design-constraint, glof-risk, and the hazard dimension of buildability. .

pages.title: India Cross-Border Trade in Electricity Guidelines (2018)
headings: India Cross-Border Trade in Electricity Guidelines (2018)
pages.excerpt: India's regulatory framework governing cross-border electricity trade. Defines approval mechanisms, designated authorities, and conditions for Nepal's electricity exports to India. Critically, includes restrictions on projects with certain foreign investment.
pages.body_text: India Cross-Border Trade in Electricity Guidelines (2018) # Summary India's regulatory framework governing cross-border electricity trade. Defines approval mechanisms, designated authorities, and conditions for Nepal's electricity exports to India. Critically, includes restrictions on projects with certain foreign investment. # Key Data Points Establishes Indian approval requirements for Nepal power imports India explicitly refuses purchase of electricity from Nepali projects with Chinese equity investment or Chinese EPC contractors Creates the monopsony market structure Nepal must navigate for exports Shapes which projects can access the Indian market vs which are locked out # Relevance to Project Central to india-energy-relationship and hydro-geopolitics. Shapes the export constraint dimension of seasonal-arbitrage-trap. source-review note. Public source review highlighted the China-exclusion mechanism. [!gap] Need updated post-2024 versions of these guidelines — the approval regime may have expanded.

pages.title: IRENA Renewable Readiness Assessment and REmap Nepal
headings: IRENA Renewable Readiness Assessment and REmap Nepal
Key data points (composite from RRA + REmap + Cost Database)
Global LCOE benchmarks (IRENA Renewable Cost Database)
Why this source matters to the project
pages.excerpt: Primary international source for solar and renewable cost benchmarking against Nepal specifics. Two IRENA document streams are relevant:
pages.body_text: IRENA Renewable Readiness Assessment and REmap Nepal Primary international source for solar and renewable cost benchmarking against Nepal specifics. Two IRENA document streams are relevant: 1. IRENA Renewable Readiness Assessment (RRA) — Nepal (2017, with subsequent updates). Country-level diagnostic; identifies barriers and potential across solar, wind, and small hydro. 2. IRENA REmap Nepal scenario analysis. Quantitative pathway modelling for higher renewables shares in Nepal's electricity and total energy mix. Plus the IRENA Renewable Capacity Statistics (annual, global) and Renewable Cost Database — which provide the global LCOE benchmarks this project uses in data-solar-hydro-lcoe and solar-lcoe-crossover. # What these sources cover Solar PV technical potential by Nepal resource zone Wind potential (limited but non-trivial in Mustang + parts of Koshi) Small hydro potential (overlaps with wecs-hydropower-potential-2019) Barriers analysis: financing, policy, grid integration, skills LCOE benchmarks for Nepal-comparable countries Scenario pathways to 2030 / 2050 with renewables share Policy recommendations and institutional framework proposals # Key data points (composite from RRA + REmap + Cost Database) ## Solar (Nepal-specific) Estimated solar PV technical potential: ~50,000–100,000 MWp (various IRENA framings; caveats on siteability) Mean Nepal GHI: ~4.7–4.9 kWh/m²/day (matches Global Solar Atlas) Suggested short-term target: >1 GW by 2030 (REmap Accelerated Case); policy + financing gaps identified as primary blocker, not resource ## Global LCOE benchmarks (IRENA Renewable Cost Database) Utility-scale solar PV weighted global LCOE 2010 → 2024: $0.359 → $0.044 per kWh (−88% real) Module price 2010 → 2024: $2.00/W → ~$0.10/W (−95%) Onshore wind LCOE 2010 → 2024: $0.113 → $0.033 per kWh BESS (4h utility) LCOE 2020 → 2024: ~$190 → ~$95 per MWh Capacity-weighted auction-low solar tariff 2024: ~$0.020–$0.030/kWh (GCC, India) These are the numbers the solar-lcoe-crossover argument rides on. # IRENA's diagnostic frame for Nepal The RRA identifies five priority barriers: 1. Policy coherence — fragmented responsibility across MoEWRI / NEA / AEPC / ERC 2. Financing access — limited local-currency long-tenor finance for renewables 3. Grid integration — transmission and distribution readiness for variable resources 4. Institutional capacity — solar-specific engineering and O&M depth is thin 5. Land and permits — no agrivoltaic framework, fragmented land tenure Most of these overlap with the bottleneck-hierarchy for hydro, with one addition specific to solar: absence of land/permitting framework for utility-scale ground-mount. # Why this source matters to the project Provides the global LCOE benchmarks the solar-lcoe-crossover and data-solar-hydro-lcoe pages rely on. Gives an externally-credentialed estimate of Nepal solar potential without the over-promise of some national documents. Frames the barriers analysis in language compatible with donor programs — useful for mapping AEPC / EIB / WB programme gaps. The REmap pathway is a reasonable benchmark against which to judge the NEA/MoEWRI 2024/25 actual trajectory (960 MW tender + 170 MW PPA + 142 MW operating). # Data-quality and caveats IRENA's Nepal "technical potential" estimates vary widely (50,000–100,000+ MWp) depending on whether high-mountain inaccessible land is counted. Only the Zone A trans-Himalayan + Zone B Terai + Zone C mid-hill deployable fraction (~15,000–25,000 MWp) matters for real planning; see data-solar-fleet-inventory potential pyramid. IRENA REmap pathways are normative scenarios, not forecasts. Use for direction of travel, not for calendar-year predictions. Global LCOE benchmarks reflect weighted averages; Nepal-specific numbers should always be context-adjusted for grid costs, land costs, and FX. # Relevance to project Anchor citation for: solar-lcoe-crossover data-solar-hydro-lcoe data-solar-fleet-inventory (potential pyramid) solar-resource-geography-nepal claim-solar-cheaper-than-small-hydro # Related wb-esmap-solar-resource-assessment — the measured-resource complement global-solar-atlas-nepal — the satellite-derived complement wecs-energy-synopsis-2024 — the domestic energy-balance frame wb-country-economic-memo-2025 — macroeconomic context

pages.title: JICA IPSDP Final Report Annexes (Vol. 2)
headings: What this source does not cover
pages.excerpt: This is the Annexes volume (Vol. 2) of the JICA Study Team's Final Report for the (IPSDP) — the same study whose compact policy conclusions are captured in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025. The executive summary is where the headline numbers and milestone ladder live; this volume i
pages.body_text: JICA IPSDP Final Report Annexes (Vol. 2) # Summary This is the Annexes volume (Vol. 2) of the JICA Study Team's Final Report for the (IPSDP) — the same study whose compact policy conclusions are captured in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025. The executive summary is where the headline numbers and milestone ladder live; this volume is where the _workings_ live — the demand forecast inputs, the MP2014 project scorecards, the existing-plant inventories, the hydropower sequencing logic, and the geology, hydrology and seismology that bound what can actually be built. The volume is 315 pages and contains Annexes 1–6 only. The PDF's own table of contents lists 16 annexes in total; Annexes 7–16 (power generation planning, cross-border trade status, power system planning, SEA, economic and financial analysis, financing, surplus utilisation, governance, milestones, capacity development) are carried in companion volumes that are not currently in the wiki's raw-source set. For this wiki, this is the authoritative upstream source for any claim of the form "JICA/MoEWRI's IPSDP study assumes X" about demand, existing fleet state, hydropower portfolio composition, or development-plan review — the executive summary is not granular enough on its own. Key cross-links: moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025, nea, seasonal-arbitrage-trap, kulekhani-cascade, kali-gandaki-a, tanahu-hydropower, upper-arun. # Contents (Annexes 1–6 only) Annex | Title | First page | -------|-------|-----------:| 1 | General Information of Nepal (economy, topography, geology, seismology, climate, hydrology, air quality) | 5 | 2 | Current Status of the Power Sector in Nepal (organisational structure, legal system, reform, corporate entities) | 55 | 3 | Current Situation of the Electric Power Facilities (existing generation fleet, transmission, distribution) | 146 | 4 | Review of Existing Development Plans (MP2014 screening, Multi-Criteria Analysis scorecards) | 174 | 5 | Power Demand Forecast (sectoral sales mix, consumer growth, electrification) | 196 | 6 | Hydropower Development Plan (bulk of the volume; portfolio composition and sequencing) | 244 | Annexes 7–16 listed in the front-matter TOC (Power Generation Development Planning; Current Status of Power Trade; Power System Development Planning; SEA; Economic and Financial Analysis; Financing; Utilisation of Surplus Electricity; Governance; Milestones & Future Outlook; Capacity Development) are _not present_ in this PDF. # Key claims Annex 3 (p. 147) states that NEA owns hydropower plants with an installed capacity of and designed annual generation of at an average plant factor of . Only Kulekhani I and Kulekhani II operate as storage (STO); the rest are ROR or PROR. Annex 3 generation-type table (p. 146) codifies four hydropower types used throughout the IPSDP optimisation — , (peak run-of-river with daily-to-weekly pondage), (storage / reservoir), and (pumped storage) — and notes the winter-peak / monsoon-spill economics that drive the seasonal-arbitrage-trap argument. Annex 4 (p. 174–175) records how the 2014 Nationwide Master Plan (MP2014) filtered candidate projects via Multi-Criteria Analysis with exclusions for installed capacity , dam height , project cost , annual flow-regulation rate , reservoir resettlement , or sites inside national parks / wildlife conservation areas / world heritage. The MP2014 scorecard table on p. 175 lists unit capacity, total energy, dry energy, project cost, unit cost, EIRR and FIRR for Dudh Koshi, Sun Koshi 3, Lower Badigad, Andhi Khola, Chera-1, Madi, Nalsyau Gad, Naumure (W. Rapti), Kokhajor-1, Lower Jhimruk and the rest of the MP2014 list. Annex 5 (p. 196) puts sectoral sales composition and consumer-growth rate at approximately with household electrification reaching in 2021 and a stated GoN target of . # Local source Local workspace copy: jica_ipsdp_main_report_vol2.pdf [!cite] jica-ipsdp-main-report-vol2 PDF created via Word → Adobe PDF. File is byte-identical to in the same directory (verified via SHA-256); the canonical path used by the wiki is . # What this source does not cover Annexes 7–16 of the IPSDP final report (power system planning, cross-border trade, SEA, financing, surplus-electricity utilisation, governance, milestone ladder, capacity development). Use moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 for the compact statement of those or queue companion JICA volumes when available. Monthly plant-level generation or trade settlement data. Project-by-project post-study construction progress after the 2024 cutoff.

pages.body_text: Kali Gandaki A — ADB Performance Evaluation & Wikipedia # Summary Reference compilation for the 144 MW Kali Gandaki A plant at Mirmi, Syangja — combining the ADB's post-project performance evaluation (the plant was ADB/JBIC co-financed) with the Wikipedia-aggregated operational history and the recent NEA rehabilitation tender. # Key Data Points Installed: 144 MW (3 × 48 MW Toshiba Francis units) Gross head: 115 m Design annual energy: ~860 GWh Average actual output: ~592 GWh/year since commissioning Construction: 1997–2002; turbines commissioned Mar / Apr / May 2002 Total cost: USD 354.8 million (~NPR 50 billion at the time) Funding: ADB + Japan Bank for International Cooperation Owner/operator: Nepal Electricity Authority (NEA) Location: Mirmi, Syangja District; Kali Gandaki–Andhikhola confluence Status (2024): Nepal sought design & supervision consultants for a rehabilitation of the three-unit plant # Relevance to Project Primary source for Kali Gandaki A's capacity, head, generation, funding stack and rehabilitation posture. Useful benchmark for Nepali- developed, Nepali-dispatched hydro at moderate scale — the foil to India-developed export projects like arun-3 and upper-karnali. # What This Source Does Not Cover Current reservoir (pondage) operation strategy Full seasonal generation shape Detailed explanation of the >250 GWh gap between design energy (~860 GWh) and average actual output (~592 GWh) # See also kali-gandaki-a gandaki-basin run-of-river-hydropower

pages.title: MoEWRI IPSDP Executive Summary
headings: What this source does not cover
pages.excerpt: This executive summary is the clearest compact statement of the current MoEWRI/JICA planning logic for Nepal's long-horizon power buildout through 2040. It ties together demand growth, hydropower sequencing, 400 kV backbone and interconnection expansion, export strategy, and fina
pages.body_text: MoEWRI IPSDP Executive Summary # Summary This executive summary is the clearest compact statement of the current MoEWRI/JICA planning logic for Nepal's long-horizon power buildout through 2040. It ties together demand growth, hydropower sequencing, 400 kV backbone and interconnection expansion, export strategy, and financing needs in one document instead of scattering them across older nea or donor studies. The report is especially valuable because page 39 turns the plan into a milestone ladder with explicit dates for export crossover, self-sufficiency, the 28 GW government target, and a later clean-export role in South Asia. Section 2.4 then links those milestones to concrete trade channels, grid buildout, and wholesale arrangements with India and Bangladesh rather than treating exports as an abstract objective. Section 3 adds the guardrails the plan says must shape buildability, including environmental-flow management, protected-area restrictions, and the need to package hydropower with transmission and institutional reform. For this wiki, the document is a backbone source for india-energy-relationship, bangladesh-trade-route, niettp, and project-sequencing questions that depend on an official state planning frame rather than a single project promoter's view. # Contents Section | Page | ---------|------| 1. Introduction | 1 | 1.1 Background | 1 | 1.2 Objectives | 1 | 1.3 Basic Approaches on IPSDP | 2 | 2. IPSDP | 3 | 2.1 Outline of IPSDP | 3 | 2.2 Basic Understandings of the Development in Power Sector | 3 | 2.2.1 Organizational Structure of the Power Sector | 3 | 2.2.2 Involvement of Existing and On-going Power Development Plans in the Power Sector | 4 | 2.2.3 Hydropower Development Plan | 5 | 2.2.4 Power Trade | 7 | 2.3 The Study for Development Scenarios | 8 | 2.3.1 Direction of Development Scenarios | 8 | 2.3.2 Setting of Development Scenarios | 10 | 2.3.3 Analysis Results of Development Scenarios and Selection of Optimum Scenario | 12 | 2.4 Formulation of Optimum Scenario | 12 | 2.4.1 Power Demand Forecast | 14 | 2.4.2 Power Generation Development Planning | 15 | 2.4.3 The Power System Plan (PSP) and power trade with neighboring countries | 18 | 2.4.4 Financial Analysis of IPSDP | 22 | 2.4.5 Investment Plan | 28 | 2.4.6 Strategic Environmental Assessment | 31 | 3. Development Milestones and Future Outlook for IPSDP | 34 | 3.1 Development Milestones and Pathways towards 2040 in IPSDP | 34 | 3.2 Challenges of IPSDP | 36 | 3.3 Required Transformations and Future Outlook for the Power Sector | 37 | 3.4 Challenges and Recommendations for IPSDP | 39 | # Key claims Figure 3.1-1 on page 39 marks as the year when power exports surpass imports. The same milestone ladder marks as the point when self-sufficiency in electricity supply is achieved. The ladder marks as the year when the government's 28,000 MW development target is achieved. The ladder marks as the stage when Nepal contributes clean export to the South Asian region. The page-39 era buckets attach named projects to each window: Rasuwagadhi, Sanjen, Upper Modi A, Rahughat, Upper Trishuli 3B, Madhya Bhotekoshi, Upper Trishuli-1, and Arun 3 in the block; Tanahu HEP, Tamor Storage, Madi Storage HEP, Manang Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki Ka, Bheri 4, Tila-1, and Tila-2 in ; Lower Seti, Uttarganga STO, Phukot Karnali, Nalsyau Gad STO, Upper Arun, Chainpur Seti, Dudhkoshi STO, Sunkoshi 3 STO, Lower Badigad STO, Budhi Gandaki, Upper Marsyangdi-2, Dudh Koshi-IV, Upper Tamor, Bheri-2, West Seti STO, and SR-06 STO in ; and Betan Karnali, Kimathanka Arun, Bharbung, Mugu Karnali STO, Tamakoshi 3, Khimti Shivalaya STO, Bajhang Upper Seti, Naumure STO, Upper Mugu Karnali, Bheri-1, and the Humla Karnali Cascade in . Section 2.4 frames the wholesale-trade menu around NEA offtake, power-trading companies, and direct overseas trade to DISCOs/customers in India and Bangladesh, with the adjacent text on pages 24-26 adding grid-connection, market, and long-term cross-border PPA mechanics that sit behind those channels. The SEA mitigation table on page 37 says hydropower planning must evaluate and manage environmental flows with reference to the IFC 2018 handbook, and it says projects in protected areas defined by Nepal's National Parks and Wildlife Conservation Act require prior permission and cannot assume river modification is automatically permissible. [!cite] moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 Cover date is December 2024 even though the local filename ends in 2025; the milestone ladder and policy framing cited above come from pages 25, 37, and 39 of the PDF. # Local source Local workspace copy: moewri_ipsdp_exec_summary_2025.pdf # What this source does not cover project-by-project construction progress after the study cutoff plant-level monthly generation or trade data # See also data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 nepal-power-trade-schemes environmental-flow-policy ipsdp-2025-vs-wiki-claims

pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 1,902 MW Mugu Karnali Storage Hydroelectric Project — Nepal's largest proposed generation asset, in pre-feasibility under Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUC) and open to public–private partnership bidding.
pages.body_text: Mugu Karnali Storage — Feasibility, Bids & EOI Record (2024–2025) # Summary Reference compilation for the 1,902 MW Mugu Karnali Storage Hydroelectric Project — Nepal's largest proposed generation asset, in pre-feasibility under Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUC) and open to public–private partnership bidding. # Key Data Points Capacity: 1,902 MW Type: Storage / pumped-storage; 10 Francis turbines Gross head: ~285 m Design annual energy: ~5,883 GWh River: Karnali District: Mugu (registry); Bajura also referenced in early materials Developer: Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUCL / VUC) — state-owned Structure: PPP, VUC seeking 51% partnership stake Survey licence: granted by DoED; 2-year feasibility deadline with extension possible to 5 years Expressions of interest (2024–2025): - Adani Group — EOI (no formal bid as of January 2025) - Chowdhury Group (CG Energo Pvt Ltd) — formal bid submitted, only competitor on record as of January 2025 Planned schedule (Power Technology): construction from ~2030, COD ~2034 # Relevance to Project Primary source for Mugu Karnali's scale, schedule, bidding posture and the India-vs-Bangladesh developer interest that makes it politically visible. Notable because 1,902 MW is larger than Nepal's entire current operating hydropower fleet. # What This Source Does Not Cover Detailed hydrology / sediment / seismic study outcomes Transmission evacuation plan — a project this size implies new 400 kV or higher corridor infrastructure that does not yet exist Environmental / social scoping; project is at pre-feasibility and no full EIA has been disclosed # See also mugu-karnali-storage karnali-basin storage-deficit

pages.title: National Water Plan, Nepal
pages.excerpt: Older but widely cited national planning document for Nepal's water resources. Provides the foundational estimates of Nepal's contribution to the Ganges system and total annual runoff.
pages.body_text: National Water Plan, Nepal # Summary Older but widely cited national planning document for Nepal's water resources. Provides the foundational estimates of Nepal's contribution to the Ganges system and total annual runoff. # Key Data Points Nepal's total average annual runoff: ~225 BCM Nepal-origin rivers contribute ~40% of Ganges mean annual flow Nepal-origin rivers contribute ~70% of Ganges dry-season flow at Farakka Per-unit-area freshwater output among the highest on earth # Relevance to Project Source for the ganges-contribution numbers used across the evidence base. . The 40%/70% figures are central to hydro-geopolitics and india-energy-relationship. [!warning] These figures are historical estimates. The exact percentages should be treated cautiously — the underlying hydrology predates recent climate shifts — but the strategic direction remains intact.

pages.title: NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25

pages.excerpt: The most current official snapshot of Nepal's electricity system. Covers installed capacity, generation mix, trade volumes, grid infrastructure, and project pipeline for fiscal year 2024/25 (Nepali FY 2081/82).
pages.body_text: NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25 # Summary The most current official snapshot of Nepal's electricity system. Covers installed capacity, generation mix, trade volumes, grid infrastructure, and project pipeline for fiscal year 2024/25 (Nepali FY 2081/82). # Image Catalog This run is ingesting roughly 130 images from the FY 2024/25 annual report; the current atlas pass surfaced 131 real-image candidates for review. The atlas manifest lives at nea_annual_report_2024_2025_image_atlas.json. # Key Data Points Total installed generation capacity: 3,591.262 MW (hydro 3,389.912 MW, solar 141.940 MW, thermal 53.410 MW, bagasse 6 MW) Hydropower is 94.4% of installed generation 22 new projects (434 MW combined) commissioned in FY 2024/25 Available energy: 15,641 GWh (34% NEA/subsidiaries, 55% IPPs, 11% imported from India) kulekhani-cascade generated 279.8 GWh tanahu-hydropower approximately 67% complete 572 projects in broader license pipeline (311 survey, 180 generation, 81 operation) # Relevance to Project Primary source for current installed capacity, fleet composition, generation split, and trade volumes. Supersedes older NEA FY 2022/23 data in several legacy summaries. # What This Source Does Not Cover Seasonal monthly breakdown of generation and trade Plant-level curtailment data Audited financial statements (separate document) [!note] The World Bank's 2025 Economic Memo cites ~2,990 MW by end-2024. The difference from 3,591 MW reflects the date gap — NEA's report covers through mid-2025. Both are correct at their respective cutoff dates.
