pages.body_text: NEA Transmission Annual Book 2077 # Summary Official Nepal Electricity Authority annual-book publication for FY 2019/20 (B.S. 2077). The volume mixes directorate narrative, project status notes, site/substation photographs, appendix tables, and map pages. For the wiki, its highest-value material is in the transmission chapter and appendix: project-by-project line/substation status, existing substation capacity, and under-construction high-voltage grid inventory. # Contents Section | Page | ---------|------| Message from the Minister | 2 | Message from the Secretary | 4 | Board of Directorates | 5 | Organisation Structure | 6 | Deputy Managing Directors | 7 | Managing Director's Report | 8 | Generation Directorate | 21 | Transmission Directorate | 43 | Distribution and Consumer Services Directorate | 76 | Planning, Monitoring and Information Technology Directorate | 103 | Engineering Services Directorate | 110 | Project Management Directorate | 141 | NEA's Subsidiary & Associate Companies | 159 | Central Activities | 192 | Administrative Directorate | 199 | Finance Directorate | 204 | Statistic & Schematics | 220 | Electricity Tariff | 232-238 | Appendix | 239-263 | Map | 264-265 | # Relevance to Project This is the primary official source for NEA's mid-2020 view of the transmission system. It is especially useful for: hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone stage/status details dhalkebar-muzaffarpur substation and energization context appendix-level substation capacity and under-construction inventory pages project/program pages that need official inline figures rather than generic district scenery # Local Source Local workspace copy: nea_transmission_annual_book_2077.pdf # What This Source Does Not Cover post-2020 commissioning outcomes for projects still under construction here later FY 2024/25 system totals already superseded in nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 detailed load-flow or dispatch modelling

pages.title: Nepal Transmission System — 400 kV Landscape & Cross-Border Plan (2025)
headings: Nepal Transmission System — 400 kV Landscape & Cross-Border Plan (2025)
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for Nepal's current and planned high-voltage transmission assets — the 400 kV east–west backbone and parallel central corridor, the legacy 220 kV and 132 kV grid, and the cross-border interconnections with India (ten links at five voltage levels). Drawn from
pages.body_text: Nepal Transmission System — 400 kV Landscape & Cross-Border Plan (2025) # Summary Reference compilation for Nepal's current and planned high-voltage transmission assets — the 400 kV east–west backbone and parallel central corridor, the legacy 220 kV and 132 kV grid, and the cross-border interconnections with India (ten links at five voltage levels). Drawn from NEA's 2024/25 annual report, NEA Transmission Directorate publications, the Millennium Challenge Account Nepal (MCA-Nepal) compact programme documents, and news reporting of individual commissioning events. # Key Data Points 400 kV operational backbone Hetauda–Dhalkebar–Inaruwa (HDDI) — first and currently only 400 kV east–west backbone inside Nepal. Commissioned in segments 2018–2022. Total spine ~300 km. Feeds the Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur cross-border link (400 kV bipole, operational since 2016, ADB + KfW co-financed). This is the sole operational 400 kV export route. 400 kV under construction / implementation MCA central 400 kV (Lapsiphedi–Ratmate–Hetauda–Damauli–Butwal) — the MCA-Nepal compact's headline 315 km transmission line, partially energised in 2025. Completion target 2026–2027. Gorakhpur–New Butwal 400 kV — second cross-border link, under construction. Commissioning target mid-2026. Inaruwa–Purnea 400 kV — third cross-border, implementation phase. Critical to Arun-3 evacuation once the plant is online. Lamki–Bareilly 400 kV — western cross-border at implementation setup; completion horizon 2027+. 220 kV operational Khimti–Dhalkebar 220 kV — original IPP evacuation corridor Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV — east–west spine at 220 kV Dana–Kushma–New Butwal 220 kV — west-central hydropower corridor 132 kV cross-border (legacy, operational) Kataiya–Kushaha (since 1971, the historic first link) Raxaul–Parwanipur, New Nautanwa–Mainahiya, Tanakpur–Mahendranagar Planned / under construction cross-border Nepalgunj–Nanpara 132 kV (under construction) Chameliya–Jauljibi 220 kV (planned, far-west) System-level parameters Nepal–India transfer capacity, 2025: ~2,300 MW total across all interconnections combined Current concurrent peak export: ~1,200 MW (monsoon 2024–25) Grid code: NEA grid code 2019 (amended 2022); Indian-side dispatch via NRLDC # Relevance to Project Primary source for the voltage levels, statuses, schedules and strategic roles of Nepal's ten currently-tracked corridors and ten cross-border interconnections. Used as the baseline citation for all per-corridor entity pages. # What This Source Does Not Cover Actual segment-level substation capacities (published only in confidential planning documents) Indian-side evacuation constraints beyond the border crossings Load flow studies under different generation dispatch scenarios Battery and STATCOM augmentation plans # See also hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone dhalkebar-muzaffarpur mca-central-400 hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation

pages.title: "Investigative Report: PPA Data Retrieval from ICRA & CARE Ratings Nepal"
headings: Investigative Report: PPA Data Retrieval from ICRA & CARE Ratings Nepal
Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) — The Benchmark
pages.excerpt: Source type: Research compilation — Google Docs export (15 pages) Original: Mission-based financial investigation sourced from ICRA Nepal surveillance reports (A.996, B.119), CARE Ratings Nepal rating rationales, and statutory filings.
pages.body_text: Investigative Report: PPA Data Retrieval from ICRA & CARE Ratings Nepal Source type: Research compilation — Google Docs export (15 pages) Original: Mission-based financial investigation sourced from ICRA Nepal surveillance reports (A.996, B.119), CARE Ratings Nepal rating rationales, and statutory filings. # Summary This investigation targets data gaps in Nepal's hydropower financial landscape, reconstructed from primary credit rating agency reports. It covers: Mission 1: Forensic reconstruction of Sahas Urja Limited (86 MW Solu Khola) — the benchmark private IPP success story Mission 2: Comparative PPA tariff matrix for five priority projects (Chilime, Sanima Mai, Rasuwagadhi, Trishuli 3A, Nyadi) Mission 3: Mathematical proof of NEA subsidiary vs. private IPP rate differential (37.1% revenue advantage via Chilime anomaly) Mission 4: Insurance market quantitative verification (Sanima Mai pays 1.80% of gross revenue in non-life premiums) # Key Findings ## PPA Rate Matrix (VERIFIED) Project | Capacity (MW) | Wet Rate (NPR/kWh) | Dry Rate (NPR/kWh) | Entity Type | PPA Year | ---|---|---|---|---|---| Chilime | 22.1 | 4.08 (Excess) | 8.17 (Regular) | NEA Subsidiary | 1997 | Sanima Mai (Phase 1) | 22.0 | 4.80 | 8.40 | Private IPP | 2010 | Sanima Mai Cascade | 7.0 | 5.52 | 9.66 | Private IPP | 2013 | Rasuwagadhi | 111.0 | 4.80 | 8.40 | NEA Subsidiary (via Chilime) | 2011 | Upper Trishuli 3A | 60.0 | N/A (Internal) | N/A (Internal) | NEA Owned | N/A | Upper Trishuli 3B | 37.0 | 4.80 | 8.40 | Subsidiary Corp | — | Nyadi | 30.0 | 4.80 | 8.40 | Private IPP | 2015 | ## Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) — The Benchmark Final build cost: NPR 172–175 million/MW (industry average often exceeds NPR 200–250M) FY 2080/81 revenue: NPR 2.648 billion Net profit: NPR 902.33 million (114.66% YoY growth) Gross margin: 95.54%; Net margin: 23.61% Plant Load Factor: 69.05%, achieving 87–89% of contracted energy Q-design: Aggressive Q41 model, 19.28% dry energy mix 10-bank syndicate led by Nepal Investment Bank, 76% debt-financed Promoter: Him Prasad Pathak — also controls Times Energy (341 MW Budhi Gandaki), Secretary at IPPAN ## Subsidiary vs. IPP Rate Differential (Proven) Chilime's "Regular/Excess" billing architecture produces an effective blended rate of NPR 7.57/kWh vs. a typical private IPP's NPR 5.52/kWh — a 37.1% revenue advantage. Chilime classified 85% of its 156 GWh generation as "Regular" at NPR 8.17/kWh. ## Insurance Burden Sanima Mai Hydropower paid NPR 13.77 million in non-life premiums against NPR 764 million gross revenue = 1.80% of top-line. Within an ~15% OPEX envelope, insurance consumes over 10% of operating budget. # Data Gaps Marked SEBON IPO prospectuses are scanned-image PDFs — no automated parsing possible IPPAN's "800% premium surge" claim source inaccessible (server timeout) Specific OAGN paragraph condemning PPA favoritism for subsidiaries not isolated # See Also sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026 ppa-pricing nea-triple-authority sahas-urja chilime rasuwagadhi upper-trishuli-3b

pages.title: "Mission 1: Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) — Benchmark IPP Success"
pages.excerpt: Source type: Research compilation — single-project deep-dive (4 pages) Original: Forensic reconstruction of Sahas Urja Limited sourced from ICRA Nepal surveillance reports (A.996, B.119) and corporate disclosures.
pages.body_text: Mission 1: Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) Source type: Research compilation — single-project deep-dive (4 pages) Original: Forensic reconstruction of Sahas Urja Limited sourced from ICRA Nepal surveillance reports (A.996, B.119) and corporate disclosures. # Summary Sahas Urja Limited is the critical positive benchmark in Nepal's private IPP landscape. It demonstrates that profitability in a fixed-tariff environment is dictated by capital efficiency and operational optimization, not anomalous PPA rates. # Corporate Details Entity: Private IPP, incorporated February 2014, converted to public September 2014 Asset: 86 MW Solu Khola (Dudh Koshi) Hydroelectric Project, Run-of-River Location: Solukhumbu District, Koshi Province Q-design: Q41 (~41% probability of exceedance) PPA: Signed February 25, 2015 | NPR 4.80 (wet) / 8.40 (dry) | 3% × 8 years escalation Contracted energy: 520.20 GWh/yr (100.27 GWh dry / 419.93 GWh wet = 19.28% dry mix) RCOD achieved: March 1, 2023 — retained all 8 escalations # Financial Performance (FY 2080/81) Metric | Value | ---|---| Build cost (final) | NPR 172–175 million/MW | Total project cost | NPR 14.7 billion | Electricity sales revenue | NPR 2.648 billion | Net profit | NPR 902.33 million (+114.66% YoY) | Gross profit margin | 95.54% | Net profit margin | 23.61% | Interest expense | NPR 805.9 million (down from NPR 1.165B) | Plant Load Factor | 69.05% (~87–89% of contracted) | Debt financing | 76% (10-bank consortium led by Nepal Investment Bank) | # Why It Works 1. Capital efficiency: ~NPR 173M/MW vs. industry average often exceeding NPR 200–250M/MW 2. Escalation retention: Achieved COD on time → all 8 annual 3% escalations intact 3. Scale: 86 MW means 19.28% dry energy mix still generates massive absolute NPR 8.40/kWh cash flows 4. Deleveraging: Interest expense dropped from NPR 1.165B to NPR 805.9M # Promoter Profile Chairman: Him Prasad Pathak Also controls: 51% stake in Times Energy (341 MW Budhi Gandaki PRoR, NPR 52.5B syndication) IPPAN: Secretary, Executive Committee — direct lobbying access to MoEWRI, ERC, NEA Prior projects: Upper Hugdi Khola (5 MW) # See Also ppa-data-retrieval-icra-care-2026 sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026 sahas-urja ppa-pricing q-design-discharge

pages.title: "Systemic Risk & Triple-Authority Conflict in Nepal's Hydropower Sector"
headings: Systemic Risk & Triple-Authority Conflict in Nepal's Hydropower Sector
IPO & Rights Share Inventory (VERIFIED from SEBON/NEPSE)
pages.excerpt: Source type: Research compilation — Google Docs export (17 pages) Original: Exhaustive investigation of structural imbalances at the intersection of capital markets, regulatory frameworks, and project finance in Nepal's hydropower IPP sector.
pages.body_text: Systemic Risk & Triple-Authority Conflict in Nepal's Hydropower Sector Source type: Research compilation — Google Docs export (17 pages) Original: Exhaustive investigation of structural imbalances at the intersection of capital markets, regulatory frameworks, and project finance in Nepal's hydropower IPP sector. # Summary The "Triple-Authority Conflict" — NEA simultaneously functioning as sole off-taker (buyer), system dispatcher (NLDC), and autonomous penalty administrator — distorts IPOs, rights share issuances, and DSCR metrics. Retail investors on NEPSE are downstream victims of a system they were never properly informed about. # IPO & Rights Share Inventory (VERIFIED from SEBON/NEPSE) Company | MW | IPO Year | IPO Amount (NPR Cr) | Rights Issue | Rights Amount (NPR Cr) | Rights Use | ---|---|---|---|---|---|---| Chilime Hydropower | 22.1 | 2010 | 24.0 | None | — | Holding company expansion (Sanjen, Rasuwagadhi) | Upper Tamakoshi | 456.0 | 2018 | 100.0 (Local) | 2023/24 | 1,059.0 (1:1) | Retire legacy commercial debt | Rasuwagadhi Hydropower | 111.0 | 2018 | 66.0 (Local/Staff) | None | — | Delayed COD due to disasters | Ankhu Khola Jalvidhyut | 8.4 | 2013 | 20.0 | 2024 | 120.0 (1:1.5) | Repay bank loans (150% dilution) | Peoples Power Ltd | 4.96 | 2022 | 31.6 | 2024 | 15.8 (1:0.5) | Repay Prime Commercial Bank loans | Joshi Hydropower | 3.0 | 2018 | 24.1 | 2024 | 15.6 (1:0.65) | Prevent NPL status (NMB Bank) | Terhathum Power Co. | 7.5 | 2021 | 12.0 | 2024 | 12.0 (1:1) | Repay bank loans + 2 MW cascade | # Key Findings ## The Chilime Anomaly Chilime operated profitably for 7 years before its 2010 IPO — distributing 35% cash dividends within a year. Modern IPOs are floated pre-commissioning, dumping execution risk onto retail investors. Today's investors buy debt-repayment vehicles, not dividend-yielding assets. ## The AD Penalty Blindspot IPO prospectuses detail PPA tariff rates to project revenues, but fail to model scenarios where up to 16% of projected revenue is clawed back by the off-taker through structural AD (Availability Declaration) penalties. ## Capital Sequencing Inversion Standard model: promoter equity → bank debt → COD → operational cash flow → IPO as exit. Nepal model: pre-COD IPO → rights shares within 24–36 months → proceeds used to prevent bank foreclosure. ## Market Concentration Hydropower equities grew from ~6% to ~16% of NEPSE total market capitalization. During FY 2020/21 liquidity surge: NPR 102B → NPR 342B. # Data Gaps "Promoters injected 2–6 Crore NPR/MW out-of-pocket to cover loan repayments" — based on regulatory corpus, not verified primary data "103 MW of small projects required NPR 2.5 billion of additional equity to stop foreclosure" — parameters align with global reports, not a verified Nepal-specific study # See Also ppa-data-retrieval-icra-care-2026 ppa-pricing nea-triple-authority ipo-hydropower-bailout stranded-generation ad-penalties

pages.title: Tanahu Hydropower Project Status (ADB, 2024–2025)
headings: What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 140 MW Tanahu Hydropower Project's construction progress and social-compliance posture, drawn from ADB's own project monitoring reports and two Accountability Mechanism complaints (2024 and 2025).
pages.body_text: Tanahu Hydropower Project Status (ADB, 2024–2025) # Summary Reference compilation for the 140 MW Tanahu Hydropower Project's construction progress and social-compliance posture, drawn from ADB's own project monitoring reports and two Accountability Mechanism complaints (2024 and 2025). # Key Data Points Installed: 140 MW reservoir-type plant River: Seti (Gandaki basin) District: Tanahu Owner: Nepal Electricity Authority Financier: Asian Development Bank (primary), co-financiers Main civil works: 140 m high roller-compacted-concrete dam (Package-1), underground powerhouse (Package-2), main water conveyance tunnel Status (FY 2024/25): ~67% complete per NEA annual report ADB monitoring visit: December 2024 — flagged buffer-zone demarcation, public-land acquisition and construction pace as open issues Accountability Mechanism complaints 2024 complaint: demarcation of buffer zones, free prior informed consent, land-for-land compensation, untitled-land registration 2025 complaint: filed 18 Feb 2025, deemed eligible for review 4 Apr 2025 # Relevance to Project Primary source for Tanahu's status, design basics and the unusually well-documented compliance issues that shape its completion schedule. The Accountability Mechanism filings are the reason Tanahu's delays are public and auditable — most Nepali storage projects lack that transparency layer. # What This Source Does Not Cover Dry-season energy contribution once the reservoir is in operation Plant-level cost escalation numbers (ADB figures redact subsidiary contractor detail) Coordination with downstream irrigation users on the Seti # See also tanahu-hydropower storage-deficit buildability

pages.title: Upper Karnali — PDA, Financial Closure & Court Record
headings: Upper Karnali — PDA, Financial Closure & Court Record
What This Source Does Not Cover
pages.excerpt: Reference compilation for the 900 MW Upper Karnali Hydroelectric Project — GMR-sponsored, repeatedly PDA-extended, and the canonical example of a stalled India-developed export project in Nepal.
pages.body_text: Upper Karnali — PDA, Financial Closure & Court Record # Summary Reference compilation for the 900 MW Upper Karnali Hydroelectric Project — GMR-sponsored, repeatedly PDA-extended, and the canonical example of a stalled India-developed export project in Nepal. # Key Data Points Project basics Capacity: 900 MW Districts: Surkhet, Dailekh, Achham (karnali-basin) River: Karnali Design annual energy: ~3,466 GWh Sponsor: GMR Upper Karnali Hydropower Limited (subsidiary of India's GMR Group) Bid won: International Competitive Bidding, 2008 Export orientation: 88% of output planned for international sale Commercial / PDA history Original PDA deadline missed repeatedly since 2014 Supreme Court of Nepal limited extensions; allowed a 186-day extension to compensate only for time lost during its own interim orders Bangladesh Power Development Board signed a PPA with GMR for 500 MW in principle; MOU in April 2017 for 300 MW; PPA planned for July 2017; in September 2021 Nepal proposed only 200 MW to Bangladesh — none of these have closed Recent financing development (2026) Government of Nepal cleared a path for financial closure GMR submitted investment commitments: INR 65 billion from India's IREDA; NPR 20 billion from Nepali banks Equity restructuring brought in SJVN and IREDA alongside GMR; NEA retains its originally-allocated stake # Relevance to Project Primary source for Upper Karnali's PDA history, export structure and the 2026 financial-closure path. Important because Upper Karnali is the reference case — alongside arun-3 — for India-developed Nepalese hydro, but with the opposite construction outcome: Arun-3 is building; Upper Karnali has been paperwork since 2008. # What This Source Does Not Cover Bangladesh-side transmission readiness (Bangladesh can only import what a new Nepal–India–Bangladesh corridor can carry) GMR Group's own financial condition and its effect on equity pace The downstream-irrigation objections that a 2014 high-level committee raised and never fully resolved # See also upper-karnali hydro-geopolitics karnali-basin

pages.title: World Bank Nepal Country Economic Memorandum (2025)
headings: World Bank Nepal Country Economic Memorandum (2025)
pages.excerpt: The World Bank's comprehensive economic assessment of Nepal with a significant energy/hydropower chapter. Provides the strongest international-institution perspective on Nepal's energy transition, trade patterns, and system constraints.
pages.body_text: World Bank Nepal Country Economic Memorandum (2025) # Summary The World Bank's comprehensive economic assessment of Nepal with a significant energy/hydropower chapter. Provides the strongest international-institution perspective on Nepal's energy transition, trade patterns, and system constraints. # Key Data Points Hydropower capacity reached ~2,990 MW by end-2024 >70% of capacity added since 2018 came from IPPs IPPs accounted for 64% of total installed hydropower by 2024 >90% of hydropower is RoR or PRoR Trade shift: 2018 imported ~2,582 GWh / exported ~3 GWh → 2023 imported ~1,895 GWh / exported ~1,946 GWh Scheduled load shedding eliminated in 2018 76% of firms still reported regular power outages in 2022 >13% of firms reported sales losses above 10% from power cuts System losses: ~12.7% in 2024 September 2024 floods/landslides damaged 11 operational projects with >600 MW combined capacity # Relevance to Project Key source for the IPP-driven growth story, the trade transformation timeline, and the reliability gap (load shedding gone but outages persist). . The firm-level outage data (76%) is uniquely valuable for the reliability argument in stranded-generation. Trade shift data central to data-trade-time-series.

pages.title: World Bank ESMAP Nepal Solar and Wind Resource Measurement
headings: World Bank ESMAP Nepal Solar and Wind Resource Measurement
Key findings (composite, from ESMAP final report)
For Nepal solar planning in general
pages.excerpt: The ground-measured solar and wind resource dataset for Nepal. Operated 2015–2019 under the World Bank ESMAP Renewable Energy Resource Mapping & Geospatial Planning programme, implemented on the ground through AEPC with technical delivery by Vaisala / 3TIER (solar) and DNV GL (wi
pages.body_text: World Bank ESMAP Nepal Solar and Wind Resource Measurement The ground-measured solar and wind resource dataset for Nepal. Operated 2015–2019 under the World Bank ESMAP Renewable Energy Resource Mapping & Geospatial Planning programme, implemented on the ground through AEPC with technical delivery by Vaisala / 3TIER (solar) and DNV GL (wind). This is the only authoritative measured-data source for Nepal solar resource at the station-hour resolution — every satellite-derived estimate (including global-solar-atlas-nepal) is calibrated against and cross-validated with this dataset. # What the source covers 14 solar measurement stations deployed across Nepal's representative zones 4 wind measurement towers (lower priority; wind is constrained in Nepal) 10-minute and hourly meteorological and radiation data GHI, DNI, DIF, temperature, wind speed, humidity — the full solar-meteorological stack Validation campaign against Global Solar Atlas and MERRA-2 satellite datasets Open-data release through ESMAP Global Solar Atlas portal and AEPC national reports # Station network (solar — representative) Station (approximate) | Zone | Elevation (m) | Measurement period | GHI mean (kWh/m²/day) | ---|---|---:|---|---:| Jumla | E / A-border | 2,370 | 2015–19 | ~5.2 | Nepalgunj | B3 | 150 | 2015–19 | ~5.1 | Simara / Birgunj | B1 | 130 | 2015–19 | ~4.9 | Biratnagar | B2 | 80 | 2015–19 | ~4.9 | Pokhara | C2 | 820 | 2015–19 | ~4.5 | Kathmandu | C1 | 1,340 | 2015–19 | ~4.3 | Dhankuta | C3 | 1,100 | 2015–19 | ~4.4 | Surkhet | C4 | 700 | 2015–19 | ~4.8 | Jomsom (Mustang) | A | 2,730 | 2015–19 | ~6.0 | (+ 5 additional stations, detail in final report) | various | | | | Specific station coordinates, exact measurement periods, and precise GHI means require cross-reference with the ESMAP final report tables; the values here are representative. # Key findings (composite, from ESMAP final report) Nepal country-weighted GHI: 4.7–5.0 kWh/m²/day GHI range across stations: 4.3 (Kathmandu Valley) to 6.0+ (Jomsom / Mustang) Strongest seasonality in: mid-hill stations (Pokhara, Dhankuta) with 35–45% monsoon penalty Weakest seasonality in: trans-Himalayan stations (Jomsom) with 5–15% monsoon penalty Station-satellite validation: Global Solar Atlas is within ±5% of measured GHI at all stations; trans-Himalayan stations had largest satellite underestimate (~5–8%) Wind resource: limited utility-scale potential; best pockets in Mustang (annual mean >6 m/s at hub height) and selected Koshi gaps # Why this source matters ## For the wiki specifically Anchor citation for solar-resource-geography-nepal zone definitions Base data for data-nepal-solar-resource-zones district-level tables Validation reference for global-solar-atlas-nepal satellite-derived zones in the map layer Empirical grounding that distinguishes Nepal's measured resource from the theoretical resource estimates in less rigorous studies ## For Nepal solar planning in general It is the dataset that moves Nepal solar discussion from "we think Nepal has good sun" to "we measured it for four years across 14 representative sites and here is the spatial and seasonal structure." This is the empirical floor under claim-solar-cheaper-than-small-hydro and the LCOE arguments downstream. # Data access Raw 10-min / hourly station data: ESMAP portal (historically), AEPC national archive (partial) Derived products: Global Solar Atlas tiles, Nepal national solar atlas (AEPC), IRENA REmap modelling Station reports: ESMAP Nepal final report (~2019–2020) # Relevance to project Primary source for: solar-resource-geography-nepal data-nepal-solar-resource-zones solar-hydro-complementarity — seasonality patterns data-solar-hydro-complementarity-profile — monthly shape Validation source for: global-solar-atlas-nepal irena-remap-nepal # Data-quality notes Station data is high-quality pyranometer-grade, calibrated, Vaisala-instrument-based. Spatial coverage is thin (14 stations across a country of Nepal's complexity); satellite products fill the interpolation gap but with systematic biases in high-mountain terrain. Temporal coverage (2015–2019) pre-dates the 2021–2024 atmospheric-aerosol shifts; there is a case for a refresh campaign, especially in the Indo-Gangetic-plain-adjacent Terai where dust aerosol has intensified. Post-2019 data is patchy; some stations continued under AEPC operation, others were decommissioned. # Gaps we would like A 2024/25 refresh campaign across at least 8 of the original 14 stations, to capture atmospheric-aerosol evolution. Publication of a per-station 10-year climatology with percentile bands (not just 10-year mean). Integration with cloud-cover indices (MODIS, Himawari) for operational forecasting value. # Related global-solar-atlas-nepal — satellite-derived complement irena-remap-nepal — scenario-modelling use of this data aepc-renewable-framework — implementation partner solar-resource-geography-nepal — the narrative interpretation data-nepal-solar-resource-zones — the operational table

pages.title: World Bank Ganges Strategic Basin Assessment
headings: World Bank Ganges Strategic Basin Assessment
pages.excerpt: Regional-scale analysis of the Ganges basin covering water storage, flood management, and transboundary dynamics. Provides the valley-shape storage paradox that Public source review highlighted.
pages.body_text: World Bank Ganges Strategic Basin Assessment # Summary Regional-scale analysis of the Ganges basin covering water storage, flood management, and transboundary dynamics. Provides the valley-shape storage paradox that Public source review highlighted. # Key Data Points Aswan High Dam (Egypt, 111m): 162 BCM storage — flat terrain allows massive reservoir Proposed Andhi Khola Dam (Nepal, 110m): only 0.9 BCM — steep V-shaped Himalayan valley Karnali Chisapani proposed gross storage: 28.2 BCM, usable live capacity only 16.2 BCM Nepal's annual water flow through territory: 175–225 BCM # Relevance to Project The valley-shape paradox (same dam height, 180x less storage) is one of the most powerful visualizations for explaining why Nepal's geography simultaneously enables hydropower and prevents easy storage. Central to storage-deficit and data-storage-comparison.

pages.title: World Bank Grid Solar and Energy Efficiency Project
headings: World Bank Grid Solar and Energy Efficiency Project
Component 1: Grid-Connected Solar PV Farms Development
Component 3: Institutional Capacity and Project Management
pages.excerpt: The institutional anchor for Nepal's first utility-scale, grid-tied solar farm and the financing channel that broke Nepal's "can we actually build solar at utility scale?" learning curve. IDA credit to the Government of Nepal, implemented by NEA under a World Bank project supervi
pages.body_text: World Bank Grid Solar and Energy Efficiency Project The institutional anchor for Nepal's first utility-scale, grid-tied solar farm and the financing channel that broke Nepal's "can we actually build solar at utility scale?" learning curve. IDA credit to the Government of Nepal, implemented by NEA under a World Bank project supervision framework. # What the project covers Three components: ## Component 1: Grid-Connected Solar PV Farms Development Pilot utility-scale solar farm development Output: 25 MWp ground-mounted grid-tied solar plant at Bidur / Trishuli, Nuwakot district (completed, commissioned 2022) — see bidur-trishuli-solar-25mwp First NEA-owned, NEA-operated utility solar plant in Nepal Interconnection to the Trishuli 33 kV grid EPC via competitive procurement; O&M staff trained under the project ## Component 2: Distribution System Upgrading Substation upgrades, distribution line reinforcement Smart-meter pilot deployments Distribution loss reduction targets ## Component 3: Institutional Capacity and Project Management NEA capacity building for solar O&M, grid-integration studies, net-metering framework Technical assistance for future solar programmes (including what became the 960 MW tender) The "cumulative grid impact study for 960 MW solar" cited in nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 was delivered under this project's TA line # Key data points Credit size: IDA concessional loan (amount: in hundreds of millions of USD; specific number in PAD and ICR) Implementation period: approximately 2014–2023 Bidur / Trishuli 25 MWp: commissioned 2022; first ~40 GWh/yr generation Follow-on tender design capacity: provided the technical framework for the 960 MW national solar tender (nea-960mw-solar-tender) # Why this project matters Three institutional firsts that changed what is operationally possible: 1. First NEA-EPC utility solar, closed on time. Broke the "NEA can't build renewables beyond hydro" narrative and put NEA's project-management bandwidth through the cycle once. 2. Established the PPA template for utility solar. The PPA structure, grid-interconnection specification, metering standard, and O&M protocol that subsequent IPP solar plants use are descendants of the Bidur/Trishuli precedent. 3. Demonstrated the site-to-grid latency. Planning → PPA → COD in ~5 years for the first plant; IPP plants now take ~2–3 years. The learning curve compression is the project's most valuable externality. # Parallel / follow-on donor programmes The WB Grid Solar & EE Project sits in a cluster of donor programmes on Nepal renewables: Programme | Donor | Focus | Target (approx.) | ---|---|---|---:| Grid Solar & EE | World Bank | Utility solar + grid upgrade | 25 MWp + distribution work | Promotion of Solar Energy in Rural and Semi-Urban Regions II | EIB | Ground-mounted grid-tied solar, 4 sites | up to EUR 8 M | Technical Assistance — Solar Tender Design | ADB / JICA (partial) | 960 MW tender grid studies | — | AEPC programmes (SHS, mini-grid) | ADB, SREP, WB, donor consortium | Off-grid + rural | ~100 MWp cumulative | Karnali off-grid solar + BESS | World Bank | Remote grid-absent districts | ~10 MWp target | Collectively these programmes have financed essentially all of Nepal's solar deployment to date; the 960 MW tender is the first programme of sufficient scale that private IPP finance dominates the capital stack rather than donor grants / concessional loans. # Documents and subsources Project Appraisal Document (PAD) — initial project design and rationale Implementation Status Reports (ISR) — periodic progress updates Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR) — post-project evaluation Technical assistance outputs — solar tender design, grid impact studies, net-metering framework Bidur / Trishuli plant completion report (NEA + WB) # Data-quality notes Project-level disclosure is high (WB disclosure standards); credit amount, disbursement, and component performance are publicly documented. The 25 MWp Bidur/Trishuli plant's actual FY 2023/24 and FY 2024/25 generation numbers are in NEA annual reports and internal operational statistics. Grid impact study outputs have fed directly into the 960 MW tender design; that traceable chain is one of the cleanest "donor TA → national policy" examples in Nepal energy. # Relevance to project Primary source for: bidur-trishuli-solar-25mwp — the flagship entity nea-960mw-solar-tender — the direct institutional descendant hybrid-siting-logic — Bidur/Trishuli is a near-hydro siting precedent rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — WB Grid Solar & EE Project touches both utility and distribution Cross-references: eib-rural-solar-phase2 — parallel programme aepc-renewable-framework — parallel programme on the off-grid side nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 — the implementation-side primary source # Related bidur-trishuli-solar-25mwp nea-960mw-solar-tender nea wb-country-economic-memo-2025 — macro frame wb-nepal-power-sector-reform-2022 — sector-reform frame this project sits inside

pages.title: World Bank Nepal Power Sector Reform Roadmap (2022)
headings: World Bank Nepal Power Sector Reform Roadmap (2022)
pages.excerpt: Policy-oriented assessment of Nepal's power sector institutions and reform needs. Used as part of the source base for hydropower potential labeling and institutional analysis.
pages.body_text: World Bank Nepal Power Sector Reform Roadmap (2022) # Summary Policy-oriented assessment of Nepal's power sector institutions and reform needs. Used as part of the source base for hydropower potential labeling and institutional analysis. # Key Data Points Economically feasible potential: ~42,000 MW Institutional reform recommendations for nea and sector governance # Relevance to Project Provides the 42,000 MW economically feasible figure used alongside WECS numbers. Important for hydropower-potential-categories provenance. source-review note.

pages.title: World Bank Nepal Water Sector Diagnostic
headings: World Bank Nepal Water Sector Diagnostic
pages.excerpt: A water-sector-wide diagnostic covering storage requirements, irrigation, and the intersection of water management with energy. Provides the most authoritative estimate of Nepal's seasonal storage gap.
pages.body_text: World Bank Nepal Water Sector Diagnostic # Summary A water-sector-wide diagnostic covering storage requirements, irrigation, and the intersection of water management with energy. Provides the most authoritative estimate of Nepal's seasonal storage gap. # Key Data Points Nepal's storage volume requirement to satisfy annual seasonal water demand: 29.86 km³ Nepal effectively has none of this required seasonal storage at national scale JICA estimates Nepal will need at least 1,993 MW of storage hydropower by 2032 (BAU) or 3,154 MW (high demand) Nepal's active dam storage: ~85 MCM (one of the lowest in Asia) # Relevance to Project The 29.86 km³ requirement vs near-zero actual storage is the single most dramatic number for the storage-deficit argument. JICA's MW requirements give a concrete planning target. Data feeds into data-storage-comparison. . [!note] The 85 MCM active storage figure and the 130-150 MCM total figure likely differ in boundary — active vs total reservoir volume. Both are vanishingly small relative to the 29.86 km³ need.

pages.title: WECS Energy Sector Synopsis Report 2024
pages.excerpt: Covers Nepal's full energy balance for FY 2079/80 (~2022/23), including all energy sources (not just electricity). Provides the broader context for where grid electricity fits within Nepal's total energy system. This is the authoritative denominator for every "hydro share of tota
pages.body_text: WECS Energy Sector Synopsis Report 2024 # Summary Covers Nepal's full energy balance for FY 2079/80 (~2022/23), including all energy sources (not just electricity). Provides the broader context for where grid electricity fits within Nepal's total energy system. This is the authoritative denominator for every "hydro share of total energy" claim. # Key Data Points (FY 2079/80) Total final energy consumption: 532.42 PJ (−16.81% YoY vs 640 PJ in FY 2078/79) Consumption by resource class: - Traditional biomass: 63.87% (340.04 PJ) — fuelwood, ag residue, animal dung - Commercial fuels: 25.80% (137.38 PJ) — coal, petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG, ATF, furnace oil - Grid electricity: 7.23% (38.49 PJ) — up from ~4.96% in FY 2078/79 (+21.18% YoY) - Modern renewables: 3.10% (16.50 PJ) — biogas, solar, micro-hydro, wind Coal consumption down −41.50% YoY; petroleum products down −16.14% YoY Sectoral shares: - Residential 60.75% (323,463 TJ) - Industrial 20.91% (111,306 TJ) - Transportation 10.43% (55,553 TJ) - Commercial 5.04% (26,835 TJ) - Construction/Mining 1.92% (10,222 TJ) - Agriculture 0.95% (5,040 TJ) Hydropower capacity referenced: 3,349 MW installed, 9,940.86 GWh generated (FY 2023/24) Transmission system: 5,742 circuit-km at 66 kV+; 8,867 MVA substation capacity # Correction note Earlier wiki pages cite grid electricity as 4.96% of final energy. That figure was the FY 2078/79 value that appears in this report's historical prose. The FY 2079/80 headline figure is 7.23%. Both are correct for their respective years; the 2.3-point jump in one year reflects real electrification growth. Full cross-tab decoded at data-final-energy-mix. # Local PDF status [!warning] The file at in this repo is a 404 HTML stub from a failed download, not the actual report. The real PDF lives at the above (giwmscdnone.gov.np). Re-download before attempting any further text extraction. # Relevance to Project The 7.23% grid-electricity share — and the fact that hydro is only ~6.8% of Nepal's total final energy — is one of the most revealing data points in the entire project. It collapses the "Nepal is a hydropower country" framing into the more honest "Nepal has a hydropower electricity system inside a biomass-and-petroleum energy system." Central to master-thesis, domestic-led-hydro-strategy, and energy-substitution-pathway. Feeds into nepal-energy-profile and data-final-energy-mix. .

pages.title: WECS Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal (2019)
headings: WECS Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal (2019)
pages.excerpt: Site Inspection during Safeguards
pages.body_text: WECS Assessment of Hydropower Potential of Nepal (2019) Site Inspection during Safeguards # Summary The revised national hydropower potential assessment. Supersedes the older 83,000 MW theoretical figure with updated screening. Provides basin-level gross and techno-economic potential breakdowns. # Key Data Points Revised gross potential: 72,544 MW (down from legacy 83,500 MW) Techno-economic potential: 32,680 MW Basin distribution of gross potential: - koshi-basin: 27,805 MW - karnali-basin: 20,385 MW - gandaki-basin: 19,803 MW - All remaining basins: ~4,551 MW ~94% of gross potential is in the three major Himalayan basins # Relevance to Project The definitive source for hydropower-potential-categories. Only surfaced the revised 72,544 MW figure — public summaries used the legacy 83,000 MW. The basin concentration data (94% in three basins) is central to the strategic argument. [!note] The legacy 83,000 MW figure remains politically prominent. The 2019 assessment's 72,544 MW is the more defensible current number, and 32,680 MW techno-economic is the more honest planning baseline.

pages.title: WECS River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plan (2024)
headings: WECS River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plan (2024)
pages.excerpt: The 2024 master planning document for Nepal's river basins. Provides basin-level discharge data, seasonal flow profiles, hydropower potential assessments, and strategic planning frameworks. This is the most authoritative recent basin-level reference.
pages.body_text: WECS River Basin Plans and Hydropower Development Master Plan (2024) # Summary The 2024 master planning document for Nepal's river basins. Provides basin-level discharge data, seasonal flow profiles, hydropower potential assessments, and strategic planning frameworks. This is the most authoritative recent basin-level reference. # Key Data Points koshi-basin: 27,818 km² in Nepal; 1,826.5 cumec average border flow; dry-season flows reduce to ~10x lower than peak gandaki-basin: 32,148 km² in Nepal; 1,952.3 cumec average border flow; ~80% of rainfall in four wet-season months karnali-basin: 43,153 km² in Nepal; 1,255.9 cumec average border flow; highest hydropower potential per WECS 75% or more of river flow occurs in the wet season (June–September) Storage and multipurpose projects treated as central to moving value from surplus to deficit basins Babai explicitly described as rain-fed; West Rapti, Kamala, Kankai as water-deficit / dry-season constrained # Relevance to Project Foundation for the hydrology evidence base. Provides the basin-level evidence for seasonal-mismatch and claim-timing-not-volume. [!warning] WECS explicitly warns that atlas flow numbers are model-derived (MIKE SHE / MIKE Hydro Basin) and may differ from DHM observations or project-specific studies.

pages.title: Alternative Energy Promotion Centre (AEPC)
pages.excerpt: Government of Nepal institution responsible for rural electrification through renewables. Technically a semi-autonomous body under the Ministry of Energy, Water Resources & Irrigation (MoEWRI); operationally the country's primary administrator of the off-grid solar, biogas, impro
pages.body_text: Alternative Energy Promotion Centre (AEPC) Government of Nepal institution responsible for rural electrification through renewables. Technically a semi-autonomous body under the Ministry of Energy, Water Resources & Irrigation (MoEWRI); operationally the country's primary administrator of the off-grid solar, biogas, improved cookstove, and community mini-grid programmes. Standing alongside (not inside) NEA. # Role summary Function | AEPC | NEA | ---|---|---| Grid-tied utility-scale solar | Technical partner on donor-track | Lead operator | Rooftop / distributed solar | Subsidy channel | Net-metering administration | Solar home systems (SHS) | Primary operator | Not involved | Community mini-grids | Primary operator | Grid-edge interconnect when reached | Biogas (~400,000 plants) | Primary operator | Not involved | Improved cookstove (~1M+ units) | Primary operator | Not involved | Off-grid institutional solar | Primary operator | Not involved | # Key Facts Founded: 1996, under the Ministry of Science, Technology and Environment originally; relocated to MoEWRI Cumulative solar home systems installed: ~1.1 million (as of ~2021 with ongoing additions) Cumulative SHS capacity: ~80–100 MWp Community mini-grids operational: ~30 sites, ~1.1 MWp aggregate Institutional solar sites: ~25,000, ~15 MWp aggregate Geographic footprint: strongest in Karnali, Sudurpaschim, remote hill districts of Koshi / Bagmati / Gandaki (see data-rooftop-shs-deployment) Donor partners: ADB, SREP (CIF), World Bank, EIB, UNDP, GIZ, DANIDA, various bilateral channels # Financing structure AEPC's renewable programmes run on a subsidy-plus-concessional-finance model: Grant element: 30–50% of capex for remote / difficult districts; lower for semi-urban Microfinance element: remainder, via Nepal Rastra Bank-approved cooperative lenders Community equity element: labour + small cash contribution in most mini-grids Donor-funded programme lines set the budget envelope (ADB, WB, EIB all operate through AEPC) The total annual AEPC renewable-programme budget has historically been in the NPR 3–8 billion range (highly donor-dependent). # Why AEPC is strategically under-weighted Three reasons AEPC is usually missed in Nepal energy analysis: 1. MW under-counting. AEPC's 100+ MWp cumulative solar is spread across a million systems; it doesn't appear on NEA's installed-capacity table, so gets omitted from "Nepal has 142 MW of solar" soundbites. The real number is ~2.3× larger if you count both. 2. The value-weight is higher than MW-weight. Off-grid solar displaces NPR 60–90/kWh diesel, not NPR 8/kWh grid; the value of AEPC's fleet is disproportionately high — 10–15× the utility-equivalent kWh value (see data-solar-hydro-lcoe). 3. AEPC is the institutional carrier of the rural-electrification political coalition. Which is the coalition most likely to sustain Nepal's solar policy past any single electoral cycle. Utility solar depends on NEA tender capacity; rural solar depends on AEPC cooperative networks — and the latter has been more durable institutionally (AEPC has run continuous SHS programmes for ~28 years). # The AEPC strategic expansion thesis The wiki argues that AEPC, not NEA, is the natural home for three emerging solar programmes: ## 1. Agrivoltaic programme administration agrivoltaics-and-land requires lease-template law, revenue-sharing contracts with smallholder cooperatives, and crop-verification protocols. These are AEPC-format skills, not NEA-format skills. Terai solar at scale likely runs through AEPC's cooperative networks even when the physical plant is a 10–50 MWp ground-mount feeding the NEA grid. ## 2. Off-grid to grid-edge integration As NEA grid reaches former off-grid villages (~8.6% of households still grid-absent, nea), each AEPC mini-grid becomes a grid-edge asset. AEPC-NEA integration protocol (who owns what, tariff structure, BESS-sharing) is currently ad-hoc and should be formalized. rooftop-minigrid-offgrid. ## 3. Solar + BESS rural hybrids The NEA FY 2024/25 annual report explicitly references off-grid solar + BESS deployments in Karnali as an emerging programme line. This is AEPC's home ground technically; NEA would be the grid-edge integrator but not the programme operator. Formalising this channel is one of the highest-leverage institutional moves in Nepal solar policy. # Institutional challenges Donor dependence. AEPC's budget oscillates with donor cycles; the 2017–2020 SREP cycle had ~2× the budget of the 2022–2024 period. Programme continuity depends on donor pipeline management. Staff capacity. Rural technical staff are overstretched; each engineer covers multiple districts in Karnali / Sudurpaschim. Community mini-grid O&M quality varies with staffing continuity. Mandate overlap. AEPC's mandate edges into MoEWRI rural electrification and NEA distribution; interagency coordination has historically been ad-hoc. Data / registry infrastructure. Cumulative deployment numbers are known at the national roll-up but district-level registries are partial post-2021. # Related aepc-renewable-framework — the source-page documentation data-rooftop-shs-deployment — the district-level deployment data rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — the tracks AEPC operates eib-rural-solar-phase2 — expanding AEPC mandate into utility nea — the institutional counterpart claim-solar-political-coalition-is-rural — the political frame AEPC anchors

pages.title: Andhi Khola
pages.excerpt: Andhi Khola is a small operating project in Syangja, but it is pinned in both the data-layer-priority-watchlist and data-layer-storage-shortlist because the same river/site family appears in JICA's promising-storage comparison. It is a useful reminder that storage value is not th
pages.body_text: Andhi Khola Andhi Khola is a small operating project in Syangja, but it is pinned in both the data-layer-priority-watchlist and data-layer-storage-shortlist because the same river/site family appears in JICA's promising-storage comparison. It is a useful reminder that storage value is not the same as operating nameplate capacity. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 9.4 MW | Status | Operating | Category | jica promising storage | River | Andhi Khola (gandaki-basin) | District | Syangja | Province | Gandaki Pradesh | Annual energy | 648.7 GWh | Dry-season energy | 137.1 GWh | Dry share | 21.1% | Total storage | 336.5 MCM | Effective storage | 238.7 MCM | # Why It Matters The operating plant is only 9.4 MW, but the storage-comparison record associated with Andhi Khola carries a much larger planning concept: 180 MW, 648.7 GWh annual energy, 137.1 GWh dry-season energy, and 21.1% dry share. The page therefore has to be read carefully: the current asset and the storage candidate are not the same kind of object. That distinction is exactly why Andhi Khola is useful in the wiki. It forces the reader to separate existing operating evidence from future storage-screening potential. # Status And Timing The existing Andhi Khola plant is operating. The storage-candidate figures come from the JICA/NEA storage master-plan material and should be treated as planning-screening values, not a confirmed construction programme. # Map Interpretation The marker is an operating-project registry point near the mapped river. For the current operating asset it is relatively useful; for the larger storage concept, it is only a planning anchor. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset # See also gandaki-basin run-of-river-hydropower storage-deficit firm-power stranded-generation data-layer-storage-shortlist data-layer-priority-watchlist

pages.title: Ankhu Khola
pages.excerpt: Ankhu Khola Jalvidhyut Company Limited (AKJCL) is a 42.9 MW run-of-river hydropower project. Beyond its generation capacity, Ankhu Khola is a textbook example of the hydropower rights-share bailout pattern documented in Nepal's capital markets.
pages.body_text: Ankhu Khola Ankhu Khola Jalvidhyut Company Limited (AKJCL) is a 42.9 MW run-of-river hydropower project. Beyond its generation capacity, Ankhu Khola is a textbook example of the hydropower rights-share bailout pattern documented in Nepal's capital markets. # The Bailout Pattern Ankhu Khola's capital markets history exemplifies the structural dynamic where Nepal's retail equity investors absorb commercial bank debt: 2013: IPO raised NPR 20 crore for construction funding 2024: Rights issue of NPR 120 crore at a 1:1.5 ratio — a 150% dilution Stated use of proceeds: explicitly to "pay off bank loans" and invest in a 20 MW downstream cascade (AKJCL-2) The rights-share issuance came within roughly a decade of the initial IPO, with the stated purpose being debt clearance rather than new capital expenditure. This pattern — where public equity is deployed to prevent bank foreclosure — is not unique to Ankhu Khola but it is one of the most extreme examples in terms of dilution ratio (1:1.5). # Systemic Pattern Ankhu Khola sits alongside other projects in the rights-share bailout inventory documented in sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026: Company | IPO Cr | Rights Cr | Dilution | Use | ---|---|---|---|---| Ankhu Khola | 20 | 120 | 150% | Repay bank loans | Upper Tamakoshi | 100 | 1,059 (1:1) | 100% | Retire legacy debt | Joshi Hydropower | 24.1 | 15.6 (1:0.65) | 65% | Prevent NPL status | Peoples Power | 31.6 | 15.8 (1:0.5) | 50% | Repay Prime Commercial Bank | The pattern is consistent: pre-COD or immediately post-COD IPOs, followed by rights issues within 2–10 years, with proceeds directed at debt servicing rather than capacity expansion. # See Also sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026 ipo-hydropower-bailout q-design-discharge # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 42.9 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Ankhu Khola | District | Nuwakot | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026 ipo-hydropower-bailout q-design-discharge run-of-river-hydropower buildability

pages.title: Arun 3
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-25 River Longitudinal Profile of the Arun River
pages.body_text: Arun 3 Figure 6.3-25 River Longitudinal Profile of the Arun River Nepal's single largest project under construction — a 900 MW peaking run-of-river scheme on the Arun River in Sankhuwasabha, developed and financed by India's state utility SJVN. When it comes online (revised target: September 2026), it will almost double the country's operating hydropower fleet — but only ~22% of its output will stay in Nepal. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 900 MW (4 × 225 MW Francis units) | Status | Under construction — 74.6% complete (Dec 2025) | Type | Peaking run-of-river, ~3.4 hours/day at full output | River | Arun (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Sankhuwasabha | Developer | SJVN Arun-3 Power Development Company (SAPDC) | Cost | ~USD 1.6 billion | Concession | 30-year BOOT | Design energy | 3,924–4,019 GWh / year | Nepal free share | 21.9% (~197 MW) royalty-free | Evacuation | 400 kV DC line to Sitamarhi, Bihar | # Significance Arun-3 is the archetype of Nepal's India-developed export-first project class. Under the 2014 Project Development Agreement, SJVN builds, owns and operates the plant for 30 years, gives Nepal 21.9% of the energy as royalty-free share, offers another 10% on commercial terms, and takes the remainder to the Indian grid — a structure that makes Arun-3 the reference case when discussing hydro-geopolitics in the basin. It also changes the shape of Nepal's fleet, not just the size. Arun-3 is designed as a peaking RoR plant — 3.4 hours of full rated output per day, not continuous. So its 900 MW nameplate translates into a much smaller day-long energy contribution than its capacity suggests. That matters because Nepal's structural problem isn't capacity; it's seasonal-mismatch and firm-power deficits in dry months, which a peaking RoR plant can help with only to the extent the transmission system can absorb concentrated evening blocks. Compared to upper-tamakoshi — which is Nepali-developed, Nepali-dispatched, and continuous-RoR at 456 MW — Arun-3 is larger but strategically narrower: more export value than domestic reliability value. # Limitations & Controversies Repeated schedule slip. Original commissioning target was March 2025; SJVN has formally applied to extend COD to September 2026. Every year of delay compounds Nepal's storage-deficit exposure because Arun-3 is in the pipeline of projects NEA's planning has already counted on as "coming online soon." Evacuation dependency. The 400 kV cross-border line to Sitamarhi is only 58% complete as of late 2025. Without it, Arun-3's Indian export cannot move; with it, a single line carries most of the value. Both sides are exposed to the cross-border transmission asset. Free power as compensation theory. The 21.9% free share is the political justification for allowing the dominant share of the energy and revenue to accrue to India. Whether 21.9% is a good deal for Nepal depends on (a) the counterfactual — would the project have been built by a Nepali developer at all, and on what timeline — and (b) how the free share interacts with dry-season firm-power accounting, which Arun-3's peaking design constrains. # Official Visual References SAPDC's monthly progress reports are a materially better official visual source than the generic Arun riverside imagery that often gets used as a placeholder for the project. The February 2023 report cover contains a compact one-page schematic tying together the intake, headrace, surge shaft, powerhouse and tailrace alignment. Cover page from SAPDC's monthly progress report up to 28 February 2023. Source: SAPDC monthly progress report up to 28 February 2023 (PDF) # See also koshi-basin upper-tamakoshi hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation firm-power seasonal-mismatch inaruwa-purnea-interconnection — the 400 kV line being built to evacuate Arun-3

pages.title: Nepal–Bangladesh Electricity Trade Route
pages.excerpt: Nepal's first electricity export to a country other than India. A trilateral arrangement routed through India's grid — making India simultaneously Nepal's competitor, buyer, and logistics gatekeeper.
pages.body_text: Nepal–Bangladesh Electricity Trade Route Nepal's first electricity export to a country other than India. A trilateral arrangement routed through India's grid — making India simultaneously Nepal's competitor, buyer, and logistics gatekeeper. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Tripartite PSA signed | October 3, 2024 | Parties | NEA, Bangladesh Power Development Board (BPDB), NTPC Vidyut Vyapar Nigam (India) | First export | 12 hours on November 15, 2024 (symbolic) | Full-scale exports began | June 15, 2025 | Export window | June 15–November 15 each year (5 months) | Duration | 5-year agreement (until Oct 2029) | Current capacity | 40 MW | Agreed expansion | 60 MW from June 2026 (+20 MW, pending India's CEA approval) | Tariff | 6.40 US cents/unit | Currency | USD (unlike India trade in INR) | Route | Nepal → Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV → Baharampur–Bheramara 400 kV → Bangladesh | # Revenue Period | GWh Exported | Revenue | --------|-------------|---------| 2024 (12 hours only) | negligible | — | Jun–Nov 2025 | 147.43 GWh | USD 9.436 million (~NPR 1.35 billion) | # Projects Supplying Bangladesh NEA currently allocates output from Trishuli (25 MW) and Chilime (22 MW) — older NEA-owned plants — to the Bangladesh export quota. India's CEA has approved these specific 40 MW until October 2, 2029. # Expansion Trajectory Nov 2025 (7th JSC meeting): Nepal and Bangladesh agreed to add 20 MW (total 60 MW) from June 2026 Bangladesh's long-term ambition: import 9,000 MW from Nepal by 2040 Alternative routes under study: Barapukuria, Panchagarh/Thakurgaon, Bheramara, Comilla corridors through India Sunkoshi III (683 MW): Expert committee formed to evaluate this project specifically for Bangladesh supply Upper Karnali (900 MW): GMR-developed project had a planned 500 MW PPA to Bangladesh — status uncertain after Sheikh Hasina's fall (Aug 2024) # Strategic Significance 1. Buyer diversification. Reduces Nepal's monopsony dependence on India's IEX for export revenue 2. USD pricing. Bangladesh trade in dollars hedges against INR-NPR forex risk — significant given nea's NPR 2.5B forex losses in FY 2024/25 3. India as gatekeeper. Every MW to Bangladesh requires India's Central Electricity Authority approval and transits Indian lines — India retains structural veto over Nepal's market diversification 4. Seasonal limitation. Exports only run Jun–Nov, perfectly overlapping Nepal's surplus window — does nothing for dry-season revenue. The arrangement is complementary to the seasonal-arbitrage-trap, not a solution to it 5. Political fragility. Bangladesh's political transition (post-Hasina) has stalled larger pipeline projects (Upper Karnali PPA, direct transmission studies) # Related seasonal-arbitrage-trap — Bangladesh trade is monsoon-only, reinforcing the trap india-energy-relationship — India's veto/facilitation role data-trade-time-series — revenue figures hydro-geopolitics — trilateral dynamics dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the 400 kV link that physically carries this trade

pages.title: Barahi Hydropower
pages.excerpt: Sub-10 MW run-of-river hydropower project, operational. This page exists as the wiki's primary audited case study for the nea-triple-authority conflict.
pages.body_text: Barahi Hydropower Sub-10 MW run-of-river hydropower project, operational. This page exists as the wiki's primary audited case study for the nea-triple-authority conflict. # The AD penalty conflict Barahi is protected by a statutory exemption from Availability Declaration (AD) penalties. The Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC) Bylaws 2076, Clause 8(2) and Schedule 5, explicitly exempt projects below 10 MW from AD penalties and financial damages for availability shortfalls. Despite this exemption, NEA deducted penalty amounts for short supply from Barahi's invoices. Out of Rs 14.9 million in trade receivables, Barahi's management classified the deducted penalties as "receivable from NEA" — openly stating in their audited annual report that NEA's actions contradict the prevailing ERC By-laws. Because NEA controls the cash disbursement, the IPP carries the deducted penalty on its balance sheet while the statutory exemption exists on paper with no enforcement mechanism. The independent arbiter is absent. # Key facts Fact | Detail | ---|---| Capacity | Sub-10 MW | Type | Run-of-River | Status | Operational | Regulatory protection | ERC Bylaws 2076, Clause 8(2), Schedule 5 | Penalty deducted | Part of Rs 14.9M in receivables [UNVERIFIED: exact fiscal year to be confirmed] | Accounting treatment | "Receivable from NEA" in audited financial statements | Source | Barahi Hydropower Public Limited audited financial statements [UNVERIFIED: exact fiscal year and report date to be confirmed] | # Significance Barahi is the best-documented instance of the structural double-bind at the core of nea-triple-authority: a regulatory protection exists on paper, but the off-taker — who is also the dispatcher and penalty administrator — can deduct penalties regardless, because it controls the cash. The ERC can issue directives, but it cannot enforce them against the entity that holds the cheque book. # See also nea-triple-authority — the institutional framework this case anchors ad-penalties — the penalty mechanism at issue

pages.title: Betan Karnali HP
pages.excerpt: Betan Karnali is a large semi-reservoir hydropower project on the Karnali, historically carried in the registry as a 688 MW survey-stage project and more recently advanced in public reporting as a 439 MW generation-licence / investment-approval project. It is pinned in the data-l
pages.body_text: Betan Karnali HP Betan Karnali is a large semi-reservoir hydropower project on the Karnali, historically carried in the registry as a 688 MW survey-stage project and more recently advanced in public reporting as a 439 MW generation-licence / investment-approval project. It is pinned in the data-layer-priority-watchlist and data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it sits directly in the Karnali buildout story: large western capacity, storage-like peaking value, institutional Nepali financing, and dependency on how upper-karnali is finally resolved upstream. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 688 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Karnali (karnali-basin) | District | Achham | Province | Sudurpashchim Pradesh | Promoter | Betan Karnali Sanchayakarta Hydropower Company Ltd | # Why It Matters Betan Karnali is one of the clearest examples of why the wiki separates registry capacity from strategic value. The map's top-10 layer uses the older 688 MW registry record because that is the source field used to rank the display set. Current public project reporting, however, describes a 439 MW semi-reservoir scheme with an estimated cost around NPR 93-93.4 billion. That revised scale still matters. A 439 MW semi-reservoir plant with peaking value is more useful to Nepal's dry-season and evening-balancing problem than a simple wet-season-heavy capacity addition. It also matters institutionally: reporting on the 2025 licence and investment approval frames the ownership/finance structure around the Employees Provident Fund, NEA, Electricity Generation Company Ltd., EPF depositors, civil servants, local communities, and the public. # Status And Timing This page should be read as a current project record, not only as a survey-stage registry entry. Public reporting in July 2025 said Nepal's energy ministry granted a generation licence for the 439 MW Betan Karnali project and that the company aimed to start construction immediately and complete within five years. The Rising Nepal then reported in November 2025 that the Investment Board Nepal approved investment for the 439 MW Betan Karnali Semi-Reservoir Hydropower Project and formed a negotiation committee for the Project Development Agreement. The wiki still preserves the 688 MW generated spec because the map layer is derived from the DoED-linked registry snapshot. The analytical status is: Question | Current Read | ----------|--------------| Registry / map capacity | 688 MW survey-stage record. | Current reported development case | 439 MW semi-reservoir project. | Licence / approval status | Generation licence reported in July 2025; IBN investment approval reported in November 2025. | Construction start | "Immediate construction" is an announced aim, not yet treated here as confirmed physical construction. | Completion outlook | Five-year completion target in public reporting; IPSDP groups Betan Karnali in the 2036-2040 project window. | # Map Interpretation The watchlist marker uses a document-backed powerhouse-village vicinity at Tatalighat, snapped to the named Karnali reach. That makes it more useful than a raw registry point for strategic reading, but the marker is still an approximate project anchor. It should not be used as a final dam or powerhouse survey coordinate. # Risks And Open Questions The first open question is the 688 MW versus 439 MW basis. The map ranking follows the older registry value; the project-development story now appears to have moved to the smaller semi-reservoir configuration. Any downstream analysis should state which number it is using. The second question is finance and sequencing. Betan's institutional-investor model is attractive because it is domestic and long-tenor, but the project still has to close financing, sign/complete PDA arrangements, award construction packages, and coordinate with upstream upper-karnali assumptions. Until those steps are visible, the five-year completion target should be read as a developer/government objective rather than a bankable COD forecast. # Registry note BKSHCL EOI document and current project registry Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset Current reporting: Fiscal Nepal on generation licence, July 2025 Current reporting: The Rising Nepal on IBN investment approval, November 2025 # See also karnali-basin stranded-generation buildability upper-karnali data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-priority-watchlist data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects

pages.title: Bheri-1 HEP
pages.excerpt: Bheri-1 HEP is a 440 MW survey-stage project on the Bheri in Rukum West, promoted in the registry by Gezhouba Group Power Investment Nepal Pvt. Ltd. It is not on the priority watchlist, but it is pinned by the data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects layer because it is one of the ten
pages.body_text: Bheri-1 HEP Bheri-1 HEP is a 440 MW survey-stage project on the Bheri in Rukum West, promoted in the registry by Gezhouba Group Power Investment Nepal Pvt. Ltd. It is not on the priority watchlist, but it is pinned by the data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects layer because it is one of the ten largest capacity records in the display set. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 440 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Bheri (karnali-basin) | District | Rukum_W | Province | Karnali Pradesh | Promoter | Gezhouba Group Power Investment Nepal Pvt. Ltd. | # Why It Matters Bheri-1 matters as a capacity-ranking marker, not because the wiki has strong evidence that it is near construction. Its main analytical role is to keep the Bheri sub-basin visible inside the larger Karnali discussion. The top-10 layer is otherwise dominated by Karnali mainstem, Arun/Koshi, and Tama Koshi projects; Bheri-1 is the large Bheri-side entry in that comparison. This is exactly the kind of project that tests claim-mw-not-equal-value. A 440 MW survey marker can look decisive on a map, but the useful question is whether it has a credible development path, transmission route, financing structure, environmental clearance, and offtake arrangement. The current wiki evidence is much stronger on its registry identity than on those delivery steps. # Status And Timing The DoED-linked registry snapshot carries Bheri-1 as a survey-stage project. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Bheri-1 in the 2036-2040 project window, alongside Betan Karnali, Mugu Karnali STO, Tamakoshi 3, Naumure STO, and other later-stage projects. The practical read is therefore conservative: Question | Current Read | ----------|--------------| Is it operating or under construction? | No evidence in the current wiki source set. | Is there a confirmed construction start? | Not found in the sources used here. | Is there a planning window? | IPSDP groups Bheri-1 in 2036-2040. | Why keep it in the wiki? | It is a top-10 capacity marker and a Bheri/Karnali strategic comparator. | # Map Interpretation Bheri-1 has the weakest locational confidence among the top-10 capacity records: the layer labels it a low-confidence registry reference, even though the registry point sits close to the mapped Bheri reach. The marker is good enough to show that a large Bheri candidate exists in the project inventory. It is not good enough for site-grade analysis. # Risks And Open Questions The open questions are basic: project design maturity, sponsor status, licence renewal or conversion, grid evacuation, environmental and social impact, and offtake. Until those are documented, Bheri-1 should be treated as a later-horizon planning candidate rather than a near-term capacity addition. # Registry note Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset # See also karnali-basin buildability claim-mw-not-equal-value data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects
