pages.title: Bheri Corridor 400 kV Transmission Line
pages.excerpt: A planned 400 kV evacuation corridor for the western Bheri system, centred in the annual book on uttarganga-storage and the wider Sani Bheri IPP pipeline. In 2020 it was still a land, survey, and substation preparation story rather than a built line.
pages.body_text: Bheri Corridor 400 kV Transmission Line A planned 400 kV evacuation corridor for the western Bheri system, centred in the annual book on uttarganga-storage and the wider Sani Bheri IPP pipeline. In 2020 it was still a land, survey, and substation preparation story rather than a built line. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 65 describes the Bheri Corridor 400 kV line as a GoN-funded project intended to evacuate power from Uttarganga and other projects in the Sani Bheri basin, with a planned ~25 km 400 kV double-circuit line from Bafikot to Nalsingadh and a 400/132 kV, 200 MVA Uttarganga substation hub. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status in 2020 book | Under development / survey-stage | Core route | Bafikot (Rukum West) to Nalsingadh (Jajarkot) | Associated substation | Uttarganga hub, 400/132 kV, 200 MVA | Strategic role | Evacuation path for Uttarganga and Sani Bheri generation | # Why It Matters This is the transmission expression of the western buildability problem: large projects such as uttarganga-storage do not have value without a corresponding evacuation corridor. The corridor sits squarely inside the wiki's claim-transmission-immediate-blocker logic and is one more example of generation ambition outrunning grid readiness. # See also uttarganga-storage claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 stranded-generation

pages.title: Bhutan Hydropower Model
pages.excerpt: Bhutan is the most frequent regional comparison for Nepal's hydropower trajectory. The comparison is instructive and somewhat brutal.
pages.body_text: Bhutan Hydropower Model Bhutan is the most frequent regional comparison for Nepal's hydropower trajectory. The comparison is instructive and somewhat brutal. # Bhutan's Success Metric | Bhutan | Nepal | --------|--------|-------| Hydro contribution to GDP | ~14% | 3–5% | Hydro share of government revenue | ~26% | Marginal | Export price to India | $0.05–0.07/kWh | $0.03–0.05/kWh | Model | India-financed, India-built, India-integrated | Self-financed, fragmented, sovereignty-preserving | Bhutan's flagship plants (Chukha, Tala, Mangdechhu) were built with Indian grants and soft loans. India is co-investor with direct economic interest in project success. This gives Bhutan an unconditionally secure export market. # Why Nepal Chose Differently Nepal's more vibrant democracy, critical civil society, and strategic desire to balance Indian influence against Chinese investment led it to resist the "Bhutan model." Nepal opted for a slower, contested, fragmented development path to preserve economic and political sovereignty. This is a defensible policy choice, but it costs money: approximately $80–120 million per year in foregone export premium at current volumes. # Lessons Bhutan proves Himalayan hydropower can generate national wealth But Bhutan's model requires granting India near-total control Bhutan's projects have also suffered massive delays and cost overruns under Indian management Nepal cannot replicate Bhutan without fundamentally changing its foreign policy posture # Related india-energy-relationship — the bilateral context hydro-geopolitics — why sovereignty preservation matters domestic-led-hydro-strategy — Nepal's alternative path

pages.title: Bidur / Trishuli 25 MWp Solar Farm
headings: Why Bidur/Trishuli sited at Nuwakot is strategically interesting
1. NEA can build solar on-schedule, on-budget, on-spec.
3. Mid-hill solar is workable but sub-optimal.
pages.excerpt: Nepal's first utility-scale NEA-EPC, NEA-owned, NEA-operated solar plant. 25 MWp ground-mounted grid-tied PV at Bidur, Nuwakot district, commissioned 2022 under the World Bank Grid Solar and Energy Efficiency Project. Interconnected to the Trishuli 33 kV grid.
pages.body_text: Bidur / Trishuli 25 MWp Solar Farm Nepal's first utility-scale NEA-EPC, NEA-owned, NEA-operated solar plant. 25 MWp ground-mounted grid-tied PV at Bidur, Nuwakot district, commissioned 2022 under the World Bank Grid Solar and Energy Efficiency Project. Interconnected to the Trishuli 33 kV grid. The plant that moved Nepal from "solar demonstration" to "utility solar." Every subsequent NEA solar programme decision rides on the engineering, procurement, operational, and commercial precedents this project established. # Specifications Item | Value | ---|---| Capacity | 25 MWp (DC) / ~22 MWac | Location | Bidur Municipality, Nuwakot District, Bagmati Province | Site elevation | ~600 m | Land area | ~25 ha (approx. 1 ha/MWp) | Technology | Crystalline silicon, fixed-tilt ground-mount | Inverters | String inverters (typical Phase II Nepal configuration) | Interconnection | Trishuli 33 kV substation, adjacent to existing NEA infrastructure | Owner | NEA | EPC | Selected through competitive procurement under WB Grid Solar & EE Project | Commissioning | 2022 | Expected annual generation | ~40 GWh/yr (CF ~18%) | Actual FY 2023/24 generation | ~38–42 GWh (NEA internal records; published aggregate) | Financing | IDA concessional credit via wb-grid-solar-ee-project | # Institutional firsts 1. First NEA-EPC utility solar. Broke the "NEA can't build anything beyond hydro" narrative. 2. First use of the now-standard NEA utility-solar PPA / O&M template. Subsequent IPP plants use descendant documentation. 3. First integration of a utility solar plant into NEA's operational dispatch / Load Dispatch Center. The forecasting and integration protocols piloted here. 4. First competitive solar EPC tender at utility scale. Established the procurement reference for Phase IV. # Why Bidur/Trishuli sited at Nuwakot is strategically interesting The site choice is a near-hydro co-location precedent without the explicit "hybrid" label. Trishuli hydropower station is ~5 km upstream; the Trishuli 33 kV substation into which Bidur/Trishuli feeds is the same substation used by multiple Trishuli-corridor hydropower projects. This is an early example of hybrid-siting-logic's Archetype 1 — shared interconnection between solar and hydro — even though Bidur/Trishuli was not tendered or designed as an explicit hybrid. The economics worked because the land was available, the grid was already there, and the terrain was workable. # Site characteristics Parameter | Value | ---|---| GHI (measured, nearest station class) | ~4.5 kWh/m²/day | Specific yield (actual) | ~1,600 kWh/kWp/yr | Zone (solar-resource-geography-nepal) | Zone C2 — Pokhara / Gandaki mid-hills edge | Monsoon cloud penalty | ~30% (mid-hill location, moderate) | Winter GHI | ~4.0 kWh/m²/day | Nuwakot's mid-hill location is not Nepal's best solar zone — Zone B Terai would produce ~10–15% more per MWp. The site choice reflects transmission-adjacency priority (Trishuli 33 kV proximity) over pure GHI optimisation. The decision is instructive: for Nepal, grid-adjacency beats resource-optimisation in siting priority, which aligns with the hybrid-siting-logic general rule. # Operational performance (FY 2023/24 + FY 2024/25) Expected specific yield: 1,600 kWh/kWp/yr (design) Actual specific yield: ~1,520–1,680 kWh/kWp/yr (within ±5% of design) Availability: high; no recorded extended outages in FY 2023/24 O&M: NEA in-house, with WB Grid Solar & EE Project technical assistance during initial years Monsoon degradation risk: moderate; 2023 monsoon cycle included no damage events # What this project teaches Three lessons forward: ## 1. NEA can build solar on-schedule, on-budget, on-spec. Bidur/Trishuli was delivered within the planned window and within the WB-project cost envelope. The "NEA procurement takes forever" critique (accurate for large hydro) was not validated here. Utility solar is fundamentally easier to deliver than hydropower; lead times of 3–5 years for NEA-EPC and 2–3 years for IPP are achievable. ## 2. Near-hydro siting works operationally. Shared substation utilisation, shared access road, shared O&M community, no measurable operational interference between hydro and PV. Validates the hybrid-siting-logic Archetype 1 thesis at 25 MWp scale; should be replicated at every operating hydropower site with ≥1 ha of adjacent buildable land. ## 3. Mid-hill solar is workable but sub-optimal. The ~30% monsoon penalty means a Zone C plant like Bidur/Trishuli produces ~10% less annual energy per MWp than a Zone B Terai plant of the same design. For new-build, Terai should be first priority. Bidur/Trishuli's site choice is defensible in hindsight because of the grid-adjacency benefit, but the default for the 960 MW tender is correctly Terai. # Related nea-solar — programme context wb-grid-solar-ee-project — financing source hybrid-siting-logic — the Archetype 1 precedent trishuli — the hydropower that shares the substation nea-960mw-solar-tender — the successor programme data-solar-fleet-inventory — the fleet this plant anchors

pages.title: Bishnu Priya Solar Farm (Surya Power, ~1 MWp)
headings: Bishnu Priya Solar Farm (Surya Power, ~1 MWp)
pages.excerpt: Small demonstration-scale IPP solar plant developed by Surya Power Company Pvt. Ltd., commissioned ~2018 in Nawalparasi district, Lumbini Province. The earliest Phase I IPP plant to reach COD and historically significant as Nepal's first grid-tied commercial IPP solar plant.
pages.body_text: Bishnu Priya Solar Farm (Surya Power, ~1 MWp) Small demonstration-scale IPP solar plant developed by Surya Power Company Pvt. Ltd., commissioned ~2018 in Nawalparasi district, Lumbini Province. The earliest Phase I IPP plant to reach COD and historically significant as Nepal's first grid-tied commercial IPP solar plant. # Specifications Item | Value | ---|---| Capacity | ~0.96 MWp | Location | Nawalparasi District, Lumbini Province | Owner / Developer | Surya Power Company Pvt. Ltd. | Zone (solar-resource-geography-nepal) | B1 — Central Terai | Interconnection | Nawalparasi 11 kV | Commissioning | ~2018 | Site elevation | ~130 m | Expected annual generation | ~1.5 GWh/yr | Status | Operating | # Why it matters Bishnu Priya is the proof-of-concept scale — <1 MWp, nominally demonstration but commissioned on commercial terms via a PPA with NEA. It pre-dated the larger Phase I plants (butwal-solar-ridi-8-5mwp, mithila-solar-dhanusha-10mwp) and was the first grid-tied IPP PV plant Nepal's interconnection and PPA machinery processed end-to-end. Its specific contribution to the learning curve: Validated the 11 kV interconnection template for sub-MW IPP solar. First PPA processed through NEA for IPP solar — the document formed part of the baseline for later rounds. Demonstrated the Central Terai GHI numbers at operational scale, confirming satellite estimates from global-solar-atlas-nepal. Bishnu Priya's <1 MWp scale limits its current significance operationally — it contributes <0.1% of the solar fleet — but its place in the institutional timeline is the reason it appears in the NEA FY 2024/25 annual report inventory tables. # Related nea-solar butwal-solar-ridi-8-5mwp, mithila-solar-dhanusha-10mwp — Phase I cohort data-solar-fleet-inventory

pages.title: Budhigandaki Hydroelectric Project
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-17 River Longitudinal Profile of the Budhi Gandaki River
pages.body_text: Budhigandaki Hydroelectric Project Figure 6.3-17 River Longitudinal Profile of the Budhi Gandaki River A 1,200 MW reservoir storage project on the border of Gorkha and Dhading districts, in the gandaki-basin. Considered a national pride initiative and the most important near-term storage intervention for grid stability. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 1,200 MW | Type | Storage / Reservoir | Dam height | 263 m (double-curvature arch) | Reservoir area | ~63 km² | Reservoir volume | 3.3 km³ | Base cost | $2.77 billion (~NPR 374 billion) | Total with interest | ~NPR 406 billion | Resettlement | 3,560 fully displaced families | # Status (updated April 2026) Moving forward — but still pre-construction. Jan 2026: Cabinet approved the investment modality (financial framework) — after years of repeated modality formation without approval Apr 2026: Bids invited for permanent camps and access bridge connecting Dhading and Gorkha — the first concrete construction procurement Land acquisition: ~90% complete. NPR 42.65 billion distributed as compensation for land, structures, plants and trees. 8,117 households in Gorkha and Dhading will be physically and economically displaced Financial modality: 70% debt / 30% equity. Government holds 80% stake, nea holds 20%, through Budhigandaki Company Limited Total cost with interest: NPR 406–460 billion (varies by estimate) Target: Construction start from January 2028, 8-year build — meaning earliest completion ~2036 Revenue projection: annual income NPR 31.48 billion; tariff split at NPR 12.40/unit (winter) and NPR 7.10/unit (monsoon) Generation: 3.38 billion units/year (1.41B winter + 1.97B monsoon) — crucially, 42% of output in winter, far better than RoR fleet ratios License term: proposed 50 years Chinese involvement: none. A previous proposal to award the project to China Gezhouba Group was politically blocked (Nepali Congress opposition). Project is now structured as a domestic investment initiative [!note] Contrast with Pancheshwar Where pancheshwar has produced zero concrete output in 30 years as a bilateral project, Budhigandaki is advancing (slowly) as a domestically controlled venture. The comparison is a data point for the domestic-led-hydro-strategy thesis. # Why It Matters If built, Budhigandaki would not just add generation — it would change the economic character of Nepal's electricity system. It addresses storage-deficit, seasonal-mismatch, and firm-power simultaneously. The revenue difference from shifting monsoon water to dry-season generation at higher prices makes storage an economic optimization instrument, not just infrastructure. [!warning] The project's repeated delays exemplify why buildability and governance matter as much as engineering. Nepal has no shortage of storage concepts — it has a shortage of executed storage. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory

pages.title: Butwal Solar (Ridi Hydropower Development, 8.5 MWp)
pages.excerpt: Early-round IPP utility solar plant developed by Ridi Hydropower Development Co. Ltd., commissioned ~2020 under the 5-developer 24 MWp competitive-bid round that constituted Nepal's first utility-scale solar IPP cycle.
pages.body_text: Butwal Solar (Ridi Hydropower Development, 8.5 MWp) Early-round IPP utility solar plant developed by Ridi Hydropower Development Co. Ltd., commissioned ~2020 under the 5-developer 24 MWp competitive-bid round that constituted Nepal's first utility-scale solar IPP cycle. # Specifications Item | Value | ---|---| Capacity | 8.5 MWp | Location | Rupandehi District, Lumbini Province (Butwal area) | Owner / Developer | Ridi Hydropower Development Co. Ltd. | Zone (solar-resource-geography-nepal) | B2 — Western Terai / Lumbini edge | Interconnection | Rupandehi 33 kV | Commissioning | ~2020 | PPA tariff | Phase I round (NPR 7.30–8.40/kWh band) | Site elevation | ~200 m | Expected annual generation | ~13–14 GWh/yr | Status | Operating | # Why it matters Butwal Solar is one of three plants from the 2016–17 Phase I IPP round that reached COD (the others: mithila-solar-dhanusha-10mwp and bishnu-priya-nawalparasi). Together they proved: 1. IPP-delivered utility solar works in Nepal. The first round successfully moved three plants from PPA to COD within 3–4 years, validating the procurement model NEA expanded in Phase III and IV. 2. Terai grid-adjacency is the default archetype. All three surviving Phase I plants were in Terai districts adjacent to 33 kV substations, validating the siting logic later encoded in the nea-960mw-solar-tender. 3. Phase I tariffs (~NPR 8/kWh) are now above market. By Phase IV (nea-960mw-solar-tender), the same IPP structure clears at ~NPR 6/kWh. Phase I plants are legacy-high-priced relative to today's tender, but their PPAs are locked in and they continue to operate. # Related nea-solar — programme context mithila-solar-dhanusha-10mwp, bishnu-priya-nawalparasi — Phase I cohort hetauda-bharatpur-bardaghat-corridor — grid the plant feeds into data-solar-fleet-inventory

pages.title: Chameliya Hydropower
headings: Internal Generation Cost vs Commercial Reality
Why Chameliya Matters to This Wiki
pages.excerpt: 30 MW Peaking Run-of-River (PROR) project on the Chameliya River, Darchula District, Sudurpashchim Province. Directly owned and operated by the Nepal Electricity Authority — not an independent corporate entity. This page exists as the wiki's primary case study in state capital in
pages.body_text: Chameliya Hydropower Project 30 MW Peaking Run-of-River (PROR) project on the Chameliya River, Darchula District, Sudurpashchim Province. Directly owned and operated by the Nepal Electricity Authority — not an independent corporate entity. This page exists as the wiki's primary case study in state capital inefficiency and the structural asymmetry between NEA's internal cost accounting for its own assets versus the rigid PPA caps it imposes on private developers. # Project Specifications Parameter | Value | ---|---| Capacity | 30 MW | Type | Peaking Run-of-River (PROR) — 6-hour regulating pondage | River | Chameliya River (Mahakali Basin) | District | Darchula, Sudurpashchim Province | Gross head | 103.7 m | Design discharge | 36 m³/s | Annual design generation | 184.2 GWh | Entity type | Direct NEA-owned generation asset | COD | February 10, 2018 | Source: NEA Generation Directorate Report 2080/2081 (16th Issue, 2024). # The Decade-Long Construction Crisis Chameliya represents one of the most troubled infrastructure developments in Nepal's modern history: Civil works began: 2007–2008 (China Gezhouba Group Corporation — civil; Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power — electro-mechanical) Original COD target: June 2011 Initial estimated cost: ~NPR 8 billion (~$100M at historical exchange rate) Actual COD: February 10, 2018 — nearly a decade behind schedule Final cost: ~NPR 15–16 billion ($226M) — capital intensity of $7.5 million per MW vs global benchmark of $1.5–2.5M/MW The primary engineering failure was an 843-meter section of the headrace tunnel where acute geological squeezing deformed the tunnel walls, requiring extensive unplanned re-excavation and structural reinforcement. Contractual disputes over variation orders triggered work stoppages, and the parliamentary Public Accounts Committee (PAC) intervened over corruption allegations and irregular variation approvals. Source: Kathmandu Post; myRepublica. # Internal Generation Cost vs Commercial Reality Chameliya has no commercial Power Purchase Agreement. As a directly NEA-owned asset, its energy is accounted for via internal transfer pricing. For FY2080/81: Metric | Value | ---|---| Total generation | 133,968 MWh | Operation cost | NPR 58.32M (NPR 0.44/kWh) | Royalties | NPR 27.07M | Depreciation | NPR 321.27M | Total allocated cost | NPR 406.66M (NPR 3.04/kWh) | Source: NEA Generation Directorate Report 2080/2081 (16th Issue, 2024). The NPR 3.04/kWh internal generation cost is an accounting artifact, not an economic price. The allocated annual depreciation of NPR 321.27M is mathematically insufficient to amortize a ~NPR 15 billion asset over a 25–30 year lifecycle without substantial unrecorded impairments, sovereign bailouts, or aggressive debt write-downs. The NEA's sovereign backing absorbs the capital failure while the utility's internal books present a deceptively low unit cost. # Why Chameliya Matters to This Wiki Chameliya is the structural mirror-image of ppa-pricing: a state utility that tolerates $7.5M/MW capital costs on its own projects — and records the resulting energy at NPR 3.04/kWh — simultaneously enforces NPR 4.80/8.40 PPA caps on private IPPs that must fully amortize commercial debt at double-digit interest rates. If a private developer faced the geological squeezing and multi-year delays seen at Chameliya, the inflexible PPA structure would force immediate bankruptcy. The private sector bears 100% of construction risk while the NEA's own construction failures are absorbed into the sovereign ledger. This duality — socializing the massive capital failures of public projects while strictly capping the revenue potential of private, highly efficient projects — is the quantitative core of the nea-triple-authority governance problem. The same institution that cannot deliver a 30 MW project on budget in a decade sets the revenue terms for the entire private sector. # Disambiguation from Upper Chameliya Chameliya (30 MW, NEA-owned, COD 2018) must not be confused with Upper Chameliya (40 MW, Api Power Company Limited, private IPP, COD August 2023). Upper Chameliya operates under a commercial PPA with a 6-month wet / 6-month dry seasonal modality and 3% annual escalation. These are entirely separate assets on the same river. Conflating the internal cost data of the NEA asset with the commercial PPA of the private IPP is a frequent error in automated research sweeps. # Prior Research Error — Retracted Earlier research cycles carried an erroneous PPA rate of NPR 8.85/kWh (wet) and NPR 5.40/kWh (dry) for Chameliya. These figures are a data hallucination — they derive from misattributed retail consumer tariffs (NEA Annual Report FY2016/17: "Street Light" at NPR 8.80, "Other Water Supply" at NPR 5.40) and a growth metric text reference ("8.85% activities"). The figures were algorithmically stripped of context and assigned as PPA rates without understanding Nepal's hydro-economic framework, where the dry-season rate must always exceed the wet-season rate. Chameliya has no PPA and never did. These figures have been retracted from the wiki. # See also ppa-pricing — the rate comparison table and internal generation cost analysis nea-triple-authority — the institutional governance framework this case exposes chilime — the NEA subsidiary with favorable PPA billing architecture (contrast case) upper-tamakoshi — the NEA subsidiary facing financial distress despite subsidiary status

pages.title: Chameliya–Jauljibi 220 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: The planned far-western Nepal–India interconnection, routed from Chameliya (Darchula, Nepal) to Jauljibi (Pithoragarh, Uttarakhand). This would be the first 220 kV interconnection in the Mahakali basin and the gateway link for any future hydropower expansion in far-western Nepal
pages.body_text: Chameliya–Jauljibi 220 kV Interconnection The planned far-western Nepal–India interconnection, routed from Chameliya (Darchula, Nepal) to Jauljibi (Pithoragarh, Uttarakhand). This would be the first 220 kV interconnection in the Mahakali basin and the gateway link for any future hydropower expansion in far-western Nepal (including the long-dormant pancheshwar prospect). # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV (proposed) | Status | Planned | Nepal terminal | Chameliya substation (Darchula) | India terminal | Jauljibi (Pithoragarh, Uttarakhand) | Primary basin served | mahakali-basin | Driving projects | chameliya-khola (30 MW operational), future Mahakali cascade | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Only far-western 220 kV cross-border. The existing Tanakpur–Mahendranagar 132 kV link is thermally saturated during monsoon; this is the upgrade path. Unlocks mahakali-basin. No large hydropower on the Mahakali basin has a viable export route at the current voltage; this line is the precondition for pancheshwar economics. Tri-country gateway. The Jauljibi side lies near the India–Nepal–Tibet trijunction; long-horizon planners have flagged it as a potential node in a broader Himalayan grid. # Limitations & Open Questions No construction schedule. Remains in planning documents; neither side has committed capex. Dependent on pancheshwar. The economics of a full 220 kV crossing hinge on the stalled Pancheshwar Multipurpose Project actually delivering. Terrain. Extreme high-altitude alignment through Darchula raises cost per km materially above Terai cross-borders. # See also chameliya-khola — the river and the existing 30 MW plant pancheshwar — the dormant anchor project mahakali-basin india-energy-relationship

pages.title: Chilime
headings: Why Chilime is not a replicable baseline
pages.excerpt: Under construction powerhouse filled with the water and debris during the flood.
pages.body_text: Chilime Under construction powerhouse filled with the water and debris during the flood. HEP Headworks & Powerhouse 22 MW · operating · on the Chilime · in Rasuwa district. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 22 MW | Status | Operating | River | Chilime | District | Rasuwa | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # Notes [!note] This is a registry-backed project record. Capacity, location, and licence status come from the Ministry of Energy registry mirrored in the map data. Narrative context and images are added where public sources are strong enough; the specification table is maintained from the registry. # Performance and Historical Context Chilime is the sector's most cited success case — and its performance metrics justify the reputation. However, the conditions that produced those metrics are not replicable for modern projects. ## Generation overperformance Chilime consistently generates above its contracted energy, a rarity in Nepal's hydro sector: Fiscal Year | Contracted (GWh/yr) | Actual (GWh/yr) | % of Contracted | ---|---|---|---| FY 2075/76 (~2018/19) | 132.9 | 153.7 | 115.6% | FY 2076/77 (~2019/20) | 132.9 | 146.3 | 110.1% | Source: Chilime Hydropower Company Limited annual reports. This overperformance places Chilime in stark contrast to modern IPPs, where zero of 100+ projects evaluated in a recent Urja Khabar audit exceeded 80% of contracted energy. See q-design-discharge for the full generation performance comparison. ## Why Chilime is not a replicable baseline Despite being routinely invoked to justify mass public equity participation in hydropower, Chilime's success is a product of a different regulatory, financial, and climatic era: 1. Conservative Q-design: Chilime was commissioned in 2003 and likely designed on Q65 or higher — a more cautious flow percentile than the Q40–Q45 now permitted under current DoED guidelines. This means its contracted energy was set conservatively low relative to actual hydrology. 2. Reliable snow-fed basin: The Chilime River is a snow-fed system with less seasonal volatility than the monsoon-dominated, rain-fed tributaries where most modern IPPs are located. 3. Pre-climate-shift commissioning: Chilime entered operation before the acceleration of cryospheric melt and shifting monsoon patterns that now undermine the stationarity assumption in hydrological models. 4. Lower financial leverage: Built in an era of lower construction costs and lower debt leverage, Chilime never faced the Debt Service Coverage Ratio pressures that now push post-2015 projects into technical default. See q-design-discharge for DSCR break-point analysis. 5. NEA subsidiary status and PPA rate advantage (VERIFIED): Chilime is an NEA subsidiary (NEA retains 51% controlling stake). Its PPA, signed June 25, 1997, uses a dual-tier billing architecture categorically different from modern IPPs: | Category | Tariff (NPR/kWh) | FY2078/79 Volume (GWh) | FY2078/79 Revenue (NPR) | |---|---|---|---| | Regular Energy | 8.17 | 133.24 | 1,088,000,000 | | Excess Energy | 4.08 | 22.82 | 93,110,000 | | Total | 7.57 (blended) | 156.06 | 1,181,740,179 | Source: Chilime Hydropower Company Limited Annual Report FY2078/79. VERIFIED. This billing architecture — not the per-unit rate alone — is the mechanism of the subsidy. Unlike modern IPPs, which must classify generation by season (wet/dry), Chilime categorizes output as "Regular" vs "Excess." In FY2078/79, 85.4% of total generation (133.24 of 156.06 GWh) was classified as Regular Energy and billed at the premium NPR 8.17 rate year-round — regardless of season. A comparable private IPP must sell the majority of its monsoon-heavy generation at the depressed NPR 4.80 wet-season rate. Quantified rate differential: - Chilime blended rate: NPR 7.57/kWh - Private IPP baseline (optimized 20% dry energy mix): (0.20 × 8.40) + (0.80 × 4.80) = NPR 5.52/kWh - Differential: NPR 2.05/kWh = 37.1% revenue advantage per unit - For a 50 MW comparable private IPP generating 200 GWh/year, this represents approximately NPR 410 million in foregone annual revenue External academic verification: The Fenner School of Environment and Society (Australian National University), Nepal State of Knowledge Report — Renewables and PSH, independently documents this dynamic: > "The major complaint of the rising IPPs is that the playing field is not level, that NEA as generator, transmitter and distributor gives preferential terms to its own projects compared to IPPs; and indeed, this is seen in the case of the Chilime hydropower company that is owned by the NEA and its staff." Citation: Fenner School of Environment and Society, ANU. URL: https://fennerschool.anu.edu.au/files/Nepal%20State%20of%20Knowledge%20Report%20-%20Renewables%20%26%20PSH.pdf. [REQUIRES EXACT CITATION: page number unconfirmed — quote verified by text extraction.] See ppa-pricing for the full rate comparison table and nea-triple-authority for the institutional framework. 6. IPO after profitability, not before: Chilime's 2010 IPO occurred after seven years of profitable operations, enabling the company to distribute a 35% cash dividend within a year of listing. Modern hydro IPOs are routinely floated pre-commissioning or immediately post-COD, when the project is hemorrhaging cash from capitalized interest. See ipo-hydropower-bailout. Chilime's subsequent role as a holding company — spinning off subsidiaries (Rasuwagadhi, Sanjen, Madhya Bhotekoshi) to bypass the terminal BOOT-model asset handover — further reflects a business environment that no longer exists for new entrants. # See also run-of-river-hydropower q-design-discharge nea-triple-authority ppa-pricing ipo-hydropower-bailout

pages.title: Damak–Godak–Phidim 132 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: The eastern 132 kV evacuation chain that the annual book describes in three stages: Damak–Godak, Godak–Phidim, and onward Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli). It is the practical corridor through which the Mai/Kabeli-side hill projects were being tied back into the national system.
pages.body_text: Damak–Godak–Phidim 132 kV Corridor The eastern 132 kV evacuation chain that the annual book describes in three stages: Damak–Godak, Godak–Phidim, and onward Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli). It is the practical corridor through which the Mai/Kabeli-side hill projects were being tied back into the national system. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 45 states that the first Damak–Godak section was already in service, the Godak–Phidim section was completed and charged in 2019, and the Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli) section was commissioned on 6 August 2019. # Key Facts Segment | Length / note | ---------|---------------| Damak–Godak | ~35 km | Godak–Phidim | ~43 km | Phidim–Amarpur (Kabeli) | ~13.33 km | # Why It Matters This is the corridor-scale version of the eastern medium-hill evacuation problem: relatively modest IPP clusters still needed a sequence of new substations and 132 kV links before their output could move. It is directly relevant to project pages such as kabeli-a, kabeli-b-1, and the broader claim-transmission-immediate-blocker argument. # See also kabeli-a kabeli-b-1 claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Dana–Kushma–New Butwal 220 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: The west-central hydropower evacuation corridor — running from the upper Kali Gandaki valley (Dana, Kushma, Parbat) down to New Butwal. Built to pick up the cluster of IPPs in the Modi and Kaligandaki tributaries and tie them into the growing western grid.
pages.body_text: Dana–Kushma–New Butwal 220 kV Corridor The west-central hydropower evacuation corridor — running from the upper Kali Gandaki valley (Dana, Kushma, Parbat) down to New Butwal. Built to pick up the cluster of IPPs in the Modi and Kaligandaki tributaries and tie them into the growing western grid. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV | Status | Operational | Length basis | 127.57 route-km / 255.6 circuit-km | Owner | nea | Terminals | Dana (Myagdi) · Kushma (Parbat) · New Butwal (Rupandehi) | Commissioned | Dana-Kushma and Kushma-New Butwal sections now operational; New Butwal fully operational since October 2023 | Corridor ID | | # Map Interpretation The major transmission network now represents this corridor as two source-backed 220 kV double-circuit features: Dana-Kushma and Kushma-New Butwal. The geometry comes from RPGCL official vector linework, while the length/status basis comes from NEA transmission and annual reports. Current validation result: 2 features, 0 inferred connectors, 0 remaining endpoint gaps, and 128.027 km traced length against a 127.57 route-km basis. Public decision: default-visible, operational, high confidence. # Significance Unlocks a trapped basin. Before this corridor, the Modi / upper Kali Gandaki IPP cluster had to evacuate through congested 132 kV toward Pokhara. Dana–Kushma–New Butwal gave the basin its own exit. Pre-positions for 400 kV. Terminates at New Butwal, the same substation that will host the mca-central-400 terminus and the gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection. Significance for kali-gandaki-a's downstream cascade. The corridor accommodates future cascade additions in the basin. # Limitations & Open Questions Terrain risk. Myagdi-Parbat segment climbs steep, landslide-prone terrain; 2023 monsoon damage required emergency tower reinforcement. Still 220 kV. As the gandaki-basin pipeline grows, 220 kV may again become the bottleneck; the mca-central-400 and gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection make New Butwal the reinforcement point to watch. # See also kali-gandaki-a — the basin anchor mca-central-400 — the 400 kV it links to gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection — the cross-border at New Butwal data-layer-transmission-connected-traced-network — the public layer where this corridor now appears

pages.title: Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: Nepal's first and — at the time of writing — only operational 400 kV cross-border link. Every exported MWh, including the bangladesh-trade-route allocation, transits this line. It is the linchpin of Nepal's current export strategy.
pages.body_text: Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV Interconnection Nepal's first and — at the time of writing — only operational 400 kV cross-border link. Every exported MWh, including the bangladesh-trade-route allocation, transits this line. It is the linchpin of Nepal's current export strategy. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV (operated initially at 220 kV, upgraded 2018) | Status | Operational | Commissioned | February 20, 2016 | Nepal terminal | Dhalkebar substation (Dhanusha) | India terminal | Muzaffarpur (Bihar) | Length | ~140 km (≈40 km Nepal + ≈100 km India) | Developers | Cross Border Power Transmission Company (CBPTC) — NEA + Indian JV | Financing | ADB, KfW | Current transfer | ~800–1,200 MW monsoon, ~300–500 MW dry season | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Export artery. Until gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection and inaruwa-purnea-interconnection commission, this is the only 400 kV door out. Roughly 95% of Nepal's 2024–25 export revenue moved through it. IEX access. Unlocks the Indian Energy Exchange market — the day-ahead pricing that has made monsoon exports profitable for the first time in Nepal's history. Bangladesh corridor. Also the Nepal-side leg of bangladesh-trade-route, which then transits through India on the Baharampur–Bheramara link. # Limitations & Open Questions Single point of failure. No 400 kV backup cross-border exists yet. An outage collapses export revenue immediately. Indian-side evacuation. Muzaffarpur terminus's onward capacity into India's Eastern Regional Grid is approaching saturation under peak Nepal export scenarios. Dispatch veto. Scheduling is set by India's NRLDC — Nepal cannot unilaterally increase flow even when physical capacity exists. # See also hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — upstream feeder bangladesh-trade-route india-energy-relationship hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Dhungesanghu–Basantapur 220/132 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: KC-3: Transmission Line Stretch from Basantapur
pages.body_text: Dhungesanghu–Basantapur 220/132 kV Corridor KC-3: Transmission Line Stretch from Basantapur Chilime Hub GIS SS day view. An eastern Koshi-corridor buildout piece anchored on the Dhungesanghu and Basantapur substations. In the annual book it appears as part of the broader 220 kV Koshi corridor work needed to collect and move eastern hill generation. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 The appendix under-construction list names Dhungesanghu (132/33 kV, 30 MVA) and Basantapur (220/132 + 132/33 kV, 230 MVA) as active substation works under the Koshi Corridor 220 kV transmission line program. # Key Facts Substation | Capacity | ------------|---------:| Dhungesanghu | 30 MVA | Basantapur | 230 MVA | # Why It Matters The corridor is one of the enabling eastern-grid assets that sit behind arun-3-adjacent evacuation logic, even if it is not the final cross-border line itself. It is also part of the wider “collect before export” architecture summarized later in nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 and structurally linked to inaruwa-purnea-interconnection. # See also inaruwa-purnea-interconnection arun-3 nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 stranded-generation

pages.title: Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-20 River Longitudinal Profile of the Dudh Koshi River
pages.body_text: Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project Figure 6.3-20 River Longitudinal Profile of the Dudh Koshi River Project area of Dudhkoshi Storage A 635–670 MW storage reservoir scheme straddling Okhaldhunga, Khotang and Solukhumbu in the eastern koshi-basin — on paper, the single largest dry-season lever in Nepal's planned pipeline, and a decades-old feasibility that is only now approaching a real financing decision. NEA-owned, AIIB-financed-in-proposal, ~2027 construction start, ~2031 commercial operation if the schedule holds. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Total capacity | 635–670 MW (two design-basis figures in circulation) | Main powerhouse | 600 MW (underground) | Dam-toe powerhouse | 70 MW (ecological flow) | Dam height | 200–220 m | River | Dudh Koshi (koshi-basin) | Districts | Okhaldhunga, Khotang, Solukhumbu | Reservoir volume | ~1,581 MCM | Design energy | 3,377–3,444 GWh / year | Pumped storage component | 200 MW planned addition | Owner | nea | Financing | AIIB sovereign proposed | Schedule | Construction from ~2027; COD target ~2031 | Cost | ~USD 2.2 billion (NPR ~308 billion) | Displacement | ~162 households fully, ~988 partially | # Significance Dudhkoshi matters on three axes at once, and it is the combination that makes it structurally important: 1. Scale. 635–670 MW of reservoir storage in a system where the entire current storage fleet is 106 MW (kulekhani-cascade) and the only storage actually under construction is 140 MW (tanahu-hydropower). Dudhkoshi alone would roughly match the combined 2025 hydropower fleet of Nepal on energy volume and transform the dry-season firm-power picture. 2. Pumped storage first mover. A 200 MW pumped storage component is planned as Nepal's first "water battery." This turns Dudhkoshi into a grid-balancing asset, not just an energy source, and is the natural counterpart to the growing RoR fleet that drives Nepal's seasonal-mismatch problem on the daily timescale. 3. Climate-aware design. The project is being scoped with landscape stabilization and sediment management built in — a response to Nepal's sediment-as-design-constraint reality and to the fact that the upper Dudh Koshi routinely receives GLOF pulses from the Everest region (see glof-risk). Compare Dudhkoshi (670 MW, storage, AIIB, domestic) to arun-3 (900 MW, peaking RoR, SJVN, export-first). Both are in the Koshi basin; both are in the 2030 horizon; their strategic purposes are almost opposite. Dudhkoshi is the one that changes Nepal's winter. # Limitations & Controversies Four-decade gestation. The original feasibility was ADB-supported in the 1980s. Land acquisition restarted only in 2021. A project that has been "about to be built" for 40 years carries a credibility tax that any new timeline must earn against. Displacement footprint. The current figures (~162 households fully, ~988 partially affected) are material and will require a resettlement plan on par with anything Nepal has attempted. Social compliance challenges at smaller projects like Tanahu (tanahu-hydropower) are a warning about how much execution discipline Dudhkoshi will need. GLOF exposure. The 2024 Thame Valley GLOF traveled down the Dudh Koshi mainstem. A 220 m dam on a GLOF-exposed river demands a design flood that correctly accounts for cascading lake outburst scenarios, not a stationary climatology. How that design flood is set — and how conservative the spillway margin is — is the safety-critical question for the entire project. Financing latency. AIIB sovereign financing is proposed, not committed. The typical latency between "proposed" and "approved" for projects at this scale is several years, and Nepal will need to demonstrate fiscal space, guarantee structures and procurement readiness to move the conversation. # Official Visual References The new DKJVCL project material is a stronger visual source than the old bridge photos currently sitting in the top strip. The two most useful figure pages in the 2026 development teaser are the detailed dam-site layout and the basin study-area map. General layout of the Dudhkoshi dam site from the 2026 development teaser. Study-area and catchments map from the same teaser. Source: Dudhkoshi Storage Hydroelectric Project Development Teaser (PDF) # Related storage-deficit — what Dudhkoshi would begin to address koshi-basin — the basin context glof-risk — the 2024 Thame Valley GLOF traveled down the Dudh Koshi river tanahu-hydropower firm-power sediment-as-design-constraint # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory

pages.title: Gandaki Basin
pages.excerpt: The Gandaki (Narayani) basin is Nepal's central major river system, draining ~32,148 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Kali Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki, Trishuli, Seti, Madi, and East Rapti. The Kali Gandaki gorge between Annapurna and Dhaulagiri drops >5,500 verti
pages.body_text: Gandaki Basin # Overview The Gandaki (Narayani) basin is Nepal's central major river system, draining ~32,148 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Kali Gandaki, Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki, Trishuli, Seti, Madi, and East Rapti. The Kali Gandaki gorge between Annapurna and Dhaulagiri drops >5,500 vertical meters — by some measures the world's deepest. This is Nepal's most developed hydropower corridor. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Area in Nepal | 32,148 km² | Average discharge (border) | 1,952 m³/s (~61.6 BCM/yr) | Monsoon share | 74–80% | Peak-to-dry ratio | 22–30:1 | Gross hydropower potential | 19,803 MW (27.3% of national total) | # Hydropower Significance The most mature "buildable" basin. Hosts Nepal's early landmark projects — Kali Gandaki A (144 MW), Middle Marsyangdi (70 MW), and the kulekhani-cascade (on a tributary). The corridor benefited from road access (Prithvi and Siddhartha highways) and historical institutional familiarity. The proposed budhigandaki (1,200 MW) would be the basin's first major storage project and a system-shaping intervention for nepal-energy-profile. # Constraints sediment-as-design-constraint — the Kali Gandaki A plant is the definitive case study for turbine erosion. Suspended sediment spikes from 20 ppm (dry) to 50,000 ppm (monsoon). 65 million tonnes/year pass the dam site. More developed does not mean fully tapped. Significant untapped tributary potential remains. # Downstream Significance The Gandak system irrigates ~1.5–2 million ha in Bihar and eastern Uttar Pradesh. The Gandak Treaty (1959) gave Nepal only 15 MW from a major barrage — one of the most cited examples of treaty asymmetry. See hydro-geopolitics. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Gorakhpur–New Butwal 400 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: Nepal's second 400 kV cross-border link, under construction. When commissioned it will double Nepal's 400 kV export capacity and break the single-point-of-failure dependency on dhalkebar-muzaffarpur. It is the direct complement of the mca-central-400 corridor, which terminates at
pages.body_text: Gorakhpur–New Butwal 400 kV Interconnection Nepal's second 400 kV cross-border link, under construction. When commissioned it will double Nepal's 400 kV export capacity and break the single-point-of-failure dependency on dhalkebar-muzaffarpur. It is the direct complement of the mca-central-400 corridor, which terminates at the same New Butwal substation. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status | Under construction | Nepal terminal | New Butwal (Rupandehi) | India terminal | Gorakhpur (Uttar Pradesh) | Length | ~130 km (~18 km Nepal + ~112 km India) | Nepal-side developer | IL&FS / NEA JV (earlier) → India-side POWERGRID | Indian target | Commissioning in 2026 | Nepal target | Commissioning with mca-central-400 (2026–27) | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Redundancy for the eastern link. Full N-1 cross-border security for the first time once both operate in parallel. Western evacuation. Opens a direct export door for gandaki-basin and karnali-basin generation — which has historically had no outlet matching its latent capacity. MCA corridor payoff. The US-funded mca-central-400 spine has little export value without this link — the two projects are schedule-linked in NEA's integrated resource plan. # Limitations & Open Questions Schedule slippage. The Indian side has slipped repeatedly since the 2018 MoU; completion dates have moved from 2022 → 2024 → 2026. Nepal-side readiness. New Butwal substation 400 kV bays await final MCA corridor completion; misalignment of the two projects could strand capacity on either side. Tariff mechanism. Cross-border trading charges for this link have not yet been fully negotiated under the 2021 cross-border guidelines. # See also mca-central-400 — the Nepal-side upstream corridor dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the link it is meant to complement claim-transmission-immediate-blocker hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: The 220 kV east–west spine across central Nepal, running through the Hetauda → Bharatpur → Bardaghat load corridor. This is the line that carried Nepal's grid before the 400 kV era — and still carries the majority of Terai distribution load.
pages.body_text: Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV Corridor The 220 kV east–west spine across central Nepal, running through the Hetauda → Bharatpur → Bardaghat load corridor. This is the line that carried Nepal's grid before the 400 kV era — and still carries the majority of Terai distribution load. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV (double circuit) | Status | Operational | Length basis | 146.5 route-km / 293.0 circuit-km | Owner | nea | Terminals | Hetauda (Makwanpur) · Bharatpur (Chitwan) · Bardaghat (Nawalparasi) | Corridor ID | | # Map Interpretation The connected transmission layer now represents this corridor as two source-controlled features: Hetauda-New Bharatpur and New Bharatpur-Bardaghat. This replaced the older over-selected RPGCL extraction, which mixed adjacent 220 kV fragments into the HBB corridor. Current validation result: 2 features, 0 inferred connectors, 0 remaining endpoint gaps, and 143.365 km traced length against a 146.5 route-km basis. Public decision: default-visible, caveated. # Significance Load-centre spine. Serves the Bharatpur–Hetauda–Butwal urban belt, Nepal's second-largest consumption cluster after the Kathmandu Valley. Redundancy for 400 kV. During HDDI outages this corridor provides a lower-voltage fallback for east–west flow, at reduced capacity. Tie-in for western generation. Connects Marsyangdi-basin and Kali Gandaki-basin output to the central grid before the mca-central-400 fully commissions. # Limitations & Open Questions Legacy capacity. 220 kV thermal limits constrain the amount of surplus generation the corridor can absorb; during monsoon peak, IPPs in the west are partly curtailed despite availability. Substation bottlenecks. Bharatpur's bus configuration has been flagged as a weak link in NEA's 2024 reliability review. Geometry precision remains caveated. The public route is now coherent and source-bounded, but the Bharatpur-Bardaghat portion is still a corridor-level SIA trace rather than a tower-by-tower alignment. # See also mca-central-400 — the 400 kV reinforcement dana-kushma-butwal-corridor — the 220 kV it links with in the west hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone

pages.title: Hetauda–Dhalkebar–Inaruwa 400 kV Backbone
pages.excerpt: 220 kV Substation at Dhalkebar
pages.body_text: Hetauda–Dhalkebar–Inaruwa 400 kV Backbone 220 kV Substation at Dhalkebar Nepal's first — and, until the MCA corridor finishes, only — 400 kV transmission line. The eastern spine that carries the country's aggregated hydropower output from central Nepal to the dhalkebar-muzaffarpur border crossing and onward to India. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status | Partially operational: Dhalkebar-Inaruwa operational; Hetauda-Dhalkebar still under construction | Total spine length | ~288 km route basis | Owner | nea (Nepal Electricity Authority) | Key substations | Hetauda · Dhalkebar · Inaruwa | Segment status | Hetauda-Dhalkebar: under construction · Dhalkebar-Inaruwa: operational since June 2024 | Financing | ADB, KfW, World Bank (segment-wise) | Pane of operation | Central / Eastern Terai belt | Corridor ID | | # Map Interpretation In the connected transmission layer, HDDI is represented as two World Bank RAP-controlled corridor features: Hetauda-Dhalkebar and Dhalkebar-Inaruwa. The RPGCL overview fragments remain available in the raw audit/source layer, but they no longer drive the public connected network because they made the corridor read too straight and materially short. Public decision: default-visible, caveated. The current validation result has 0 inferred connectors, 0 remaining gaps, and a -1.22% length delta against the current 288 route-km basis. The geometry is still a document-grounded corridor trace, not a tower-by-tower alignment. # Significance First 400 kV domestic backbone. The operational Dhalkebar-Inaruwa section and the partly complete Hetauda-Dhalkebar section are the core east-west transfer path behind Nepal's export-readiness story. Aggregation point for IPP clusters. Evacuates the khimti-dhalkebar-corridor, Sunkoshi, Tamakoshi and (once online) arun-3 blocks toward India. Decouples load centres. Before HDDI, the Kathmandu Valley and the eastern Terai were weakly linked; HDDI re-wrote the grid topology. Mixed construction and operating state. Dhalkebar-Inaruwa is operational, while Hetauda-Dhalkebar remains in the build queue. Treating the whole backbone as operational would overstate current transfer readiness. # Limitations & Open Questions Single-point of failure for exports. With no parallel 400 kV inside Nepal until the MCA corridor commissions, any HDDI outage collapses export revenue within hours. This is the operational form of the claim-transmission-immediate-blocker argument. Thermal margin tightens as generation grows. NEA has flagged loading rates approaching design limits during the 2024 monsoon peak; mca-central-400 cannot arrive soon enough. N-1 reliability not fully certified. Sub-station-level redundancy at Dhalkebar is the subject of ongoing reinforcement projects. Map precision caveat. The public trace is now source-bounded and length consistent, but it is still not a tower-level engineering alignment. A future pass should replace it only if NEA or a lender publishes alignment sheets or tower coordinates. # See also mca-central-400 — the parallel 400 kV spine dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the border link HDDI feeds inaruwa-purnea-interconnection — future secondary export route off HDDI claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Inaruwa–Purnea 400 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: The planned third 400 kV Nepal–India link, critical to evacuating the arun-3 export block (900 MW) and later upper-karnali-tier eastern generation. Currently in implementation-setup phase — the route exists on paper and in the bilateral MoU record, but construction has not begun.
pages.body_text: Inaruwa–Purnea 400 kV Interconnection The planned third 400 kV Nepal–India link, critical to evacuating the arun-3 export block (900 MW) and later upper-karnali-tier eastern generation. Currently in implementation-setup phase — the route exists on paper and in the bilateral MoU record, but construction has not begun. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status | Implementation setup | Nepal terminal | Inaruwa substation (Sunsari) — eastern terminus of hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone | India terminal | Purnea (Bihar) | Length | ~160 km total (≈50 km Nepal, ≈110 km India) | Primary purpose | Evacuation of arun-3 to the Indian grid | Status driver | Tied to arun-3 COD — originally 2023, now 2027+ | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Arun-3 depends on it. SJVN's 900 MW arun-3 was approved under an export-first model. Without this line, its generation cannot reach the Indian market at rated capacity — a direct instance of stranded-generation. Third 400 kV cross-border. Combined with dhalkebar-muzaffarpur and gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection, it would give Nepal three east/central/west 400 kV export paths — the first real geographic redundancy. Koshi basin anchor. Establishes the first 400 kV link serving the koshi-basin directly, opening the door to future eastern storage (e.g. dudhkoshi-storage). # Limitations & Open Questions Schedule tied to Arun-3. Each slip in Arun-3 construction pushes the cross-border line as well — and vice versa. Environmental route. Alignment through Sunsari and Saptari districts still contested in local consultations. Indian-side approvals. Bihar state government concurrence and CEA tariff framework are the remaining gating items. # See also arun-3 — the project this line is built for hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — upstream feeder from Inaruwa koshi-basin stranded-generation

pages.title: India — Energy Relationship with Nepal
pages.excerpt: Energy Balance (GWh) in FY 2076/77 (2019/20)
pages.body_text: India — Energy Relationship with Nepal Energy Balance (GWh) in FY 2076/77 (2019/20) India is the decisive external actor in Nepal's hydropower future. Not just as a neighbor, but as the main practical export market, balancing supplier in dry periods, transit state for Bangladesh sales, and counterpart in major treaty-linked water and power projects. # India's Multiple Roles Role | Mechanism | ------|-----------| Primary buyer | Only viable large-scale export market for Nepali electricity | Dry-season supplier | Nepal imports 400–500 MW daily in winter at NPR 11.50–16/unit | Approval gatekeeper | Indian regulatory approval required for cross-border trade | Transit state | Bangladesh electricity sales route through Indian grid | Treaty counterpart | Koshi (1954), Gandak (1959), Mahakali (1996) treaties | Investment filter | India blocks Chinese equity/EPC projects from cross-border export market | # Treaty History Treaty | Year | What Nepal Got | What India Got | --------|------|---------------|----------------| Koshi | 1954/1966 | Flood control (shared); INR 19,200/yr rent | Barrage on Nepali soil; irrigation rights | Gandak | 1959 | 15 MW | Irrigation over ~50x Nepal's allocation | Mahakali | 1996 | 50% of pancheshwar output | 50% of output + flood control + irrigation | Net result | | 15 MW received after 70 years of treaties | Significant irrigation and flood infrastructure | # The China Factor India views Nepal strictly as its strategic sphere of influence. It has explicitly blocked purchase of electricity from Nepali projects with Chinese equity or Chinese EPC contractors, forcing Nepali developers to align with Indian interests for market access. See india-cbte-2018. # Bangladesh Diversification Bangladesh entered as a buyer (FY 2024/25: NPR 266.7 million initial export). But the route depends on Indian transit, Indian approval, and Indian grid. Bangladesh diversifies the buyer set more than it diversifies the corridor and political dependence structure. # Related hydro-geopolitics — the analytical frame seasonal-arbitrage-trap — the trade dynamic pancheshwar — the stalled mega-project bhutan-hydropower-model — the comparison case

pages.title: Kabeli / Godak-Soyak-Amarpur 132 kV Corridor
pages.excerpt: The Kabeli 132 kV corridor is an eastern Nepal branch network around the Soyak-Godak-Amarpur/Phidim area. It is represented as a branch corridor because the recovered NEA Kabeli IEE describes a bifurcated route rather than a single linear line.
pages.body_text: Kabeli / Godak-Soyak-Amarpur 132 kV Corridor The Kabeli 132 kV corridor is an eastern Nepal branch network around the Soyak-Godak-Amarpur/Phidim area. It is represented as a branch corridor because the recovered NEA Kabeli IEE describes a bifurcated route rather than a single linear line. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132 kV | Status | Under construction | Corridor ID | | Public map features | , , | Source basis | NEA Kabeli IEE pages 25-27 | Official route length | 83.74 km | Traced network length | 94.112 km | Validation delta | +12.39% | # Map Interpretation The connected network keeps Kabeli as a branch system: a southern trunk up to Soyak, one arm toward Godak, and one arm toward Phidim/Amarpur. The branch ends are not artificially connected to one another. This matters for reader trust. A single forced line would look cleaner, but it would misrepresent the project structure. # Confidence Public decision: default-visible, caveated. The branch topology is source-supported and has no remaining endpoint gaps. The caveat is route precision: the current manual trace is still a document-grounded reconstruction, not a digitized alignment sheet. # Caveats The highest-value improvement is to digitize the branch geometry more carefully from the Kabeli IEE route figures while preserving the trunk, Godak branch, and Amarpur/Phidim branch as separate network features. # See also data-layer-transmission-connected-traced-network claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Kali Gandaki A
pages.excerpt: Nepal's largest operating power plant of any kind (144 MW), the Kali Gandaki A run-of-river scheme at Mirmi has anchored the national grid since 2002. It is Nepali-owned (NEA), ADB/JBIC-financed, and a useful foil to the newer India-developed export projects: it shows what a mult
pages.body_text: Kali Gandaki A Nepal's largest operating power plant of any kind (144 MW), the Kali Gandaki A run-of-river scheme at Mirmi has anchored the national grid since 2002. It is Nepali-owned (NEA), ADB/JBIC-financed, and a useful foil to the newer India-developed export projects: it shows what a multilaterally-funded, domestically-dispatched fleet backbone looks like — and also what two decades of wear do to one. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 144 MW (3 × 48 MW Toshiba Francis units) | Status | Operating since 2002 | Type | Run-of-river with pondage | River | Kali Gandaki at Mirmi (gandaki-basin) | District | Palpa / Syangja (powerhouse at Mirmi, Syangja; dam upstream in Palpa) | Owner / Operator | nea | Gross head | 115 m | Design energy | ~860 GWh / year | Actual avg output | ~592 GWh / year | Construction cost | USD 354.8 million (ADB + JBIC) | # Significance Kali Gandaki A still matters out of proportion to its size. At 144 MW it is only about 4% of Nepal's 2025 installed fleet, but it was for two decades the single largest plant on the grid — and in most years it produced more firm, dispatchable energy than any other asset NEA operates. It remains the reference point when discussing what a Nepali-utility-run, multilaterally-financed project can look like on the buildability spectrum. Compare kali-gandaki-a to two neighbours. upper-tamakoshi (456 MW, 2021) is larger, newer and also Nepali-developed, but pure RoR without pondage. arun-3 (900 MW, under construction) will be nameplate-bigger but peaking-design and India-dispatched. Kali Gandaki A sits between them in era and design philosophy — shared ADB lineage with Tanahu, NEA ownership like Upper Tamakoshi, and RoR-with-pondage shape that smooths evening load. # Limitations & Controversies The design-energy gap. The project was designed for roughly 860 GWh/year but has averaged closer to 592 GWh — ~30% below design. The ADB's own performance evaluation flagged sedimentation, variable hydrology and aging electromechanical equipment as factors. That gap is a real-world reminder that Nepal's nameplate capacity is not the same as its delivered energy, a distinction central to firm-power and seasonal-mismatch analysis. Rehabilitation overhang. In 2024 NEA floated a tender for design and supervision consultants to rehabilitate all three units. Any rehab of a fleet anchor plant introduces real derating risk during outage windows — exactly when the dry-season storage-deficit bites hardest. Timing the rehab around Nepal's import / export contracts with India is a non-trivial coordination problem. Pondage vs storage. Kali Gandaki A is not a reservoir project. The pondage it does have smooths daily load, not seasonal, so when monsoon flows fall off in January–May, Kali Gandaki A's contribution falls with them. Nepal's structural answer to that problem still sits in pages like dudhkoshi-storage, tanahu-hydropower and budhigandaki — not here. # See also gandaki-basin upper-tamakoshi run-of-river-hydropower firm-power nea

pages.title: Kali Gandaki Kowan
pages.excerpt: Kali Gandaki-Kowan is a 400 MW hydropower project under construction on the Kali Gandaki River — one of the largest projects in Nepal's active construction pipeline.
pages.body_text: Kali Gandaki-Kowan Kali Gandaki-Kowan is a 400 MW hydropower project under construction on the Kali Gandaki River — one of the largest projects in Nepal's active construction pipeline. # Kali Gandaki Basin The Kali Gandaki is one of Nepal's three major river systems, draining the region between Dhaulagiri and Annapurna — the deepest gorge in the world. The river's steep gradient and substantial dry-season baseflow (fed by glacial and snow melt) make it one of Nepal's most attractive hydropower corridors. Existing projects in the basin include Kali Gandaki A (144 MW, operating), Upper Modi A, and multiple projects across the Marsyangdi and Seti tributaries. # Transmission Context A 400 MW project requires significant transmission infrastructure for power evacuation. The Kali Gandaki corridor is served by the Dana-Kushma-Butwal 220 kV corridor and connects into the national east-west backbone. However, evacuation capacity at this scale remains a binding constraint — projects in the Kali Gandaki and Marsyangdi valleys compete for limited transmission slots toward the Butwal and Kathmandu load centers. See claim-transmission-immediate-blocker. # Construction Risk at Scale Projects in this capacity range face proportionally higher execution risk. The Kali Gandaki valley's complex geology, combined with the logistical challenges of moving heavy equipment through narrow mountain roads, has historically pushed build costs well above the NPR 200 million/MW mark for comparable large projects. Whether Kali Gandaki-Kowan can achieve competitive per-MW costs will determine whether it becomes a debt-burdened asset or a genuinely viable generator. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 400 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Kali Gandaki (gandaki-basin) | District | Mustang | Province | Gandaki Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability

pages.title: Karnali Basin
pages.excerpt: The Karnali basin is Nepal's western major river system and the largest by area at ~43,153 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Bheri, West Seti, Humla Karnali, Mugu Karnali, and Tila. The Karnali feeds the Ghaghara, which contributes ~40% of the Ganges' baseflow at Varanasi
pages.body_text: Karnali Basin # Overview The Karnali basin is Nepal's western major river system and the largest by area at ~43,153 km² within Nepal. Its tributaries include Bheri, West Seti, Humla Karnali, Mugu Karnali, and Tila. The Karnali feeds the Ghaghara, which contributes ~40% of the Ganges' baseflow at Varanasi. This is Nepal's most dramatically underused basin — essentially zero large-scale hydropower despite having the best sites. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Area in Nepal | 43,153 km² | Average discharge (border) | 1,256 m³/s (~39.6 BCM/yr) | Monsoon share | 65–72% | Peak-to-dry ratio | 15–20:1 (best stability) | Gross hydropower potential | 20,385 MW (28.1% of national total) | # Why It Matters Most The Karnali has the best dry-season flow stability of any major Nepali system (lowest peak-to-dry ratio at 15–20:1 vs 25–50:1 for Koshi). WECS calls it the basin with the highest hydropower potential. It is the most obvious strategic priority for future development. # Why It Remains Undeveloped Remoteness — western Nepal has historically had fewer roads, less political attention, and less financing reach Transmission gap — the grid west of Butwal relies on legacy 132 kV lines incapable of handling large generation Political neglect — eastern-centric development patterns systematically disadvantaged the west The proposed karnali-chisapani (10,800 MW) alone would transform Nepal's energy posture, but it remains conceptual # Climate Projections CMIP6 modeling projects the Karnali will experience 3–23% decrease in winter/spring discharge by end of century, alongside 18–51% increase in monsoon/post-monsoon discharge. Temporal concentration of water will become even more extreme. See peak-water. A useful basin-specific warning from ICIMOD: over 1981–2012, average precipitation in the Karnali study basin trended downward by 4.91 mm/year, yet discharge stayed broadly constant over the observed record. That is exactly the kind of rainfall-flow decoupling that makes annual averages misleading for energy planning. # Downstream Significance The Karnali/Ghaghara system serves ~2–3 million ha in UP and connects to Bangladesh's Ganges flow. See ganges-contribution. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Karnali Chisapani Project
pages.excerpt: The largest single hydropower project ever proposed for Nepal at 10,800 MW. Located on the karnali-basin main stem. Currently conceptual — requires massive downstream bilateral buy-in.
pages.body_text: Karnali Chisapani Project The largest single hydropower project ever proposed for Nepal at 10,800 MW. Located on the karnali-basin main stem. Currently conceptual — requires massive downstream bilateral buy-in. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 10,800 MW | Dam height | ~270 m | Gross storage | 28.2 BCM | Usable live capacity | 16.2 BCM | Status | Conceptual | # Significance If ever realized, Karnali Chisapani would make Nepal one of Asia's major power producers. It would provide massive storage-deficit relief, flood control for downstream India, and irrigation regulation for the Ghaghara system. The project also illustrates the wb-ganges-strategic-basin-assessment valley-shape paradox: despite the enormous dam, usable storage is only 16.2 of 28.2 BCM due to steep valley walls. # Why It Remains Conceptual Scale of investment far exceeds Nepal's fiscal capacity Requires India as co-investor and primary buyer Downstream bilateral politics (water sharing, flood control valuation) Environmental and social displacement at unprecedented scale See buildability and hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Karnali Solar Energy Project
headings: 1. It is the first formal NEA–AEPC co-delivery structure at scale.
2. It validates solar + BESS economics at the remote site
3. It prepares the grid-edge integration question
pages.excerpt: A programme line referenced in the NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25 (Distribution / Customer Service Directorate) covering off-grid solar plants with Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in Karnali, with operation and maintenance support from NEA. The programme is emerging as Nepal'
pages.body_text: Karnali Solar Energy Project A programme line referenced in the NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25 (Distribution / Customer Service Directorate) covering off-grid solar plants with Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in Karnali, with operation and maintenance support from NEA. The programme is emerging as Nepal's institutional template for off-grid solar + BESS hybrid delivery in grid-absent districts. # What the project is Location: Karnali Province — the grid-weakest and resource-strongest off-grid zone in Nepal (Dolpa, Humla, Mugu, Jumla, Kalikot, Jajarkot, Rukum, Surkhet, Salyan, Dailekh). Configuration: Multi-site, each site ~50 kWp – 1 MWp solar + BESS (4–8 hour). Operational model: NEA + AEPC joint delivery with community co-operative O&M. Scale indication: approximate aggregate ~5–15 MWp across multiple sites (scope continuing to evolve; project documentation is preliminary). Status: In-development / early implementation as of FY 2024/25; firm aggregate MW to be verified from AEPC progress reports. # Why this programme matters Three things that make Karnali Solar the institutional template rather than just another remote project: ## 1. It is the first formal NEA–AEPC co-delivery structure at scale. Historically AEPC delivered off-grid solar through community cooperatives; NEA delivered grid-tied solar through tenders. Karnali Solar Energy Project is the first programme where NEA provides O&M support and technical backstop to AEPC-delivered off-grid systems, pre-structured for eventual grid-edge integration when the NEA radial feeder eventually reaches each site. ## 2. It validates solar + BESS economics at the remote site Diesel generation in remote Karnali sites costs NPR 60–90/kWh (delivered fuel + logistics + wear). Solar + 8-hour BESS at ~NPR 25–35/kWh (grant-supported) is 2–3× cheaper than the incumbent — the value gap is enormous. This is the highest-leverage per-kWh solar in Nepal, by far. ## 3. It prepares the grid-edge integration question As NEA's grid finally reaches former off-grid villages (~8.6% of households still grid-absent), what happens to the community solar + BESS asset? Karnali Solar Energy Project pre-formalises this: the asset continues to operate, is tariff-integrated with the NEA grid, and the BESS provides local reactive-power / voltage-stability service at the grid edge. Without this pre-formalisation, each off-grid system becomes stranded on grid arrival (the historical pattern). # Site-by-site data Preliminary publication from NEA FY 2024/25 references the programme at aggregate level; per-site details (exact location, MWp, BESS hours, developer, community partner) require cross-reference with AEPC monitoring reports. # Relevance to project Anchors the rooftop-minigrid-offgrid track for Karnali specifically Evidence for claim-solar-political-coalition-is-rural — the rural-coalition politics in action Operational template for solar + BESS economics beyond data-solar-hydro-lcoe's utility frame Institutional precedent for the AEPC–NEA integration protocol argued in aepc # Gaps / next data Exact site list, MWp per site, BESS specification, developer / community partner Cumulative CAPEX / CAPEX per kWp FY 2024/25 generation + diesel-displacement data AEPC monitoring report for the programme # Related aepc nea-solar rooftop-minigrid-offgrid karnali-basin — geographic context data-rooftop-shs-deployment — the Karnali SHS deployment footprint this programme builds on

pages.title: Kataiya–Kushaha 132 kV Cross-Border Interconnection
pages.excerpt: The oldest Nepal–India interconnection still in operation — the historic first link that defined the Nepal–India power trade template. Originally energised in 1971 at 33 kV, upgraded to 132 kV, it has been through five decades of bilateral tariff and exchange-rate politics.
pages.body_text: Kataiya–Kushaha 132 kV Interconnection The oldest Nepal–India interconnection still in operation — the historic first link that defined the Nepal–India power trade template. Originally energised in 1971 at 33 kV, upgraded to 132 kV, it has been through five decades of bilateral tariff and exchange-rate politics. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132 kV (originally 33 kV) | Status | Operational (since 1971) | Nepal terminal | Kushaha (Sunsari) | India terminal | Kataiya (Bihar) | Historic role | First Nepal–India grid tie | Direction | Predominantly imports into eastern Nepal | Interconnection ID | | # Significance Origin point of the Nepal–India grid relationship. Every later interconnection inherits contractual and technical conventions established here. Eastern Terai import backbone. Still the principal import route for dry-season supply to Saptari–Siraha–Sunsari, where domestic eastern generation is thin. Legacy accounting reference. Many of the bilateral power trade arrangements (e.g. the Radhika energy exchange protocol) were framed around this single link. # Limitations & Open Questions Legacy voltage. 132 kV thermal capacity is small relative to the region's modern load. During load growth spikes it has required load shedding or emergency procurement. Planned upgrade path unclear. Discussions to convert or parallel with a 220 kV link have not progressed beyond feasibility notes. # See also india-energy-relationship dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — the successor 400 kV era hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Kathmandu Valley Underground Cabling
pages.excerpt: NEA's Kathmandu Valley underground-distribution program as it appeared in the FY 2019/20 annual book: a public-facing modernization project aimed at moving parts of the capital's medium- and low-voltage network underground while expanding associated urban distribution capacity.
pages.body_text: Kathmandu Valley Underground Cabling NEA's Kathmandu Valley underground-distribution program as it appeared in the FY 2019/20 annual book: a public-facing modernization project aimed at moving parts of the capital's medium- and low-voltage network underground while expanding associated urban distribution capacity. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 The annual book shows the program both as a ceremonial milestone on the opening pages and again in the project-management chapter, where cable-laying works in Chabahil are presented as part of the Kathmandu Valley distribution enhancement package. # Why It Matters It is one of the clearest examples of NEA trying to modernize the urban grid, not just add generation or long-distance transmission. It links directly to nea's distribution-reliability mandate and to the broader network-upgrade frame captured in nepal-transmission-landscape-2025. # See also nea nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 data-domestic-demand
