pages.title: Khimti Cascade
pages.excerpt: The Khimti Khola in Dolakha district is one of Nepal's earlier IPP-developed hydropower corridors. The cascade includes multiple projects at different stages, with the original Khimti-I plant serving as one of the country's first large private hydropower investments.
pages.body_text: Khimti Cascade The Khimti Khola in Dolakha district is one of Nepal's earlier IPP-developed hydropower corridors. The cascade includes multiple projects at different stages, with the original Khimti-I plant serving as one of the country's first large private hydropower investments. # Plants in the Cascade Plant | Capacity (MW) | Status | Developer | -------|--------------|--------|-----------| khimti-i | 60 | Operating | Himal Power Limited | khimti-ii | 48.8 | Under construction | — | Upper Khimti | 12.0 | Under construction | — | Upper Khimti II | 7.0 | Under construction | — | Khimti Ghwang Khola | 9.8 | Survey | — | Total | ~137.6 | | | # Significance Khimti-I (commissioned 2000) was one of Nepal's first major private hydropower projects and helped establish the BOOT (Build-Own-Operate-Transfer) model that subsequent IPPs followed. The Khimti corridor became a test case for whether private developers could execute large hydropower projects in Nepal's challenging Himalayan geology. # Transmission The khimti-dhalkebar-corridor (220 kV) was purpose-built to evacuate power from the Khimti Khola plants into the national grid backbone, connecting the Dolakha valley to the Dhalkebar substation on the east-west corridor. # Context The Khimti Khola is a tributary of the Tamakoshi River in the koshi-basin. The broader Khimti-Tamakoshi corridor in Dolakha includes multiple significant projects including upper-tamakoshi (456 MW), tamakoshi-v (99.8 MW), and tamakoshi-3 (287 MW). # See also khimti-i khimti-ii khimti-dhalkebar-corridor koshi-basin upper-tamakoshi

pages.title: Khimti–Dhalkebar 220 kV Hydropower Corridor
pages.excerpt: The original "IPP-to-grid" line in Nepal. Built to evacuate khimti-i — the country's first large IPP (1996 PPA, 2000 commissioning) — toward the central grid at Dhalkebar. Its history mirrors the birth of private hydropower in Nepal and the long legal and political battles that s
pages.body_text: Khimti–Dhalkebar 220 kV Corridor The original "IPP-to-grid" line in Nepal. Built to evacuate khimti-i — the country's first large IPP (1996 PPA, 2000 commissioning) — toward the central grid at Dhalkebar. Its history mirrors the birth of private hydropower in Nepal and the long legal and political battles that surround it. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV (double circuit) | Status | Operational | Length | ~75 km | Owner | nea | Terminals | Khimti substation (Dolakha) · Dhalkebar substation (Dhanusha) | Commissioned | 2016 | Financing | World Bank (IDA) — Nepal India Regional Trade and Transportation Project | Corridor ID | | # Significance First line built for an IPP. Before khimti-i, Nepal had no practice of building high-voltage lines specifically to evacuate independent power producers — the Khimti–Dhalkebar project was the template. Legal precedent. The project's land-acquisition lawsuits (2011–15) set Nepali case law on the public-interest use of private land for transmission. Feeds the export funnel. Terminates at Dhalkebar, which is also the origin of hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone and dhalkebar-muzaffarpur. Roughly half of Nepal's exportable hydropower still routes through it. # Limitations & Open Questions Capacity is effectively full. Contracted flow from Khimti-I + downstream IPPs exceeds 300 MW in peak monsoon; a second 220 kV or 400 kV reinforcement from the Tamakoshi–Khimti cluster has been studied but not funded. Sediment disruption risk. The corridor crosses steep Sindhupalchok and Dolakha terrain; landslide damage to tower foundations was flagged in the 2023 monsoon assessment. # See also khimti-i — the project it was built for khimti-ii — the sister plant that shares the corridor hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — the 400 kV it hands off to

pages.title: Khungri Solar Hybrid (50 MW, Pyuthan)
headings: 1. It is the template for Archetype 1 scale-up.
3. It is geographically near the Dana–Kushma–Butwal corridor.
pages.excerpt: Planned 50 MW solar project at Khungri, Pyuthan district, designed to integrate into the Khungri substation adjacent to Madi Khola and Lungri Khola hydropower. The first at-scale solar-hydro hybrid (Archetype 1) configuration in Nepal, referenced in the NEA Annual Report FY 2024/
pages.body_text: Khungri Solar Hybrid (50 MW, Pyuthan) Planned 50 MW solar project at Khungri, Pyuthan district, designed to integrate into the Khungri substation adjacent to Madi Khola and Lungri Khola hydropower. The first at-scale solar-hydro hybrid (Archetype 1) configuration in Nepal, referenced in the NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25 as part of the Madhesh-Lumbini corridor reinforcement. # Specifications Item | Value | ---|---| Capacity (solar) | 50 MW | Location | Khungri village, Pyuthan District, Lumbini Province | Interconnection | Khungri 33/132 kV substation (shared with Madi Khola and Lungri Khola hydropower) | Zone (solar-resource-geography-nepal) | C4 — Western mid-hills (elevation ~1,300–1,800 m) | Configuration | Ground-mount PV integrated with existing hydro evacuation | Status | Planned / early implementation (FY 2024/25 report) | Expected COD | 2026 (subject to PPA and financial close) | Owner | NEA + IPP (structure to be finalised) | # Why this project matters Khungri is the first operational implementation of the hybrid siting logic encoded in hybrid-siting-logic, at a meaningful scale: ## 1. It is the template for Archetype 1 scale-up. Bidur/Trishuli (bidur-trishuli-solar-25mwp) was near-hydro co-location by accident; Khungri is designed as hybrid from the start. If the project delivers on schedule, it validates: Shared substation loading between solar mid-day and hydro evening-peak. Anti-phased transformer utilisation (solar loads midday, hydro loads evening-peak). Reduced grid-reinforcement capex compared to greenfield 50 MW. ## 2. Mid-hill siting with evacuation advantage. Pyuthan is Zone C4 (western mid-hills, GHI ~4.4–4.8). This is not top-tier Nepal solar resource — Terai would produce ~10–15% more MWh per MWp. But the shared substation with two hydropower plants means the project's all-in LCOE (including grid costs) is likely lower than a Terai greenfield. The economics here are being driven by grid cost, not GHI, exactly as hybrid-siting-logic argues. ## 3. It is geographically near the Dana–Kushma–Butwal corridor. The site sits on the dana-kushma-butwal-corridor interconnection axis, so the plant's output is naturally evacuated to the NEA backbone via the Butwal hub. This gives Khungri an evacuation headroom that purely rural Zone C mid-hill solar sites do not have. # Operational questions to watch Question | Why it matters | ---|---| Does the shared substation handle simultaneous hydro + solar peak days? | Validates (or breaks) the hybrid thesis at 50 MW scale | What is the actual monsoon-day solar output relative to hydro output? | Tests whether Pyuthan zone solar is a net winter complement or a monsoon cannibaliser | How is the PPA structured: joint for hydro + solar, or separate? | Precedent for future hybrid deals | What is the specific GHI at Khungri vs the Pokhara reference station? | Tests the Zone C4 GHI assumption used in the resource map | # Lessons the project can teach If Khungri delivers successfully, it provides the precedent for rolling hybrid into the next NEA tender — mandating that any new hydropower project above ~20 MW include a co-located solar feasibility study, with capex sharing for the substation upgrade. This is the lowest-friction way to scale Archetype 1 from 50 MW (Khungri) to ~500–800 MW across the hydro fleet by 2030. # Related hybrid-siting-logic — the concept this project operationalises nea-solar — programme parent dana-kushma-butwal-corridor — transmission axis bidur-trishuli-solar-25mwp — precedent, if unintentional naumure-w-rapti — nearby hydro developments that could inform future hybrid design data-solar-fleet-inventory

pages.title: Kimathanka Arun HEP
pages.excerpt: Kimathanka Arun HEP is a 450 MW survey-stage project on the Arun in Sankhuwasabha. It is pinned in data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is the eighth-largest marker in the display set and part of the same Arun corridor that makes arun-3 nationally important.
pages.body_text: Kimathanka Arun HEP Kimathanka Arun HEP is a 450 MW survey-stage project on the Arun in Sankhuwasabha. It is pinned in data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is the eighth-largest marker in the display set and part of the same Arun corridor that makes arun-3 nationally important. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 450 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Arun (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Sankhuwasabha | Province | Province No 1 | Promoter | Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited | # Why It Matters Kimathanka Arun matters because it extends the Arun corridor story upstream. arun-3 is the near-term, India-developed 900 MW anchor; Kimathanka Arun is a later-horizon state-promoted project that would add another large block in the same eastern basin. That makes it important for transmission and sequencing. A 450 MW project in upper Arun geography is not just a generation question; it depends on road access, evacuation planning, environmental constraints, and how the broader Arun cascade is prioritized. # Status And Timing The current layer treats Kimathanka Arun as survey-stage. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Kimathanka Arun in the 2036-2040 project window. There is no confirmed construction start in the current wiki evidence set. # Map Interpretation The marker is a river-aligned project reference derived from a registry point. Use it for corridor and top-10 comparison, not final site analysis. # Registry note Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset # See also koshi-basin buildability arun-3 data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects

pages.title: Kohalpur-Surkhet-Dailekh 132 kV Grid Reach
pages.excerpt: The under-construction 132 kV line extending the western grid north from Kohalpur toward Surkhet and Dailekh.
pages.body_text: Kohalpur-Surkhet-Dailekh 132 kV Grid Reach The under-construction 132 kV line extending the western grid north from Kohalpur toward Surkhet and Dailekh. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132 kV | Status | Under construction | Length basis | 84.0 route-km / 168.0 circuit-km | Source geometry | Manual route through RPGCL-labeled Kohalpur, Surkhet, and Dailekh anchors | Status/length basis | NEA FY 2024/25 project narrative and under-construction line table | Corridor ID | | # Map Interpretation This line is the key Karnali grid-reach story. It explains why the map should not treat the western hills as simply disconnected, while still showing the corridor as under construction rather than operational. Current validation result: 1 feature, 0 inferred connectors, 0 remaining endpoint gaps, and 76.144 km traced length against an 84.0 route-km basis. Public decision: default-visible, caveated. # Caveats The public geometry is a route-level reconstruction through named anchors, not a tower alignment. NEA reports 52 km from Kohalpur to Surkhet and 32 km from Surkhet to Dailekh, with construction progress uneven across the two sections. # See also western-132-backbone karnali-basin data-layer-transmission-connected-traced-network

pages.title: Kokhajor-1
pages.excerpt: Kokhajor-1 is a 111.5 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Koshi basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it has a high dry-energy share for a relatively small project.
pages.body_text: Kokhajor-1 Kokhajor-1 is a 111.5 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Koshi basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it has a high dry-energy share for a relatively small project. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 111.5 MW | Category | jica promising storage | Basin | Koshi (koshi-basin) | Annual energy | 278.9 GWh | Dry-season energy | 94.1 GWh | Dry share | 33.7% | Total storage | 218.7 MCM | Effective storage | 166.1 MCM | # Why It Matters Kokhajor-1 contributes 94.1 GWh of dry-season energy from 278.9 GWh annual energy, a 33.7% dry share. That is the point of the project in the wiki: it is not a top-capacity candidate, but it helps show that smaller storage sites can have strong seasonal value. Its analytical role is portfolio logic. Nepal's storage deficit will not be solved only by one large dam; distributed storage candidates can matter if they provide dependable dry-season energy near usable grid corridors. # Status And Timing Kokhajor-1 is a planning candidate from the JICA/NEA storage material. The current wiki evidence set does not carry a confirmed construction start, developer award, or COD target. The next useful evidence would be an updated feasibility package, project sponsor, environmental review, grid-interconnection plan, and whether it remains in the preferred storage portfolio after newer screening. # Map Interpretation The marker uses a Kokhajor Khola river anchor from Nominatim. It is suitable for orientation, not final engineering geography. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 # See also koshi-basin storage-deficit firm-power data-layer-storage-shortlist

pages.title: Koshi Basin
pages.excerpt: The Koshi (Sapta Koshi) basin is Nepal's eastern major river system. It drains ~27,818 km² within Nepal through seven major tributaries, including the Arun, Tamor, and Sunkoshi. The Arun is one of Nepal's oldest rivers — older than the Himalayas themselves, predating the mountain
pages.body_text: Koshi Basin # Overview The Koshi (Sapta Koshi) basin is Nepal's eastern major river system. It drains ~27,818 km² within Nepal through seven major tributaries, including the Arun, Tamor, and Sunkoshi. The Arun is one of Nepal's oldest rivers — older than the Himalayas themselves, predating the mountain uplift. The basin is the largest glacierized in Nepal (278 glaciers, 51 km³ ice volume) and carries the largest gross hydropower potential of any single basin. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Area in Nepal | 27,818 km² | Average discharge (border) | 1,827 m³/s (~57.6 BCM/yr) | Monsoon share | 67–78% | Peak-to-dry ratio | 25–50:1 | Gross hydropower potential | 27,805 MW (38.3% of national total) | Glaciers | 278 (51 km³ ice) | Sediment significance | ~120 Mt/yr, nearly 25% of Ganges sediment load | # Hydropower Significance Largest gross potential of any basin in Nepal per wecs-hydropower-potential-2019. Contains the dudhkoshi-storage project (670 MW) and upper-tamakoshi (456 MW, largest operating plant). The Arun corridor hosts Upper Arun (900 MW, under construction). # Constraints Extreme sediment loads — ICIMOD framing puts the basin near 120 million tonnes/year into the Ganges system, or nearly 25% of the total sediment load. See sediment-as-design-constraint. Flood hazard — known as the "River of Sorrow" in Bihar due to catastrophic flooding and course shifts. The 2008 Koshi breach displaced 2+ million people. glof-risk — the 2024 Thame Valley GLOF traveled >80 km down the Dudh Koshi, destroying hydropower facilities. High hazard does not eliminate the basin's value, but it demands buildability filters on every project. # Downstream Significance Koshi irrigates ~500,000–600,000 ha in Bihar. Supports >40 million people downstream. Contributes disproportionate sediment to the Ganges system. Central to hydro-geopolitics and ganges-contribution. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Kulekhani Cascade
pages.excerpt: Repair works on draft tube during overhauling
pages.body_text: Kulekhani Cascade Repair works on draft tube during overhauling 6.6 kV Bus conductor maintenance Machine floor of KL1 Energy generation pattern of PROR/ROR and storage (GWh) Nepal's only operational reservoir hydropower system. A cascade of three plants south of Kathmandu. # Specifications Plant | Capacity (MW) | Type | Page | -------|--------------|------|------| Kulekhani I | 60 | Reservoir | kulekhani-i | Kulekhani II | 32 | Cascade | kulekhani-ii | Kulekhani III | 14 | Cascade | | Total | 106 | | | Combined generation in FY 2024/25: 279.8 GWh Storage volume: ~85–150 MCM (see data-storage-comparison) # Significance Kulekhani is the outlier that proves the rule. It is the only facility in Nepal that can shift water from wet to dry season for power generation. Everything else is run-of-river-hydropower. The fact that 106 MW of storage represents Nepal's entire reservoir base — against 3,400+ MW of total hydro — encapsulates the storage-deficit. # Related kulekhani-i — the upper reservoir plant (60 MW) kulekhani-ii — the second cascade plant (32 MW) data-storage-comparison — Nepal's storage in international context storage-deficit — why this matters firm-power — what Kulekhani can provide that RoR cannot # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory

pages.title: Kulekhani I
pages.excerpt: 60 MW · operating · on the Kulekhani · in Makawanpur district.
pages.body_text: Kulekhani I 60 MW · operating · on the Kulekhani · in Makawanpur district. [!note] Cascade context Kulekhani I is the upper reservoir plant in Nepal's only operational reservoir hydropower system. See kulekhani-cascade for the full three-plant system overview including kulekhani-ii (32 MW) and Kulekhani III (14 MW). Combined generation in FY 2024/25: 279.8 GWh across all three plants. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 60 MW | Status | Operating | Type | Reservoir | River | Kulekhani | District | Makawanpur | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | Promoter | Nepal Electricity Authority | # Significance Kulekhani I is the anchor of Nepal's storage-deficit story: the largest plant in the country's only operating reservoir cascade. As a true reservoir facility, it provides the firm, dispatchable generation that Nepal's RoR-heavy fleet lacks. See kulekhani-cascade for the full system context and firm-power for why reservoir capacity matters. # Notes [!note] This is a registry-backed project record. Capacity, location, and licence status come from the Ministry of Energy registry mirrored in the map data. Narrative context and images are added where public sources are strong enough; the specification table is maintained from the registry. # See also kulekhani-cascade — full cascade overview kulekhani-ii — downstream cascade plant storage-deficit firm-power

pages.title: Likhu Cascade
pages.excerpt: The Likhu Khola is a hydropower-rich tributary in the koshi-basin, straddling Solukhumbu and Ramechhap districts. The Likhu system has attracted significant IPP investment with multiple generation-licence projects across the upper and lower reaches.
pages.body_text: Likhu Cascade The Likhu Khola is a hydropower-rich tributary in the koshi-basin, straddling Solukhumbu and Ramechhap districts. The Likhu system has attracted significant IPP investment with multiple generation-licence projects across the upper and lower reaches. # Plants in the Cascade Plant | Capacity (MW) | Status | District | -------|--------------|--------|----------| likhu-1 | 77.0 | Under construction | Solukhumbu | likhu-2 | 55.0 | Under construction | Solukhumbu | likhu-khola-a | 51.0 | Under construction | Solukhumbu | likhu-4 | 52.4 | Under construction | Ramechhap | lower-likhu | 28.1 | Under construction | Ramechhap | Nupche Likhu HEP | 57.5 | Survey | Ramechhap | Total | ~321 | | | [!note] Likhu-3 does not appear in the current MoE/DoED registry. The numbering (1, 2, 4) reflects the registry's actual naming — Likhu-3 may have been a planned designation that was never licensed, or the gap reflects the staggered licensing history of the corridor. # Corridor Context The upper Likhu projects (1, 2, Likhu Khola A) cluster in the Solukhumbu highlands, while Likhu-4, Lower Likhu, and Nupche Likhu sit further downstream in Ramechhap. The Likhu is a tributary of the Sun Koshi, and its projects share transmission corridors with the broader Khimti-Tamakoshi system via khimti-dhalkebar-corridor. # See also likhu-1 likhu-2 likhu-4 likhu-khola-a lower-likhu koshi-basin

pages.title: Lower Badigad
pages.excerpt: Lower Badigad is a 380.3 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Gandaki basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it pairs large carryover storage with meaningful dry-season output, making it more relevant to system value than many larger wet-season-heavy pr
pages.body_text: Lower Badigad Lower Badigad is a 380.3 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Gandaki basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it pairs large carryover storage with meaningful dry-season output, making it more relevant to system value than many larger wet-season-heavy projects. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 380.3 MW | Category | jica promising storage | Basin | Gandaki (gandaki-basin) | Annual energy | 1366.0 GWh | Dry-season energy | 354.7 GWh | Dry share | 26.0% | Total storage | 995.9 MCM | Effective storage | 505.5 MCM | # Why It Matters Lower Badigad is one of the stronger Gandaki storage candidates in the shortlist: 995.9 MCM total storage, 505.5 MCM effective storage, and 354.7 GWh of dry-season energy. Its dry share is lower than Nalsyau Gad or Dudhkoshi, but the reservoir scale is large enough that it remains important for seasonal regulation. Its strategic role is also geographic. The Gandaki basin is already a central hydropower corridor, and storage in that basin has a different grid value than remote far-western storage. Lower Badigad belongs in the wiki because it is a credible comparator to tanahu-hydropower, kali-gandaki-a, and other Gandaki assets. # Status And Timing Lower Badigad is a planning candidate from the JICA/NEA storage material, not an under-construction project. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Lower Badigad STO in the 2031-2035 window. Treat that as a planning horizon rather than a confirmed construction-start date. The next evidence to watch is whether the project advances from storage-master-plan candidate to a named developer, updated feasibility package, environmental assessment, financing plan, and grid-connection commitment. # Map Interpretation The map marker is a river-aligned project reference. It is appropriate for comparing storage candidates and basin leverage, but not for property-level impact analysis. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 # See also gandaki-basin storage-deficit firm-power tanahu-hydropower data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-storage-shortlist

pages.title: Lower Jhimruk
pages.excerpt: Lower Jhimruk is a 142.5 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist as a medium-basin storage option, with the important caveat that its layout conflicts with Naumure in the current design set.
pages.body_text: Lower Jhimruk Lower Jhimruk is a 142.5 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist as a medium-basin storage option, with the important caveat that its layout conflicts with Naumure in the current design set. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 142.5 MW | Category | jica promising storage | Basin | West Rapti (karnali-basin) | Annual energy | 454.7 GWh | Dry-season energy | 94.4 GWh | Dry share | 20.8% | Total storage | 386.0 MCM | Effective storage | 211.6 MCM | # Why It Matters Lower Jhimruk contributes 94.4 GWh of dry-season energy, with 386 MCM total storage and 211.6 MCM effective storage. It is not a top-capacity project, but it belongs in the storage shortlist because it helps illustrate the range of medium-basin options. The caveat is central: the shortlist notes that Lower Jhimruk's layout conflicts with naumure-w-rapti in the current design. That means the project should not be counted naively alongside Naumure as if both could necessarily be built in the same configuration. # Status And Timing Lower Jhimruk is a planning candidate from the JICA/NEA storage material. The current wiki evidence set does not confirm construction start, financing, or a COD target. The next question is scheme selection: if Naumure advances, Lower Jhimruk may become less relevant or need redesign. If Naumure stalls, Lower Jhimruk remains a smaller West Rapti storage comparator. # Map Interpretation The marker uses an operating-project registry point near a mapped river reach as a reference. It is a planning-layer marker, not a final site boundary. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 # See also karnali-basin storage-deficit firm-power naumure-w-rapti data-layer-storage-shortlist

pages.title: Lower Solu Hydropower Project
pages.excerpt: Lower Solu is an 82 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction in the Solukhumbu district — part of the Dudh Koshi river system that includes the benchmark Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) project.
pages.body_text: Lower Solu Hydropower Project Lower Solu is an 82 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction in the Solukhumbu district — part of the Dudh Koshi river system that includes the benchmark Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW) project. # Solu Corridor The Solu Khola / Dudh Koshi river system in Solukhumbu has emerged as a significant eastern Nepal hydropower corridor. Sahas Urja's 86 MW Solu Khola project (commissioned March 2023) demonstrated that with disciplined capital deployment — achieving NPR 172–175 million/MW vs. industry averages exceeding NPR 200–250 million/MW — projects in this corridor can generate strong financial returns even under the standard NPR 4.80/8.40 PPA framework. Lower Solu, at a comparable 82 MW, enters a corridor with an established benchmark and transmission infrastructure shaped by its predecessor. # The Sahas Urja Benchmark The success of Sahas Urja creates both opportunity and pressure for Lower Solu: Opportunity: Transmission infrastructure exists, river hydrology data is available, and the financing community has a positive reference point in the same valley Pressure: Sahas Urja's NPR 172–175 million/MW build cost, 69% PLF, and NPR 900M+ annual profit set a high bar that investors will compare against # Build Cost Discipline The single most important variable determining Lower Solu's financial outcome is construction cost per MW. In the fixed-tariff regime where every MW-hour sells at the same regulated rate regardless of how much it cost to build, capital efficiency is the only lever for profitability. Sahas Urja proved that ~NPR 173M/MW is achievable in this corridor; projects that drift toward NPR 250M/MW or beyond will struggle to achieve positive equity returns. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 82 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Solu Khola | District | Solukhumbu | Province | Province No 1 | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability sahas-urja-benchmark-icra-2026

pages.title: Madhya Bhotekoshi
pages.excerpt: Madhya Bhotekoshi (Middle Bhotekoshi) is a 102 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Sindhupalchok district. It is one of the key capacity additions in the Bhote Koshi corridor, an area that has seen repeated geological setbacks includi
pages.body_text: Madhya Bhotekoshi Madhya Bhotekoshi (Middle Bhotekoshi) is a 102 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Sindhupalchok district. It is one of the key capacity additions in the Bhote Koshi corridor, an area that has seen repeated geological setbacks including landslides and glacial lake outburst floods. # Chilime Group Strategy Madhya Bhotekoshi is part of the Chilime Hydropower Company Limited (CHCL) expansion strategy. After Chilime's own 22.1 MW plant established a highly profitable operational track record, CHCL used its internal accruals and brand equity to spin off downstream subsidiaries — Rasuwagadhi (111 MW), Sanjen (42.5 MW), and Madhya Bhotekoshi (102 MW) — effectively bypassing the terminal BOOT-model cliff where assets must be handed over to the state. This holding company model is the mechanism through which Nepal's most successful hydro entity perpetuates itself. # Bhote Koshi Corridor Risk The Bhote Koshi valley is one of Nepal's most geologically active river corridors. Projects in this stretch — including the original Bhote Koshi project, Upper Bhotekoshi (45 MW, operating), Madhya Bhotekoshi, and Rasuwagadhi — face elevated construction risk from landslides, flash floods, and seismic activity. Madhya Bhotekoshi's construction timeline and cost profile must be understood within this risk context. # Significance At 102 MW, Madhya Bhotekoshi represents a significant block of capacity in the under-construction pipeline. Along with Rasuwagadhi downstream and Sanjen in the adjacent valley, it forms a ~256 MW cluster of Chilime-promoted projects that will shape water allocation and transmission planning in the Trishuli-Bhote Koshi system. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 102 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Bhote koshi (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Sindhupalchok | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability chilime sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026

pages.title: Madhya Marsyangdi
pages.excerpt: Madhya Marsyangdi (Middle Marsyangdi) is a 70 MW operating hydropower project on the Marsyangdi River — one of Nepal's established generation assets with a multi-decade operational track record.
pages.body_text: Madhya Marsyangdi Madhya Marsyangdi (Middle Marsyangdi) is a 70 MW operating hydropower project on the Marsyangdi River — one of Nepal's established generation assets with a multi-decade operational track record. # Marsyangdi Cascade Madhya Marsyangdi sits in the middle of one of Nepal's most productive hydropower corridors. The Marsyangdi River, a tributary of the Trishuli/Narayani system in the Gandaki basin, hosts a cascade of projects: Upper Marsyangdi A (50 MW), Madhya Marsyangdi (70 MW), and Marsyangdi Besi (50 MW, under construction). This ~170 MW cumulative capacity makes the Marsyangdi cascade a significant block of the national generation portfolio. # Operating History As an operating plant, Madhya Marsyangdi provides a different lens from the pre-COD and under-construction projects that dominate the pipeline: actual generation data, real maintenance costs, and historical PLF performance under the standard PPA framework. Operating projects reveal whether the financial models in IPO prospectuses materialize in practice — and at what cost. # Transmission The Marsyangdi valley's generation feeds into the national grid via the Marsyangdi corridor infrastructure, connecting to the east-west backbone. The Upper Marsyangdi 220 kV line provides the key evacuation route for this cascade. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 70 MW | Status | Operating | River | Marsyangdi (gandaki-basin) | District | Lamjung | Province | Gandaki Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower

pages.title: Madi
pages.excerpt: Madi is a 199.8 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because its dry-energy ratio is useful even though it is not a headline-MW project.
pages.body_text: Madi Madi is a 199.8 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because its dry-energy ratio is useful even though it is not a headline-MW project. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 199.8 MW | Category | jica promising storage | Basin | West Rapti (karnali-basin) | Annual energy | 621.1 GWh | Dry-season energy | 170.7 GWh | Dry share | 27.5% | Total storage | 359.5 MCM | Effective storage | 235.1 MCM | # Why It Matters Madi contributes 170.7 GWh of dry-season energy in the storage shortlist, with a 27.5% dry share. That makes it a moderate-size but system-relevant option in a basin where storage can support both seasonal power and broader water-management questions. The page matters because the storage shortlist is not just a list of mega-projects. Madi shows the middle tier of storage: not nationally transformative on its own, but potentially useful in a portfolio that needs multiple dry-season contributors. # Status And Timing Madi is a planning candidate from the JICA/NEA storage material. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Madi Storage HEP in the 2026-2030 window, but the current wiki evidence set does not confirm physical construction. Next signals to watch are project sponsorship, feasibility refresh, environmental and social work, and whether it competes with or complements other West Rapti storage options such as naumure-w-rapti. # Map Interpretation The marker is a river anchor suitable for map storytelling and basin comparison. It should not be used as a site-grade coordinate. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 # See also karnali-basin storage-deficit firm-power naumure-w-rapti data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-storage-shortlist

pages.title: Mahakali Basin
pages.excerpt: The Mahakali river forms Nepal's far-western border with India. It is a transboundary basin with far smaller current development than the three major eastern systems. The basin's significance is primarily geopolitical — it is the site of the pancheshwar multipurpose project and t
pages.body_text: Mahakali Basin # Overview The Mahakali river forms Nepal's far-western border with India. It is a transboundary basin with far smaller current development than the three major eastern systems. The basin's significance is primarily geopolitical — it is the site of the pancheshwar multipurpose project and the subject of the 1996 Mahakali Treaty. # Key Facts Fact | Value | ------|-------| Average discharge | ~600 m³/s | Monsoon share | ~73% | Lakes in basin (Nepal side) | 16 | # Hydropower Significance The Mahakali Treaty (1996) entitles Nepal to 50% of pancheshwar's output (6,480 MW total, or ~3,240 MW for Nepal). After 28+ years, Nepal has received zero megawatts from this treaty. See hydro-geopolitics. The Mahakali already supports irrigation via the Mahakali irrigation system on the Indian side. # Strategic Notes The basin is less about sheer resource volume and more about the diplomatic relationship with India. The failure of Pancheshwar to materialize is one of the strongest examples of how hydro-geopolitics shapes — and stalls — Nepal's energy future. # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Main Load Dispatch Centre
pages.excerpt: NEA's main system-operations node in Kathmandu, paired in the annual book with the Emergency Control Center at Hetauda as the backup facility for national grid dispatch and contingency management.
pages.body_text: Main Load Dispatch Centre NEA's main system-operations node in Kathmandu, paired in the annual book with the Emergency Control Center at Hetauda as the backup facility for national grid dispatch and contingency management. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 72 describes the main LDC in Kathmandu as the primary operating center and the Hetauda ECC building as the backup center for transmission system control during emergencies. # Why It Matters The dispatch center is the operational layer behind every corridor page: hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone, dhalkebar-muzaffarpur, and the rest only matter if NEA can actually control them as one system. It is one of the clearest institutional links between nea as owner and the real-time grid described in nepal-transmission-landscape-2025. # See also nea hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Marsyangdi Corridor Manang-Khudi-Udipur 220 kV
pages.excerpt: The Marsyangdi upper 220 kV corridor is the upstream evacuation path from the Manang/upper Marsyangdi area south through Khudi and Besishahar toward Udipur. It matters because western and central hydropower build-out depends on moving generation out of the Marsyangdi basin withou
pages.body_text: Marsyangdi Corridor Manang-Khudi-Udipur 220 kV The Marsyangdi upper 220 kV corridor is the upstream evacuation path from the Manang/upper Marsyangdi area south through Khudi and Besishahar toward Udipur. It matters because western and central hydropower build-out depends on moving generation out of the Marsyangdi basin without overloading older lower-voltage paths. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV | Status | Under construction | Corridor ID | | Public map feature | | Source basis | Upper Marsyangdi RAP pages 33-36 | Official route length | 46.0 km | Traced network length | 40.385 km | Validation delta | -12.21% | # Map Interpretation The map trace is document-grounded: it follows the recovered upper Marsyangdi RAP location, project-area, and access-map context, anchored to Dharapani, Khudi, Besishahar, and Udipur. This is not an official GIS alignment. It is better than a conceptual substation spine, but it should still be read as a basin-corridor trace rather than a precise tower alignment. # Confidence Public decision: default-visible, caveated. The feature has no endpoint gaps in the connected network. The caveat is precision: the current trace is manual/document-grounded and about 12% short against the working 46 km route basis. # Caveats The next improvement is source triangulation rather than topology repair. The branch endpoints and route length should be checked against the upper Marsyangdi RAP and the EIB/NEA companion material before promoting this to a higher-confidence route. # See also udipur-damauli-bharatpur-220 marsyangdi mca-central-400 data-layer-transmission-connected-traced-network

pages.title: MCA Central 400 kV Corridor (Lapsiphedi–Ratmate–Hetauda–Damauli–Butwal)
pages.excerpt: The second 400 kV spine Nepal is building — the Millennium Challenge Account (MCA-Nepal) compact's headline infrastructure, running from Lapsiphedi (Kathmandu Valley ring) through Ratmate, Hetauda, Damauli and terminating at New Butwal, where it feeds the gorakhpur-butwal-interco
pages.body_text: MCA Central 400 kV Corridor The second 400 kV spine Nepal is building — the Millennium Challenge Account (MCA-Nepal) compact's headline infrastructure, running from Lapsiphedi (Kathmandu Valley ring) through Ratmate, Hetauda, Damauli and terminating at New Butwal, where it feeds the gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection. When complete, this corridor ends the single-point-of-failure problem on the eastern backbone. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Status | Under construction (partial energisation 2025) | Route-length basis | 308.65 route-km | Circuit-length basis | 617.3 circuit-km | Owner | nea (construction by MCA-Nepal contractors) | Terminal substations | Lapsiphedi · Ratmate · Hetauda · Damauli · New Butwal | Financing | US MCC grant (~US$500M corridor share) + Government of Nepal | Target full commissioning | 2026–2027 | Corridor ID | | # Map Interpretation The connected transmission layer now uses a source-aware MCA atlas trace rather than the older RPGCL overview fragments. It is modeled as five project segments: Lapsiphedi-Ratmate, Ratmate-New Hetauda, Ratmate-New Damauli, New Damauli-New Butwal, and New Butwal-India border. Public decision: default-visible, high confidence. Validation shows 5 atlas-derived features, no inferred connectors, no endpoint gaps, and a -1.32% delta against the 308.65 route-km compact basis. # Significance Second 400 kV spine. Parallels hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone via an entirely different geographic axis (Kathmandu → Western Terai), providing real N-1 redundancy for the first time. Gateway to the second cross-border link. Terminates at New Butwal, the Nepal end of gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection. Evacuates the western IPP cluster. Picks up output from the Marsyangdi and Kali Gandaki basins that has historically been trapped behind legacy 132 kV — partially solving claim-transmission-immediate-blocker for the west. Symbolic role. First large US-funded infrastructure grant in Nepal; its progress is read as a proxy for Nepal's willingness to diversify foreign partners beyond India and China. # Limitations & Open Questions Right-of-way disputes. Sections through Nuwakot and Kavre saw protracted community opposition over tower siting and compensation. Partial commissioning ≠ full rating. Early 2025 energisation covers the Ratmate–Hetauda link only; the full end-to-end 400 kV transfer capability awaits terminal substation completion at Damauli and New Butwal. Dependent on cross-border terminal. Full value is unlocked only when gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection also commissions. Atlas trace, not tower survey. The public geometry is route-grade for map and topology use, with sheet-level provenance, but it should not be read as a cadastral tower alignment. # See also hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — the existing backbone it parallels gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection — the border link it feeds dana-kushma-butwal-corridor — the 220 kV it relieves claim-transmission-immediate-blocker

pages.title: Mithila Solar PV Electric Project (Eco Power, 10 MWp)
headings: Mithila Solar PV Electric Project (Eco Power, 10 MWp)
pages.excerpt: Early-round IPP utility solar plant developed by Eco Power Development Company Pvt. Ltd., commissioned ~2021 in Dhanusha district, Madhesh Province. The largest single Phase I IPP plant by MWp.
pages.body_text: Mithila Solar PV Electric Project (Eco Power, 10 MWp) Early-round IPP utility solar plant developed by Eco Power Development Company Pvt. Ltd., commissioned ~2021 in Dhanusha district, Madhesh Province. The largest single Phase I IPP plant by MWp. # Specifications Item | Value | ---|---| Capacity | 10.0 MWp | Location | Dhanusha District, Madhesh Province | Owner / Developer | Eco Power Development Company Pvt. Ltd. | Zone (solar-resource-geography-nepal) | B2 — Eastern Terai / Madhesh | Interconnection | Dhanusha 33 kV | Commissioning | ~2021 | PPA tariff | Phase I round (~NPR 8/kWh blended) | Site elevation | ~80 m | Expected annual generation | ~16 GWh/yr | Status | Operating | # Why it matters Mithila Solar's siting is the clearest Phase I validation of the grid-adjacent Terai siting archetype later encoded in the nea-960mw-solar-tender: Mithila / Janakpur is the heart of Nepal's densest 132 kV grid cluster (hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone). Dhanusha has Zone B2 GHI of ~4.9–5.1 kWh/m²/day — among the best Terai solar resource. Flat agricultural terrain with <2° slope. Proximity (within ~70 km) to the Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV cross-border interconnection (dhalkebar-muzaffarpur). Mithila Solar is the pattern match for dozens of 960 MW tender awards in Dhanusha, Mahottari, Sarlahi, and Bara; its operational track record is the evidence base IPPs used when bidding the Phase IV tender at near-LCOE prices. # Related nea-solar butwal-solar-ridi-8-5mwp, bishnu-priya-nawalparasi — Phase I cohort hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — grid backbone dhalkebar-muzaffarpur — adjacent cross-border data-solar-fleet-inventory

It also shows how even relatively short 132 kV lines were carrying both evacuation and reliability roles at the same time. # See also lower-modi-khola upper-modi-a hetauda-bharatpur-bardaghat-corridor nepal-transmission-landscape-2025
