pages.title: Mugu Karnali Storage HEP
pages.excerpt: At a proposed 1,902 MW, Mugu Karnali would be the single largest generation asset Nepal has ever planned — by itself larger than the country's entire current operating hydropower fleet. Still at pre-feasibility stage under state-owned Vidhyut Utpadan Company, open to public–priva
pages.body_text: Mugu Karnali Storage HEP At a proposed 1,902 MW, Mugu Karnali would be the single largest generation asset Nepal has ever planned — by itself larger than the country's entire current operating hydropower fleet. Still at pre-feasibility stage under state-owned Vidhyut Utpadan Company, open to public–private partnership bidding, and attracting expressions of interest from India's Adani Group and Bangladesh's Chowdhury Group. Its schedule (construction from ~2030, commissioning ~2034) and transmission dependencies place it on a longer horizon than any of the other top-10 anchor projects. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 1,902 MW | Type | Storage / pumped-storage reservoir | Turbines | 10 × Francis units | Gross head | ~285 m | Status | Pre-feasibility; PPP bidding underway | River | Karnali (karnali-basin) | District | Mugu (registry primary); Bajura referenced in early materials | Province | Karnali Pradesh | Developer | Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited (VUCL / VUC) | Design annual energy | ~5,883 GWh | PPP structure | VUC seeking 51% partnership stake | Schedule | Construction ~2030, COD target ~2034 | # Significance Mugu Karnali changes the arithmetic of Nepal's planning rather than the arithmetic of the current grid. At 1,902 MW and ~5,883 GWh of design annual energy, it is the one pipeline project whose success or failure genuinely rewrites the national storage-deficit and firm-power picture — more than dudhkoshi-storage, budhigandaki or any combination of smaller reservoirs in the current pipeline. It is also the project that most clearly surfaces Nepal's post-India export opening. Adani's EOI reflects the India-developer pattern that runs through arun-3, upper-karnali and west-seti. Chowdhury Group's formal bid represents Bangladeshi developer interest — the first time a Bangladeshi private group has bid for a project of this scale in Nepal. Whichever way the 51% PPP lands, Mugu Karnali is where the question "who builds Nepal's biggest reservoirs?" stops being rhetorical. Because it is storage / pumped-storage, Mugu Karnali is also the project that would finally give Nepal the grid-scale balancing asset the run-of-river fleet needs. RoR over-generation in the monsoon and RoR under-generation in the dry season are the two faces of seasonal-mismatch; a 1,900 MW asset that can time-shift energy across days and partly across seasons is the structural answer the country has not yet built. # Limitations & Controversies Horizon risk. Construction from ~2030 and COD ~2034 places Mugu Karnali outside the 5-year energy planning window most of NEA's current decisions use. Every dry season between now and 2034 has to be managed without it — by imports, by tanahu-hydropower and kulekhani-cascade augmentation, by demand response, or by accepting unserved energy. Transmission gap. A 1,902 MW generator at the head of the Karnali implies a new 400 kV (or higher) evacuation corridor through some of Nepal's most rugged terrain. That corridor is not in the current NEA transmission masterplan at scale. Without it, Mugu Karnali is stranded-generation by construction. Feasibility depth. The project is at survey / pre-feasibility stage with a two-year DoED deadline (extensible to five). None of the capacity, head or energy figures above has yet been locked down by a bankable feasibility study. Expect all three to move. Geopolitical visibility. Any bidder decision that confers control of 1,900 MW of upstream Karnali generation — and the reservoir operation rules that accompany it — will be read in Delhi and Dhaka. The downstream implications for Indian flood management and irrigation are material and not yet publicly modelled. # See also karnali-basin storage-deficit upper-karnali west-seti arun-3 hydro-geopolitics seasonal-mismatch stranded-generation

pages.title: Mustang / Trans-Himalayan High-Altitude Solar Zone
headings: Why the zone is strategically interesting
1. It is the only Nepal zone where *winter* is the best solar month.
2. Off-grid solar + BESS at these sites has extraordinary kWh-value.
3. It is the long-dated solar export corridor.
pages.excerpt: Not a project, an entity classification of the geographic zone with Nepal's best solar resource. Covers the trans-Himalayan rain-shadow valleys of Mustang, Dolpa, upper Manang, upper Humla, Mugu (upper), and parts of Jumla — a band of high-elevation (2,500–4,500 m) valleys shield
pages.body_text: Mustang / Trans-Himalayan High-Altitude Solar Zone Not a project, an entity classification of the geographic zone with Nepal's best solar resource. Covers the trans-Himalayan rain-shadow valleys of Mustang, Dolpa, upper Manang, upper Humla, Mugu (upper), and parts of Jumla — a band of high-elevation (2,500–4,500 m) valleys shielded from the monsoon by the Greater Himalaya. This zone has world-class GHI and DNI (5.8–6.5 kWh/m²/day, 6.0–6.8 kWh/m²/day respectively) with ~260–290 clear-sky days per year. It is also, for the foreseeable future, the hardest solar zone in Nepal to monetise because of grid distance, low local demand, and terrain-induced transmission costs. # Resource characteristics Parameter | Value | ---|---| Mean GHI | 5.8–6.5 kWh/m²/day | Mean DNI | 6.0–6.8 kWh/m²/day | Clear-sky days / year | 260–290 (among highest in Nepal) | Monsoon GHI penalty | 5–15% (rain-shadow intact) | Winter GHI (Dec–Feb) | 5.2–5.8 kWh/m²/day — highest in Nepal | Elevation | 2,500–4,500 m | Ambient temperature | Cool; module efficiency ~3–5% better than Terai | Zone (solar-resource-geography-nepal) | A | Comparative reference | Ladakh, Atacama, Tibetan Plateau | # Representative districts and sites District | Representative site | Elevation | GHI | Notes | ---|---|---:|---:|---| Mustang | Jomsom | 2,730 | ~6.0 | ESMAP pyranometer station | Mustang | Upper Mustang (Lo Manthang) | 3,850 | ~6.3 | Trekking-dependent demand | Dolpa | Dunai, Dho Tarap | 2,090–4,050 | 5.9–6.4 | Remote, low demand | Upper Manang | Manang village | 3,540 | ~6.1 | Seasonal demand | Upper Humla | Simikot area | 2,960 | ~6.0 | Grid-absent | Mugu (upper) | Gamgadhi area | 2,080–3,500 | 5.8–6.2 | Grid-absent | # Why the zone is strategically interesting Three genuine reasons, despite the grid-distance challenge: ## 1. It is the only Nepal zone where winter is the best solar month. Winter clear-sky days are more numerous than summer clear-sky days here (summer can have valley-mist + high-altitude storms; winter is cloudless). The complementarity argument in solar-hydro-complementarity is strongest here: Zone A in winter generates ~110% of its April peak, exactly when Nepal's hydro fleet is at its weakest. ## 2. Off-grid solar + BESS at these sites has extraordinary kWh-value. Incumbent energy source is mostly: (a) no electricity at all, (b) diesel generation at NPR 60–90/kWh, (c) firewood for heating and cooking. Displacing (b) makes Zone A solar the highest-value per-kWh energy investment in Nepal — 10–15× the utility-tariff-equivalent value. ## 3. It is the long-dated solar export corridor. The ratmate-rasuwagadhi-kerung-400kv line, if completed, opens a trans-Himalayan interconnection to Tibet. Zone A solar, transmitted northward, becomes relevant to China's western-grid imports. This is a 15–20 year story, not a near-term strategy, but Nepal's long-dated Zone A solar optionality is real and should be preserved through conservation-compatible land zoning. # Near-term constraints Grid distance: Nearest 132 kV substation is typically 100–200 km away. Terrain multiplies per-km transmission cost 3–5×. Local demand: Mustang's total population ~14,000; Dolpa's ~37,000. Grid-scale off-take is absent. Land tenure: Much of upper Mustang and Dolpa is inside Annapurna Conservation Area and Shey Phoksundo National Park. Utility-scale ground-mount is largely excluded. Access: Road connectivity seasonal; airlift-only sites in winter. Construction: Cold-climate installation window is April–October only. # What Zone A is for, realistically In order of near-term deployability: 1. Off-grid + BESS for remote institutional and community sites — the karnali-solar-energy-project template, extended to Mustang and Dolpa. 2. Diesel-displacement at trekking lodges and research stations — 5–50 kWp each, high unit economics. 3. Pilot research sites for high-altitude PV degradation / snow-load / bifacial performance — valuable to global solar R&D, underrated in Nepal. 4. Long-dated grid export optionality via trans-Himalayan interconnection. Utility-scale GW-class solar in Mustang is not a 2030 project. It is a 2040 project and the value is in preserving the land zoning and interconnection optionality now. # Related solar-resource-geography-nepal — zone framework data-nepal-solar-resource-zones — numerical tables karnali-solar-energy-project — template programme rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — track this zone lives in ratmate-rasuwagadhi-kerung-400kv — long-dated interconnection wb-esmap-solar-resource-assessment — the measurement source global-solar-atlas-nepal — the satellite cross-reference

pages.title: Nalsyau Gad Storage Hep
pages.excerpt: Nalsyau Gad Storage is a 410 MW storage hydropower project at the survey/study stage — one of the largest storage projects in Nepal's development pipeline.
pages.body_text: Nalsyau Gad Storage HEP Nalsyau Gad Storage is a 410 MW storage hydropower project at the survey/study stage — one of the largest storage projects in Nepal's development pipeline. # Scale & Significance At 410 MW, Nalsyau Gad ranks among the largest proposed hydropower projects in Nepal, and critically, it is explicitly a storage project — not run-of-river. If developed, it would rival Upper Tamakoshi (456 MW) in capacity while providing the firm, dispatchable generation that Nepal's system lacks. See storage-deficit. The project appears on the priority watchlist and top capacity curated layers in the map explorer, indicating its system-level significance in capacity planning. # Storage vs. RoR Economics The economic case for large storage projects in Nepal is fundamentally different from run-of-river: Aspect | Run-of-River | Storage | ---|---|---| Revenue profile | Seasonal: wet surplus, dry deficit | Firm: dispatchable year-round | Capital cost | Lower per MW | Higher (dam, reservoir, resettlement) | System value | Energy (kWh) | Capacity + energy + ancillary services | PPA framework | NPR 4.80/8.40 seasonal | Requires cost-plus or capacity payment | The ERC's 2026 "Directive on Electricity Purchase and Sale of Reservoir-Based Power Plants" represents a regulatory pivot toward recognizing storage's differentiated value, but legacy PPA frameworks have historically treated all hydro as interchangeable energy — systematically undervaluing storage. Nalsyau Gad's commercial viability depends on whether the new directive creates a genuinely bankable framework. # Development Status At the survey/study stage, Nalsyau Gad faces years of geological investigation, environmental assessment, and financing negotiations before reaching construction. The gap between survey-stage identification and financial close is where most large Nepali hydro projects stall. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 410 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Nalsyau | District | Jajarkot | Province | Karnali Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower storage-deficit firm-power

pages.title: Nalsyau Gad
pages.excerpt: Nalsyau Gad is a 410 MW storage candidate in the Karnali basin and the best dry-energy performer in the JICA promising-storage set used by this wiki. It is pinned in both data-layer-storage-shortlist and data-layer-priority-watchlist because it contributes the kind of dry-season
pages.body_text: Nalsyau Gad Nalsyau Gad is a 410 MW storage candidate in the Karnali basin and the best dry-energy performer in the JICA promising-storage set used by this wiki. It is pinned in both data-layer-storage-shortlist and data-layer-priority-watchlist because it contributes the kind of dry-season energy Nepal's run-of-river-heavy system is missing. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 410 MW | Category | jica promising storage | Basin | Karnali (karnali-basin) | Annual energy | 1406.1 GWh | Dry-season energy | 581.8 GWh | Dry share | 41.4% | Total storage | 419.6 MCM | Effective storage | 296.3 MCM | # Why It Matters Nalsyau Gad's value is not just its 410 MW nameplate. The JICA/NEA storage-screening figures give it 581.8 GWh of dry-season energy, equal to 41.4% of annual energy. That is the strongest dry-energy output in the promising-storage shortlist and one of the few project records that directly addresses the storage-deficit rather than only adding wet-season energy. This is why it appears in the priority watchlist despite being survey-stage. A project can be strategically important before it is construction-ready if it changes the dry-season balance, especially in a system where kulekhani-cascade is still the only operating seasonal-storage anchor and tanahu-hydropower is the only large storage project in active construction. # Status And Timing Nalsyau Gad should be read as a planning / promising-storage candidate, not a confirmed near-term build. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Nalsyau Gad STO in the 2031-2035 project window. That is a planning sequence, not a construction contract. Key watch items are feasibility update, developer assignment, environmental and social screening, financing structure, and evacuation planning from the western hill grid into the larger transmission backbone. # Map Interpretation The map marker is a river-aligned project reference derived from the registry/project point. It is suitable for basin-level and corridor-level comparison, but it should not be treated as a final dam-axis coordinate. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 # See also karnali-basin storage-deficit firm-power data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-storage-shortlist data-layer-priority-watchlist

pages.title: Naumure (W. Rapti)
pages.excerpt: Naumure is a 245 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because its effective storage is unusually large for its installed capacity, giving it strong seasonal-regulation value in a water-deficit medium basin.
pages.body_text: Naumure (W. Rapti) Naumure is a 245 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the West Rapti basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because its effective storage is unusually large for its installed capacity, giving it strong seasonal-regulation value in a water-deficit medium basin. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 245 MW | Category | jica promising storage | Basin | West Rapti (karnali-basin) | Annual energy | 1157.5 GWh | Dry-season energy | 309.9 GWh | Dry share | 26.8% | Total storage | 1021.0 MCM | Effective storage | 580.0 MCM | # Why It Matters Naumure is not the largest storage candidate by MW, but it is one of the most interesting by reservoir function: 1,021 MCM total storage, 580 MCM effective storage, and 309.9 GWh of dry-season energy. That combination makes it important to the wiki's argument that Nepal's deficit is about timing and controlled water, not only installed capacity. The project also matters because the West Rapti is not usually the headline basin in Nepal hydropower discussions. Including Naumure keeps the storage conversation from collapsing into only Karnali, Koshi, and Gandaki mega-projects. # Status And Timing Naumure should be read as a planning candidate. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Naumure STO in the 2036-2040 window. There is no confirmed construction start in the current wiki evidence set. The key open questions are scheme selection, interaction with other West Rapti candidates, environmental and downstream water-use implications, financing, and whether the project becomes a power-only scheme or a broader multipurpose water-storage project. # Map Interpretation The map marker uses a named West Rapti / Rapti River anchor near Dang. It is a basin-level reference for map reading, not a final project-coordinate claim. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 # See also karnali-basin storage-deficit firm-power data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-storage-shortlist

pages.title: Nawalpur 132 kV Substation
pages.excerpt: A planned 132/33 kV substation project intended to improve supply reliability in Nawalpur and connect back into the Haripur 33/11 kV node through new 33 kV sub-transmission links.
pages.body_text: Nawalpur 132 kV Substation A planned 132/33 kV substation project intended to improve supply reliability in Nawalpur and connect back into the Haripur 33/11 kV node through new 33 kV sub-transmission links. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 54 describes the Nawalpur substation as a 132/33 kV, 63 MVA node with additional 33 kV double-circuit line works to feed the local load area. # Why It Matters This is the kind of mid-voltage reinforcement asset that sits between the headline 220/400 kV corridors and the distribution-level service outcome. It adds detail to the broader story of nea trying to convert bulk power growth into local reliability improvements. # See also nea nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 data-domestic-demand

pages.title: NEA 960 MW National Solar Tender
headings: Why this tender matters (beyond the MW)
1. It establishes the price floor for Nepal solar.
4. It is the test case for [[agrivoltaics-and-land]].
pages.excerpt: The single largest solar procurement event in Nepal's history. A national-scale competitive-bid tender launched 2024 for 960 MWp of utility-scale grid-tied solar PV across Nepal, awarded to 63 projects from multiple IPPs. PPA signing and financial closure staged over FY 2025/26 a
pages.body_text: NEA 960 MW National Solar Tender The single largest solar procurement event in Nepal's history. A national-scale competitive-bid tender launched 2024 for 960 MWp of utility-scale grid-tied solar PV across Nepal, awarded to 63 projects from multiple IPPs. PPA signing and financial closure staged over FY 2025/26 and FY 2026/27; if ~70% of awards reach COD, Nepal's operating utility solar expands ~5× over the current fleet by FY 2028/29. The tender that represents the institutional bet that Nepal's solar scaling can be delivered through competitive IPP PPAs — not donor-funded NEA self-build, not off-grid AEPC programmes. # Key Facts Item | Value | ---|---| Total capacity tendered | 960 MWp | Number of projects selected | 63 | Typical project size | 5–25 MWp | Geographic distribution | Terai-dominant (Madhesh + Lumbini + Sudurpaschim + Koshi provinces) | Procurement model | Tariff-based competitive bidding | Tariff range (awarded) | NPR 5.70–6.20/kWh (gazette) ≈ $0.043–$0.047 | PPA tenor | 25 years, standard NEA PPA structure | Grid integration study | Cumulative Grid Impact Study completed FY 2024/25 under wb-grid-solar-ee-project TA line | Expected PPA closures | FY 2025/26–2026/27 | Expected first CODs | FY 2026/27 | Expected full portfolio COD | FY 2028/29 (if ~70% conversion rate) | # Tender structure by province (approximate) Province | MW in tender (approx.) | Notes | ---|---:|---| Madhesh (Bara, Parsa, Rautahat, Sarlahi, Dhanusha) | ~350–400 | Densest Terai grid; most bids received | Lumbini (Nawalparasi, Rupandehi, Kapilvastu, Banke) | ~200–250 | Hetauda-Bharatpur-Bardaghat corridor access | Sudurpaschim (Kailali, Kanchanpur) | ~80–120 | Far-west Terai | Koshi (Jhapa, Morang, Sunsari, Saptari) | ~150–200 | Eastern backbone | Bagmati (Makwanpur, Chitwan) | ~30–60 | Central, smaller allocations | Gandaki + Karnali (limited) | ~20–40 | Hydro co-location and resource-zone pockets | Total | ~960 | | The geographic weighting toward Terai provinces reflects the solar-resource-geography-nepal grid-adjacency priority — most awards sit within 10 km of existing 132 or 220 kV substations on the hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone and dana-kushma-butwal-corridor. # Why this tender matters (beyond the MW) Four reasons: ## 1. It establishes the price floor for Nepal solar. The awarded tariff range (5.70–6.20 NPR/kWh) is the first portfolio-scale solar price discovery in Nepal. Earlier rounds (Phase I at 8.40, Phase III at 6.40) were single-digit-count project awards; 960 MW with 63 developers is the first price point that reflects actual competitive equilibrium. Future project evaluations should benchmark against this tariff, not against 2017 numbers. ## 2. It validates the market. 63 IPPs winning awards implies a functional pipeline of developers, advisors, local EPC firms, and debt financiers. The Nepal solar market was single-digit-developer-deep before this tender; it is now dozens-of-developer-deep. This matters more than the physical MW — a market of depth 1 cannot scale; a market of depth 60 can. ## 3. It stress-tests the institutional fit. The tender has four binding constraints: land acquisition (Terai agricultural politics), grid integration (penetration levels, forecast management), financial close (local-currency long-tenor debt), PPA discipline (NEA single-buyer liability). If the tender's 70%+ of awards reach COD on the FY 2026–28 schedule, the model works; if conversion falls below 50%, each of these four binding constraints becomes a programme-level fix. ## 4. It is the test case for agrivoltaics-and-land. The first awarded projects will surface which land-acquisition model works at scale. If pure-purchase / fee-simple conversion dominates and succeeds, agrivoltaics is merely an add-on. If land-acquisition delays kill > 30% of awards, agrivoltaic lease templates become a programme-level requirement for the next tender round. # Tariff context The tender tariff sits at the crossover point between solar and hydro (solar-lcoe-crossover): Benchmark | Price (NPR/kWh) | ---|---:| 960 MW tender awarded (blended) | ~5.94 | NEA small RoR hydro PPA (blended) | 6.20 | NEA PRoR hydro PPA (blended) | 6.80 | NEA time-weighted-value of solar | ~4.80 | NEA time-weighted-value of small RoR | ~7.00 | Each MW in this tender is already cheaper than equivalent small RoR hydro on a blended basis and ~30% cheaper on time-weighted basis. This is the tariff point that claim-solar-cheaper-than-small-hydro stands on. # Implementation risks and watch items Risk | Mitigation | Status | ---|---|---| Land-acquisition delays on fee-simple conversion | Lease templates, agrivoltaic structures | Not yet in tender docs | Grid-integration penetration issues | Completed Grid Impact Study + future BESS requirements | First pass complete | Local-currency long-tenor debt availability | Nepal Rastra Bank policy; donor blending | Partial; FX risk persists | PPA settlement delays from NEA | Escrow accounts; performance guarantees | Standard PPA mechanisms | Weather / monsoon COD delays | Schedule discipline, grid-extension coordination | Developer-by-developer | Tariff-crash political backlash | Transparent bidding + price disclosure | Tender ran transparently | # Related nea-solar — programme parent nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 — primary source solar-lcoe-crossover — the price story this tender anchors data-solar-fleet-inventory — the fleet this tender expands data-solar-hydro-lcoe — the numerical crossover agrivoltaics-and-land — the next-round institutional enhancement hybrid-siting-logic — the siting logic this tender operationalises claim-solar-cheaper-than-small-hydro, claim-solar-terai-only-short-cycle-build — the tracked claims this tender evidences

pages.title: NEA Solar Programme
headings: Major utility plants under the programme
1. Land acquisition is the new bottleneck
pages.excerpt: The utility-scale grid-tied solar programme operated by Nepal Electricity Authority. Sits inside NEA as a distinct programme line that has grown from a single 25 MWp NEA-EPC plant (Bidur/Trishuli, 2022) to a ~1.1 GWp pipeline as of FY 2024/25.
pages.body_text: NEA Solar Programme The utility-scale grid-tied solar programme operated by Nepal Electricity Authority. Sits inside NEA as a distinct programme line that has grown from a single 25 MWp NEA-EPC plant (Bidur/Trishuli, 2022) to a ~1.1 GWp pipeline as of FY 2024/25. # Programme evolution Phase | Years | Character | Key output | ---|---|---|---:| Pre-programme | 2010–2015 | Isolated small pilots | <1 MWp demonstration sites | Phase I — first IPP auctions | 2016–2018 | 5-developer competitive bid | 24 MWp awarded, 3 plants commissioned 2019–20 | Phase II — NEA self-build | 2019–2022 | WB Grid Solar & EE Project | 25 MWp Bidur/Trishuli commissioned 2022 | Phase III — IPP rounds | 2023–2024 | Competitive-bid IPP PPAs | 170 MWp PPAs signed (8 plants) | Phase IV — the 960 MW tender | 2024–2025 | National-scale IPP portfolio | 960 MWp awarded (63 projects) (nea-960mw-solar-tender) | Phase V (emerging) | 2025– | Hybrid / floating / grid-impact | Khungri 50 MW hybrid, reservoir floating PV, ~585 MW additional study-stage | # Key Facts (FY 2024/25) Operating utility-scale solar: 141.94 MWp (all grid-tied; NEA-owned + IPP-owned combined) NEA-owned share: ~25 MWp (Bidur/Trishuli + smaller pilots) IPP-owned share: ~117 MWp (commissioned under Phase I / III) PPA-signed, pre-COD: 170 MWp (8 plants, FY 2024/25 signings) Tender-awarded, pre-PPA: 960 MWp (63 projects from 2024/25 tender) Under grid-impact study, beyond 960 MW: ~585 MWp Annual PV generation (FY 2024/25): ~220 GWh (CF ~18%) # Tariff structure NEA's tariff-based competitive bidding framework for utility solar: Phase I (2016–18): NPR 7.30–8.40/kWh, single-round competitive Phase III (2023–24): NPR 5.94–6.50/kWh, competitive bidding with ceiling Phase IV (960 MW, 2024–25): NPR 5.70–6.20/kWh, national tender with province-balanced allocation See data-solar-hydro-lcoe for the full time-series and solar-lcoe-crossover for the cross-over narrative. # Programme organizational structure NEA's utility solar programme is scattered across several NEA directorates — a structural weakness: Generation Directorate — NEA-owned plant operation (Bidur/Trishuli) Project Management Directorate — tender design, PPA negotiation Transmission Directorate — grid-impact studies and interconnection Distribution Directorate — net-metering for rooftop Engineering Services Department / NEC — EPC supervision No single dedicated "solar cell" exists at senior-management level. The 960 MW tender exposed coordination friction across these five lines; a consolidated Renewable Energy Directorate is the obvious institutional fix. # Major utility plants under the programme Plant | Capacity (MWp) | Status | Owner | Notes | ---|---:|---|---|---| Bidur / Trishuli | 25.00 | Operating (2022) | NEA | bidur-trishuli-solar-25mwp | Butwal Solar | 8.50 | Operating (2020) | Ridi HP Development | butwal-solar-ridi-8-5mwp | Mithila Solar PV | 10.00 | Operating (2021) | Eco Power Development | mithila-solar-dhanusha-10mwp | Bishnu Priya Solar Farm | ~0.96 | Operating (2018) | Surya Power Company | bishnu-priya-nawalparasi | Khungri Solar Hybrid | 50 | Planned (2026) | NEA + IPP | khungri-solar-hybrid-50mw | Karnali Solar Energy Project | ~10 (pilot) | Planned/implementation | NEA / AEPC hybrid | karnali-solar-energy-project | 960 MW tender portfolio | 960 (63 projects) | PPA in progress | Various IPPs | nea-960mw-solar-tender | Other small IPP plants (~17 plants) | ~90 (combined) | Operating | Various IPPs | From Phase I + III rounds | # Institutional challenges ## 1. Land acquisition is the new bottleneck Phase I–III plants were small enough (0.5–25 MWp) that land was project-specific. Phase IV (960 MW) triggers the land-at-scale question that agrivoltaics-and-land addresses. NEA does not have a land-programme office; this function needs to move to a new body or to AEPC. ## 2. Grid integration at 1+ GW solar The cumulative grid impact study NEA completed under Phase IV anticipates 4–6% instantaneous-penetration events by 2027 when 960 MW is partially commissioned. Forecast management, BESS requirements, and reactive-power compensation need to be designed in now. ## 3. PPA exposure balance NEA's PPA liability has grown to NPR 77.1B annual electricity purchase cost (see nea). Solar PPAs are adding ~NPR 6–8B/year to this. The single-buyer model (nepal-power-trade-schemes) plus fixed-price solar PPAs creates a structural interest-rate-sensitive liability that needs transparent treatment. ## 4. Talent depth NEA's solar engineering depth is thin; the Grid Solar & EE Project invested in training but the talent base for an additional 1 GW programme is a 3–5 year capacity-build, not a tender-cycle fix. # Related nea — the parent institution nea-960mw-solar-tender — the defining programme bidur-trishuli-solar-25mwp — the flagship wb-grid-solar-ee-project — the financing anchor for Phase II data-solar-fleet-inventory — the full portfolio solar-lcoe-crossover — the tariff trajectory hybrid-siting-logic — the direction Phase V should take

pages.title: Nepal Electricity Authority (NEA)
pages.excerpt: Figure 2.1-2 NEA Organization Chart (as of September 2022)
pages.body_text: Nepal Electricity Authority (NEA) Figure 2.1-2 NEA Organization Chart (as of September 2022) Timeline of Hydropower Development by NEA Nepal's central electricity institution. Simultaneously serves as primary generator, monopsony buyer, and sole grid operator — a combination that creates structural conflicts of interest. # Roles Role | Description | ------|-------------| Generator | Operates NEA-owned plants (Kulekhani, Kali Gandaki A, etc.) | Single buyer | Purchases all IPP output through long-term PPAs | Grid operator | Runs the Integrated Nepal Power System (INPS) | System planner | Coordinates dispatch via Load Dispatch Center | # Key Facts NEA and subsidiaries: ~36% of installed hydro; IPPs: ~64% System losses: ~13.46% (FY 2024/25) Eliminated scheduled load shedding in 2018 76% of firms still reported regular outages in 2022 Electricity access: 91.41% of households (5.586 million metered connections) — lower than the 99% claimed by previous management # Financial Health (FY 2024/25) Metric | FY 2023/24 | FY 2024/25 | Change | --------|-----------|-----------|--------| Profit before tax (NPR B) | 14.46 | 9.06 | −37.3% | Revenue from electricity sales (NPR B) | — | 125.27 | +8.46% | Total income (NPR B) | — | 138.87 | — | Total operating expenses (NPR B) | — | 97.79 | +10.15% | Electricity purchase cost (NPR B) | — | 77.10 | +11.73% | Total assets (NPR B) | — | 684.91 | +6.2% | Forex losses (NPR B) | — | ~2.50 | (yen appreciation) | May 2025 white paper (new MD Hitendra Dev Shakya) contested the previous management's claimed NPR 8B profit, revealing a NPR 5.26B loss and total liabilities of NPR 385 billion (including NPR 171.4B in long-term foreign loans under government guarantee). The discrepancy between the audited profit and the white-paper loss figure reflects different accounting treatments and periods — but signals that NEA's true fiscal position is worse than public-facing reports suggest. Debt Service Coverage Ratio: 2.27 (above 1.2 minimum — still comfortable) Return on Historic PPE: 4.50% (below 6% requirement) Debt-Equity Ratio: 47.59:52.41 # Institutional Issues Frequently dominates policy landscape, leaving the Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC) sidelined Generation licensing not coordinated with transmission line planning Inter-agency disputes over EIAs, land acquisition, and forest clearance delay projects The single-buyer model limits market flexibility and creates PPA liability risk PPA liability exposure is growing: electricity purchase costs rose 11.73% in one year to NPR 77.1B, while export prices (the revenue relief valve) are falling — creating a structural squeeze between obligated payments to IPPs and realized market income Leadership instability: MD changeover (Ghising to Shakya, April 2025) resulted in dueling public narratives about NEA's financial health # Related stranded-generation — NEA's grid cannot absorb what generators produce seasonal-arbitrage-trap — NEA manages the import/export trade that produces value loss india-energy-relationship — NEA is the counterpart for cross-border commercial arrangements

pages.title: Nepal Energy Profile
pages.excerpt: Energy-specific profile of Nepal for electricity-system context.
pages.body_text: Nepal Energy Profile Energy-specific profile of Nepal for electricity-system context. # System Snapshot (FY 2024/25) Metric | Value | --------|-------| DoED operating registry | 3,791.874 MW (Apr 10, 2026, 189 plants >1 MW) | Separate DoED sub-MW list | 15.197 MW across 19 plants (last updated Jul 7, 2025; not the same cut-off date) | NEA annual report figure | 3,591.262 MW total / 3,389.912 MW hydro (FY 2024/25 cut-off) | Hydro share | 94.4% of the FY 2024/25 installed mix | Fleet type | >90% run-of-river-hydropower | Storage capacity | 106 MW (kulekhani-cascade) | System peak demand | 2,901 MW (Jul 1, 2025) | National peak demand | 2,409 MW | Monsoon surplus | ~850 MW exported during peak production | Per-capita electricity use | 370–465 kWh/yr | Grid electricity share of final energy | 7.23% (FY 2079/80) — up from 4.96% (FY 2078/79). See data-final-energy-mix | Net trade position | Seasonal exporter (since 2023); FY 2024/25 net exports: 699 GWh | Export revenue | NPR 17.46B (India + Bangladesh) | Approved export capacity | 941 MW (400 MW bilateral + 540 MW IEX) | Transmission | 5,742 circuit-km at 66 kV+; 8,867 MVA substations | System losses | 13.46% (NEA FY 2024/25) / ~12.7% (broader 2024 report framing) | Cross-border transfer capacity | ~1,141 MW approved | Household electrification | 91.41% households (5.586M metered connections); broader access indicators often round toward ~95% | Sep 2024 flood damage | 11 operational projects, 600+ MW affected | [!warning] Supreme Court Hydropower Halt In early 2025, Nepal's Supreme Court ordered a halt to development activities — including hydropower — within all protected areas. The ruling covers 12 national parks, 6 conservation areas, 1 hunting reserve, 1 wildlife reserve, and 13 buffer zones. IPPAN estimates this could affect ~300 hydropower projects and up to 20 GW of potential capacity. This is a significant new constraint on the "developable potential" claim. # The Core Paradox Nepal has crossed from chronic shortage into seasonal surplus. It now exports electricity in the monsoon and still imports in the dry season. The country has more electricity than it can sell and less than it needs — at the same time, every year. See seasonal-mismatch. # Reliability Transition The country is far better off than during the classic shortage era, but that recent success can be overstated if the timeline is forgotten: Scheduled load shedding ended in 2018. Before that, outages lasted up to 14 hours/day between 2007 and 2017. World Bank-linked framing in the merged notes puts the macroeconomic cost of that era at roughly 7% of GDP per year. That history matters because today's system should be read as a transition from shortage to fragile seasonal surplus, not as a finished reliability story. # Energy Beyond Electricity Nepal's broader energy system is not electricity-centered. Biomass still dominates. In the FY 2079/80 WECS balance, residential use was 60.75% of final energy, grid electricity 7.23% (up from 4.96% the prior year — a real jump driven by +21.2% electricity consumption against −41.5% coal and −16.1% petroleum). Hydro's share of total final energy is about 6.8%. Nepal's electricity story is more advanced than its total energy transition. Full cross-tab at data-final-energy-mix; strategic frame at energy-substitution-pathway and domestic-led-hydro-strategy. # Related data-fleet-composition — fleet breakdown data-trade-time-series — import/export trajectory data-potential-pyramid — from 83,000 MW to 3,591 MW installed data-map-inventory — merged map, layer, and figure catalog nea — the institutional center

pages.title: Nepal–India Electricity Transmission and Trade Project (NIETTP)
headings: Nepal–India Electricity Transmission and Trade Project (NIETTP)
pages.excerpt: 400 kV Tower at Koshi River
pages.body_text: Nepal–India Electricity Transmission and Trade Project (NIETTP) 400 kV Tower at Koshi River The World Bank-backed program that bundled Nepal's first large cross-border-capacity push with domestic reinforcement works. In the annual book's 2020 framing, NIETTP is the umbrella program linking the cross-border-exchange objective to both hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone and the hetauda-bharatpur-bardaghat-corridor reinforcement works. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 66 describes NIETTP as a World Bank-funded project intended to establish roughly 1,000 MW of Nepal–India high-voltage exchange capacity while also executing the Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV line and associated substations. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Program type | Transmission and trade program | Core objective | Increase Nepal–India exchange capacity and improve reliability | Funding | World Bank | Linked assets in 2020 book | HDDI 400 kV works; Hetauda–Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV works | Framing | Cross-border trade + domestic reinforcement in one package | # Why It Matters NIETTP is the programmatic bridge between domestic grid hardening and the export-opening logic that later runs through dhalkebar-muzaffarpur and india-energy-relationship. It shows that Nepal's cross-border story was never just a border-wire story. The domestic substations and 220/400 kV reinforcements were part of the same reliability package. # See also dhalkebar-muzaffarpur india-energy-relationship hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone hetauda-bharatpur-bardaghat-corridor

pages.title: Pancheshwar Multipurpose Project
pages.excerpt: A bi-national mega-project on the mahakali-basin river forming the Nepal-India border. The largest proposed hydropower project in the region. Stalled for nearly three decades.
pages.body_text: Pancheshwar Multipurpose Project A bi-national mega-project on the mahakali-basin river forming the Nepal-India border. The largest proposed hydropower project in the region. Stalled for nearly three decades. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Total capacity | 6,480 MW (divided equally between Nepal and India) | Dam height | 315 m (proposed — would be world's largest rockfill dam) | Type | Storage / Multipurpose | Treaty basis | Mahakali Treaty (1996) | DPR | Drafted by India's WAPCOS (2016) | # Status (updated April 2026) Stalled. No construction has started after 30 years. Feb 2025: Energy Ministers Khadka (Nepal) and Patil (India) met in New Delhi; India "assured commitment to finalizing the DPR" — a promise made repeatedly since 2016. The headline "India assures yet again" was the literal Republica headline. 6th Joint Expert Group (JEG) meeting was to be convened by August 2025 to finalize DPR. No public confirmation it occurred. Core impasse: water/irrigation share. Nepal demands 50-50 sharing of irrigation and flood-control benefits; India insists on 75-25. Electricity is already split equally, so the fight is about the non-electricity value — precisely the multi-purpose benefits that make the project strategically important. Saptakoshi High Dam and Sunkoshi Storage projects face similar JTE stalls; the 17th JTE meeting was October 2023. Additional stalling factors: - India's internal federal politics (Uttarakhand and UP have conflicting interests) - Nepal's political instability (18+ governments in 30 years) - Financing uncertainty (initial phase est. NPR 336 billion; Nepal share NPR 126–130B) - Seismic zone 4–5 risk, with ~50,000 people affected across both sides - Environmental concerns including biodiversity loss and flood risk from a 315 m dam Nepal has received zero megawatts from the Mahakali Treaty after 30 years (signed 1996). # Significance Pancheshwar is the ultimate case study for hydro-geopolitics. It shows how bilateral treaty structures, domestic politics on both sides, and the difficulty of valuing non-electricity benefits (flood control, irrigation) can prevent even the most strategically obvious projects from materializing. # Related mahakali-basin — the river india-energy-relationship — the bilateral context hydro-geopolitics — the analytical frame Nepal River Geopolitics Explorer data-map-inventory

pages.title: Phukot Karnali
pages.excerpt: Phukot Karnali is a 426-480 MW peaking run-of-river project on the Karnali in Kalikot district, promoted by Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited. It is pinned in both the data-layer-priority-watchlist and data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is one of the few large Karnali p
pages.body_text: Phukot Karnali Phukot Karnali is a 426-480 MW peaking run-of-river project on the Karnali in Kalikot district, promoted by Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited. It is pinned in both the data-layer-priority-watchlist and data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is one of the few large Karnali projects with a stronger document-backed project-area anchor, a state developer, and a plausible place in Nepal's 2030s buildout sequence. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 426 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Karnali (karnali-basin) | District | Kalikot | Province | Karnali Pradesh | Promoter | Vidhyut Utpadan Company Limited | # Why It Matters Phukot is a medium-large Karnali project, not a mega-reservoir like mugu-karnali-storage. That makes it strategically different: it is big enough to matter for the western generation stack, but small enough that its buildability, access, and evacuation questions are less extreme than the very largest Karnali schemes. VUCL's project-status material describes a project in Kalikot using Karnali River flow, feeding the integrated power system at the proposed Regil grid substation. The same status document lists a 474 MW main underground powerhouse, six 79 MW Francis units, an additional 6 MW environmental-release powerhouse, a 400 kV double-circuit evacuation line of about 2.3 km to Regil, and an estimated construction duration of about five years once full construction starts. The wiki keeps the registry value of 426 MW in the generated spec table because that is the value carried through the DoED-linked project dataset. The wider project literature often uses 480 MW or 474+6 MW. That discrepancy is not a contradiction in the map; it is a sign that the page is combining registry, VUCL, and planning-source views of the same project. # Status And Timing As of the current wiki evidence set, Phukot should be read as pre-construction / survey-stage, not an operating or under-construction plant. A 2025 news item reported renewed ministerial direction to take Karnali projects forward with NHPC collaboration, but the stronger planning anchor for this wiki is the IPSDP milestone ladder, which places Phukot Karnali in the 2031-2035 project window. The practical read is therefore: Question | Current Read | ----------|--------------| Is construction visibly underway? | Not confirmed by the sources used here. | Is there a construction-duration estimate? | Yes: VUCL status material estimates about five years for construction. | Is there a dated planning window? | Yes: IPSDP groups Phukot Karnali in 2031-2035. | What has to be watched next? | Development-vehicle clarity with NHPC/VUCL, generation licence status, financing, PPA/offtake, Regil grid hub readiness, and land/environmental approvals. | # Map Interpretation The map point is based on an official project-area center from the VUCL status report and is already close to the mapped Karnali reach. That makes it more defensible than a plain registry coordinate, but it still should not be treated as a final dam-axis coordinate. The project includes a dam, underground powerhouse, tunnel system, and grid connection; the marker is a project reference, not a detailed engineering layout. # Risks And Open Questions Phukot's main risk is not the absence of hydropower resource. The Karnali flow is the reason the project exists. The binding questions are delivery questions: who finances it, whether NHPC participation becomes a final investment structure, whether the Regil evacuation path advances on time, and whether the five-year construction estimate survives detailed contracting. It also matters for the claim-mw-not-equal-value argument. A 426-480 MW Karnali project can be valuable, but its value depends on dispatch timing, dry-season contribution, grid absorption, and export or domestic offtake arrangements. Capacity alone does not settle the case. # Registry note Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset VUCL PKHEP status report and current project registry VUCL project-status PDF: Phukot Karnali Project Status # See also karnali-basin stranded-generation buildability data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-priority-watchlist data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects

pages.title: Rasuwagadhi
pages.excerpt: Rasuwagadhi is a 111 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Rasuwa district — one of the larger projects in Nepal's under-construction pipeline and a key asset in the Chilime Hydropower group portfolio.
pages.body_text: Rasuwagadhi Rasuwagadhi is a 111 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Bhote Koshi River in Rasuwa district — one of the larger projects in Nepal's under-construction pipeline and a key asset in the Chilime Hydropower group portfolio. # Ownership & Structure Rasuwagadhi Hydropower Company Limited is an NEA subsidiary, structurally nested under Chilime Hydropower Company Limited (CHCL). Chilime holds a 33% stake, while the NEA directly holds 18%, keeping the project within the state umbrella. As a Chilime subsidiary, Rasuwagadhi benefits from CHCL's institutional brand — but unlike its parent, it does not enjoy the anomalous "Regular/Excess" tariff architecture. # PPA & Financial Model Rasuwagadhi's PPA was signed in November 2011 under the standardized IPP tariff structure of NPR 4.80/kWh (wet season) and NPR 8.40/kWh (dry season), with a 3% annual escalation cap for eight years. By 2011, regulatory pressure from private IPPs had forced the NEA to subject even its own subsidiary ventures to the same commercial tariff schedule as independent developers. There is no evidence of renegotiation to elevate these rates. The project raised NPR 66 crore through a local/staff IPO in 2018. Unlike Chilime (which IPO'd after 7 years of profitable operations), Rasuwagadhi's shares were sold while the project was still under construction. # Construction & Delays Rasuwagadhi has faced delayed Commercial Operation Date (COD) due to disasters, including geological setbacks in the Bhote Koshi corridor. The project trades on its Chilime subsidiary reputation, but its financial trajectory is shaped by the same structural constraints facing all projects locked into the 4.80/8.40 PPA regime. # Geopolitical Position Located near Nepal's northern border with Tibet in Rasuwa district, Rasuwagadhi sits in a corridor that includes the Kerung border crossing — an increasingly strategic trade and infrastructure point between Nepal and China. This geographic positioning gives the project significance beyond its 111 MW capacity. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 111 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Bhote koshi (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Rasuwa | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability chilime ppa-data-retrieval-icra-care-2026 sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026

pages.title: Ratmate–Rasuwagadhi–Kerung 400 kV Transmission Line
pages.excerpt: Nepal's planned first grid interconnection with China in the annual-book 2020 planning frame. The project is both a power-trade idea and a geopolitical signal: a proposed 400 kV route from the Ratmate side of the Nepal grid to the Rasuwagadhi/Kerung border axis.
pages.body_text: Ratmate–Rasuwagadhi–Kerung 400 kV Transmission Line Nepal's planned first grid interconnection with China in the annual-book 2020 planning frame. The project is both a power-trade idea and a geopolitical signal: a proposed 400 kV route from the Ratmate side of the Nepal grid to the Rasuwagadhi/Kerung border axis. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 65 says the Ratmate–Rasuwagadhi–Kerung line was being advanced as Nepal's first power-grid interconnection with China, with a planned ~70 km 400 kV double-circuit line from the Rasuwagadhi border point to Ratmate and a power-exchange substation at Ratmate. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 400 kV | Counterparty direction | China / Tibet Autonomous Region | Status in 2020 book | Detail survey and feasibility stage | Nepal endpoint | Ratmate | Border axis | Rasuwagadhi–Kerung | # Why It Matters This is the clearest 2020-era public example of Nepal trying to widen its grid geopolitics beyond the India-only transmission frame. Even as a survey-stage line, it belongs with rasuwagadhi and hydro-geopolitics because the point of the project was energy-security optionality, not just another domestic corridor. # See also rasuwagadhi hydro-geopolitics mca-central-400 nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Sahas Urja — Solu Khola (Dudh Koshi) Hydroelectric Project
headings: Sahas Urja Limited — Solu Khola (Dudh Koshi) Hydroelectric Project
Why Sahas Urja Matters to This Wiki
pages.excerpt: 86 MW run-of-river hydropower project on the Solu Khola, a tributary of the Dudh Koshi, in Solukhumbu District, Koshi Province. Nepal's most-cited private IPP success story — and the project that proves the standard PPA rate is only viable at exceptional scale.
pages.body_text: Sahas Urja Limited — Solu Khola (Dudh Koshi) Hydroelectric Project 86 MW run-of-river hydropower project on the Solu Khola, a tributary of the Dudh Koshi, in Solukhumbu District, Koshi Province. Nepal's most-cited private IPP success story — and the project that proves the standard PPA rate is only viable at exceptional scale. # Project Specifications Parameter | Value | ---|---| Capacity | 86 MW | Type | Run-of-River | River | Solu Khola (tributary of Dudh Koshi) | District | Solukhumbu, Koshi Province | Entity type | Private IPP | Incorporated | February 2014 (private limited), converted to public limited September 2014 | COD | March 1, 2023 | Q-design parameter | Q41 (41% probability of exceedance) | The Q41 design parameter is more aggressive than the DoED 2018 Q45 mandate. The developers accepted higher dry-season underutilization risk in exchange for maximizing monsoon energy capture — a calculated bet that only works at scale. Source: ICRA Nepal surveillance reports A.996 and B.119; Sahas Urja project disclosures. # PPA Structure PPA signed: February 25, 2015 with NEA (take-or-pay) Wet season rate: NPR 4.80/kWh Dry season rate: NPR 8.40/kWh Escalation: 3% annually for 8 years, contingent on meeting RCOD milestones Sahas Urja retained all 8 escalation tranches by achieving on-time commissioning. Many peer IPPs lose escalation rights due to construction delays. Contracted annual energy: 520.20 GWh (Dry: 100.27 GWh / Wet: 419.93 GWh = 19.28% dry mix) Source: Sahas Urja Progress Report W010; ICRA Nepal. # Financial Performance (FY2023/24) Metric | Value | ---|---| Total revenue | NPR 2.648 billion | Net profit | NPR 902.33 million (114.66% YoY increase) | Gross profit margin | 95.54% | Net profit margin | 23.61% (after interest) | Interest expense | NPR 805.9 million (down from NPR 1.165 billion prior year) | Plant Load Factor | 69.05% | Generation performance | 87–89% of contracted energy in initial full operating years | Build cost (final) | NPR 172–175 million/MW (vs initial budget NPR 138M/MW, vs industry average NPR 200–300M/MW) | Financing | 76% debt, 10-bank consortium led by Nepal Investment Bank | Source: ICRA Nepal B.119 (April 2026); ClickMandu financial reporting; Himalayan Capital analysis. The company's 114.66% profit growth was driven fundamentally by interest cost reduction — base rates fell between FY2022/23 and FY2023/24, dropping interest expense from NPR 1.165 billion to NPR 805.9 million. This demonstrates that even the sector's strongest IPP remains heavily leveraged to macroeconomic rate cycles, not just hydrological performance. # Why Sahas Urja Matters to This Wiki Sahas Urja is Nepal's most-cited private IPP success story. It operates on the identical NPR 4.80/8.40 PPA rate as every other private run-of-river IPP of its era. Its strong financial performance does not prove the standard IPP rate is adequate — it proves the opposite: the rate is only viable at exceptional scale and cost discipline that the overwhelming majority of Nepal's hydro project pipeline cannot replicate. The median Nepal IPP is 10–30 MW. At that scale and at industry-average build costs of NPR 200–300 million/MW, the NPR 4.80/8.40 rate does not generate sufficient revenue to service debt and cover operating costs — as documented across the generation performance data in q-design-discharge and the debt service analysis in upper-tamakoshi. Sahas Urja's success is structural arbitrage: it captured the same fixed tariff as smaller projects but with a cost base and generation volume that made the math work. It is not a replicable model. At NPR 172–175 million/MW build cost on 86 MW, it achieved a capital efficiency that a 15 MW project on a remote tributary cannot match regardless of management quality. The single most important financial mechanic: Sahas Urja's 86 MW scale means that its 19.28% dry energy mix still yields 100 GWh of premium-priced (NPR 8.40/kWh) dry-season generation — enough absolute volume to cover fixed costs and debt service even if the remaining 80% of generation is sold at the depressed NPR 4.80 wet rate. A 15 MW IPP with the same 19% dry mix generates only ~17 GWh of dry-season revenue — insufficient to carry its debt load. See ppa-pricing for the full rate comparison table and nea-triple-authority for the institutional framework. # Promoters and Institutional Connections Chairman: Him Prasad Pathak Managing Director: Sushil Thapa Directors: Mukti Ram Pandey, Mahendra Kumar Giri Him Prasad Pathak is a central figure in Nepal's energy landscape. He began with the modest 5 MW Upper Hugdi Khola project before scaling to the 86 MW Solu Khola. He serves as a Secretary and Executive Committee member of IPPAN (Independent Power Producers' Association, Nepal), giving him direct lobbying access to MoEWRI, ERC, and NEA. Pathak orchestrated Sahas Urja's acquisition of a 51% controlling stake in Times Energy (341 MW Budhi Gandaki PRoR project) for approximately NPR 2 billion. He subsequently secured an NPR 52.5 billion consortium loan from 10 commercial banks for the Budhi Gandaki project — one of the largest private syndications in Nepal's history. Source: Sahas Urja corporate disclosures; ICRA Nepal; Annapurna Express; ShareSansar. Political connections: The company states no political affiliations. Independent verification of promoter PEP (Politically Exposed Person) status is not possible — the SEBON IPO prospectus exists but is in non-machine-readable scanned PDF format, frustrating automated accountability tracking. Local share distribution to 8,000+ promoters including 2,900 project-affected locals required coordination with local political structures — a documented pattern per the IFC local shares report. [UNVERIFIED: political affiliation verification beyond IFC pattern documentation.] # See also ppa-pricing — the rate comparison table placing Sahas Urja in context nea-triple-authority — the institutional framework governing PPA enforcement q-design-discharge — why Sahas Urja's 87–89% contracted generation performance is exceptional upper-tamakoshi — the NEA subsidiary comparison case chilime — the subsidiary with the favorable billing architecture

pages.title: Sanjen Khola
pages.excerpt: Sanjen Khola is a 78 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Sanjen Khola in Rasuwa district. At nearly double the capacity of the adjacent Sanjen project (42.5 MW), it is the larger of the two Sanjen River developments.
pages.body_text: Sanjen Khola Sanjen Khola is a 78 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Sanjen Khola in Rasuwa district. At nearly double the capacity of the adjacent Sanjen project (42.5 MW), it is the larger of the two Sanjen River developments. # Sanjen Cluster The Sanjen River valley in Rasuwa hosts two closely-related projects: Sanjen (42.5 MW, Chilime subsidiary) and Sanjen Khola (78 MW). Together, they represent ~120 MW of capacity from a relatively small catchment, raising questions about optimized water use, cumulative environmental impact, and whether both projects can simultaneously achieve their contracted energy yields from the same river system. # Development Context Both Sanjen projects benefit from the Chilime-Trishuli transmission corridor infrastructure that connects Rasuwa district generation to the national grid. The corridor's capacity to evacuate the combined output of Chilime (22.1 MW), Sanjen, Sanjen Khola, Rasuwagadhi (111 MW), and other projects in the Trishuli-Bhote Koshi system is a binding operational constraint — not just an abstract planning concern. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 78 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Sanjen | District | Rasuwa | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability

pages.title: Sanjen
pages.excerpt: Sanjen is a 42.5 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Sanjen River in Rasuwa district, promoted as part of the Chilime Hydropower Company Limited (CHCL) subsidiary network.
pages.body_text: Sanjen Sanjen is a 42.5 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Sanjen River in Rasuwa district, promoted as part of the Chilime Hydropower Company Limited (CHCL) subsidiary network. # Subsidiary Structure Sanjen Hydropower Company is one of three major subsidiaries spun off by CHCL to expand its generation portfolio beyond the original 22.1 MW Chilime plant. The holding company model — where a profitable legacy asset incubates and partially finances downstream projects — is the mechanism that has allowed CHCL to dominate Nepal's hydro-market narrative. Sanjen, Rasuwagadhi (111 MW), and Madhya Bhotekoshi (102 MW) together form a ~256 MW pipeline under the CHCL umbrella. # PPA Context Like Rasuwagadhi, Sanjen operates under the standard IPP tariff architecture of NPR 4.80/kWh (wet) and NPR 8.40/kWh (dry), not the preferential "Regular/Excess" billing system enjoyed by its parent company Chilime. This means Sanjen faces the same revenue structure as private IPPs despite its state-backing, and its financial viability depends on cost control and construction execution rather than bespoke tariff advantages. # Rasuwa District Context Sanjen and the nearby Sanjen Khola (78 MW) project are located in Rasuwa district, north of the Trishuli valley. This area has seen concentrated hydropower development driven by proximity to the Chilime-Trishuli transmission corridor and the Kathmandu load center. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 42.5 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Sanjen | District | Rasuwa | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability chilime sector-financial-analysis-triple-authority-2026

pages.title: Solu Corridor Tingla-Mirchaiya 132 kV
pages.excerpt: The Solu Corridor Tingla-Mirchaiya 132 kV line evacuates generation from the Solu/Okhaldhunga area south toward Mirchaiya. It is operational, but the public route geometry is still lower-confidence than the main 220 kV and 400 kV corridors because the current trace is reconstruct
pages.body_text: Solu Corridor Tingla-Mirchaiya 132 kV The Solu Corridor Tingla-Mirchaiya 132 kV line evacuates generation from the Solu/Okhaldhunga area south toward Mirchaiya. It is operational, but the public route geometry is still lower-confidence than the main 220 kV and 400 kV corridors because the current trace is reconstructed from NEA narrative and anchor points rather than an alignment atlas. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132 kV | Status | Operational | Corridor ID | | Public map feature | | Source basis | NEA Transmission Annual Book 2077 pages 47-52 | Official route length | 90.0 km route basis | Traced network length | 78.844 km | Validation delta | -12.40% | # Map Interpretation The map trace is a corridor-level reconstruction from the source-stated Mirchaiya and Tingla terminals, the Maruwa/Katari reroute context, and the municipal corridor through Okhaldhunga. It should be read as a faithful directional corridor, not as final engineering alignment. # Confidence Public decision: default-visible with explicit QA warning. The line is important and operational, so it remains visible. The warning exists because the geometry is low-confidence and because NEA sources appear to mix route-km and circuit-km: the older yearbook describes a 90 km route, while later inventory-style reporting lists 180 km, likely double-circuit conductor accounting. # Caveats This corridor needs a true route-grade IEE/RAP/alignment source before it can be promoted. Until then, the map should keep the confidence caveat visible. # See also data-layer-transmission-connected-traced-network nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 claim-transmission-immediate-blocker

pages.title: Sun Koshi No.3
pages.excerpt: Sun Koshi No.3 is a 536 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Koshi basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it combines large reservoir scale with meaningful dry-season output in Nepal's eastern-central hydro corridor.
pages.body_text: Sun Koshi No.3 Sun Koshi No.3 is a 536 MW JICA promising-storage candidate in the Koshi basin. It is pinned in data-layer-storage-shortlist because it combines large reservoir scale with meaningful dry-season output in Nepal's eastern-central hydro corridor. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 536 MW | Category | jica promising storage | Basin | Koshi (koshi-basin) | Annual energy | 1883.6 GWh | Dry-season energy | 335.9 GWh | Dry share | 17.8% | Total storage | 1220.0 MCM | Effective storage | 555.0 MCM | # Why It Matters Sun Koshi No.3 has 1,220 MCM total storage, 555 MCM effective storage, and 335.9 GWh of dry-season energy in the storage-shortlist table. Its dry share, 17.8%, is lower than Nalsyau Gad, Dudhkoshi, or Kokhajor-1, so the case for the project rests more on total reservoir block and basin position than on dry-share efficiency. This makes it a useful comparator for dudhkoshi-storage. Both are Koshi-basin storage candidates, but they occupy different strategic roles: Dudhkoshi is the stronger quantified dry-season block, while Sun Koshi No.3 is a large east-central storage option with different location and transmission implications. # Status And Timing Sun Koshi No.3 is a planning candidate, not an active construction project in the current wiki evidence set. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Sunkoshi 3 STO in the 2031-2035 window. Watch for updated feasibility, developer structure, environmental approvals, financing, and how the project is treated in relation to downstream river regulation and cross-border hydrology concerns. # Map Interpretation The current map marker is a river anchor. Use it for basin and strategic comparison, not for final dam-axis or reservoir-boundary analysis. # Registry note JICA/NEA storage master plan volume 2 # See also koshi-basin storage-deficit firm-power dudhkoshi-storage data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-storage-shortlist

pages.title: Super Madi
pages.excerpt: Super Madi is a 44 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Madi Khola in Kaski district, Gandaki Province.
pages.body_text: Super Madi Super Madi is a 44 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Madi Khola in Kaski district, Gandaki Province. [!note] Disambiguation "Super" is the official tier designation in the Nepal DoED/MoE registry — distinct from "Upper". On the same Madi Khola, upper-madi (25 MW) is an upstream operating plant, while Super Madi (44 MW) is a larger downstream project under construction. These are separate assets with different promoters and PPAs. # Significance At 44 MW, Super Madi is a mid-sized project by Nepal's under-construction standards, but noteworthy for its location in the Madi River system — a tributary of the Seti Gandaki/Narayani system in western Nepal's hydropower-rich Gandaki basin. The Madi Khola valley in Kaski has seen successive rounds of hydropower development, with multiple projects operating or under construction in close proximity. # Context Super Madi sits within the broader Gandaki basin development landscape, where projects must navigate the interactions between upstream-downstream water use, environmental flow requirements, and the transmission infrastructure needed to evacuate power from relatively remote valleys into the national grid. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 44 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Madi Khola (gandaki-basin) | District | Kaski | Province | Gandaki Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability

pages.excerpt: 155 MW · survey/study stage · on the Tamor · in Taplejung district.
pages.body_text: Super Tamor 155 MW · survey/study stage · on the Tamor · in Taplejung district. [!note] Disambiguation "Super" is the official Nepal DoED/MoE registry tier — distinct from "Upper". See also the broader koshi-basin development context. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 155 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Tamor (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Taplejung | Province | Province No 1 | # Significance At 155 MW, Super Tamor is one of the larger survey-stage projects in eastern Nepal, though it sits in the shadow of the much larger upper-tamor (285 MW, under construction) on the same river. The Tamor basin has attracted attention for both run-of-river and storage development potential, with multiple projects at different licence stages competing for water allocation and transmission evacuation from a relatively undeveloped eastern corridor. # Notes [!note] This is a registry-backed project record. pages.title: Super Trishuli
pages.excerpt: Super Trishuli is a 100 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Trishuli River — the largest project in the Trishuli cascade's current development pipeline.
pages.body_text: Super Trishuli Super Trishuli is a 100 MW run-of-river hydropower project under construction on the Trishuli River — the largest project in the Trishuli cascade's current development pipeline. [!note] Disambiguation "Super" is the official Nepal DoED/MoE registry tier — distinct from "Upper". The Trishuli corridor hosts multiple named tiers: upper-trishuli-3a (60 MW), upper-trishuli-3b (37 MW), upper-trishuli-1 (216 MW), trishuli (Devighat), and Super Trishuli (100 MW). Each is a separate asset with its own promoter, PPA, and status. See trishuli-cascade for the corridor overview. # Trishuli System The Trishuli River is Nepal's most heavily developed hydropower corridor, benefiting from proximity to Kathmandu's load center and established transmission infrastructure. The cascade includes multiple projects at various stages: the original Trishuli Devighat plant, Upper Trishuli 3A (60 MW, NEA-owned, operating), Upper Trishuli 3B (37 MW, subsidiary, under construction), and Super Trishuli (100 MW). Together, these projects extract significant energy from the Trishuli's flow, but also concentrate risk — geological events, sediment surges, or flow alterations from upstream projects cascade through the entire system. # PPA Structure As a private/IPP project, Super Trishuli operates under the standard tariff framework of NPR 4.80/kWh (wet) and NPR 8.40/kWh (dry) with 3% annual escalations over eight years. At 100 MW of run-of-river capacity in the Trishuli's seasonal flow regime, the project's financial model is heavily dependent on achieving competitive build costs and maximizing the dry-season energy share. # Industry Context The 100 MW mark represents a significant threshold: projects at this scale are large enough to require complex syndicated financing but not large enough to command the bespoke PPA terms that have been applied to mega-projects like Upper Karnali or West Seti. Super Trishuli sits at the intersection of Nepal's standard IPP model and the challenges of scaling that model to triple-digit capacities. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 100 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Trishuli (gandaki-basin) | District | Gorkha | Province | Gandaki Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability

pages.title: Tamakoshi 3
pages.excerpt: Tamakoshi 3 is a 650 MW survey-stage project on the Tama Koshi in Dolakha. It is pinned in data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is the sixth-largest capacity marker in the display set and sits in the same high-head eastern hydropower geography as upper-tamakoshi.
pages.body_text: Tamakoshi 3 Tamakoshi 3 is a 650 MW survey-stage project on the Tama Koshi in Dolakha. It is pinned in data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects because it is the sixth-largest capacity marker in the display set and sits in the same high-head eastern hydropower geography as upper-tamakoshi. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 650 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Tama Koshi (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Dolakha | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | Promoter | TBI Holding Co. Ltd. | # Why It Matters Tamakoshi 3 matters as a scale comparator. At 650 MW, it is larger than the already-commissioned upper-tamakoshi, but the two projects are at very different delivery stages. The contrast helps the wiki keep capacity and execution separate. It also matters for basin concentration. Dolakha and the Tama Koshi corridor already carry major hydropower infrastructure; adding Tamakoshi 3 would deepen that eastern-central concentration and increase the importance of transmission evacuation through the Khimti/Tamakoshi grid path. # Status And Timing The current layer treats Tamakoshi 3 as survey-stage. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Tamakoshi 3 in the 2036-2040 project window. There is no confirmed construction start in the current wiki evidence set. # Map Interpretation The marker is a river-aligned project reference derived from a registry point. It is suitable for top-10 capacity comparison, not final project engineering. # Registry note Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset # See also koshi-basin buildability upper-tamakoshi data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects
