pages.title: Tamor Storage
pages.excerpt: Tamor Storage is a proposed 200 MW storage hydropower project on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal, currently at the survey/study stage.
pages.body_text: Tamor Storage Tamor Storage is a proposed 200 MW storage hydropower project on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal, currently at the survey/study stage. # Storage Value Storage hydropower is the critical missing piece in Nepal's generation portfolio. Run-of-river projects dominate the fleet — they produce abundant energy during the monsoon but collapse during the dry season when system demand peaks. See storage-deficit and seasonal-mismatch. Tamor Storage, at 200 MW with a reservoir, would provide firm, dispatchable capacity to the eastern grid — the kind of generation that can be scheduled to meet evening peak demand in the dry winter months. This is fundamentally different from the run-of-river model where output is at the mercy of seasonal hydrology. # Development Hurdles Storage projects in Nepal face three compounding challenges: 1. Geology: Dam sites in the young, seismically active Himalaya require extensive investigation 2. Resettlement: Reservoirs displace communities, creating political and social costs that run-of-river projects avoid 3. Financing: Storage projects have higher capital costs and longer construction timelines, requiring a PPA framework that compensates firm capacity — not just energy (kWh) The ERC's 2026 directive on reservoir-based power plants attempts to address this by transitioning large projects to a cost-plus determination model, explicitly allowing developers to factor in loan interest, depreciation, and foreign exchange risk. See nea-triple-authority. # System Context In a system increasingly saturated with monsoon-heavy run-of-river generation, Tamor Storage's value lies not in its annual energy contribution but in its seasonal firmness. A 200 MW reservoir that can deliver into the dry-season evening peak would have outsized system value compared to a 200 MW run-of-river project that adds to the monsoon surplus. See firm-power and solar-hydro-complementarity for the integrated portfolio argument. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 200 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Tamor (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Panchthar | Province | Province No 1 | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower storage-deficit firm-power

pages.title: Tanahu Hydropower Project
pages.excerpt: A 140 MW storage-type reservoir scheme on the Seti River — the only large dam-and-reservoir project actually in active construction in Nepal, and therefore the single most important test case for whether the country can execute storage-deficit-closing assets at all. ADB-financed,
pages.body_text: Tanahu Hydropower Project A 140 MW storage-type reservoir scheme on the Seti River — the only large dam-and-reservoir project actually in active construction in Nepal, and therefore the single most important test case for whether the country can execute storage-deficit-closing assets at all. ADB-financed, NEA-owned, ~67% complete as of FY 2024/25, and shadowed by two Accountability Mechanism complaints over land and buffer-zone compensation. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 140 MW | Type | Reservoir (storage) | Status | ~67% complete (NEA FY 2024/25), Dec 2024 ADB monitoring ongoing | River | Seti (gandaki-basin) | District | Tanahu | Main dam | 140 m high roller-compacted concrete | Powerhouse | Underground | Owner | nea | Lead financier | Asian Development Bank | # Significance Tanahu matters less for its nameplate 140 MW and more as proof of concept — is Nepal capable of delivering a large reservoir project at all, on a reasonable timeline, with multilateral financing? Until Tanahu commissions, the only operating storage plant in the country is kulekhani-cascade (106 MW). Tanahu will nearly triple the storage fleet on day one. The strategic implication cascades upward. If Tanahu completes, dudhkoshi-storage (670 MW) and budhigandaki (1200 MW) become easier to finance: the next multilateral conversation no longer begins "Nepal has never actually built a large reservoir since 1982." If Tanahu fails — especially on the social-compliance dimension the Accountability Mechanism is reviewing — future large storage proposals will carry that memory as a risk premium. Compare Tanahu (140 MW, reservoir, ADB, domestic) to arun-3 (900 MW, peaking RoR, SJVN-India, export-oriented). Both are under construction but solve different problems: Tanahu addresses dry-season firm-power for Nepal; Arun-3 adds monsoon export capacity to India. Nepal's structural energy problem is the former, not the latter. # Limitations & Controversies Social-compliance complaints. The 2024 and 2025 Accountability Mechanism filings are the visible surface of a harder question: can a large reservoir in the middle hills be executed with buffer-zone demarcation, untitled-land registration, and free prior informed consent done properly? Current complaints flag all three. ADB accepted the 2025 complaint for eligibility review in April. Schedule discipline. 67% physical progress after ~10 years of construction is slower than comparable multilateral-financed projects elsewhere. Each additional year compounds NEA's dry-season firm-power exposure, since the entire medium-term planning rationale for Tanahu depends on it arriving before the seasonal-mismatch pressure peaks. Not a basin-scale answer. Even at full output, Tanahu cannot close the dry-season gap on its own. It is one node in a portfolio that would need Dudh Koshi Storage, Budhi Gandaki or similar scale to operate alongside. Framing Tanahu as "the storage solution" sets up disappointment; framing it as "the first storage receipt" sets up a reasonable expectation. # Official Visual References Tanahu's current public photo trail is still messy from a reuse and licensing standpoint, but the project overview deck circulated by Tanahu Hydropower Limited in 2016 does include two official visuals that are materially better than generic Seti River scenery. Project location map from the THL overview deck. General layout slide from the same deck. Source: An Overview of Tanahu Hydropower Project (PDF) # Related storage-deficit — what Tanahu begins to address buildability — Tanahu's progress suggests storage is difficult but not impossible kulekhani-cascade — the only current storage asset firm-power dudhkoshi-storage budhigandaki # Map Assets Nepal Tributary Explorer Cross-Border View With Projects data-map-inventory

pages.title: Tingla 132/33 kV Substation
pages.excerpt: A 132/33 kV, 30 MVA substation commissioned as part of the Solu corridor build. In the annual-book snapshot, Tingla is the bridge intended to connect Solu basin IPP output into the larger 132 kV system once the Mirchaiya–Tingla line is complete.
pages.body_text: Tingla 132/33 kV Substation A 132/33 kV, 30 MVA substation commissioned as part of the Solu corridor build. In the annual-book snapshot, Tingla is the bridge intended to connect Solu basin IPP output into the larger 132 kV system once the Mirchaiya–Tingla line is complete. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Book page 45 states that Tingla substation (132/33 kV, 30 MVA) was commissioned on Bhadra 07, 2076, and identifies it as part of the Solu Corridor 132 kV transmission line project. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132/33 kV | Capacity | 30 MVA | Status in 2020 book | Commissioned | Corridor | Solu Corridor 132 kV | Upstream line noted | Mirchaiya–Tingla 132 kV double circuit, ~90 km | # Why It Matters Tingla is a concrete example of the smaller corridor substations that had to exist before generation in eastern hill basins could actually reach the grid. It belongs in the same “transmission before value” story as claim-transmission-immediate-blocker and the later nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 synthesis. # See also solu solu-khola-dudha-koshi claim-transmission-immediate-blocker nepal-transmission-landscape-2025

pages.title: Trishuli Cascade
pages.excerpt: The Trishuli River is Nepal's most heavily developed hydropower corridor, benefiting from proximity to Kathmandu's load center and well-established transmission infrastructure. Starting from the original 1967 Trishuli plant, the corridor has accumulated layers of development acro
pages.body_text: Trishuli Cascade The Trishuli River is Nepal's most heavily developed hydropower corridor, benefiting from proximity to Kathmandu's load center and well-established transmission infrastructure. Starting from the original 1967 Trishuli plant, the corridor has accumulated layers of development across different licensing eras, sponsors, and ownership models (NEA-owned, subsidiary, and private IPP). # Plants in the Cascade Plant | Capacity (MW) | Status | Ownership | -------|--------------|--------|-----------| Upper Trishuli-I Cascade HEP | 24.6 | Survey | — | upper-trishuli-1 | 216 | Under construction | IPP | upper-trishuli-3a | 60 | Under construction | NEA | upper-trishuli-3b | 37 | Under construction | Subsidiary (Trishuli Jal Vidyut Co.) | trishuli-galchhi | 75 | Under construction | IPP | super-trishuli | 100 | Under construction | IPP | trishuli (Devighat) | 24 | Operating | NEA | Trishuli Khola | 4.4 | Survey | — | Total | ~540.6 | | | # Corridor Significance The Trishuli-Gandaki corridor is the backbone of central Nepal's generation. Projects in this corridor supply directly to Kathmandu's load center via the shortest transmission paths in the country. The corridor road (Pasang Lhamu Highway / Prithvi Highway) provides construction access, and the established marsyangdi-upper-220 and trishuli-galchhi transmission infrastructure provides evacuation. A persistent analytical challenge: upper-trishuli-3b (37 MW, subsidiary) is frequently conflated with upper-trishuli-3a (60 MW, NEA-owned) — they are separate assets with different promoters and PPAs on adjacent reaches of the Trishuli. # Concentration Risk The corridor's density concentrates systemic risk: geological events, sediment surges, or flow alterations from upstream projects cascade through the entire system. The September 2024 floods demonstrated how a single extreme event can affect multiple plants simultaneously. # Related Corridors The Trishuli River is a tributary of the Narayani/Gandaki system (see gandaki-basin). The adjacent Bhote Koshi corridor (rasuwagadhi, madhya-bhotekoshi, upper-bhotekoshi) shares transmission infrastructure and Chilime-group sponsorship patterns with several Trishuli projects via the chilime-trishuli-220kv-transmission-line. # See also upper-trishuli-3a upper-trishuli-3b — frequently confused with 3A upper-trishuli-1 super-trishuli trishuli trishuli-galchhi gandaki-basin chilime-trishuli-220kv-transmission-line

pages.title: Udipur-Damauli-Bharatpur 220 kV Reinforcement
pages.excerpt: The Udipur-Damauli-Bharatpur 220 kV reinforcement is the lower Marsyangdi evacuation route from the Udipur area toward Bharatpur/Aptari. In the map it is represented as the source-backed Udipur-Markichowk-Bharatpur line because that is the route basis stated in the recovered NEA
pages.body_text: Udipur-Damauli-Bharatpur 220 kV Reinforcement The Udipur-Damauli-Bharatpur 220 kV reinforcement is the lower Marsyangdi evacuation route from the Udipur area toward Bharatpur/Aptari. In the map it is represented as the source-backed Udipur-Markichowk-Bharatpur line because that is the route basis stated in the recovered NEA Marsyangdi RAP. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 220 kV | Status | Under construction | Corridor ID | | Public map feature | | Source basis | NEA Marsyangdi RAP pages 22-24 | Official route length | 64.45 km | Traced network length | 65.311 km | Validation delta | +1.34% | # Map Interpretation This corridor is now represented by a single RAP-backed trace in the connected transmission layer. It replaces the older RPGCL overview extraction, which showed the same corridor as many disconnected fragments. The trace should be read as a corridor-level alignment, not a tower-by-tower engineering route. The RAP text gives 64.45 route-km, while the project map labels the same Udipur-Markichowk-Bharatpur line as 67 km. The current map trace falls inside that source envelope. # Confidence Public decision: default-visible, high confidence. The confidence comes from three checks: the route is backed by a project RAP, the public feature has no inferred connectors, and the traced length is within 2% of the RAP route-length basis. # Caveats Khudi-Udipur is not forced into this corridor. It should remain a separate scope item unless a route-grade source confirms that it belongs in this segment rather than in the upper Marsyangdi package. # See also marsyangdi-upper-220 mca-central-400 data-layer-transmission-connected-traced-network claim-transmission-immediate-blocker

pages.title: Upper Arun Hydroelectric Project
pages.excerpt: ERT survey at IkhuwaKhola HPP (IKHPP) Core Drilling at JawaTila
pages.body_text: Upper Arun Hydroelectric Project ERT survey at IkhuwaKhola HPP (IKHPP) Core Drilling at JawaTila An Upper Arun scheme in Sankhuwasabha that the FY 2019/20 annual book presents as one of NEA's major study-to-development priorities, with route access, geology, detailed engineering preparation, and 3D scheme visualization all still in progress. [!cite] nea-transmission-annual-book-2077 Across book pages 113–116, NEA frames Upper Arun as a priority project with access-road work, feasibility optimization, project-location mapping, and draft engineering preparation still underway. # Why It Matters Upper Arun is a good example of how long the pipeline is between identifying a large Himalayan project and reaching bankable execution. It belongs with arun-3 and nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 because any future Upper Arun build would need both project delivery and corridor evacuation to line up. # See also arun-3 nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 nea

pages.title: Upper Bhotekoshi
pages.excerpt: Upper Bhotekoshi is a 45 MW operating project on the Bhote Koshi in Sindhupalchok. It is pinned in the data-layer-priority-watchlist not because it is large, but because it is a useful reality-check marker: an existing high-gradient Koshi-basin plant where the river valley, opera
pages.body_text: Upper Bhotekoshi Upper Bhotekoshi is a 45 MW operating project on the Bhote Koshi in Sindhupalchok. It is pinned in the data-layer-priority-watchlist not because it is large, but because it is a useful reality-check marker: an existing high-gradient Koshi-basin plant where the river valley, operating status, and project geography are legible. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 45 MW | Status | Operating | River | Bhote Koshi (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Sindhupalchok | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # Why It Matters Upper Bhotekoshi is part of the operating hydro fleet that grounds the wiki's map layers in real assets rather than only future projects. It shows the classic Himalayan run-of-river logic: steep relief, compact valley geometry, and generation that depends on river flow rather than large seasonal storage. Its watchlist role is therefore methodological. When the map displays dozens of survey-stage records, Upper Bhotekoshi is one of the markers that helps calibrate what an operating point looks like in the source data. # Status And Timing The project is already operating. There is no construction-start or finish-date issue to track in this layer context. The relevant updates are operational performance, outage history, flood/sediment exposure, and how Bhote Koshi valley generation connects into the eastern grid. # Map Interpretation The marker is an operating-project registry point near the mapped river and is labeled as a higher-confidence site point in the priority-watchlist layer. # Registry note Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset # See also koshi-basin run-of-river-hydropower stranded-generation data-layer-priority-watchlist

pages.title: Upper Karnali
pages.excerpt: A 900 MW run-of-river scheme on the Karnali, licensed to India's GMR Group since 2008 and still in survey / pre-construction 18 years later. Upper Karnali is the canonical example of an India-developed Nepali hydropower project that has not been built: repeatedly extended PDA dea
pages.body_text: Upper Karnali A 900 MW run-of-river scheme on the Karnali, licensed to India's GMR Group since 2008 and still in survey / pre-construction 18 years later. Upper Karnali is the canonical example of an India-developed Nepali hydropower project that has not been built: repeatedly extended PDA deadlines, stalled financial closure, a Supreme Court that finally refused open-ended extensions, and a Bangladesh export PPA that has never actually closed. In 2026 the government cleared a new path for financial closure — the first credible forward motion in more than a decade. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 900 MW | Status | Survey / pre-construction (as of 2026, financial closure approaching) | Type | Run-of-river | River | Karnali (karnali-basin) | Districts | Surkhet, Dailekh, Achham (registry lists Surkhet) | Province | Karnali Pradesh | Sponsor | GMR Upper Karnali Hydropower Limited | Design annual energy | ~3,466 GWh | Planned export share | ~88% | Licence awarded | 2008, via international competitive bidding | Equity (2026) | GMR + SJVN + IREDA + NEA | # Significance Upper Karnali is the twin case to arun-3 — same licence era, similar capacity, same India-developer / export-first model — but with the opposite outcome. Arun-3 is at 74% physical progress; Upper Karnali has been at "about to reach financial closure" for a decade. Reading the two projects together is how you understand the variance in Nepal's India-developed export class: the contract is not the product, execution is. The project also marks how hydro-geopolitics has widened in Nepal's hydropower conversation. The original export target was India, but a Bangladesh PPA has been live in some form since 2017 — 250 MW, 300 MW, 500 MW and 200 MW figures have all been quoted at different moments. Bangladesh is the canonical "second Indian neighbour" in Nepali energy strategy, and Upper Karnali is the project that has been asked to test that market opening first. None of the PPAs have yet actually closed. Finally, the 2026 financing stack — IREDA, SJVN and Nepali banks stepping in alongside GMR — is a pattern worth noting: Indian state-owned entities are now effectively taking equity in Nepali projects their original sponsor could not alone finance. That is a structural change in what "India-developed" means, and Upper Karnali is the first at-scale instance. # Limitations & Controversies Eighteen years without a turbine. The 2008 licence award predates the current nea grid code in some dimensions; deadlines have been extended repeatedly. The Supreme Court ultimately ruled against open-ended extensions, granting only a 186-day allowance for time lost under its own interim orders — a signal that the judiciary no longer accepts the PDA-extension cycle as routine. Bangladesh transmission dependency. Even if a final PPA closes, the 200–500 MW export to Bangladesh depends on a Nepal–India– Bangladesh transmission corridor that is still under development. Upper Karnali's export model contains an unresolved infrastructure prerequisite that is not on its balance sheet but is on its critical path. Downstream irrigation overhang. A 2014 high-level committee raised concerns about impacts on downstream irrigation in India's Ganga system. That objection was never fully resolved, only procedurally managed, and resurfaces in each PDA extension debate. GMR Group's execution posture. Unlike SJVN, GMR is a private Indian conglomerate whose project pipeline has shifted substantially since 2008. Whether GMR remains the right primary sponsor — now that SJVN and IREDA are in the equity stack — is an open strategic question that Nepal has not formally asked. # Official Visual Source Gap Upper Karnali is still unusually thin on stable public document figures. The Investment Board Nepal project and factsheet pages remain visible in search indexes, but the live page and factsheet download currently resolve to 404s, while the active GMR project page exposes basic project text without a reusable layout plate or site figure. This page therefore avoids generic valley scenery in both the top strip and body until a durable brochure, E&S disclosure, or layout PDF is recovered from the official source stack. Tracked official references: Investment Board Nepal project page Investment Board Nepal factsheet page GMR Upper Karnali project page # See also karnali-basin arun-3 hydro-geopolitics stranded-generation run-of-river-hydropower mugu-karnali-storage

pages.title: Upper Marsyangdi A
pages.excerpt: Upper Marsyangdi A is a 50 MW operating run-of-river hydropower project on the Marsyangdi River. As one of the earlier projects in the Marsyangdi cascade, it provides operational data that informs the financial models of newer projects in the same river system.
pages.body_text: Upper Marsyangdi A Upper Marsyangdi A is a 50 MW operating run-of-river hydropower project on the Marsyangdi River. As one of the earlier projects in the Marsyangdi cascade, it provides operational data that informs the financial models of newer projects in the same river system. # Marsyangdi Corridor Upper Marsyangdi A is the upstream anchor of the Marsyangdi cascade, which includes Madhya Marsyangdi (70 MW, operating) and Marsyangdi Besi (50 MW, under construction). As the uppermost project, its operations — particularly sediment management and flow diversion — affect downstream plants. The cascade's cumulative water use and environmental flow requirements are increasingly significant as more capacity comes online in the Marsyangdi valley. # Learning Curve Asset Projects like Upper Marsyangdi A serve as benchmarks for the cost and performance assumptions embedded in newer developments. Key questions: actual build cost vs. initial budget, PLF achieved vs. contracted energy, maintenance cost trajectory over 10–20 years, and the financial impact of sedimentation on turbine efficiency over time. These operational data points are what separate the Chilime-style post-COD IPO success stories from projects that delivered disappointing realized returns. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 50 MW | Status | Operating | River | Marsyangdi (gandaki-basin) | District | Lamjung | Province | Gandaki Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower

pages.title: Upper Tamakoshi
pages.excerpt: Permanent Camp Facility
pages.body_text: Upper Tamakoshi Permanent Camp Facility Overall Project Layout Nepal's largest operating hydropower plant at 456 MW. Located on the Tama Koshi River in Dolakha district. Commissioned in 2021. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 456 MW | Type | Run-of-River | Gross head | 822 m | Headrace tunnel | 7.2 km | River | Tama Koshi (eastern koshi-basin) | # Significance Upper Tamakoshi is the plant that broke Nepal's load-shedding era. Its commissioning, combined with other IPP additions and improved import contracts, brought load shedding from 16 hours/day (2015–16) to near-zero by 2022. It represents ~16% of total installed hydro capacity on its own. It is also the archetype of Nepal's dominant engineering form: high-head diversion scheme with no significant dam. River drops 822 meters naturally; the engineering concentrates it through tunnels and penstocks to an underground powerhouse. # Limitations As a RoR plant, Upper Tamakoshi's output mirrors the seasonal hydrograph. It contributes heavily to monsoon surplus but cannot provide firm-power in dry season. It exemplifies why adding more RoR amplifies both surplus and deficit. See seasonal-mismatch. # Financial Performance Upper Tamakoshi was built at a total cost of approximately NPR 87 billion (~NPR 19–20 Crore per MW), financed roughly 75% by debt [CONFIRM YEAR FROM UKHLL ANNUAL REPORT: figures from UKHLL Annual Report FY 2079/80-2080/81]. Metric | Value | Source | ---|---|---| Total project cost | ~NPR 87B (NPR 19–20 Cr/MW) | UKHLL annual report | Equity base | ~NPR 21.18B (~25% of capital) | UKHLL annual report | FY 2079/80 revenue | ~NPR 8.68B (NPR 1.90 Cr/MW) | UKHLL audited accounts | FY 2079/80 net loss | ~NPR 2.52B (~29% of revenue) | UKHLL audited accounts | Interest expense | ~NPR 6.68B (~77% of revenue) | UKHLL audited accounts | First-year PPA tariff | ~NPR 3.74/kWh wet, ~6.96/kWh dry | UKHLL disclosures | Generation vs contracted | ~65% of contracted 2,281 GWh/yr [UNVERIFIED] | Estimated from revenue data | At current PPA rates, Upper Tamakoshi is not breaking even. Combined costs (depreciation, interest, O&M) consume more than total revenue. Debt service obligations (~NPR 14.2 billion including principal) dramatically exceed sales. ## Structural implication The gap between revenue and debt service at Upper Tamakoshi is not a commissioning-phase issue that will resolve with time. At its current PPA rate — which is among the lowest in the sector — the project's revenue cannot cover its interest, let alone principal. To break even by year 15, estimates suggest it would require an effective tariff roughly 2–3 times higher than current levels. See ppa-pricing. Upper Tamakoshi is the most prominent case of the structural dynamic explored in nea-triple-authority: the same institution (NEA) that is the project's sole buyer also sets its PPA rate, and the rate does not reflect the cost of capital. It also exemplifies the q-design-discharge problem: the project was sized on hydrology assumptions that the river is not delivering. Actual generation has tracked well below contracted energy, with water flow during Spring 2023 collapsing to 45.12% of the expectations derived from historical hydrological studies. See q-design-discharge for the full generation performance comparison.

pages.title: Upper Tamor
pages.excerpt: Upper Tamor is a 285 MW hydropower project under construction on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal — one of the largest private/IPP projects in the under-construction pipeline.
pages.body_text: Upper Tamor Upper Tamor is a 285 MW hydropower project under construction on the Tamor River in eastern Nepal — one of the largest private/IPP projects in the under-construction pipeline. # Tamor Basin Significance The Tamor River is a major tributary of the Koshi system, draining the eastern Himalayan slopes of the Kanchenjunga massif. Unlike the Bhote Koshi and Trishuli corridors closer to Kathmandu, the Tamor basin has historically seen less hydropower development due to its distance from load centers and transmission constraints. Upper Tamor, at 285 MW, represents a step-change in eastern Nepal's generation capacity. # Development Context The Tamor River system has attracted attention for both run-of-river and storage development potential. Upper Tamor sits alongside Tamor Storage (200 MW, survey stage) in the project pipeline. Together, these projects would fundamentally reshape the eastern grid — but also require significant transmission investment to evacuate power from the Tamor valley to the national backbone. # Financing Challenge At 285 MW, Upper Tamor's capital requirements place it in the top tier of Nepali private hydro projects. The project's financial viability will depend on achieving competitive build costs, managing construction risk in a relatively remote location, and securing transmission evacuation — all within the standard NPR 4.80/8.40 PPA framework. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 285 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Tamor (eastern koshi-basin) | District | Taplejung | Province | Province No 1 | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability

pages.title: Upper Trishuli-1
pages.excerpt: Upper Trishuli-1 is a 216 MW generation-licence / under-construction-stage project on the Trishuli in Rasuwa. It is pinned in the data-layer-priority-watchlist because it sits in the central-north Trishuli corridor, where hydropower value depends directly on evacuation capacity i
pages.body_text: Upper Trishuli-1 Upper Trishuli-1 is a 216 MW generation-licence / under-construction-stage project on the Trishuli in Rasuwa. It is pinned in the data-layer-priority-watchlist because it sits in the central-north Trishuli corridor, where hydropower value depends directly on evacuation capacity into the Kathmandu and wider national grid. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 216 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Trishuli (gandaki-basin) | District | Rasuwa | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # Why It Matters Upper Trishuli-1 is not a top-10 capacity project, but it is strategically important because of location. Trishuli projects sit close to the country's central load geography and to transmission corridors that matter immediately for domestic reliability. A 216 MW project in this corridor can be more system-relevant than a larger but remote survey marker if it reaches commissioning and evacuates cleanly. The project also helps explain why the priority watchlist is not just a biggest-MW layer. Upper Trishuli-1 is included for corridor and buildout relevance. # Status And Timing The generated spec classifies the project as under construction / generation licence. The IPSDP milestone ladder places Upper Trishuli-1 in the ~2025 project bucket, alongside other near-term projects such as Rasuwagadhi, Sanjen, Upper Trishuli 3B, Madhya Bhotekoshi, and Arun 3. The practical watch items are physical progress, commissioning schedule, grid-connection readiness, and whether output is constrained by the central transmission network. # Map Interpretation The marker is a river-aligned project reference derived from the registry point. It is suitable for corridor reading, but not a final engineering coordinate. # Registry note Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset # See also gandaki-basin stranded-generation data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-priority-watchlist

pages.title: Upper Trishuli 3B
pages.excerpt: Upper Trishuli 3B is a 37 MW run-of-river hydropower project in the Trishuli cascade, developed under Trishuli Jal Vidyut Company Limited — a subsidiary corporate model distinct from the NEA's directly-owned Trishuli assets.
pages.body_text: Upper Trishuli 3B Upper Trishuli 3B is a 37 MW run-of-river hydropower project in the Trishuli cascade, developed under Trishuli Jal Vidyut Company Limited — a subsidiary corporate model distinct from the NEA's directly-owned Trishuli assets. # Distinction from Trishuli 3A A persistent confusion in sector research conflates Upper Trishuli 3B (37 MW) with Upper Trishuli 3A (60 MW). The distinction is critical: Trishuli 3A (60 MW) is wholly owned, constructed, and operated directly by the NEA as an internal Generation Directorate asset. It has no commercial PPA — electricity is dispatched via internal transfer pricing based on cost of generation. Trishuli 3B (37 MW) is developed under a subsidiary corporate model and holds a standard commercial PPA at NPR 4.80/kWh (wet) and NPR 8.40/kWh (dry), consistent with the prevailing IPP tariff schedule. # Trishuli Cascade Dynamics Upper Trishuli 3B is part of the larger Trishuli River cascade that includes multiple projects at different stages — from the operating Trishuli Devighat plant to the NEA-owned Upper Trishuli 3A (60 MW), the subsidiary Trishuli 3B (37 MW), and Super Trishuli (100 MW, under construction). The cascade's cumulative impact on Trishuli River flows, sediment transport, and downstream water availability makes coordination across entity boundaries important for system-level planning. # Build Cost Context Industry analysis notes that projects in the Trishuli corridor, including Upper Trishuli 3B, have faced build costs exceeding NPR 200–250 million per MW — significantly above the NPR 172–175 million/MW benchmark set by optimal projects like Sahas Urja (Solu Khola, 86 MW). # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 37 MW | Status | Under construction (Generation licence) | River | Trishuli (gandaki-basin) | District | Rasuwa | Province | Bagmati Pradesh | # See Also run-of-river-hydropower buildability ppa-data-retrieval-icra-care-2026

pages.title: Uttarganga Storage Hydropower Project
pages.excerpt: Uttarganga is an 828 MW storage hydropower project in Baglung promoted by NEA. It is pinned in the data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects layer because it is the fourth-largest capacity marker in the display set and one of the few top-10 records that is explicitly a storage project.
pages.body_text: Uttarganga Storage Hydropower Project Uttarganga is an 828 MW storage hydropower project in Baglung promoted by NEA. It is pinned in the data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects layer because it is the fourth-largest capacity marker in the display set and one of the few top-10 records that is explicitly a storage project. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 828 MW | Status | Survey / study | River | Uttar Ganga | District | Baglung | Province | Gandaki Pradesh | Promoter | Nepal Electricity Authority | # Why It Matters Uttarganga matters because it combines two properties that rarely overlap in Nepal's project stack: very large capacity and storage. Most top-10 capacity markers are survey-stage run-of-river or peaking run-of-river concepts. Uttarganga is a direct part of the storage-deficit question. Its location in Gandaki Province also makes it a counterweight to the Karnali-heavy top-10 list. If it advanced, it would strengthen the western-central storage portfolio rather than adding another remote Karnali mainstem marker. # Status And Timing The current map layer treats Uttarganga as survey / study stage. NEA annual-book material provides official visual references for the project site and proposed reservoir area, but the wiki evidence set does not confirm physical construction. The IPSDP milestone ladder groups Uttarganga STO in the 2031-2035 project window. That is the best planning-window anchor currently in the wiki. # Map Interpretation The marker is a river-aligned project reference derived from the registry point. Use it for strategic comparison, not for final dam-axis or reservoir-boundary analysis. # Registry note Naxa / DoED-linked public hydropower project dataset # Official Visual References NEA's annual-book material is currently the strongest accessible official visual source for Uttarganga. The project-company pages carry both a site-visit image and a second page showing the proposed reservoir area alongside core dam, tunnel and powerhouse parameters. NEA annual-book page covering the project company and a high-level visit to the site. NEA annual-book page showing the proposed reservoir area and project description. Source: Local workspace copy of NEA transmission annual book 2077 (PDF) # See also buildability storage-deficit data-ipsdp-milestone-ladder-2022-2040 data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects

pages.title: Water and Energy Commission Secretariat (WECS)
pages.excerpt: Nepal's government planning body for water and energy. Produces the authoritative basin plans, potential assessments, and energy balances that form the data backbone of this research.
pages.body_text: Water and Energy Commission Secretariat (WECS) Nepal's government planning body for water and energy. Produces the authoritative basin plans, potential assessments, and energy balances that form the data backbone of this research. # Key Publications wecs-river-basin-plan-2024 — Basin-level discharge, seasonal profiles, strategic planning wecs-hydropower-potential-2019 — Revised national hydropower potential (72,544 MW gross / 32,680 MW techno-economic) wecs-energy-synopsis-2024 — Full energy balance showing grid electricity as only 4.96% of final energy # Role in Sector WECS handles theoretical planning while nea handles operational execution. The Department of Electricity Development (DoED) manages licensing between them. This fragmented institutional structure is part of the governance weakness identified across the evidence base. # Related nepal-energy-profile — the system WECS plans for hydropower-potential-categories — WECS provides the definitive numbers

pages.title: West Seti Hydroelectric Project
pages.excerpt: Figure 6.3-2 River Longitudinal Profile of the West Seti River
pages.body_text: West Seti Hydroelectric Project Figure 6.3-2 River Longitudinal Profile of the West Seti River A long-stalled reservoir storage project in Sudurpashchim Province — the most prominent example of how Chinese withdrawal and project handoff have shaped Nepal's hydro-geopolitics. # Specifications Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Capacity | 750–800 MW (varying by source) | Type | Conventional storage (reservoir) | Dam | 195 m concrete-faced rockfill, 433 m long | Location | Seti River, Bajhang/Baitadi/Doti/Dadeldhura districts | Affected areas | 16 wards across 8 local units | # Timeline of Stalling Year | Event | ------|-------| ~1980s | Project conceived | 2001–2011 | SMEC (Australia) held license; 17 years, limited progress | 2012 | Awarded to China Three Gorges Corporation (CTG) | 2018 (Sep) | CTG withdrew — cited geological issues, high altitude, resettlement difficulty. Rejected Nepal's proposal to reduce capacity from 750 to 620 MW | 2022 (Sep) | Transferred to India's NHPC Limited | 2025 (Oct) | Public hearings begun for Environmental Impact Assessment | # Current Status (as of late 2025) NHPC (India) is conducting EIA public hearings across affected areas Detailed project report is being updated An associated project — SR-6 (450 MW) — has been proposed below the confluence of Seti River and Budhiganga, in Doti/Achham districts, with a 207 m dam and potential 652 MW capacity No construction timeline announced # Why China Left CTG's withdrawal was not purely technical. The project illustrates the limits of Chinese hydropower engagement in Nepal: 1. Geological and altitude risk — genuine engineering concerns in far-west Nepal 2. Resettlement complexity — Nepal lacked a clear R&R framework 3. Commercial terms — Nepal proposed PPA payment in USD (unusual for Nepal), and CTG rejected a capacity reduction 4. Geopolitical signaling — India's sensitivity to Chinese infrastructure in border-adjacent areas created an unfavorable operating environment # Significance West Seti, alongside budhigandaki's history of Chinese-then-domestic pivots, represents a pattern: Chinese entities engage with Nepal's large storage projects but do not complete them. India then steps in (NHPC for West Seti, domestic for Budhigandaki) — but progress remains slow. The net result is decades lost on storage projects that would directly address storage-deficit. # Related hydro-geopolitics — the China-India triangulation storage-deficit — what West Seti would help solve budhigandaki — parallel trajectory of Chinese withdrawal, domestic/Indian pivot pancheshwar — another stalled storage mega-project, for different reasons

pages.title: Western Nepal 132 kV Backbone
pages.excerpt: The operational 132 kV Terai-side grid chain that carries the map west of Butwal: Butwal-Shivapur-Lamahi-Kohalpur, Kohalpur-Bhurigaun-Lamki, and Lamki-Pahalwanpur-Attariya-Mahendranagar.
pages.body_text: Western Nepal 132 kV Backbone The operational 132 kV Terai-side grid chain that carries the map west of Butwal: Butwal-Shivapur-Lamahi-Kohalpur, Kohalpur-Bhurigaun-Lamki, and Lamki-Pahalwanpur-Attariya-Mahendranagar. # Key Facts Parameter | Value | -----------|-------| Voltage | 132 kV | Status | Operational | Length basis | 404.89 route-km / 809.78 circuit-km | Source geometry | RPGCL 2021 official geospatial vector map | Status/length basis | NEA FY 2024/25 existing high-voltage line inventory | Corridor ID | | # Map Interpretation This corridor is now in the major transmission network because otherwise western Nepal looks emptier than it is. It is not a 400 kV export spine or a 220 kV bulk-transfer corridor; it is the operational 132 kV backbone that keeps Lumbini, Banke, Bardiya, Kailali, and Kanchanpur connected across the west. Current validation result: 3 features, 0 inferred connectors, 0 remaining endpoint gaps, and 418.149 km traced length against a 404.89 route-km basis. Public decision: default-visible, caveated. # Caveats The route is source-backed but national-scale. It comes from RPGCL vector linework and NEA inventory control, not a tower-by-tower alignment sheet. It should be read as the western grid-reach backbone, with lighter visual weight than 220/400 kV lines. # See also data-layer-transmission-connected-traced-network chameliya-attariya-132 kohalpur-surkhet-dailekh-132 dana-kushma-butwal-corridor gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection

pages.title: AD Penalties
pages.excerpt: Availability Declaration (AD) penalties are financial deductions levied by NEA against hydropower producers when actual generation falls below the availability the producer declared in advance. The AD penalty mechanism is the primary financial instrument through which the nea-tri
pages.body_text: AD Penalties Availability Declaration (AD) penalties are financial deductions levied by NEA against hydropower producers when actual generation falls below the availability the producer declared in advance. The AD penalty mechanism is the primary financial instrument through which the nea-triple-authority conflict operates. # The Penalty Mechanism The Availability Declaration system requires hydropower projects to declare their expected generation output in advance. When actual generation falls below the declared threshold, the project incurs a financial penalty deducted directly from its trade receivables owed by NEA. Verified penalty formula: Penalty = (0.8 × Contracted Energy − Actual Output) × Tariff The 0.8 threshold means a project must achieve at least 80% of its contracted energy output before penalties cease. Any shortfall below this threshold is penalized at the full per-unit tariff rate on the gap. Penalty destination: Penalties are deducted directly from the project's NEA trade receivables. They do not flow to an independent regulatory fund or system stability pool. NEA retains the deducted amount. [REQUIRES EXACT CITATION: statutory confirmation of destination mechanism — from PPA clause or ERC regulation] Revenue impact: Industry estimates suggest AD penalties can consume up to 16% of project revenue in severe cases. [UNVERIFIED: no statutory cap or aggregate data source confirmed. Retained as severity anchor pending verification.] The standard AD system requires generators to declare their available generation 30 days in advance for projects above 10 MW. The ERC formally acknowledged this burden for small projects, amending by-laws (ERC Bylaws 2076) to allow plants under 10 MW to declare availability one week in advance and exempting them from financial damages. See nea-triple-authority for the institutional framework. # The Barahi Hydropower Case Barahi Hydropower Public Limited is the best-documented case of the AD penalty mechanism overriding its own exemption clause. Barahi is a sub-10 MW run-of-river project. The Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC) Bylaws 2076, Clause 8(2) and Schedule 5, explicitly exempt projects below 10 MW from AD penalties. Despite this exemption, NEA deducted penalty amounts for short supply from Barahi's invoices. Out of Rs 14.9 million in trade receivables, Barahi's management was forced to classify the deducted penalties as "receivable from NEA" in audited financial statements — publicly stating that NEA's actions contradict the prevailing ERC By-laws. Because NEA controls cash disbursement, the IPP carries the deducted penalty on its balance sheet while the ERC exemption exists on paper with no enforcement mechanism. The independent arbiter is absent. Project: Barahi Hydropower Public Limited Capacity: sub-10 MW (RoR) Regulatory protection: ERC Bylaws 2076, Clause 8(2), Schedule 5 Penalty deducted: part of Rs 14.9M in receivables Accounting treatment: "receivable from NEA" in audited financial statements Source: Barahi Hydropower audited financial statements [UNVERIFIED: exact fiscal year to be confirmed] The Barahi case is significant precisely because it documents the penalty mechanism overriding its own exemption clause. The project recorded the deduction as "receivable from NEA" — a forced loan to the off-taker with no resolution timeline on record. See barahi-hydropower. # The Structural Impossibility For run-of-river projects without storage, predicting output 30 days in advance in a Himalayan monsoon basin is not an engineering failure — it is an inherently unsolvable forecasting problem. RoR projects, lacking storage reservoirs, are at the absolute mercy of daily river hydrology. Holding RoR developers financially liable for standard deviation in monsoon hydrology via punitive AD penalties is fundamentally irrational from an engineering and grid-design perspective. The ERC acknowledged this by exempting projects under 10 MW from 30-day declarations and financial damages. The structural impossibility for larger projects remains unaddressed in regulatory or judicial rulings. [Under research — no primary regulatory or judicial source found yet.] # The Curtailment-Penalty Double-Bind This section covers the scenario where NEA, acting as dispatcher, orders a project off-line — and then, acting as penalty administrator, fines it for failing to meet its availability declaration. Whether the PPA contains an explicit exemption clause protecting IPPs from penalties when the root cause of the generation shortfall was an NEA/NLDC curtailment instruction is the most important unresolved legal question in the wiki. Research to date has found no such clause, but primary PPA text has not been accessed. See nea-triple-authority for the full institutional analysis. [Under research — awaiting PPA clause text.] # Challenge Cases and Outcomes Beyond Barahi, no documented ERC arbitration rulings or court judgments on AD penalty disputes have been found in research passes to date. Any IPPAN legal challenges, ERC proceedings, or judicial outcomes concerning AD penalties should be documented here when located. [Under research.] # Analytical links nea-triple-authority — the institutional framework this mechanism operates within barahi-hydropower — primary audited case study q-design-discharge — how design discharge sets the contracted energy threshold that triggers penalties hydro-insurance — the insurance market failure that strips working capital during penalty periods

pages.title: Agrivoltaics and Land
headings: What agrivoltaics is, in Nepal-specific terms
Why it is the politically survivable path
The policy moves this concept implies
pages.excerpt: Module costs have collapsed, grid costs are middling, LCOE is already below small hydro (solar-lcoe-crossover). The binding constraint on the next 5–10 GW of Nepal solar is neither technology nor money. It is land. Specifically, it is the politics of converting Terai agricultural
pages.body_text: Agrivoltaics and Land Module costs have collapsed, grid costs are middling, LCOE is already below small hydro (solar-lcoe-crossover). The binding constraint on the next 5–10 GW of Nepal solar is neither technology nor money. It is land. Specifically, it is the politics of converting Terai agricultural land into ground-mounted solar at the scale the grid can absorb. This page argues that agrivoltaics is not a "nice to have" framing for Nepal; it is the only politically survivable at-scale configuration for Terai utility solar, and explains why. # The land arithmetic Utility-scale ground-mount solar needs ~2.5 ha per MWp at typical Nepal spacing (row-to-row, tilt, O&M lanes). Building 5 GW on fee-simple purchased land requires ~12,500 ha — roughly 0.6% of Nepal's total cultivated area, but concentrated in a narrow belt: Terai agricultural land: ~1.4 million ha Within 10 km of a 132+ kV substation in Terai: ~220,000 ha Non-forest, non-protected, slope < 3%, within that belt: ~160,000 ha Of which, publicly-owned (sarkari) or non-permanently-cultivated: ~5,000–15,000 ha (estimates vary; no single cadastral register) So the fee-simple, non-agricultural, grid-adjacent land available for 5 GW of Terai solar is roughly the land needed for 2–6 GW. Nothing past that is buildable without either (a) buying agricultural land from smallholders, or (b) finding a way to share the land with agriculture. Option (a) is a political non-starter at that scale. Nepal's hydropower-era displacement politics (Upper Tamakoshi, Budhigandaki, Pancheshwar) has produced a sensitised Terai farming public, distrustful of large land conversions, with active civil society and elected representation. Fee-simple conversion of rice paddy to PV modules — at the ten-district Terai scale — produces a food-security / landlessness fight that a government elected with an agricultural base cannot win and should not try. Option (b) is agrivoltaics. # What agrivoltaics is, in Nepal-specific terms The generic agrivoltaic definition — dual-use of land for crop production and PV generation, through elevated or spaced modules — covers a range of configurations: Configuration | Module height | Crop compatibility | Yield interaction | Capex multiplier | ---|---|---|---|---:| Low-mount vegetable rotation | 0.8–1.2 m | Leafy greens, onion, garlic, turmeric, shade-tolerant veg | +5 to −10% crop yield under partial shade | 1.0–1.1× | High-canopy rice / wheat | 3.0–4.5 m | Rice, wheat, maize (partial-shade-tolerant varieties) | −10 to −25% crop yield; tractor access preserved | 1.35–1.55× | Animal-grazing (agri-PV) | 1.2–1.8 m with fencing | Goat / sheep grazing under; moderates heat | Forage gains; livestock welfare up | 1.05–1.15× | Tree / plantation (orchard-PV) | 2.5–4.0 m | Mango, litchi, guava, banana | Microclimate benefit; water saving | 1.2–1.4× | Pond-edge / irrigation canal | 1.5–3.0 m over canal | Fish / aquaculture below | Reduces evaporation from canals | 1.1–1.3× | Two of these configurations fit Nepal's Terai farming calendar especially well: Leafy greens + low-mount PV on the 6-month paddy-to-fallow rotation. Paddy in monsoon (when PV yield is lowest anyway), leafy greens Oct–May under the modules (when PV yield is highest and the crop benefits from shade in the pre-monsoon heat). Ideal match. High-canopy PV over rice + wheat double-crop. Higher capex but preserves the dominant Terai land-use pattern; yield penalty of 10–20% on rice is offset by a PV revenue-share of NPR 40,000–80,000 per ropani per year, a 4–10× uplift on smallholder cash income. The economics of this are not marginal. # Why it is the politically survivable path A solar project structured as: 25-year lease (not purchase) of 1–5 ha from each participating smallholder, with a right of return at lease-end. Revenue share of a defined percentage of PPA receipts, indexed to inflation. Preserved agricultural use under / between modules, with the farmer as the agricultural tenant. Shared community-scale services — rural electrification credit, pump irrigation at cost, cold-storage co-development. …has a fundamentally different political valence than "buy the land for a solar farm." It resembles a successful cooperative more than a land grab. It mirrors the structure that made India's Gujarat and Maharashtra agrivoltaic deployments land-lightly, while the pure-purchase models in Rajasthan and Karnataka have had recurring land-conflict problems. It is also the structure most compatible with Nepal's cooperative-heavy rural economy (aepc-renewable-framework) and with the farmers' cooperative (krishak sahakari) networks that already operate at the 10–500-household scale across Terai. # What this concept is not saying Not that every Terai solar site must be agrivoltaic. Government-owned sarkari parcels, degraded / alkali land, oxbow scrub, industrial-zone-adjacent land, and hydro-co-location sites (hybrid-siting-logic) remain the first choice where available — perhaps the first 1.5–2 GW. Not that agrivoltaics automatically solves all politics. It changes the coalition, but requires specific contract drafting (lease-not-sale, revenue-share, return rights) that Nepal does not yet have template law for. Not that it is capex-free. The ~1.3–1.5× capex multiplier on high-canopy configurations is real and must be priced into tariffs. At current module prices, the agrivoltaic LCOE is still well below hydro PPA (solar-lcoe-crossover). # The policy moves this concept implies If the argument holds, Nepal needs four things it does not yet have: 1. A lease-template law for 20–25 year solar leases on agricultural land, separating usufruct transfer from fee-simple conversion. Without this, every agrivoltaic site is negotiated one-off, which kills scaling. 2. An agrivoltaic tariff adder — NPR 0.30–0.50/kWh uplift for dual-use configurations, conditional on post-build crop verification. Creates the capex-offset incentive. 3. A recognised agricultural-technical standard for minimum module height, row spacing, and crop-light requirements — Nepal currently has none. 4. A VGF / green-finance channel for the incremental capex — the eib-rural-solar-phase2 and wb-grid-solar-ee-project are the most natural hosts, but neither currently prices an agrivoltaic multiplier. Without these four, Terai utility solar hits the land wall at roughly 2 GW and stalls. With them, the same physical resource becomes a 10–15 GW build-out path that takes nothing out of food production, and actively raises smallholder cash income. # Related solar-resource-geography-nepal — why Terai is the zone that matters here hybrid-siting-logic — the first ~2 GW without triggering the land fight solar-lcoe-crossover — why the capex multiplier is absorbable rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — the complementary non-ground-mount story nea-960mw-solar-tender — the first portfolio-scale test claim-solar-political-coalition-is-rural — the tracked claim this page operationalises

pages.title: Bay Of Bengal Terminal
pages.excerpt: This marker is the terminal reach of the generated route-to-ocean trace.
pages.body_text: Bay Of Bengal Terminal This marker is the terminal reach of the generated route-to-ocean trace. # Why It Matters It gives the map a complete endpoint. Without it, the downstream river story visually stops in the plains or delta, even though the strategic route continues through the lower Padma-Meghna system to the sea. # How To Read The Marker Use it as the end of the strategic route trace, not as a detailed coastal or tidal model. # Caveat The terminal point comes from the HydroRIVERS route trace. It does not represent all distributaries, estuarine channels, or coastal sediment dynamics. # Related data-layer-nepal-origin-ocean-routes padma-meghna-delta lower-delta-bay-interface how-to-read-geopolitics-map

pages.title: Bihar Ganges Trunk Zone
pages.excerpt: This dependency zone marks the shared Ganges corridor where separate Nepal-linked routes become one downstream political geography.
pages.body_text: Bihar Ganges Trunk Zone This dependency zone marks the shared Ganges corridor where separate Nepal-linked routes become one downstream political geography. # Why It Matters The map needs this zone because users can otherwise over-focus on individual tributaries. By the Bihar Ganges trunk, Koshi, Gandaki, Karnali/Ghaghara, and Mahakali/Sharda routes are part of a broader lower-basin story. # How To Read The Zone Use it as the bridge between the upstream route merges and the ganges-padma-transition. It is where individual Nepal-linked routes become a shared downstream trunk. # Caveat The polygon is a broad context zone. It is not a model of flow shares, river operations, or local water allocation. # Related kosi-ganges-merge gandak-ganges-merge ghaghara-ganges-merge ganges-padma-transition ganges-contribution

pages.title: Buildability
headings: Strategic implication: rank system value, not headline MW
pages.excerpt: Buildability is the first-order filter on Nepal’s hydropower future: a project is not “good” because a desk study assigns a large MW number—it is good if the entire delivery package remains credible after geology, hydraulics, hazards, and grid realities are stressed.
pages.body_text: Buildability Buildability is the first-order filter on Nepal’s hydropower future: a project is not “good” because a desk study assigns a large MW number—it is good if the entire delivery package remains credible after geology, hydraulics, hazards, and grid realities are stressed. WECS and licensing discussions note a persistent pattern: many licensed schemes remain stalled for years, not because Nepal lacks river energy on paper, but because paper feasibility ≠ executable project under Nepal’s combination of mountain geology, extreme sediment, slope access constraints, and thin EPC markets. # What buildability actually tests A serious buildability review chains interdependent risks: Geology and underground works — tunneling and cavern stability under high cover, fault zones, and variable rock mass ratings. Sediment and abrasion — turbine technology, wear life, and maintenance cycles are not O&M trivia when concentrations swing orders of magnitude seasonally (see sediment-as-design-constraint). Hydraulic transients and flood/GLOF exposure — hazard tolerance and intake resilience. Evacuation — generation without stranded-generation-grade transmission is incomplete infrastructure. Execution realism — contractor capacity, local logistics winter, and financing covenants that survive delay. # Strategic implication: rank system value, not headline MW Nepal should rank options by buildable system value: incremental dependable energy, loss factors, curtailment risk, and delivered cost—not by the largest number in a license. That reframing connects naturally to storage-deficit (seasonal re-timing is worthless if the dam never reaches COD) and to basin-scale complexity in karnali-basin megaproject corridors. Megaprojects such as budhigandaki are instructive case files: the controversy is not only “how big,” but whether the integrated package—reservoir, tunnels, communities, finance—can survive the decades-long path to operation.

pages.title: Consumption Trajectory Outlook
headings: The Consumption Story in One Sentence
Risk Factors — What Could Slow or Accelerate Growth
What the Data Cannot Yet Answer
pages.excerpt: What Nepal's electricity consumption growth trajectory implies for the country's energy strategy — the import-displacement opportunity, the growth risks, and the metric that determines whether the hydro build-out succeeds or fails on its own terms.
pages.body_text: Consumption Trajectory Outlook What Nepal's electricity consumption growth trajectory implies for the country's energy strategy — the import-displacement opportunity, the growth risks, and the metric that determines whether the hydro build-out succeeds or fails on its own terms. This page synthesizes data-domestic-demand, data-industrial-electricity-consumers, data-agricultural-energy-consumption, and data-consumption-geographic-distribution into a forward view. The companion supply-side analysis is twenty-year-strategy. # The Consumption Story in One Sentence Nepal's electricity consumption has grown ~55% in four years (from ~7,277 GWh to 11,288 GWh), but electricity is still only 7.23% of total final energy and 0.5% of households cook with it — meaning the growth runway is measured in decades, not years. # Current State — Electricity Consumption (FY 2024/25) Segment | GWh | Growth (4yr CAGR) | Electricity Penetration in Segment | ---------|------:|------:|------:| Residential | ~4,742 | ~10.8% | 5.0% of residential energy | Industrial | ~4,136 | ~10.1% | 11.6% of industrial energy (FY 2079/80) | Commercial | ~879 | ~14.5% | 9.9% of commercial energy (FY 2079/80) | Construction/Mining | ~591 | — | 55.5% of sector energy — already majority-electric | Agriculture | ~315 | — | 22.5% of agricultural energy | Transport | ~25 | — | 0.05% — essentially zero | Other (streetlight, irrigation, temp) | ~620 | ~17.8% | — | Total electricity | ~11,288 | ~11.6% | 7.23% of total final energy | \ Estimated from WECS sector share applied to FY 2024/25 total # The Consumption Growth Model Per-capita electricity consumption is 465 kWh/yr and growing ~10% annually. At this rate: Year | Per-capita (kWh) | Implied total (GWh) | Context | ------|------:|------:|---------| FY 2024/25 | 465 | 11,288 | Current | ~2029 | ~750 | ~18,000 | Nepal reaches current India per-capita | ~2034 | ~1,200 | ~28,800 | Reaches lower-middle-income norm | ~2040 | ~2,000 | ~48,000 | Reaches middle-income norm | This trajectory is not a forecast — it's a sense-check. At 10% annual demand growth, domestic consumption alone would absorb roughly the output of the planned hydro pipeline (see data-potential-pyramid) without needing massive export growth. But sustaining 10% demand growth requires the substitution pathway in energy-substitution-pathway to actually happen — cooking electrification, industrial fuel-switching, and EV uptake. # The Import Displacement Ledger The single most powerful number in the consumption profile is the gap between what Nepal currently imports (fossil fuels) and what it could displace with domestic electricity: Fuel | Total Consumption (TJ) | Import Cost (NPR Bill./yr, est.) | Displaceable by Electricity? | Electricity Equivalent (GWh) | ------|------:|------:|------:|------:| Diesel (all sectors) | 49,728 | ~120 | Yes — transport, ag pumps, gensets | ~13,800 | LPG (all sectors) | 23,677 | ~55 | Yes — induction cooking | ~6,600 | Petrol (all sectors) | 21,932 | ~75 | Partial — EV passenger vehicles | ~6,100 | Coal (industrial) | 34,016 | ~40 | Partial — electric kilns, hydrogen | ~9,400 | ATF / jet fuel | 6,191 | ~20 | No — aviation | — | Total displaceable | ~129,353 | ~290 | | ~35,900 GWh | [!finding] Total displacement opportunity is ~3× current electricity consumption If Nepal electrified all technically-displaceable fossil fuel consumption, it would need ~35,900 GWh of additional electricity — roughly triple the current 11,288 GWh. This is the domestic demand ceiling that the domestic-led-hydro-strategy is designed to capture. ## Displacement already underway EV passenger vehicles: >70% of new passenger vehicle imports in FY 2024/25 were EVs, driven by tax differentials Electric cooking: NEA FY 2024/25 report explicitly flags government policy to encourage electric cooking, transportation, and industry Industrial switching: diesel genset → grid connection is happening as reliability improves ## Displacement not yet started Brick kilns: ~1,500+ coal-burning kilns, essentially zero electric kilns deployed Freight transport: diesel trucks and buses, no material electrification Cement clinker: coal-fired, hydrogen co-firing still in concept stage (see green-hydrogen-nepal) LPG-to-induction cooking at scale: 0.5% household penetration, needs appliance cost reduction and reliability improvements # Risk Factors — What Could Slow or Accelerate Growth ## Upside risks (faster consumption growth) Factor | Mechanism | Magnitude | --------|-----------|-----------| LPG subsidy removal / rebalancing | Shift households from LPG to induction cooking | +50–75 PJ (~14,000–21,000 GWh) potential | Industrial policy for ferroalloys | Attract electro-intensive industry with cheap reliable power | +500–2,000 GWh per major plant | EV adoption beyond passenger vehicles | Electric buses, two-wheelers, three-wheelers | +500–1,500 GWh by 2030 | Data centres / ICT expansion | Nepal's competitive advantage — cheap green power + English-speaking workforce | +200–500 GWh | Cold chain build-out | Refrigerated transport and storage for agriculture | +500–1,000 GWh | ## Downside risks (slower consumption growth) Factor | Mechanism | Magnitude | --------|-----------|-----------| Manufacturing decline continues | Manufacturing already fell from 7.3% to 5.7% of GDP | −200–500 GWh vs trend | Grid reliability stalls | If outages persist, firms stay on diesel, households don't buy induction cookers | −300–800 GWh vs potential | Fuelwood inertia | Cultural preference + zero cash cost keeps biomass dominant in cooking | −100–200 PJ cooking load | Tariff distortion | If industrial tariffs don't reflect the true cost advantage over diesel, fuel-switching economics erode | −100–300 GWh | Migration / depopulation | Rural areas lose electricity consumers faster than urban gains | −100–200 GWh | # The Metric That Determines Success From energy-substitution-pathway: Biomass + fossil share of Nepal's final energy consumption. Today ~92.8%. A serious transition by 2040 should bring it below 70%, implying ~25–30 PJ of net displacement per year. Applied to 2025-2030 specifically, the intermediate targets are: Metric | Current (FY 2024/25) | 2030 Target | Status | --------|------:|------:|--------| Electricity share of final energy | 7.23% (FY 2079/80) | 12–15% | On track if 10% demand CAGR holds | Electric cooking penetration | 0.5% | 5–10% | Requires active policy push | EV share of new vehicle sales | >70% (passenger only) | >90% (all light vehicles) | Ahead on passenger, behind on freight | Industrial electricity share | 11.6% (FY 2079/80) | 18–22% | Requires grid reliability + industrial policy | Per-capita electricity | 465 kWh | 800–1,000 kWh | On track | Diesel displaced in agriculture | ~0 (currently rising) | 20–30% of ag diesel | Requires tubewell electrification programme | # The Export Surplus Question If domestic consumption grows at 10% CAGR and generation grows at roughly the same pace (the IPSDP pipeline implies ~12,000-15,000 MW installed by 2035), the net export profile depends on which grows faster: Scenario | Demand CAGR | Supply CAGR | 2030 Net Export (GWh) | Character | ----------|------:|------:|------:|-----------| Demand-led | 12% | 10% | ~500–1,500 | Imports still needed in winter | Balanced | 10% | 10% | ~2,000–3,500 | Monsoon exports, winter imports | Supply-led | 8% | 12% | ~5,000–7,000 | Large monsoon surplus, seasonal trade deepens | The current trajectory (~11.6% demand growth, ~10% supply growth) most closely resembles the "Demand-led" scenario — if it sustains. The key variable is whether the demand-side policies (cooking electrification, EV uptake, industrial switching) materialize at the same pace as the supply-side pipeline (22 projects commissioned in FY 2024/25 alone). The nightmare scenario is the "Supply-led" one: large monsoon surplus with weak domestic demand → exports at low spot prices while winter imports at high peak rates persist → the seasonal-arbitrage-trap institutionalizes. # What the Data Cannot Yet Answer 1. Hourly consumption by consumer category — we know the evening peak shape but not which sectors drive it (residential cooking + lighting probably dominates). Without this, the storage + demand-side management sizing is approximate. 2. Actual industrial sub-sector GWh — the estimates in data-industrial-electricity-consumers are indicative. NEA has this data but does not publish it. 3. Captive generation inventory — how much diesel generation capacity exists behind industrial meters, and how much is actually dispatched, is unmeasured. This is the hidden consumption layer. 4. Province-level sectoral splits — we have provincial totals but not the domestic/industrial/commercial breakdown within each province. 5. Price elasticity of demand — how sensitive is each consumer category to tariff changes? Essential for demand-side policy design but unresearched for Nepal. # Related data-domestic-demand — the core electricity demand data data-industrial-electricity-consumers — industrial sub-sector profile data-agricultural-energy-consumption — agricultural energy and diesel displacement data-consumption-geographic-distribution — where consumption is geographically data-final-energy-mix — the total-energy denominator energy-substitution-pathway — the strategic framework for displacement domestic-led-hydro-strategy — why domestic load matters more than exports twenty-year-strategy — the supply-side counterpart seasonal-arbitrage-trap — what happens if demand lags supply unresolved-questions — data gaps this page inherits

pages.title: Domestic-Led Hydro Strategy
headings: Why domestic demand is not a small story
The constraint is not "can Nepal export?" — it can
pages.excerpt: Domestic-led hydro means planning the power system around Nepal's own load growth, reliability, and productivity — using exports to monetize surplus, not to define the investment program. After the solar audit, the better operational phrase is domestic-led power strategy with a h
pages.body_text: Domestic-Led Hydro Strategy Domestic-led hydro means planning the power system around Nepal's own load growth, reliability, and productivity — using exports to monetize surplus, not to define the investment program. After the solar audit, the better operational phrase is domestic-led power strategy with a hydro backbone: hydropower remains central, but solar-hydro-complementarity, BESS, and demand shaping now belong inside the strategy rather than beside it. # The consensus across analytical lines Across multiple research threads in this project, the core strategic recommendation converges: Nepal should pursue domestic-led hydro with export support, not export-led hydro with domestic leftovers. The distinction is not anti-export; it is about sequencing and the objective function. Exports are valuable when they absorb genuine surplus at acceptable prices; they are costly when they institutionalize the seasonal-arbitrage-trap — cheap monsoon sales and expensive winter imports on the same national balance sheet. Recent trade and demand evidence frames the current failure plainly: "The system is getting better at monetizing surplus, but it still pays a winter scarcity tax, and it has not yet built enough domestic electrification depth to turn hydropower into broad-based wealth." # Why domestic demand is not a small story nepal-energy-profile makes clear that "low consumption" is better read as headroom than as lack of need. Per-capita electricity use is ~370–465 kWh/year — roughly 5–7x below a middle-income norm of 2,000–3,000 kWh/year. Electricity's share of final energy is small (7.23% in FY 2079/80, up from 4.96% the year before — see data-final-energy-mix) — so electrification of cooking, heat, mobility, and process heat is a multi-decade demand builder. The specific sinks to target, ranked by volume, are worked out in energy-substitution-pathway. The sectoral breakdown (FY 2020/21, NEA) reveals a critical structure (see data-domestic-demand): Residential: 43% of electricity sales (~3,138 GWh) Industrial: 39% of electricity sales (~2,816 GWh) — nearly as large despite being a tiny fraction of customer count Industry is already the second-biggest user and the highest-leverage expansion target The electric cooking gap is the most dramatic underdevelopment: fewer than ~1% of Nepal's households use electricity for cooking. Biomass and imported LPG dominate. This is not a preference story — it is a reliability and appliance-access story. Shifting even 20–30% of cooking loads to electricity would create large, distributed, flexible domestic demand that simultaneously absorbs monsoon surplus and cuts the LPG import bill. Industrialization, urbanization, and replacement of costly fossil inputs add concurrent load. Highest-value uses include displacing captive diesel, electrifying cooking and transport, and feeding manufacturing where reliability converts kWh into jobs and tax base. Order-of-magnitude estimates suggest ferroalloys and similar electro-intensive industry can yield 8–10x the domestic value of selling the same energy as a commodity export to India at spot-like realizations. # The constraint is not "can Nepal export?" — it can Nepal is improving its exchange-linked value capture: the IEX price spread flipped positive in 2023-24 (Nepal sold at ₹5.61/kWh, bought at ₹4.43/kWh). But the whole-system average was still negative (sold Rs 8.72/unit, bought Rs 9.17/unit) because structural winter imports at peak-hour rates wash out the monsoon gains. The hard constraint is "whether Nepal can build domestic demand and system efficiency fast enough that exports become a high-value optimization layer, not the primary story." # Exports as complement, not compass india-energy-relationship will remain decisive for corridors, approvals, and timing. bhutan-hydropower-model is often invoked as a managed export reference — useful less as a blueprint to copy than as a reminder that host-country load and fiscal design shaped outcomes. Nepal's geography and politics differ, but the lesson holds: contracts and domestic anchors matter as much as megawatts. # Links to supply-side innovation When surplus is structural in the monsoon, domestic absorption can be engineered — not only through wires but through electrolysis and ammonia. green-hydrogen-nepal is the most ambitious demand-creation path: it turns seasonal glut into chemical industry and food security inputs instead of low-priced spill. Solar adds a second supply-side innovation: it lets Nepal build dry-season daytime energy on a short cycle while reservoir hydro moves slowly through buildability and finance constraints. The domestic strategy should therefore treat nea-960mw-solar-tender as a bridge, not a distraction: solar buys time for storage hydro, and storage hydro makes solar useful after sunset. # Related data-domestic-demand — demand distribution data, cooking gap, load profile seasonal-arbitrage-trap — the cost of not building domestic demand stranded-generation — monsoon surplus that domestic load could absorb green-hydrogen-nepal — industrial-scale demand from surplus solar-in-the-master-narrative — how solar changes the strategy without replacing hydro claim-solar-terai-only-short-cycle-build — why solar is the short-cycle bridge twenty-year-strategy — how demand-building fits the phased strategy

pages.title: Eastern UP Ghaghara-Gandak Belt
pages.excerpt: This dependency zone gives context for the western and central Nepal-linked routes entering the agricultural plains of eastern Uttar Pradesh.
pages.body_text: Eastern UP Ghaghara-Gandak Belt This dependency zone gives context for the western and central Nepal-linked routes entering the agricultural plains of eastern Uttar Pradesh. # Why It Matters The zone is included because the Ghaghara and Gandak routes connect Nepal-linked basin timing to dense agricultural and settlement geography. It is a downstream exposure area, not a water-control area. # How To Read The Zone Read it with ghaghara-ganges-merge, gandak-ganges-merge, and data-layer-future-regulation-scenario. The question is how future upstream storage and river timing could matter in a region already sensitive to floods and seasonal water availability. # Caveat The polygon is broad and strategic. It is not an irrigation command boundary, crop raster, or flood-inundation model. # Related ghaghara-ganges-merge gandak-ganges-merge downstream-river-geopolitics how-to-read-geopolitics-map

pages.title: Energy Substitution Pathway
headings: The three dominant sinks, ranked by volume
Sink 1 — Fuelwood in residential cooking and heating (257 PJ)
Sink 2 — Coal in brick kilns, cement, and steel rerolling (34 PJ)
Sink 3 — Diesel, petrol, and ATF in transportation (55.5 PJ)
What this pathway implies for strategy
pages.excerpt: Energy substitution pathway names the specific question a hydro build-out must answer if it is to transform Nepal's energy system and not just its export ledger: which non-electric energy flows are being displaced, in what order, by what policy and infrastructure? Without this le
pages.body_text: Energy Substitution Pathway Energy substitution pathway names the specific question a hydro build-out must answer if it is to transform Nepal's energy system and not just its export ledger: which non-electric energy flows are being displaced, in what order, by what policy and infrastructure? Without this lens, "more hydro MW" and "more electricity consumption" can both rise while Nepal's overall energy system — dominated by fuelwood, coal, and imported petroleum — barely shifts. This is the strategic complement to domestic-led-hydro-strategy: where that page argues why domestic load matters more than exports, this one argues which specific loads to build. # Why the substitution frame exists The master-thesis treats hydropower as a conversion problem: turning hydrological advantage into firm, deliverable, financeable power. But even a perfectly converted fleet only creates kWh. Those kWh become welfare only when they displace something Nepal currently burns. From data-final-energy-mix, Nepal's FY 2079/80 final energy is: Biomass 68.7% (340 PJ) — overwhelmingly fuelwood in cooking Petroleum 19.4% (103 PJ) — diesel, petrol, LPG, ATF, all imported Electricity 7.23% (38.5 PJ) — 94% of it from hydro Coal 6.39% (34 PJ) — 99% of it burned in brick kilns and cement plants Other renewables (biogas, solar, µhydro, wind) 3.1% (16.5 PJ) Everything outside electricity is a candidate substitution target. Ranking them by physical magnitude yields a clear priority stack. # The three dominant sinks, ranked by volume ## Sink 1 — Fuelwood in residential cooking and heating (257 PJ) The single largest energy flow in Nepal. 48.3% of all final energy consumed. Affected population: essentially all of rural Nepal plus significant urban pockets. Substitute: electric induction cooking, heat-pump water heating, electric room heating in winter. Why it's hard: appliance cost, grid reliability (evening peak 18:30–19:25 is exactly cooking time), LPG subsidy currently undercuts electric cooking on marginal-cost terms. Why it's the biggest lever: displacing 20–30% of cooking load creates ~50–75 PJ of new electric demand — roughly doubling current electricity consumption. That is bigger than any plausible export contract. Connected wiki pages: data-domestic-demand (the Biomass + fossil share of Nepal's final energy consumption. Today that share is ~92.8% (68.7% biomass + 19.4% petroleum + 6.4% coal − small overlap). A serious transition by 2040 should bring that share below 70%, implying ~25–30 PJ of net biomass+fossil displacement per year. That in turn implies specific quantities of induction cookers deployed, kilns retrofitted, EVs sold, and industrial processes electrified — all measurable, all currently unmeasured at the system level. If Nepal builds 20,000 MW of hydro and the biomass+fossil share in 2040 is still 85%, the build-out has failed on its own terms. If Nepal builds only 10,000 MW but the share falls to 65%, the strategy has succeeded. # Related data-final-energy-mix — the underlying data domestic-led-hydro-strategy — the strategic envelope data-domestic-demand — sectoral electricity demand today green-hydrogen-nepal — the coal-displacement pathway twenty-year-strategy — phased implementation seasonal-arbitrage-trap — what happens if substitution is not built claim-mw-not-equal-value — the value-capture logic master-thesis — the overarching argument

pages.title: Environmental Flow Policy
pages.excerpt: The IPSDP executive summary treats environmental flow and protected-area restrictions as buildability constraints, not as side notes. In the SEA mitigation table on page 37, moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 says hydropower planning must evaluate and manage environmental flows and m
