pages.body_text: Environmental Flow Policy The IPSDP executive summary treats environmental flow and protected-area restrictions as buildability constraints, not as side notes. In the SEA mitigation table on page 37, moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 says hydropower planning must evaluate and manage environmental flows and must respect statutory restrictions in protected areas. # What the IPSDP says directly Hydropower can alter downstream flow, habitat continuity, and river-wide flow patterns. Where adverse effects are expected, project planning should refer to the IFC Good Practice Handbook: Environmental Flows for Hydropower Projects (2018) and build an explicit E-flow management plan. For projects inside national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, hunting reserves, conservation areas, or buffer zones under Nepal's National Parks and Wildlife Conservation Act (2029/1973), prior permission is required. The same table says river modification and damming are not assumptions a developer can make inside protected areas; maintenance-flow and mitigation requirements remain in force. # Why this matters This is one of the clearest official signals in the current wiki that hydropower potential categories are not simply a matter of head and discharge. Protected-area law, river-ecology constraints, and mitigation hierarchy all cut directly into what is realistically developable. That makes this page a direct bridge between buildability, hydropower-potential-categories, and glof-risk: those concepts are all about why paper potential collapses when real-world constraints are applied. # Confidence boundary This page is limited to the policy and mitigation language stated in the IPSDP executive summary. It does not attempt to summarize Nepal's whole EIA or hydropower-permitting regime beyond what that table states. # Related buildability hydropower-potential-categories glof-risk moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025

pages.title: Firm Power
headings: Installed MW ≠ what you can count on in February
Potential pyramids are not firm-power statements
pages.excerpt: Firm power is dependable, year-round, dispatchable electrical capability under credible drought and system stress assumptions—not a branding label for “big projects,” and not interchangeable with installed megawatts, annual energy (GWh), or even average seasonal output.
pages.body_text: Firm Power Firm power is dependable, year-round, dispatchable electrical capability under credible drought and system stress assumptions—not a branding label for “big projects,” and not interchangeable with installed megawatts, annual energy (GWh), or even average seasonal output. Nepal’s public discourse routinely collapses these quantities. That conflation is not harmless: it steers policy toward headline capacity and away from scarcity months, which is exactly where seasonal-mismatch bites a RoR-heavy fleet (see run-of-river-hydropower). # Installed MW ≠ what you can count on in February A plant can be “online” while being strategically irrelevant for reliability if its energy is concentrated in monsoon weeks when the grid is already long—or if its output is volatile within-day in ways that the transmission system cannot absorb. Conversely, a smaller reservoir or import contract may contribute more firm value than a large RoR string, depending on commit rights, hydrology, and dispatch rules. The right planning question is therefore not “how many MW?” but how much electricity is deliverable when the alternative is rationing, diesel, or blackout—and at what cost. # Potential pyramids are not firm-power statements National “potential” figures sit at the top of a reduction chain from theoretical resource to what is financeable and buildable; see hydropower-potential-categories and the tabulated reductions in data-potential-pyramid. Those layers measure energy opportunity, not contract-grade dependability. Firm power is the bottom of the stack in strategic terms: it is what you underwrite, what you trade, and what industrialization plans should assume in dry weeks. # Analytical payoff Keeping firm distinct from big clarifies why winter deficits can persist even as installed capacity rises, why export discussions hinge on commitment windows not annual averages, and why system adequacy is a seasonal adequacy problem first.

pages.title: Gandak-Ganges Merge
pages.excerpt: This marker explains where the gandaki-basin route enters the Ganges trunk through the Narayani/Gandak downstream system.
pages.body_text: Gandak-Ganges Merge This marker explains where the gandaki-basin route enters the Ganges trunk through the Narayani/Gandak downstream system. # Why It Matters Gandaki is a central Nepal route with strong irrigation and plains relevance downstream. It connects Nepal's river timing story to the Bihar and eastern Uttar Pradesh agricultural belt. # How To Read The Marker Use this point to understand the central route: Gandaki in Nepal, Narayani near the border, Gandak in the plains, and then the Ganges trunk. # Caveat The marker is approximate and interpretive. It does not model canal commands, barrage operations, or local water allocation. # Related gandaki-basin north-bihar-kosi-gandak-floodplain bihar-ganges-trunk-zone downstream-river-geopolitics

pages.title: Nepal's Contribution to the Ganges
headings: Western basins as part of the same puzzle
pages.excerpt: Nepal occupies only about 13% of the Ganges basin area, yet contributes roughly 40–45% of mean annual flow and on the order of 70–80% of dry-season flow measured at Farakka-style accounting points in strategic basin literature. This hydrological asymmetry—small footprint, large l
pages.body_text: Nepal's Contribution to the Ganges Nepal occupies only about 13% of the Ganges basin area, yet contributes roughly 40–45% of mean annual flow and on the order of 70–80% of dry-season flow measured at Farakka-style accounting points in strategic basin literature. This hydrological asymmetry—small footprint, large leverage on timing—is the physical basis for Nepal’s upstream role in South Asian water security. # Underused abundance Nepal's problem is not that the basin is empty. In National Water Plan framing, the country has roughly 225 BCM of annual water availability but uses only about 15 BCM for economic and social purposes. The strategic issue is therefore conversion: storage, delivery, irrigation timing, and coordinated releases — not raw annual scarcity. # What the asymmetry implies downstream Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, and—through the concatenated regime—Bangladesh are sensitive to Nepal-origin contributions for: Kharif and rabi irrigation scheduling (canal drafts depend on low-flow stability). Flood peaks shaped by transboundary storms and sediment pulses (see koshi-basin for the extreme case). Drought resilience when monsoon spatial correlation fails. Aggregated planning numbers in basin assessments commonly cite ~5–7 million hectares of irrigated agriculture and ~200–250 million people as significantly affected by Nepal-influenced flow regimes (definitions vary; treat as order-of-magnitude strategic scale, not a census). # Climate change raises the stakes If variability intensifies and low flows become less reliable while extreme events increase, the marginal value of upstream storage, forecasting, and coordinated releases rises. Nepal’s geography could translate into bargaining power—but only if institutional capacity converts hydrology into credible, enforceable cooperation. Today, the more common pattern is leverage without durable agreement: episodic crisis diplomacy rather than rules-based benefit sharing. # Geopolitics and national planning The strategic read is developed in parallel on hydro-geopolitics and india-energy-relationship: electrons and cubic meters are joint products in peoples’ minds even when markets separate them. Nepal’s National Water Plan discourse (see national-water-plan-nepal) is the domestic mirror—attempting to align IWRM, energy, and disaster risk under one national narrative. # Western basins as part of the same puzzle karnali-basin feeds the Ghaghara complex and therefore UP’s vast irrigated plains. Storage and diversion debates there are not “local” Nepali stories; they are subcontinental stories with Nepali headwaters at the center. # Analytical bottom line Nepal’s contribution to the Ganges is not proportional to its land share. That fact creates real upstream leverage—and also moral hazard if Nepal builds unilateral infrastructure that ignores downstream sensitivities. The research task is to map where cooperation creates surplus for both sides versus where zero-sum framing is unavoidable.

pages.title: Ganges-Padma Transition
pages.excerpt: This marker explains the naming and political transition where the lower Ganges route enters Bangladesh and becomes part of the Padma system.
pages.body_text: Ganges-Padma Transition This marker explains the naming and political transition where the lower Ganges route enters Bangladesh and becomes part of the Padma system. # Why It Matters For Nepal's upstream story, this is where the downstream frame shifts from northern India plains politics into Bangladesh delta dependence. The same route that began as Himalayan basin geography becomes a lower-basin, dry-season, sediment, and delta-stability question. # How To Read The Marker Use it as a hinge between the Ganges trunk and the padma-meghna-delta. It helps readers understand why the Nepal-origin route-to-ocean layer crosses beyond India. # Caveat This marker is a strategic transition point. It does not resolve border hydrology, treaty allocations, or local distributary behavior. # Related padma-meghna-delta padma-meghna-delta-dependency ganges-contribution bangladesh-trade-route

pages.title: Ghaghara-Ganges Merge
pages.excerpt: This marker explains the downstream handoff for the karnali-basin route. Nepal's Karnali becomes the Ghaghara system downstream and eventually joins the Ganges trunk.
pages.body_text: Ghaghara-Ganges Merge This marker explains the downstream handoff for the karnali-basin route. Nepal's Karnali becomes the Ghaghara system downstream and eventually joins the Ganges trunk. # Why It Matters Karnali is one of Nepal's most important future storage and hydropower basins. If large storage is eventually built, the downstream question is not only how much energy Nepal generates. It is how timing, flood attenuation, dry-season releases, and sediment behavior are perceived through the Ghaghara-Ganges corridor. # How To Read The Marker The marker connects karnali-basin and mahakali-sharda-handoff to the shared Ganges trunk. It is a map-reading handoff, not a hydrodynamic attribution point. # Caveat The marker does not say Nepal controls the Ghaghara or Ganges. It says Nepal-linked routes enter this downstream political geography. # Related karnali-basin eastern-up-ghaghara-gandak-belt bihar-ganges-trunk-zone data-layer-future-regulation-scenario

pages.title: GLOF Risk
headings: Process: from lake instability to basin-scale impact
A concrete 2024 illustration: Thame / Dudh Koshi
Implications for hydropower design and siting
pages.excerpt: Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are rapid releases of water from moraine-dammed or ice-dammed lakes, often triggered by avalanches, seismicity, or hydrostatic/overpressure failure. In Nepal’s context, they are a first-order constraint on how confidently one can site run-of-r
pages.body_text: GLOF Risk Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) are rapid releases of water from moraine-dammed or ice-dammed lakes, often triggered by avalanches, seismicity, or hydrostatic/overpressure failure. In Nepal’s context, they are a first-order constraint on how confidently one can site run-of-river corridors, headworks, and storage in transboundary basins—because a GLOF is not a “flood” in the sense of a predictable monsoon stage; it can arrive as a high-energy sediment slurry with invented hydrographs that exceed historical design bases. # Process: from lake instability to basin-scale impact Glacier retreat creates and enlarges supraglacial and proglacial lakes, frequently impounded by unconsolidated moraines with weak spillway geometry. When the impoundment fails, the release can cascade: one lake drains into another valley reach, remobilizing sediment, eroding channels, and amplifying peak discharge far downstream. Flow paths can extend hundreds of kilometers, affecting tributaries and mainstems long after the originating valley. # A concrete 2024 illustration: Thame / Dudh Koshi The 2024 Thame Valley sequence—where a rock avalanche impacted Upper Ngole Cho, then contributed to failure dynamics affecting Lower Ngole Cho, producing on the order of a ~22 m head drop through a ~51 m breach width (figures as reported in rapid assessments)—generated a hyper-concentrated flow tracked >80 km down the Thame and Dudh Koshi systems, damaging settlements and hydropower works. The event is a textbook case for why stationary hydrology assumptions are inadequate: the initiating process was mass movement + lake chain, not a monsoon exceedance. # Implications for hydropower design and siting Planned RoR and storage projects along GLOF-prone corridors face three intertwined issues: 1. Hydraulic load cases that historically used gauge-based floods may understate tail scenarios driven by sudden moraine breaches and sediment bulking. 2. Sediment pulses from GLOFs connect hazard to turbine wear, intake burial, and channel avulsion—overlapping with sediment-as-design-constraint in basins like koshi-basin. 3. Insurance, financing, and EPC risk feed back into buildability: projects that look attractive on LCOE spreadsheets can fail non-market tests once tail risks are priced honestly. Storage concepts in high-hazard reaches—dudhkoshi-storage is a prominent example—must therefore integrate landscape stabilization, monitoring, and cascading-failure scenarios, not only energy-yield optimization. # Link to the glacio-hydrological arc The same deglaciation dynamics that define the peak-water window also produce unstable lakes. Mitigation (lowering, controlled drainage, early warning) competes for fiscal and institutional bandwidth with generation targets. Treating GLOFs as a side environmental issue is a category error: they are infrastructure solvency issues.

pages.title: Green Hydrogen Nepal
headings: Why fertilizer is the natural first anchor
pages.excerpt: Green hydrogen in Nepal is best understood as a bridge technology between surplus monsoon electricity and hard-to-electrify domestic industry—not as a export commodity fantasy disconnected from grid physics. Electrolysis converts low-utilization wet-season kWh into storable molec
pages.body_text: Green Hydrogen Nepal Green hydrogen in Nepal is best understood as a bridge technology between surplus monsoon electricity and hard-to-electrify domestic industry—not as a export commodity fantasy disconnected from grid physics. Electrolysis converts low-utilization wet-season kWh into storable molecules (hydrogen, then ammonia or other carriers), reshaping the economics of seasonal-arbitrage-trap by absorbing energy when the system is long and delivering products when the economy needs them. # Why fertilizer is the natural first anchor Nepal imports essentially all chemical fertilizer; food security and farm productivity are therefore foreign-exchange and supply-chain dependent. A domestic ammonia pathway—hydrogen → Haber-Bosch → urea/ammonium products—ties directly to sovereignty and base-load demand. Order-of-magnitude sector sketches in national roadmaps suggest a modern fertilizer complex can present hundreds of megawatts of baseload-class demand (commonly cited ~500 MW in policy-grade scenarios—exact sizing is plant-design specific). That scale matters: it is large enough to change dispatch rather than decorate it. # Cement and other industrial offtake Cement decarbonization is another hydrogen sink: replacing fossil fuel combustion in clinker/pyroprocessing with hydrogen or hybrid fuels stacks up large annual H₂ demand in national modeling discourse (illustrative national figures on the order of ~228,000 tonnes H₂/year implying ~2 GW of electrolysis capacity are used in roadmap arithmetic—treat as planning magnitude, not a signed FEED package). The point for domestic-led-hydro-strategy is structural: these are domestic value chains that pay for reliability differently than spot export. # Policy trajectory The Green Hydrogen Policy (2024) signals official intent to coordinate standards, pilots, incentives, and safety. The typical staged narrative runs pilots 2025–2027 toward industrial scale by ~2035, with cost projections in policy documents clustering around ~$2.15/kg by mid-2030s under favorable renewable input cost paths—again, a directional planning anchor. # Relationship to curtailment and surplus If new RoR continues to outpace demand and storage, stranded-generation risk rises: energy with nowhere to go at non-trivial variable cost. Electrolysis is one of the few scalable sinks that can ride renewable variability if system integration (oxygen use, water treatment, grid connection, industrial symbiosis) is solved. Detailed citations and program structure belong in green-hydrogen-roadmap-nepal.

pages.title: How To Read The Geopolitics Map
headings: How To Read The Geopolitics Map
Why India And Bangladesh Marks Are Here
pages.excerpt: The geopolitics preset is not a Nepal-only map. It follows Nepal-linked rivers into the northern Indian plains, Bangladesh, and the Bay of Bengal because the political question is about downstream exposure, not just upstream origin.
pages.body_text: How To Read The Geopolitics Map The geopolitics preset is not a Nepal-only map. It follows Nepal-linked rivers into the northern Indian plains, Bangladesh, and the Bay of Bengal because the political question is about downstream exposure, not just upstream origin. # Visual Grammar Read the layers in this order: Map mark | Meaning | ----------|---------| Nepal-linked basin polygons | Where Nepal sits inside the upstream basin geography. | Nepal rivers and downstream rivers | The main water routes from Nepal into the plains. | Ocean route | A strategic continuation through the Ganges/Padma/Meghna system to the Bay of Bengal. | Route notes | Merge, handoff, naming, and delta markers that explain the route. | Dependency zones | Broad downstream exposure areas for flood, dry-season, agricultural, population, sediment, and delta sensitivity. | Population anchors | City and delta points that help orient the human geography around the routes. | Flow regulation | Storage projects that could change timing, dry-season support, flood attenuation, sediment behavior, and cooperation risk. | # The Core Rule Do not read the downstream layers as water-control claims. They are exposure claims. The map does not say Nepal controls Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, or Bangladesh water security. It says Nepal-linked rivers pass through sensitive downstream geographies where timing, flood pulses, sediment, dry-season flow, and future storage decisions can matter politically. # How To Click Around Start with downstream-river-geopolitics. Then follow the route notes: 1. mahakali-sharda-handoff 2. ghaghara-ganges-merge 3. gandak-ganges-merge 4. kosi-ganges-merge 5. ganges-padma-transition 6. padma-meghna-delta 7. bay-of-bengal-terminal Then read the exposure zones: eastern-up-ghaghara-gandak-belt north-bihar-kosi-gandak-floodplain bihar-ganges-trunk-zone padma-meghna-delta-dependency lower-delta-bay-interface # Why India And Bangladesh Marks Are Here The downstream marks exist because Nepal's hydropower and storage choices are also timing choices. A run-of-river project mostly follows the hydrograph. A storage project can shift water across time, trap sediment, attenuate floods, or change the reliability of dry-season releases. That is why the map keeps data-layer-future-regulation-scenario visible with the downstream layers. The political value of storage is not just megawatts. It is the ability to regulate time. # Related downstream-river-geopolitics ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics data-layer-nepal-origin-ocean-routes data-layer-downstream-dependency-zones

pages.title: Hybrid Siting Logic
headings: Archetype 1 — Solar at existing hydropower sites ("hydro co-location")
pages.excerpt: Solar plants are not free-floating technology choices; they are three things co-located — a module field, a land parcel, and an interconnection. Cheap modules have collapsed the first; solar-lcoe-crossover. Land and interconnection are now the binding constraints on Nepal solar,
pages.body_text: Hybrid Siting Logic Solar plants are not free-floating technology choices; they are three things co-located — a module field, a land parcel, and an interconnection. Cheap modules have collapsed the first; solar-lcoe-crossover. Land and interconnection are now the binding constraints on Nepal solar, and the siting archetypes that minimise those two costs beat the ones that optimise GHI alone. This page frames the three siting archetypes — hydro co-location, substation-adjacent greenfield, floating PV on reservoirs — and argues that the first and third together will carry most of the political coalition for utility-scale solar in Nepal through 2035. # Archetype 1 — Solar at existing hydropower sites ("hydro co-location") The cheapest MW Nepal can build, in the specific sense that almost every non-module cost is already paid. What's already there at a typical operating hydropower site: An access road (often 3–15 km of graded mountain road, the single most expensive item in hill-country solar). An interconnecting substation (11/33 → 132/220 kV). Spare evacuation headroom, especially in dry season when the hydro plant runs at 30–40% of monsoon output and the transformer / line is underused. A skilled O&M crew, security, and a fenced site. Water for panel cleaning. Permitting precedent with NEA, local government, forest department, and the community. What the solar build adds: 1–10 MWp of PV modules (1–8 ha of dry / desilted terrain adjacent to the powerhouse or along the headrace road). A combiner box, inverters, and a tap into the existing step-up transformer. ~NPR 40–60 million per MWp installed (vs ~NPR 70–90 million for greenfield) — a 35–40% capex reduction from land, road, and interconnection savings. Why it is also operationally elegant: The interconnection loading pattern is anti-phased with the hydro plant. In the dry-season afternoon, the hydropower plant under-utilizes its evacuation; the solar PV fills the spare capacity of the same substation. In the monsoon-day overlap, dispatch holds hydro pondage through midday and releases it in the evening peak (see solar-hydro-complementarity). Net transformer capacity utilization goes up, not down. Candidate sites with published feasibility or public tender interest: kulekhani-cascade — reservoir + three cascade plants; ~10–20 MWp ground + ~15–30 MWp floating potential. kali-gandaki-a — 144 MW NEA flagship; dam-adjacent flats suited to 25–40 MWp ground PV. khimti-i — 60 MW IPP; headrace-adjacent terraces, 10–15 MWp candidate. marsyangdi / madhya-marsyangdi — cascade co-location, 20–40 MWp combined. tanahu-hydropower — storage reservoir under construction; co-location should be designed in before impoundment. The 50 MW solar project at Khungri village, designed to integrate into the Khungri substation adjacent to Madi Khola and Lungri Khola hydropower, is the first operational example of the archetype at scale in Nepal (NEA annual report FY 2024/25). # Archetype 2 — Substation-adjacent greenfield (Terai) The 960 MW NEA tender (nea-960mw-solar-tender) and the next 2–3 GW of utility-scale solar will overwhelmingly be this archetype: 10–50 MWp ground-mount, 1–8 km from an existing 132 or 220 kV substation, on the Terai plain. Why it works: Highest 132 kV grid density in the country (hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone, dana-kushma-butwal-corridor). Flat terrain (<3° slope), lowest construction cost in Nepal. Proximity to load centres and to Indian-grid interconnections for future export. Water for panel cleaning (Terai is canal-irrigated). Why it is politically fragile: Terai is Nepal's prime agricultural land; fee-simple conversion to solar triggers food-security objections (agrivoltaics-and-land). Land acquisition — even at 2–5 ha per site — collides with fragmented tenure, smallholder resistance, and the memory of hydropower-era displacement. Non-agricultural alienable land (government-owned sarkari parcels, dried-out oxbows, public waste) is scarce and over-subscribed. The design move that makes it durable: do not buy land. Lease for 25 years with a revenue-share clause, anchor on agrivoltaic configuration, and bundle rural-electrification / pump-irrigation offtake into the PPA. The 960 MW tender is experimenting with variants of this; the next decade of Terai solar depends on whether that politics stabilises. # Archetype 3 — Floating PV on reservoirs The highest-leverage single lever in Nepal's solar portfolio, by an uncomfortable margin. What floating PV buys that ground-mount does not: No land acquisition — the reservoir exists; deploying on ~5–20% of its surface takes nothing out of agricultural rotation. Cooler panel operating temperature — ~2–5% yield uplift from evaporative cooling. Reduced reservoir evaporation — 30–50% evaporation reduction on covered surface; in a country with storage-deficit and a dry-season water-tight operating regime, this is not a side benefit, it is a water-system benefit sold separately. Shared interconnection — dam powerhouse already carries the 11/33 kV step-up and the substation. Floating PV taps in at the tail. Anti-algae — covered water bodies see fewer blooms; Kulekhani in particular has had sedimentation / eutrophication concerns that floating PV partially mitigates. Candidate reservoirs: Reservoir | Surface (ha) | 10% coverage (ha) | Nameplate (MWp) | Status | ---|---:|---:|---:|---| kulekhani-cascade | ~220 | 22 | ~17 MWp | Operating; pilot-ready | tanahu-hydropower | ~700 | 70 | ~56 MWp | Under construction; design in now | budhigandaki | ~6,300 | 630 | ~500 MWp | Proposed; 10+ year horizon | dudhkoshi-storage | ~1,800 | 180 | ~144 MWp | Proposed | mugu-karnali-storage | ~9,000 | 900 | ~720 MWp | Proposed | uttarganga-storage | ~2,400 | 240 | ~192 MWp | Proposed | Even at conservative 5% coverage and discounting the long-dated projects, the near-term-buildable floating PV at Kulekhani + Tanahu is ~70–90 MWp — more than the entire operating utility-scale solar fleet as of FY 2024/25. The design move that matters most: require floating-PV integration in the tender documents for every new reservoir project, starting with Tanahu. Retrofitting is ~2× more expensive than designing it in. # The siting-cost stack, one table Archetype | Land cost | Grid cost | GHI (typical) | LCOE index (Terai greenfield = 100) | ---|---|---|---|---:| Hydro co-location | 0 (already owned) | 0 (spare headroom) | 4.5–5.5 | 60–70 | Substation-adjacent Terai | 15–25 | 5–10 | 4.8–5.3 | 100 | Floating PV | 0 | low | 4.5–5.5 | 75–85 | Trans-Himalayan (Mustang) | low | very high | 6.0–6.5 | 180–250 | Rooftop (Kathmandu Valley) | 0 (existing) | net-metering | 4.2–4.6 | 90–110 | Hill greenfield | 10–20 | 30–60 | 4.3–4.8 | 140–180 | Grid cost is the single widest column. In a country with stranded-generation as a current, real constraint, archetypes that spend zero new transmission (hydro co-location, floating PV, rooftop) are worth their ~5% higher module-side costs many times over. # The portfolio rule Over 2026–2035, roughly: ~60% of solar MW should be substation-adjacent Terai greenfield (Archetype 2), with ~70% of that as agrivoltaics. ~20% as hydro co-location (Archetype 1), increasingly standard in the next NEA tender batches. ~10% as floating PV on Kulekhani, Tanahu, and the earliest-operating reservoir sites (Archetype 3), scaling to more as reservoirs commission. ~10% as rooftop / distributed + off-grid mini-grids (see rooftop-minigrid-offgrid), which is a separate political track. This is the sort of portfolio a competent NEA + ERC could produce in a 10-year plan. Nothing in the 2024/25 annual report refutes it; the infrastructure for all four archetypes is under way. The missing piece is an explicit portfolio rule that treats the four archetypes as complements rather than competitors. # Related solar-hydro-complementarity — why these sites are valuable solar-resource-geography-nepal — which zones suit which archetype solar-lcoe-crossover — the price argument each archetype compounds agrivoltaics-and-land — the political condition for Archetype 2 rooftop-minigrid-offgrid — the fourth archetype, treated separately kulekhani-cascade, tanahu-hydropower, budhigandaki, dudhkoshi-storage — floating PV candidates nea-960mw-solar-tender — the first portfolio-scale test of Archetype 2

pages.title: Hydro-Geopolitics
headings: Downstream scale: why Nepal’s choices ripple
India as decisive actor; China as absent partner
pages.excerpt: Hydro-geopolitics is the lens that treats Nepal’s rivers as co-produced by geology, monsoon, ice, and downstream human systems—and therefore as objects of bargaining, not merely as engineering inputs. Electricity is one slice of a larger portfolio that includes irrigation, flood
pages.body_text: Hydro-Geopolitics Hydro-geopolitics is the lens that treats Nepal’s rivers as co-produced by geology, monsoon, ice, and downstream human systems—and therefore as objects of bargaining, not merely as engineering inputs. Electricity is one slice of a larger portfolio that includes irrigation, flood management, navigation where relevant, ecosystem services, and disaster risk. Ignoring this layer turns technically “optimal” projects into politically unrealizable ones. # Downstream scale: why Nepal’s choices ripple Nepal-origin rivers contribute a large share of Ganges annual discharge (commonly cited ~40–45%) and an even larger share of dry-season flow (~70–80% in Farakka-style accounting used in basin assessments). Those percentages imply hundreds of millions of people and many million hectares of irrigated command indirectly indexed to Nepal-influenced regimes. Illustrative orders of magnitude appear repeatedly in basin literature: Koshi irrigation on the order of 500–600 thousand hectares in Bihar; Gandak systems on the order of 1.5–2 million hectares across Bihar/UP; Karnali/Ghaghara complexes affecting roughly 2–3 million hectares in UP. The exact numbers shift with dataset and boundary definition; the strategic fact is extreme downstream dependence on upstream timing and quality of flows—developed analytically in ganges-contribution. # Treaties, asymmetry, and unfinished business Historical agreements (Koshi, Gandak, Mahakali) are often described as asymmetric in implementation experience: formal principles of equity and benefit sharing collide with as-built financing, maintenance, and interpretation disputes. pancheshwar remains the emblematic large joint project whose stall shows how sovereignty, resettlement, benefit splits, and India’s internal federal politics can freeze decades of studies into zero concrete storage. # India as decisive actor; China as absent partner india-energy-relationship is decisive for cross-border trade, transmission corridors, grid codes, and perceptions of security. China's role in Nepal hydro has been receding, not growing: China Three Gorges withdrew from west-seti in 2018; China Gezhouba was blocked from budhigandaki on political grounds. No major Chinese-linked storage project is currently under active construction in Nepal. India, meanwhile, has expanded its presence: NHPC took over West Seti (2022), SJVN is building the 900 MW Arun-3, and India's Power Grid Corporation agreed in April 2025 to build high-capacity cross-border transmission. The triangulation is not symmetric: India can veto or complicate Chinese-backed projects through market access, transmission denial, and geopolitical pressure, while China has no equivalent lever over India-backed schemes. Nepal's ability to play one against the other is limited in practice — a finding that tempers the "strategic leverage" narrative. # Bhutan as comparator, not clone bhutan-hydropower-model illustrates how export-led hydro can work when fiscal rules, load management, and a patron relationship align. Nepal’s larger population, internal political fragmentation, and different downstream co-riparian geometry mean copy-paste is misleading—but the comparison clarifies that governance is where opportunity is won or lost. # Basin anchors Transboundary technical narratives often anchor on koshi-basin as the hazard–sediment–irrigation nexus par excellence. Hydro-geopolitics is therefore not a “soft” add-on to power-sector economics; it is frequently the binding constraint on what can be built, financed, and operated.

pages.title: Hydro Insurance
headings: Market Structure and the Premium Surge
Interaction with AD Penalties and Force Majeure
pages.excerpt: The insurance market for Nepal's hydropower sector — mandatory coverage requirements, premium escalation, claim settlement failures, and the systemic interaction with AD penalties and debt service obligations. The central connection is to nea-triple-authority: when physical damag
pages.body_text: Hydro Insurance The insurance market for Nepal's hydropower sector — mandatory coverage requirements, premium escalation, claim settlement failures, and the systemic interaction with AD penalties and debt service obligations. The central connection is to nea-triple-authority: when physical damage prevents generation, NEA may still apply AD penalties while the insurer delays settlement, draining the developer's working capital from both sides. # Premium Burden — Verified Project-Level Data Two verified data points establish the insurance premium burden on operational hydro projects: project | gross_revenue_npr | annual_premium_npr | premium_pct_revenue | fiscal_year | source | ---|---|---|---|---|---| Sanima Mai HPP | 764,000,000 | 13,770,000 | 1.80% | FY2023 | CARE Ratings Nepal + Sanima Reliance Life disclosures | Upper Tamakoshi | 8,678,000,000 | 99,000,000 | 1.14% | FY2080 | UTKHPL Annual Report | For infrastructure assets with 85%+ PBILDT margins, an insurance line consuming 1.8% of top-line revenue represents 10–12% of the entire operating cost envelope. Hydro project finance models assume minimal variable OPEX once construction is complete — insurance premium escalation directly compresses the DSCR headroom these models depend on. # Market Structure and the Premium Surge The premium surge originated from two simultaneous events: 1. Mandatory minimum premium policy introduced FY2078 BS by Beema Samiti (now Nepal Insurance Authority). This set statutory minimum premium floors across the hydro sector regardless of individual project risk profiles. 2. International reinsurer withdrawal from Nepal hydro risk portfolios post-2021, driven by accumulated flood, landslide, and GLOF loss experience. This removed the competitive pricing pressure that had previously kept domestic premiums manageable. Consequence: Nepal Re and Himalayan Re were ordered to raise paid-up capital from NPR 13 billion to NPR 20 billion to absorb the reinsurance gap. Source: Insurance Khabar (English edition). On the "800% surge" claim: This figure has no locatable primary source. IPPAN's documented public statements (Ekantipur/Kantipur, April 2025) cite "up to 500%" as the maximum increase. This figure should be used in preference to the 800% figure. [PARTIALLY VERIFIED: sourced to IPPAN lobbying statement, not independent actuarial calculation.] Data accessibility note: The IPPAN digital news archive (ippan.org.np/news/) was completely inaccessible during the research pass due to server timeout. Primary IPPAN documentation requires direct access to the IPPAN Secretariat in Kathmandu or retrieval of the specific Beema Samiti minimum tariff directive. This is flagged as a data transparency gap. # Market Concentration Shikhar Insurance Company Limited is the dominant non-life insurer in Nepal, collecting NPR 4.80 billion in premium in an 8-month period (FY2080/81 annual: NPR 5.25 billion). Source: Investopaper / Shikhar Insurance Annual Reports. The 14 non-life insurance companies in Nepal collectively collected over NPR 31.70 billion in premiums by the close of Falgun FY2082/83, reflecting a 13.11% year-over-year expansion. Source: Nepal Insurance Authority (NIA) via Insurance Khabar. The banking sector's requirement for comprehensive project insurance as a loan disbursement condition creates a captive market for domestic insurers. IPPs cannot self-insure or go uninsured without triggering lender covenant breaches — giving domestic insurers effective pricing power without competitive constraint. # Claim Settlement Delays The "90% claim settlement delays" claim circulating in industry discussions has not been numerically verified. No named project claim case — with documented damage, filing date, settlement date, and financial impact during the gap — has been found in research passes to date. The Nepal Insurance Authority (NIA) has recognized the systemic threat and proposed developmental roadmaps, but no market-making intervention has been implemented. [Under research — awaiting named claim cases and NIA settlement data.] # Interaction with AD Penalties and Force Majeure Whether the PPA contains force majeure or physical damage exemption from AD penalties during the insurance claim settlement period is unresolved. This is the mirror image of the ad-penalties curtailment double-bind: a damaged project unable to generate may simultaneously face AD penalty invoices from NEA while waiting for an insurance payout, draining working capital from both sides. [Under research — awaiting PPA clause text for force majeure and AD exemption language.] # Analytical links ad-penalties — the penalty mechanism that may compound insurance gaps nea-triple-authority — the institutional framework of NEA as off-taker and penalty administrator

pages.title: Hydropower Potential Categories
pages.excerpt: “Nepal has 83,000 MW” is a political slogan and a methodological landmine. Hydropower potential categories exist precisely because theoretical river power is not the same as financeable capacity, buildable projects, or firm winter electricity (see firm-power).
pages.body_text: Hydropower Potential Categories “Nepal has 83,000 MW” is a political slogan and a methodological landmine. Hydropower potential categories exist precisely because theoretical river power is not the same as financeable capacity, buildable projects, or firm winter electricity (see firm-power). # The reduction pyramid (conceptual chain) Think of a pyramid—quantified in data-potential-pyramid—where each step applies filters the previous step ignores: 1. Theoretical / gross potential — legacy national figures near ~83,000 MW appear in older discourse; WECS (2019) reframed theoretical potential around ~72,500 MW depending on methodological choices. 2. Technical potential — what is physically plausible with conventional technology; commonly discussed in the ~42,000–46,000 MW range in national studies (exact bounds depend on site database and assumptions). 3. Techno-economic potential — WECS (2019) reports 32,680 MW at this layer: sites that meet engineering and economic screening rules as modeled. 4. Economically viable (market window) — often discussed as ~25,000–35,000 MW under price and financing assumptions that move with interest rates, carbon policy, and India import terms. 5. Realistically developable by ~2040 — planning horizons frequently land around ~10,000–15,000 MW once execution risk, transmission, and social license are acknowledged. 6. Installed — roughly ~3,591 MW hydro installed (order-of-magnitude anchor for “today”). These categories are not interchangeable. Moving down the chain is not “being conservative”; it is changing the question. # Basin concentration A large share of gross potential concentrates in three major basins—koshi-basin, karnali-basin, and gandaki-basin—often cited on the order of ~94% of national potential. That concentration matters for cascade externalities, sediment, and geopolitical export corridors: “national potential” is geographically lumpy. # Why this matters for analysis Potential-category discipline prevents two common failures: inflating creditworthiness with theoretical MW, and confusing annual energy with dry-season dependability. Primary methodology and citations live under wecs-hydropower-potential-2019.

pages.title: IPO Hydropower Bailout
pages.excerpt: How Initial Public Offerings (IPOs) and rights share issuances in Nepal's hydropower sector are used to transfer project debt risk onto retail investors — and why the "Chilime success model" is not replicable.
pages.body_text: IPO Hydropower Bailout How Initial Public Offerings (IPOs) and rights share issuances in Nepal's hydropower sector are used to transfer project debt risk onto retail investors — and why the "Chilime success model" is not replicable. This page is under research. Verifiable data on IPO use-of-funds is blocked by SEBON's systemic use of non-machine-readable scanned PDFs for critical financial disclosures — frustrating automated accountability tracking. # What is known Six hydro companies have issued IPOs and subsequent rights shares, documented in SEBON and NEPSE registries: Company | Capacity (MW) | IPO Year | IPO Amount (NPR Cr) | Rights Year | Rights Amount (NPR Cr) | Stated Use | ---|---|---|---|---|---|---| Rasuwagadhi | 111 | 2018 | 66 | — | — | Construction funding; delayed COD | Ankhu Khola Jalvidhyut | 8.4 | 2013 | 20 | 2024 | 120 (1:1.5) | Pay off bank loans | Peoples Power Ltd | 4.96 | 2022 | 31.6 | 2024 | 15.8 (1:0.5) | Repay commercial bank debt | Joshi Hydropower | 3 | 2018 | 24.1 | 2024 | 15.6 (1:0.65) | Prevent NPL status | Terhathum Power | 7.5 | 2021 | 12 | 2024 | 12 (1:1) | Blended debt restructuring | [UNVERIFIED: entries above compiled from media and secondary rating agency sources — SEBON prospectuses not independently audited.] NEPSE has suspended trading on multiple hydro companies: Upper Syange Hydropower (USHL) for insider trading and delayed disclosure of rights share bailout plans; Khani Khola Hydropower (KKHC) amid controversial Q4 reporting and promoter share dumping. # The Chilime contrast Chilime Hydropower's 2010 IPO occurred after seven years of profitable operations and distributed a 35% dividend within a year. Modern IPOs float pre-revenue, when the project is hemorrhaging cash from capitalized interest. See chilime for the pre-IPO profitability context and q-design-discharge for why Chilime's overperformance is not replicable. # The triple-authority blindspot Retail investors purchasing rights shares in distressed companies are not informed that the off-taker (NEA) can curtail the project and then penalize it for non-generation. IPO prospectuses project future revenue using the PPA tariff but do not model the scenario where a portion of that revenue is clawed back through AD penalties. See nea-triple-authority for the institutional framework and ad-penalties for the penalty mechanism. # What is missing The primary blocker is SEBON's non-machine-readable PDF filing system. Key questions that remain unresolvable without primary source access: What percentage of IPO/rights proceeds was actually used to retire promoter bridge loans vs bank debt vs new project investment? What risk disclosures about hydrological underperformance, PPA rate risk, and AD penalty exposure appear in hydro IPO prospectuses? What is the full list of NEPSE-listed hydro companies that issued rights shares within 3 years of IPO? What is NRB's specialized hydro loan book — the true extent of off-balance-sheet restructuring and informal forbearance? [Under research — awaiting SEBON prospectus data extraction or regulatory reform mandating machine-readable disclosures.]

pages.title: Kosi-Ganges Merge
pages.excerpt: This marker explains where the koshi-basin route reaches the Ganges trunk after crossing the North Bihar floodplain.
pages.body_text: Kosi-Ganges Merge This marker explains where the koshi-basin route reaches the Ganges trunk after crossing the North Bihar floodplain. # Why It Matters Koshi is the clearest hazard and sediment case in the Nepal-linked downstream story. Its route matters because flood memory, embankment politics, sediment behavior, and dense settlement all meet in North Bihar before the system enters the lower Ganges. # How To Read The Marker Read it together with north-bihar-kosi-gandak-floodplain. The merge point is the route handoff; the floodplain zone is the human and agricultural exposure context. # Caveat This is not a claim that Nepal alone determines Kosi or Ganges outcomes. It is a route and exposure marker for a shared river system. # Related koshi-basin north-bihar-kosi-gandak-floodplain ganges-contribution how-to-read-geopolitics-map

pages.title: Lower Delta And Bay Interface
pages.excerpt: This dependency zone marks the coastal-delta context where river timing, sediment, salinity, and Bay of Bengal exposure meet.
pages.body_text: Lower Delta And Bay Interface This dependency zone marks the coastal-delta context where river timing, sediment, salinity, and Bay of Bengal exposure meet. # Why It Matters The lower delta is not just the end of a river. It is where upstream flow timing interacts with sediment delivery, coastal risk, storm exposure, and salinity pressure. That makes it relevant to a strategic map of Nepal-linked routes, even though it is far outside Nepal. # How To Read The Zone Read it after padma-meghna-delta-dependency and before bay-of-bengal-terminal. The route reaches the ocean; the dependency zone explains why the coastal endpoint is more than a cartographic endpoint. # Caveat The polygon is low-to-medium confidence context. It is not a salinity-front model, sediment budget, storm-surge model, or tidal channel map. # Related padma-meghna-delta padma-meghna-delta-dependency bay-of-bengal-terminal how-to-read-geopolitics-map

pages.title: Mahakali/Sharda Handoff
pages.excerpt: This marker explains where the far-western Nepal-linked route stops being only a border-river story and starts becoming part of the wider downstream Ganges geography.
pages.body_text: Mahakali/Sharda Handoff This marker explains where the far-western Nepal-linked route stops being only a border-river story and starts becoming part of the wider downstream Ganges geography. # Why It Matters The mahakali-basin is already politically sensitive because it is a border river tied to sovereignty, irrigation, and the Pancheshwar/Mahakali treaty frame. Downstream, the route joins the Sharda-Ghaghara-Ganges logic rather than staying a self-contained Nepal-India border question. # How To Read The Marker Use it as an orientation point for data-layer-nepal-origin-ocean-routes, not as a surveyed confluence coordinate. Its job is to show the western handoff from Nepal-linked hydrology into the northern plains. # Caveat This is a strategic route marker. It does not model local withdrawals, barrages, canals, or exact flow contribution. # Related mahakali-basin ghaghara-ganges-merge downstream-river-geopolitics how-to-read-geopolitics-map

pages.title: NEA Triple-Authority Conflict
headings: The incentive to favor NEA-owned generation
pages.excerpt: The NEA Triple-Authority Conflict describes how the Nepal Electricity Authority simultaneously holds three powers over private hydropower producers (IPPs) that, in a well-designed market, would be separated across independent institutions:
pages.body_text: NEA Triple-Authority Conflict The NEA Triple-Authority Conflict describes how the Nepal Electricity Authority simultaneously holds three powers over private hydropower producers (IPPs) that, in a well-designed market, would be separated across independent institutions: 1. Dispatcher — through the National Load Dispatch Centre (NLDC), NEA decides which plants run and which are curtailed. 2. Single Buyer — under the standard PPA framework, NEA is the sole off-taker. IPPs cannot sell to third parties, enter direct corporate PPAs, or export independently. 3. Penalty Administrator — NEA calculates, levies, and deducts Availability Declaration (AD) penalties from IPP receivables. The same entity controls the physical flow of electrons, the commercial flow of money, and the enforcement flow of penalties. No independent arbiter exists between these roles. # The structural double-bind When NEA exercises role (1) to curtail a project — citing grid stability, N-1 transmission limits, or low domestic demand — the project's revenue stops. When NEA then exercises role (3), it can apply AD penalties for the generation shortfall that it itself ordered. Because role (2) guarantees no alternative market, the IPP cannot recoup the loss elsewhere. The standard PPA contains no explicit exemption clause protecting an IPP from AD penalties when the root cause of the generation shortfall was an NLDC/NEA curtailment instruction. Exhaustive reviews of legacy PPA templates have not found such a carve-out. This means NEA can legally order a project off-line and legally extract cash for it being off-line, in the same transaction. # The incentive to favor NEA-owned generation NEA generates approximately 34% of national power through its own plants and subsidiaries. When NLDC dispatches an NEA-subsidiary plant, NEA pays itself — no cash leaves the institution. When NLDC dispatches a private IPP, NEA must release budget funds. The structure gives NEA a built-in incentive to prioritize its own output, particularly during periods of surplus when curtailment decisions must be made. This structural favoritism has been independently verified in academic audit. The Fenner School of Environment and Society (Australian National University), Nepal State of Knowledge Report — Renewables and PSH, states: "The major complaint of the rising IPPs is that the playing field is not level, that NEA as generator, transmitter and distributor gives preferential terms to its own projects compared to IPPs; and indeed, this is seen in the case of the Chilime hydropower company that is owned by the NEA and its staff." Citation: Fenner School of Environment and Society, ANU. URL: https://fennerschool.anu.edu.au/files/Nepal%20State%20of%20Knowledge%20Report%20-%20Renewables%20%26%20PSH.pdf. [REQUIRES EXACT CITATION: page number unconfirmed — quote verified by text extraction.] No published merit order or dispatch protocol exists. NLDC's curtailment decisions affecting billions of rupees in annual revenue are made without public criteria. Neither the Auditor General nor any parliamentary committee has formally investigated dispatch discrimination. # Primary case study: Barahi Hydropower The Barahi Hydropower case provides the wiki's primary audited documentation of this double-bind in operation. NEA deducted AD penalties from a sub-10 MW project despite an explicit ERC statutory exemption (ERC Bylaws 2076, Clause 8(2)), and the project was forced to classify the deductions as "receivable from NEA" — a forced loan to the off-taker. See ad-penalties for full case detail and barahi-hydropower for the entity page. # The AD penalty formula The standard penalty formula documented in investment and tender materials is: Penalty = (0.8 × Contracted Energy − Electricity Output) × Electricity Tariff If a project's generation falls beneath 80% of its contracted energy (or declared availability, depending on the specific PPA tier), the developer pays a penalty effectively equal to the revenue of the undelivered power. These penalties are deducted directly from trade receivables owed by NEA — not remitted to an independent fund. Industry sources assert that AD penalties can consume up to 16% of project revenue [UNVERIFIED: no statutory confirmation found; sector aggregate claim only]. The penalty impact is variable and project-specific. See ad-penalties. # The metrological impossibility The AD system requires generators to declare their available generation 30 days in advance. For a run-of-river project with no storage reservoir, predicting daily output in a Himalayan monsoon watershed 30 days ahead is not an engineering failure — it is an inherently unsolvable forecasting problem. The ERC formally acknowledged this for small projects, amending by-laws to allow plants under 10 MW to declare availability one week in advance and exempting them from financial damages. However, the Barahi case demonstrates that even explicit statutory exemption does not prevent NEA from deducting penalties when the institutional structure gives it unilateral power over cash disbursement. # Reform models A healthy market would separate the three functions: Independent system operator (ISO) — dispatch decisions separated from NEA's commercial interests. Multiple buyers or open access — IPPs able to sell to alternative off-takers (industrial consumers, export markets, trading companies). Neutral penalty authority — AD enforcement managed by the ERC or an independent entity, not the off-taker. The 2003 World Bank Nepal power sector review recommended "eliminating conflicts of interest" in NEA's structure. The draft 2025 Open Access policy envisions moving beyond the single-buyer model. No legislation implementing these reforms has been enacted. # Analytical links The Triple-Authority Conflict is the institutional mechanism underlying: stranded-generation — curtailment without compensation seasonal-arbitrage-trap — pricing structure that extracts value from IPPs ad-penalties — the penalty mechanics enforced through this conflict ppa-pricing — rate-setting by the same entity that enforces performance barahi-hydropower — primary audited case study of penalty enforcement sahas-urja — why the standard IPP rate succeeds only at exceptional scale, not as a replicable model chameliya-hydropower — state capital inefficiency absorbed off-balance sheet while private developers face rigid caps hydro-insurance — the insurance market failure that compounds the financial squeeze when NEA applies penalties during claim settlement gaps

pages.title: Nepal Power Trade Schemes
headings: Generator-facing wholesale channels named in the IPSDP
Commercial modes layered on top of those channels
Why this matters to the wiki
pages.excerpt: In Section 2.4 of moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025, the IPSDP does two different but related things. It names three generator-facing wholesale customer categories, and it separately describes the commercial modes those channels may use, including exchange-market sales and long-term
pages.body_text: Nepal Power Trade Schemes In Section 2.4 of moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025, the IPSDP does two different but related things. It names three generator-facing wholesale customer categories, and it separately describes the commercial modes those channels may use, including exchange-market sales and long-term PPAs. # Generator-facing wholesale channels named in the IPSDP Channel | Buyer | How the PDF frames it | ---------|-------|------------------------| NEA default | nea | Conventional domestic trade. NEA buys power, serves the domestic market, and exports surplus itself. Lowest administrative burden for smaller projects. | Power-trading company | Nepal trading company such as NPTCL | A future channel in which trading companies intermediate between generators and markets. The PDF explicitly says sales may be either PPA-based or market-linked. | Direct overseas trade | DISCOs or large customers in India/Bangladesh | A generator deals directly with overseas buyers. The PDF ties this to larger projects, investor participation, and more complex transmission and contracting requirements. | # Commercial modes layered on top of those channels The executive summary then adds a second distinction that should not be collapsed into the buyer categories above: Long-term PPA mode: PPAs with NEA, with a trading company, or directly with foreign distribution companies / major customers. Market-linked mode: power sold through trading-company arrangements into exchange-style markets such as IEX, including the green-market discussion in the surrounding text. That is why the section reads as more than a simple "three-scheme" menu. The PDF clearly names three buyer categories, but it also makes room for multiple price-setting and dispatch arrangements inside them. # Why this matters to the wiki It clarifies that Nepal is not moving from one monolithic single-buyer system to one monolithic export system. The IPSDP is imagining a mixed commercial stack. It sharpens india-energy-relationship and bangladesh-trade-route by showing that cross-border trade is not just a transmission problem; it is also a buyer-structure and contract-structure problem. It explains why niettp and other interconnection corridors are enabling infrastructure rather than the whole trade model by themselves. # Confidence boundary This page sticks to what the executive summary says explicitly on pages 24-26. It does not claim that Nepal has already implemented this market structure. The document is a planning frame, not proof of execution. # Related india-energy-relationship bangladesh-trade-route niettp seasonal-arbitrage-trap

pages.title: North Bihar Kosi-Gandak Floodplain
pages.excerpt: This dependency zone explains why the Kosi and Gandak routes cannot be read as simple blue lines. They pass into one of the most sensitive floodplain and agricultural geographies in the downstream story.
pages.body_text: North Bihar Kosi-Gandak Floodplain This dependency zone explains why the Kosi and Gandak routes cannot be read as simple blue lines. They pass into one of the most sensitive floodplain and agricultural geographies in the downstream story. # Why It Matters North Bihar is central to the geopolitics map because dense settlement, agriculture, sediment, embankments, and flood memory sit directly downstream of Nepal-linked river systems. # How To Read The Zone Read it with kosi-ganges-merge and gandak-ganges-merge. The zone explains exposure; the merge markers explain route handoff. # Caveat This is not a hydrodynamic or flood-risk model. It does not say Nepal controls North Bihar water outcomes. It says Nepal-linked routes pass through a high-sensitivity downstream geography. # Related koshi-basin gandaki-basin kosi-ganges-merge gandak-ganges-merge data-layer-future-regulation-scenario

pages.title: Padma-Meghna Delta Dependency
pages.excerpt: This dependency zone explains why the geopolitics map follows Nepal-linked routes into Bangladesh.
pages.body_text: Padma-Meghna Delta Dependency This dependency zone explains why the geopolitics map follows Nepal-linked routes into Bangladesh. # Why It Matters The lower delta is sensitive to flood timing, dry-season flow, sediment delivery, salinity pressure, and population exposure. Nepal is not the sole driver of any of these outcomes, but Nepal-linked river timing is part of the larger Ganges/Padma/Meghna system. # How To Read The Zone Read it with ganges-padma-transition and padma-meghna-delta. The transition marker explains route naming and border geography; the dependency zone explains why the downstream context matters. # Caveat This zone is strategic context. It is not a distributary map, salinity model, flood model, or Bangladesh water-allocation claim. # Related ganges-padma-transition padma-meghna-delta lower-delta-bay-interface bangladesh-trade-route how-to-read-geopolitics-map

pages.title: Padma-Meghna Delta
pages.excerpt: This marker explains where the shared downstream route becomes a lower-delta system rather than a single clean river line.
pages.body_text: Padma-Meghna Delta This marker explains where the shared downstream route becomes a lower-delta system rather than a single clean river line. # Why It Matters The delta is where upstream timing, sediment, monsoon flood pulses, dry-season flow, salinity pressure, and coastal exposure accumulate. That does not make Nepal the controller of the delta. It does make Nepal-linked upstream regulation part of a larger regional timing conversation. # How To Read The Marker Read this with padma-meghna-delta-dependency and lower-delta-bay-interface. The point shows route convergence; the zones show why the downstream geography is sensitive. # Caveat The v1 map does not model dense delta distributaries, tidal hydraulics, embankments, or salinity fronts. The marker is a route-reading device. # Related ganges-padma-transition padma-meghna-delta-dependency lower-delta-bay-interface bay-of-bengal-terminal

pages.title: Peak Water
headings: Mechanism: more melt before less water
Why it matters for hydropower planning
The Karnali decomposition: what "glacier-fed" actually means
pages.excerpt: Peak water (in the glacio-hydrological sense used across Hindu Kush-Himalaya work) is the period when a glacierized catchment reaches maximum annual meltwater yield before long-term decline sets in. It is not "peak" in the oil-depletion sense of a single day; it is a multi-decade
pages.body_text: Peak Water Peak water (in the glacio-hydrological sense used across Hindu Kush-Himalaya work) is the period when a glacierized catchment reaches maximum annual meltwater yield before long-term decline sets in. It is not "peak" in the oil-depletion sense of a single day; it is a multi-decade phase where retreat temporarily increases exposed ice area and melt efficiency, then yields to shrinking ice volume and lower dry-season baseflows. # Mechanism: more melt before less water As glaciers retreat, many basins first see higher annual melt — more energy at the surface, longer melt seasons, reconfiguration of flow paths — before entering a regime where ice storage is depleted and late-century flows fall, especially in the low-flow months that hydropower and irrigation care about most. For most Nepal river basins, literature and regional assessments commonly place peak water between ~2040 and 2070, with uncertainty driven by emissions pathway, debris cover, and sub-grid hypsometry. # Why it matters for hydropower planning Nepal's dominant fleet is sensitive to intra-annual and inter-annual hydrographs; see seasonal-mismatch. Peak water defines a planning window: roughly 20-40 years during which glacial contribution is near its maximum for many catchments — not a stable eternity, but a phase where high-head, glacier-fed rivers can look deceptively "strong" in mean annual flow statistics. The climate-rational engineering strategy is dual: 1. Build and finance during peak water where projects clear benefit, consent, and hazard screens — because energy and water yields for glacier-dependent schemes are comparatively favorable. 2. Design for post-peak decline — reservoir operating rules, sediment management, environmental flows, and downstream expectations must assume a worsening dry-season regime, not a stationary 20th-century hydrograph. Basin-specific discharge framing lives in koshi-basin and karnali-basin pages; cryosphere evidence synthesis is anchored on icimod-hkh-glacier-change. # Hazards ride the same trend Retreat does not only alter means; it reconfigures risk. Lake formation, moraine instability, and cascading processes connect peak-water-era construction to glof-risk: the same decades that maximize certain yields also maximize some tail-risk exposures unless design assumptions are updated. Glacial lake area has been expanding at ~0.83%/year since 1987 — a slow accumulation of potential energy with episodic catastrophic release. # Orders of magnitude for urgency Quantitative markers from recent Himalayan climate reviews and ICIMOD: Indicator | Value | Confidence | -----------|-------|------------| Nepal temperature rise rate | 0.02–0.16 °C/year (recent decades) | Moderate — regional variation wide | HKH glacier mass balance | −0.3 to −0.8 m water-equivalent/year | Moderate — peer-reviewed | Glacial lake area expansion | ~0.83%/year since 1987 | Moderate — satellite-derived | Nepal glacier area (1980s -> 2010) | 5,168.3 -> 3,902.4 km² (−24.5%) | Moderate — inventory comparison | Snow/ice cover decline (Nepal) | ~40% documented | Medium — boundary definition varies | Long-run volume loss (high emissions) | ~65% by 2100 across HKH | Low precision — model range is wide | HKH glacier area loss 1990-2020 | ~12% | Moderate — ICIMOD aggregates | The policy point is not precision to the third decimal: it is that Nepal's hydropower golden window is time-bounded and should be used to build durable domestic value, not only nameplate MW. # The Karnali decomposition: what "glacier-fed" actually means A peer-reviewed high-resolution hydrological decomposition of the Karnali basin breaks long-term discharge into contributions: Rainfall runoff: ~40% Baseflow (groundwater): ~35% Snowmelt: ~24% (peaks pre-monsoon, buffers late dry season) Glacier ice melt: ~0.8% of annual volume This is the corrective to the "glacier-fed = reliable" assumption. Glacier ice melt is a tiny share of annual Karnali flow — snowmelt and baseflow are the real dry-season buffers. As warming shifts more precipitation from snow to rain at high elevations and depletes snowpack, the dry-season buffer degrades even before glacier volume is substantially reduced. The near-term risk is loss of snowmelt buffer, not yet major glacier volume loss — the timing of this risk is earlier than most public narratives acknowledge. # Climate change and seasonal amplification Recent climate synthesis points to this directional consensus: Monsoon precipitation: likely to intensify (heavier events, more flood risk), while total annual rainfall shows mixed regional trends Snowpack: declining as warming shifts precipitation from snow to rain at progressively higher elevations Net seasonal effect: wetter wet seasons, drier dry seasons — amplifying seasonal-mismatch over time Hydropower implication: more extreme flood risk in monsoon (structural and safety design), more acute lean-season deficit (reliability design) — both costs rise simultaneously The seasonal-mismatch that already defines Nepal's energy problem today will structurally worsen over the planning horizon. Storage becomes more valuable, not less, as climate instability increases.

pages.title: PPA Pricing
headings: NEA Internal Generation Cost vs Commercial PPA Rates
NEA Subsidiary vs Private IPP Differential Analysis
The billing architecture is the mechanism, not the rate
pages.excerpt: How Power Purchase Agreement rates are set for hydropower projects in Nepal — and whether rate-setting follows an economic formula or a discretionary process. The central institutional framework is nea-triple-authority: the same entity that sets PPA rates (NEA as single buyer) is
pages.body_text: PPA Pricing How Power Purchase Agreement rates are set for hydropower projects in Nepal — and whether rate-setting follows an economic formula or a discretionary process. The central institutional framework is nea-triple-authority: the same entity that sets PPA rates (NEA as single buyer) is also the penalty enforcer and dispatcher. # PPA Rate Comparison Table Nepal's Power Purchase Agreement rates are set project-by-project through bilateral negotiation between the developer and NEA, without a publicly disclosed cost-reflective formula prior to 2026. The table below compiles verified PPA rates from primary sources. Rates marked with escalation notes reflect the initial contracted rate; actual current rates will be higher for projects within their escalation window. project | capacity_mw | ppa_wet_npr_kwh | ppa_dry_npr_kwh | ppa_blended_npr_kwh | entity_type | ppa_year | escalation | source | confidence | ---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| Chilime Hydropower | 22.1 | 4.08 (Excess) | 8.17 (Regular) | 7.57 (derived FY2078/79) | NEA_subsidiary | 1997 | No escalation clause found in legacy contract | Chilime Annual Report FY2078/79 | VERIFIED | Sanima Mai — Mai HPP | 22.0 | 4.80 (promo, expired mid-Apr 2022) → 5.08 (current) | 8.40 → 8.89 (current) | — | private_IPP | 2010 | 3% × 5 years promotional, then standard escalation | CARE Ratings Nepal Apr 2025 | VERIFIED | Sanima Mai — Mai Cascade | 7.0 | 5.52 | 9.66 | — | private_IPP | 2013 | 3% annual | CARE Ratings Nepal | VERIFIED | Rasuwagadhi | 111.0 | 4.80 | 8.40 | — | NEA_subsidiary | 2011 | 3% × 8 years | ShareSansar / NEA Annual Report | VERIFIED | Upper Trishuli 3A | 60.0 | N/A (internal transfer pricing) | N/A | N/A | NEA_owned | N/A | N/A | NEA Annual Report 2021/22 | VERIFIED | Nyadi Hydropower | 30.0 | 4.80 | 8.40 | — | private_IPP | 2015 | 3% × 8 years | CARE Ratings Nepal Oct 2024 | VERIFIED | Sahas Urja (Solu Khola) | 86.0 | 4.80 | 8.40 | ~5.1 (implied from 520 GWh design and NPR 2.648B revenue) | private_IPP | 2015 | 3% × 8 years, retained via on-time COD | ICRA Nepal A.996 and B.119 | VERIFIED | Upper Tamakoshi | 456.0 | 3.63 | 6.96 | — | NEA_subsidiary | ~2012 | — | UKHLL Annual Report | VERIFIED | Upper Chameliya (Api Power) | 40.0 | 4.00–4.80 | 7.00–8.40 | — | private_IPP | ~2020 | 3% annual | CARE/ICRA Nepal rating rationales | PARTIALLY VERIFIED | NOTE 1 — Trishuli 3A and Chameliya classification: Upper Trishuli 3A (60 MW) and Chameliya Hydropower (30 MW) are directly NEA-owned generation assets, not independent corporate entities. They use internal transfer pricing based on cost of generation rather than commercial PPAs. Trishuli 3A is frequently confused with Trishuli Jal Vidhyut Company Limited (37 MW, the 3B cascade), which IS a subsidiary entity with a standard commercial PPA at NPR 4.80/8.40. Similarly, Chameliya (NEA-owned, 30 MW, Darchula) is frequently confused with Upper Chameliya (Api Power, 40 MW, private IPP), which DOES have a commercial PPA. Prior research cycles carried an erroneous NPR 8.85/5.40 rate for Chameliya — this figure is a data hallucination derived from misattributed retail consumer tariffs in the NEA Annual Report FY2016/17. Chameliya has no commercial PPA. Source: NEA Generation Directorate Report 2080/2081 (16th Issue, 2024). See chameliya-hydropower. NOTE 2 — Sanima Mai promotional tariff expiry: The Mai HPP (22 MW) initially operated under a government promotional tariff of NPR 4.80 wet / 8.40 dry. This expired mid-April 2022 after 7 years from COD. The current active rate of NPR 5.08/8.89 reflects accumulated escalations post-expiry. The promotional tariff mechanism — and its expiry — is itself a structural feature of Nepal's PPA framework, not an anomaly. NOTE 3 — Sahas Urja reframing: Sahas Urja operates on the identical base rate as Nyadi and Rasuwagadhi (NPR 4.80/8.40). Its superior financial performance (NPR 902M net profit on NPR 2.648B revenue, FY2023/24) is entirely attributable to cost efficiency (NPR 172–175M/MW build cost vs industry average NPR 200–300M/MW) and scale (86 MW generating large absolute dry-season cash flows despite a ~19% dry energy mix). Sahas Urja does not demonstrate that the standard IPP PPA rate is viable — it demonstrates that the rate is only viable at exceptional scale and cost discipline unavailable to most developers. See sahas-urja. NOTE 4 — Upper Chameliya seasonal modality: Unlike the standard 8-month wet / 4-month dry split, Api Power's Upper Chameliya PPA uses a 6-month wet / 6-month dry modality, with the dry-season rate mathematically double the wet-season rate. This significantly bolsters the blended tariff average and enhances cash flow for commercial debt service. Source: CARE Ratings Nepal. [PARTIALLY VERIFIED: exact base rate is a range rather than single confirmed figure — CARE/ICRA rationales give wet ~NPR 4.00–4.80, dry ~NPR 7.00–8.40.] -- # NEA Internal Generation Cost vs Commercial PPA Rates Directly NEA-owned generation assets do not operate under commercial PPAs. They use internal transfer pricing based on operational cost, royalty, and allocated depreciation — accounting figures that bear no relation to the full capital cost of the asset. These internal generation costs are structurally incomparable to commercial PPA rates and should not appear in rate comparison tables alongside IPP or subsidiary PPAs. The table below shows FY2080/81 internal generation costs for five major NEA-owned assets: NEA Direct Asset | Capacity (MW) | FY2080/81 Generation (MWh) | Total Cost (Million NPR) | Generation Cost (NPR/Unit) | ---|---|---|---|---| Kaligandaki 'A' | 144 | 841,596 | 1,805.87 | 2.15 | Middle Marsyangdi | 70 | 441,127 | 1,684.58 | 3.82 | Upper Trishuli 3A | 60 | 427,055 | 538.62 | 1.26 | Chameliya | 30 | 133,968 | 406.66 | 3.04 | Trishuli | 24 | 117,042 | 316.41 | 2.70 | Source: NEA Generation Directorate Report 2080/2081 (16th Issue, 2024). Why these figures cannot be compared to commercial PPA rates: NEA internal generation costs reflect post-subsidy accounting where sovereign capital failures are absorbed off-balance sheet. The state utility tolerates catastrophic inefficiency in its own construction — Chameliya cost $7.5M/MW vs the global benchmark of $1.5–2.5M/MW — without letting those financial failures reflect in the energy pricing metric. Simultaneously, the NEA enforces rigid PPA caps on private developers who must fully amortize commercial debt at double-digit interest rates. A private IPP that suffered Chameliya's $226M cost overrun on a 30 MW asset would be insolvent at any standard PPA rate. The NEA's own asset registers that same energy at NPR 3.04/kWh. This asymmetry is the quantitative core of the nea-triple-authority governance problem. See chameliya-hydropower. # Rate-Setting Mechanism and Regulatory History The Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC) now vests with rate-setting authority. The 2026 Directive on reservoir-based PPAs transitions large projects (>100 MW) to a transparent cost-plus determination model — factoring in loan interest, depreciation, foreign exchange risks, and hedging costs. Sub-100 MW reservoir projects are capped at NPR 14.80/kWh (winter) and NPR 8.45/kWh (monsoon). Legacy contracts like Chilime's 1997 PPA remain grandfathered into the system, perpetually skewing the competitive balance. [Under research — needs full regulatory citation, ERC Act section references, and directive document name.] # Royalty and Taxation Schedule Years 1–15: NPR 100/kW capacity royalty + 2% of average tariff as energy royalty. Years 16+: NPR 1,000/kW capacity royalty + 10% of tariff. Corporate income tax is 20% with a 100% rebate for the first 10 years of operation and 50% rebate in years 11–15. Free-power provisions (typically 10–22% of generation to the government) are negotiated project-by-project in concession PPAs rather than codified in statute. At end of license (30–35 years), the plant transfers to the state. [Under research — needs Electricity Act section citation and free-power provision statutory basis.] # NEA Subsidiary vs Private IPP Differential Analysis The PPA rate comparison table reveals a structural divergence in how NEA treats its own subsidiaries versus private IPPs. This differential is not executed through a published discriminatory rate card — the Electricity Act theoretically mandates uniform treatment — but through bespoke contract structuring that allows subsidiaries to bypass seasonal pricing constraints. ## The mathematical proof Take the two clearest comparable cases: NEA subsidiary — Chilime Hydropower (22.1 MW): Chilime's 1997 PPA discards the wet/dry seasonal constraint entirely. Instead, it uses a "Regular" and "Excess" quota system. In FY2078/79, Chilime generated 156.06 GWh — and 85.4% of it (133.24 GWh) was billed at the premium NPR 8.17/kWh Regular rate year-round, regardless of season. Only 22.82 GWh was classified as Excess at NPR 4.08/kWh. Total revenue: NPR 1.18 billion. Blended rate: NPR 7.57/kWh. Private IPP baseline (Nyadi/Sahas Urja model): A standard private RoR IPP is locked into NPR 4.80/kWh (wet) and NPR 8.40/kWh (dry). Assuming an optimized 20% dry energy yield — generous for most Nepalese rivers — the blended rate is: (0.20 × 8.40) + (0.80 × 4.80) = NPR 5.52/kWh. The differential: NPR 2.05/kWh = 37.1% revenue advantage per unit for the NEA subsidiary. For a 50 MW comparable private IPP generating 200 GWh/year, this represents approximately NPR 410 million in foregone annual revenue relative to an identically-sized project that received the Chilime billing architecture. ## The billing architecture is the mechanism, not the rate Chilime's per-unit rates (8.17/4.08) are not dramatically higher than the private IPP cap (8.40/4.80). The advantage comes from classification escape: Chilime's PPA categorizes output by "Regular" vs "Excess" rather than by season, allowing the vast majority of its generation to bill at the premium rate continuously. A private IPP cannot reclassify its monsoon output this way — the season determines the rate. ## Independent academic verification The Fenner School of Environment and Society (Australian National University), Nepal State of Knowledge Report — Renewables and PSH, independently documents this dynamic: "The major complaint of the rising IPPs is that the playing field is not level, that NEA as generator, transmitter and distributor gives preferential terms to its own projects compared to IPPs; and indeed, this is seen in the case of the Chilime hydropower company that is owned by the NEA and its staff." Citation: Fenner School of Environment and Society, ANU. URL: https://fennerschool.anu.edu.au/files/Nepal%20State%20of%20Knowledge%20Report%20-%20Renewables%20%26%20PSH.pdf. [REQUIRES EXACT CITATION: page number unconfirmed — quote verified by text extraction.] ## Legacy contract grandfathering Chilime's 1997 PPA remains fully active and has not been downgraded to match contemporary IPP standards. The ERC now vests with rate-setting authority and the 2026 Directive on reservoir-based PPAs transitions large projects to cost-plus determination, but legacy contracts like Chilime's are grandfathered — perpetually skewing the market's competitive balance. See chilime for the full PPA billing architecture and nea-triple-authority for the institutional framework. # Analytical links nea-triple-authority — the institutional conflict that pervades PPA rate-setting, dispatch, and enforcement chilime — the primary documented case of subsidiary PPA rate advantage sahas-urja — why the standard IPP rate only works at exceptional scale q-design-discharge — how design discharge determines contracted energy (the denominator of all PPA economics) ad-penalties — the penalty mechanism that compounds the PPA rate disadvantage

pages.title: Q-Design Discharge
headings: Generation performance: what projects actually deliver
The Chilime anomaly: why it overperforms and why it is not replicable
pages.excerpt: Q-design discharge is the regulatory parameter that mechanically determines a hydropower project's installed capacity and its Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) contracted energy obligation. A design discharge set too high — based on optimistic or outdated hydrology — inflates both t
pages.body_text: Q-Design Discharge Q-design discharge is the regulatory parameter that mechanically determines a hydropower project's installed capacity and its Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) contracted energy obligation. A design discharge set too high — based on optimistic or outdated hydrology — inflates both the turbine size and the annual generation promise, locking the developer into performance targets that the river cannot sustain. # What Q_x means In hydrology, Q_x denotes the flow rate (m³/s) that is exceeded X% of the time in the historical record. Q40 is the flow equaled or exceeded 40% of days; Q65 is exceeded 65% of days.
