Stacked bar by province — split between domestic/industrial/commercial/other if provincial-level splits become available # Related data-domestic-demand — national sectoral demand split data-industrial-electricity-consumers — which industries are the top consumers data-agricultural-energy-consumption — agricultural consumption, important in Terai provinces nea — the distribution utility nepal-energy-profile — country-level system snapshot

pages.title: Domestic Electricity Demand Distribution
headings: The Electrification Gaps — Where Demand Is Not Yet Created
Electric cooking: the largest untapped demand
Residential electricity still dominated by lighting
pages.excerpt: Where Nepal's electricity actually goes — by sector, by consumer category, and how fast it's growing. Central evidence for domestic-led-hydro-strategy and the seasonal-arbitrage-trap value-capture argument.
pages.body_text: Domestic Electricity Demand Distribution Where Nepal's electricity actually goes — by sector, by consumer category, and how fast it's growing. Central evidence for domestic-led-hydro-strategy and the seasonal-arbitrage-trap value-capture argument. # Sectoral Demand Split — FY 2024/25 (NEA) Total electricity sales: 11,288 GWh (including internal consumption). Total consumers: 5,707,528. Source: NEA Annual Report FY 2024/25, Distribution & Consumer Services Directorate. Category | Share of Consumers | Share of Sales | Share of Revenue | Sales (GWh approx.) | ----------|------:|------:|------:|------:| Domestic (residential) | 91.08% | 42.02% | 39.05% | ~4,742 | Industrial | 1.23% | 36.64% | 36.52% | ~4,136 | Commercial | 0.77% | 7.79% | 11.33% | ~879 | Non-Commercial | 0.72% | 3.07% | 4.47% | ~347 | Others (streetlight, irrigation, temp, etc.) | 6.21% | 10.48% | 8.62% | ~1,183 | [!finding] Industry is 1.23% of customers, 36.64% of consumption The revenue and sales shares are nearly identical (36.64% vs 36.52%), indicating industrial customers face average tariffs close to the system average (~NRs 9.44/kWh). Combined, commercial + industrial = 44.43% of sales from only 1.99% of customers. This concentration makes industrial load the highest-leverage demand lever: winning or losing a few large industrial users has an outsized system effect. See data-industrial-electricity-consumers for the sub-sector breakdown. # Sectoral Shift — FY 2020/21 vs FY 2024/25 Category | FY 2020/21 Sales | FY 2024/25 Sales | Change | CAGR | ----------|------:|------:|------:|------:| Domestic | ~3,138 GWh (43.1%) | ~4,742 GWh (42.0%) | +51% | ~10.8% | Industrial | ~2,816 GWh (38.7%) | ~4,136 GWh (36.6%) | +47% | ~10.1% | Commercial | ~511 GWh (7.0%) | ~879 GWh (7.8%) | +72% | ~14.5% | Non-Commercial | ~204 GWh (2.8%) | ~347 GWh (3.1%) | +70% | ~14.2% | Others | ~614 GWh (8.4%) | ~1,183 GWh (10.5%) | +93% | ~17.8% | Total | ~7,277 GWh | ~11,288 GWh | +55% | ~11.6% | [!note] Data comparison notes FY 2020/21 figures are from NEA Distribution and Consumer Services Directorate as cited in earlier wiki pages (sectoral shares applied to ~7,277 GWh sales). FY 2024/25 uses the same source methodology (NEA DCSD). The two data points span approximately 4 Nepali fiscal years. Per-capita consumption rose from ~370 to 465 kWh over the same window. Three pattern observations: 1. Domestic and industrial grew at roughly the same pace (~10-11% CAGR). This is notable given the WB CEM finding that manufacturing output fell to ~6% of GDP — implying industrial electricity growth may reflect process switching (electric vs thermal) and/or new capacity rather than manufacturing expansion. 2. "Others" is the fastest-growing category (+93%), driven by streetlight electrification, irrigation pump connections, and temporary construction supply — the long tail of grid extension. 3. Domestic share is gently declining (43.1% → 42.0%) while industrial share is also declining (38.7% → 36.6%). The growth is in commercial, non-commercial, and others — the smaller categories gaining share as electrification deepens beyond basic household connections. This is structurally healthy: it means the grid is reaching firms, institutions, and public infrastructure, not just homes. # Total System Energy Balance ## FY 2024/25 Metric | Value | --------|-------| Total available energy | 15,641 GWh | NEA + subsidiaries share | 34% | IPP share | 55% | Import from India share | 11% | Total domestic consumption | 11,343 GWh | Export to India | 2,380 GWh | Net export | 699 GWh | System peak demand | 2,901 MW (Jul 1, 2025) | National peak demand | 2,409 MW (Jun 10, 2025) | ## FY 2020/21 (for comparison) Metric | Value | --------|-------| Total consumption | 7,319 GWh | NEA own generation share | 31.66% | IPP generation share | 36.51% | Import from India share | 31.83% | System loss | 17.18% | [!warning] The import-to-exporter transformation is real but recent In FY 2020/21, Nepal imported nearly one-third of its electricity. By FY 2024/25, imports fell to 11% of available energy while exports hit 2,380 GWh — a structural shift driven by ~1,400+ MW of new IPP hydro commissioned in the intervening years. However, this net-export status is monsoon-weighted: dry-season imports persist at the margin. The transformation is fragile and depends on continued generation additions outpacing demand growth. System losses also improved from 17.18% (FY 2020/21) to 10.39% at the distribution level in FY 2024/25 — a meaningful gain. # Per-Capita Consumption Trend Year | Per-capita (kWh/yr) | Source | ------|------:|--------| 2012 | ~100 | Estimated | 2018 | ~200 | WB CEM baseline | FY 2020/21 | ~370 | NEA DCSD | FY 2023/24 | ~400 | NEA Annual Report | FY 2024/25 | 465 | NEA Annual Report | [!note] At 465 kWh/capita, Nepal is still roughly 5–7× below middle-income norms (2,000–3,000 kWh/yr) and ~60× below its theoretical hydropower potential per capita (~29,400 kWh/yr). The gap between current consumption and a plausible electrified economy is the central demand-creation opportunity in domestic-led-hydro-strategy. # Load Profile — Evening Peak Dominates Monthly operational reports show peak demand consistently hitting 18:30–19:25 — the daily cooking-and-lighting window. This pattern persists in FY 2024/25: System peak of 2,901 MW recorded on July 1, 2025 (13 Jestha 2082) — of which 2,214 MW was internal demand and 687 MW exported National peak of 2,409 MW recorded on June 10, 2025 Winter peak hours still require import support: structural mismatch between RoR flat-ish output and evening spike The structural features have not changed since FY 2020/21: Run-of-river hydro generates at near-constant output (flow-following) Demand spikes in the evening when people return home The mismatch is most acute in dry-season evenings, when hydro output is at its annual floor and demand at its daily peak This evening peak pattern is what makes storage + solar the physical complement to the hydro fleet. See data-nepal-peak-load-curve-fy2024-25 for the annual load shape. # The Electrification Gaps — Where Demand Is Not Yet Created ## Electric cooking: the largest untapped demand Electric cooking used by 0.5% of households (2021 WB estimate, up from 0.1% in 2011) Biomass (firewood) and imported LPG dominate cooking energy NEA's FY 2024/25 report explicitly flags government policy to "encourage the use of electricity for cooking, transportation, and industry to reduce reliance on fossil fuels" The strategic implication: if Nepal could shift even 20–30% of cooking to electricity, it would create ~50–75 PJ of new electric demand — roughly doubling current electricity consumption. That is bigger than any plausible export contract. See energy-substitution-pathway for the full substitution logic. ## Residential electricity still dominated by lighting Household electricity use has tripled since 2010, but remains only ~5% of residential energy (the rest is biomass + LPG) 92.2% of households used electricity for lighting in 2021 (up from 67.3% in 2011) — lighting drove the first wave of residential electrification The second wave — cooking, heating, appliances — has barely started # Value-Capture Framing Electricity "captures value" when: 1. It sells scarce kWh at high value (dry-season firm power, peak hours) 2. It buys deficit kWh at low cost (off-peak imports when available) 3. It uses the bulk of output domestically in high-productivity activities Nepal currently does (1) partially (improving IEX spreads) and (2) adequately in monsoon (exports at NPR 7.11/unit average) but still pays a winter scarcity tax. On (3) — the domestic transformation pathway — the electric cooking statistic (0.5% of households) captures the scale of the gap. The deepest value-capture move is not better export contracts. It is electrifying cooking, industrial heat, and transport at scale — turning Nepal from a seasonal kWh exporter into a country where electricity is the backbone of economic productivity. See consumption-trajectory-outlook for the forward view. # Linked Data nea_monthly_energy_balance_fy2024_2025.csv — monthly demand, imports, storage generation, and supply composition. nea_monthly_capacity_balance_fy2024_2025.csv — monthly capacity-side view of imports, storage MW, and peak demand. # Chart Specification 1. Pie chart — sectoral sales split FY 2024/25 (domestic 42% / industrial 37% / commercial 8% / other 13%) 2. Time-series bar chart — sectoral consumption 2020/21 vs 2024/25, showing absolute growth and share shift 3. Per-capita line chart — Nepal 2012 → 2025 (100 → 465 kWh), with middle-income benchmark line at 2,500 kWh 4. Load profile curve — daily MW demand showing 18:30–19:25 peak vs flat-ish RoR output # Related data-consumption-geographic-distribution — provincial/regional breakdown data-industrial-electricity-consumers — which industries are the top consumers data-agricultural-energy-consumption — agricultural energy profile data-final-energy-mix — the total-energy denominator energy-substitution-pathway — what non-electric flows electrification would displace consumption-trajectory-outlook — forward growth outlook seasonal-arbitrage-trap — the trade cost of not building domestic demand domestic-led-hydro-strategy — the strategic response stranded-generation — monsoon surplus that domestic load could absorb nea — the utility that must enable this demand growth

pages.title: Nepal Final Energy Mix
headings: The five structural facts in this table
1. Fuelwood in household cooking is the single biggest energy flow in the country
2. Industry is 21%, but it's not factories — it's bricks, cement, and steel rerolling
3. Transport is 10.4%, and it is essentially 100% imported fossil
4. Electricity is where Nepal's sector-mix hides a surprise
5. The "hydro share" of Nepal's total energy
pages.excerpt: The full fuel × sector matrix of Nepal's final energy consumption, straight from the WECS Energy Sector Synopsis 2024 (FY 2079/80). This is the page that answers "how much of Nepal's total energy is actually hydro?" with a number other than the one everyone quotes. It is the deno
pages.body_text: Nepal Final Energy Mix The full fuel × sector matrix of Nepal's final energy consumption, straight from the WECS Energy Sector Synopsis 2024 (FY 2079/80). This is the page that answers "how much of Nepal's total energy is actually hydro?" with a number other than the one everyone quotes. It is the denominator for every serious claim in master-thesis and domestic-led-hydro-strategy. # Headline numbers (FY 2079/80 / ~2022–23) Metric | Value | --------|-------| Total final energy consumption | 532.42 PJ (532,420 TJ) | Year-on-year change | −16.81% vs FY 2078/79 (640 PJ) | Traditional biomass share | 63.87% (340.04 PJ) | Commercial fuel share (excl. electricity) | 25.80% (137.38 PJ) | Grid electricity share | 7.23% (38.49 PJ) | Modern renewable share | 3.10% (16.50 PJ) | Hydro share of total final energy | ~6.8% (electricity × 94.4% hydro) | [!note] Correction to older wiki pages Earlier pages (nepal-energy-profile, data-domestic-demand, domestic-led-hydro-strategy) cite grid electricity as 4.96% of final energy. That figure was carried over from the earlier 2020/21 balance quoted in explanatory prose. The official FY 2079/80 figure is 7.23%. Both are internally consistent with their respective years; the jump reflects genuine electrification (+21.18% electricity consumption YoY) combined with a drop in coal (−41.5%) and petroleum (−16.1%). # Axis 1 — Consumption by source Source | TJ | Share | Category | --------|------:|------:|----------| Fuelwood | 304,625 | 57.22% | Traditional biomass | Diesel | 49,728 | 9.34% | Petroleum | Grid electricity | 38,490 | 7.23% | Commercial | Coal | 34,016 | 6.39% | Commercial | Agricultural residue (incl. bagasse) | 25,623 | 4.81% | Traditional biomass | LPG | 23,677 | 4.45% | Petroleum (imported) | Petrol | 21,932 | 4.12% | Petroleum | Biogas | 10,722 | 2.01% | Modern renewable | Animal dung | 9,791 | 1.84% | Traditional biomass | ATF (jet fuel) | 6,191 | 1.16% | Petroleum | Other petroleum (furnace oil, kerosene) | ~1,840 | 0.35% | Petroleum | Modern renewable non-biogas (solar, micro-hydro, wind) | ~5,800 | 1.09% | Modern renewable | Total | 532,420 | 100% | | # Axis 2 — Consumption by sector × fuel (TJ) Decoded from WECS 2024 Table 4 / Chapter 4.2.2 cross-tab. All rows sum to within rounding of the published sector totals. Sector | Fuelwood | Ag res | Dung | Petrol | Diesel | Kero | LPG | ATF | Coal | Electricity | Biogas | Solar/µhydro/wind | Total | Share | --------|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:|------:| Residential | 257,226 | 8,909 | 9,486 | 0 | 111 | 0 | 18,174 | 0 | 35 | 16,139 | 10,714 | 2,669 | 323,463 | 60.75% | Industrial | 31,894 | 16,337 | 0 | 2,502 | 11,646 | 296 | 765 | 0 | 33,622 | 12,874 | 0 | 0 | 111,306 | 20.91% | Transportation | 0 | 0 | 0 | 18,862 | 30,473 | 2 | 2 | 6,191 | 0 | 25 | 0 | 0 | 55,553 | 10.43% | Commercial | 15,414 | 377 | 305 | 175 | 0 | 47 | 4,413 | 0 | 359 | 2,653 | 9 | 3,082 | 26,835 | 5.04% | Construction/Mining | 91 | 0 | 0 | 331 | 3,789 | 18 | 322 | 0 | 0 | 5,670 | 0 | 0 | 10,222 | 1.92% | Agriculture | 0 | 0 | 0 | 61 | 3,819 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1,134 | 0 | 26 | 5,040 | 0.95% | Total | 304,625 | 25,623 | 9,791 | 21,932 | 49,728 | 363 | 23,676 | 6,191 | 34,016 | 38,495 | 10,723 | 5,777 | 532,420 | 100% | ("Other petroleum" — furnace oil (1,369 TJ in industry) and kerosene totals — is folded into the petroleum columns above; small residual rounding is in the totals row.) # The five structural facts in this table ## 1. Fuelwood in household cooking is the single biggest energy flow in the country 257,226 TJ of fuelwood burned in residential use is 48.3% of all final energy consumed in Nepal. Not industry, not transport. Kitchens. See data-domestic-demand for the companion "<1% of households cook with electricity" fact — together these two numbers are the central feature of Nepal's energy system, not a footnote. ## 2. Industry is 21%, but it's not factories — it's bricks, cement, and steel rerolling Inside the 111.3 PJ industrial sector: Coal 33.6 PJ (30.2%) — this is ~99% of all coal consumed in Nepal, almost entirely brick kilns (fixed-chimney Bull's-trench type, ~1,500+ operating), cement clinker, and iron/steel rerolling. Biomass 48.2 PJ (43.3%) — fuelwood + bagasse. Long tail of small plants: tea dryers, sugar mills, cardamom dryers, food processing, textiles. Petroleum 16.6 PJ (14.9%) — diesel gensets (captive power), process heat. A direct reliability cost of the grid, linked to stranded-generation. Electricity 12.9 PJ (11.6%) — the grid touches only ~12% of industrial energy. Electrification of industry is largely still ahead of Nepal. Industry is bigger than a casual reader expects precisely because Nepal's energy statistics classify brick kilns and a long tail of small biomass-fed plants as "industrial." Few of them are large factories in the modern sense. ## 3. Transport is 10.4%, and it is essentially 100% imported fossil 55,528 TJ petroleum vs 25 TJ electricity in transport. Split inside petroleum: Diesel 54.9% (trucks, buses, tractors) Petrol 34.0% (motorcycles, cars) ATF 11.1% (aviation — all of Nepal's aviation fuel, 6,191 TJ) Electricity 0.05% (trolley bus + statistical noise of early EVs) Transport is ~10% of energy volume but close to 40–50% of the foreign-exchange cost of Nepal's energy system, because every drop is imported from India via the Motihari–Amlekhgunj pipeline (operational 2019) plus road tankers. ## 4. Electricity is where Nepal's sector-mix hides a surprise Electricity shares within each sector: Sector | Electricity as % of sector energy | --------|------:| Construction/Mining | 55.5% — stone crushers, quarries, cement grinding | Agriculture | 22.5% — tubewells, some pumping | Industrial | 11.6% | Commercial | 9.9% | Residential | 5.0% | Transportation | 0.05% | Construction/mining is the only sector in Nepal that is already majority-electric. Residential (the biggest sector) is only 5% electric because wood still does the cooking and heating. See energy-substitution-pathway for the implications. ## 5. The "hydro share" of Nepal's total energy Hydro is 94.4% of installed electricity capacity (see nepal-energy-profile, data-fleet-composition). Electricity is 7.23% of final energy. So: Hydro ≈ 6.8% of Nepal's final energy. Solar PV (~0.6%), biogas (2.0%), and micro-hydro (~0.4%) add ~3% more. The other ~90% of Nepal's final energy is still biomass + fossil. This number collapses the "Nepal is a hydropower country" frame into something more honest: Nepal has a hydropower electricity system. Its total energy system is still a biomass-and-petroleum system with an electric overlay that is growing fast but starts from ~7%. The solar section should be read against this denominator. Today's solar fleet is too small to move final-energy shares by itself, but its marginal value is high because it lands in the binding season and can be built faster than large hydro. solar-in-the-master-narrative is therefore not a claim that solar dominates Nepal's energy balance; it is a claim that solar changes the next marginal investment decision. # Historical trajectory Year | Total (PJ) | Fuelwood share | Electricity share | Source | ------|------:|------:|------:|--------| 2008/09 | 401 | 77.7% | <2% (elec+coal=4%) | WECS 2010 synopsis | 2019 | 589 | 62.0% | 3.9% | WECS historical series | 2020 | 566 | 64.9% | 4.1% | WECS | 2021 | 626 | 60.4% | ~4.2% | WECS | 2021/22 | 640 | ~60% | 4.96% | WECS (quoted on earlier wiki pages) | 2022/23 (FY 2079/80) | 532 | 57.22% | 7.23% | wecs-energy-synopsis-2024 | The 16.81% drop in total consumption in FY 2079/80 vs FY 2078/79 is striking. WECS attributes it primarily to sharp declines in coal (−41.5%) and petroleum (−16.1%) imports, partially offset by electricity growth (+21.2%). Whether this reflects genuine substitution or one-off import-compression from 2022's FX crisis is a forward-research question — the FY 2080/81 balance will tell us. # Chart Specification Three public-facing chart panels: 1. Sankey — sources → sectors, widths in TJ. The single fattest stream is "Fuelwood → Residential" (257 PJ). "Coal → Industrial" (33.6 PJ) and "Diesel → Transportation" (30.5 PJ) are the next two fattest. Hydro/electricity threads are visually thin across the whole diagram. This is the single most powerful image for the essay's pivot from "hydro story" to "energy story." 2. Stacked 100% bar per sector — show that residential is dominated by biomass, industrial by coal+biomass, transport by petroleum, while only construction/mining and (to a lesser extent) agriculture are electricity-majority. 3. Time-series — total PJ and electricity share, 2009 → 2023, showing the ~5x slow rise of electricity share against nearly flat fuelwood share. # Linked Source wecs-energy-synopsis-2024 — full report; cross-tab is in Chapter 4.2.2 and Appendix Table 4. # Related energy-substitution-pathway — the "three sinks to displace" strategic read of this table nepal-energy-profile — country-level snapshot of the electricity system data-domestic-demand — sectoral electricity sales and the electric-cooking gap data-fleet-composition — what's inside that 7.23% electricity slice domestic-led-hydro-strategy — the strategic implication: displace biomass + fossil with hydro-electric loads green-hydrogen-nepal — one of the few paths to displace industrial coal (brick kilns, cement) solar-in-the-master-narrative — why a small current share can still matter at the margin master-thesis — the denominator this table provides

pages.title: Hydropower Fleet Composition
pages.excerpt: Nepal's installed hydropower fleet, broken down by technology type. The RoR dominance is the structural cause of seasonal-mismatch in generation.
pages.body_text: Hydropower Fleet Composition Nepal's installed hydropower fleet, broken down by technology type. The RoR dominance is the structural cause of seasonal-mismatch in generation. # Fleet by Type Type | Share of Installed Capacity | Characteristics | Source | ------|----------------------------|-----------------|--------| Run-of-River (RoR) | ~85.7% | No storage; output mirrors river flow | NEA FY 2022/23 review | Peaking Run-of-River (PRoR) | ~9.9% | Small daily pondage; 2–6 hrs peaking | NEA FY 2022/23 review | Reservoir / Storage | ~3.7% | Seasonal regulation; only kulekhani-cascade | NEA FY 2022/23 review | [!note] Public summaries use the broader ">90% RoR+PRoR" framing from wb-country-economic-memo-2025. Both framings are consistent. The granular breakdown is the most granular. # Installed Capacity Timeline Date | Total Installed (MW) | Source | ------|---------------------|--------| FY 2022/23 | ~2,800 | NEA FY 2022/23 | End 2024 | ~2,990 | wb-country-economic-memo-2025 | FY 2024/25 | 3,591.262 (total) / 3,389.912 (hydro) | nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 | Apr 10 2026 | 3,791.874 (>1 MW hydro plants in DoED registry) | DoED registry snapshot used in nepal-energy-profile | # Generation Seasonal Split (FY 2022/23) Season | Hydro Generation (GWh) | Share | --------|----------------------|-------| Dry season | 2,437 | ~27% | Wet season | 6,533 | ~73% | Wet/dry ratio | 2.68x | | [!cite] fleet composition review "RoR alone delivered only about 24.5% of its annual generation in dry season." # Ownership Split IPPs: 64% of installed hydropower by 2024 NEA and subsidiaries: remaining ~36% >70% of capacity added since 2018 came from IPPs # Linked Data nea_monthly_capacity_balance_fy2024_2025.csv — monthly capacity contribution by IPPs, NEA, imports, and storage. naxa_hydropower_projects.csv — 572 project records with coordinates, license stage, promoter, and river; useful for separating operating hydro from the broader development pipeline. [!warning] The project CSV is a licensing/pipeline dataset, not a clean "currently installed operational fleet" table. Use it for geography, corridor clustering, and license-stage mix, not as a substitute for NEA installed-capacity accounting. # Linked Figures nea_capacity_mix.png — capacity mix visual. license_type_mix.png — pipeline split by license stage. top_operational_projects.png — concentration of operating MW in a small set of plants. # Chart Specification Stacked bar chart: RoR / PRoR / Storage shares of installed capacity, with a second bar showing their shares of dry-season generation. The disparity between capacity share and dry-season contribution makes firm-power vs MW distinction visually clear.

pages.title: Industrial Electricity Consumers in Nepal
headings: Industrial Energy Mix — All Fuels (FY 2079/80, WECS)
Tier 1 — Large electro-intensive (>500 GWh/yr each, estimated)
Tier 2 — Medium industrial (100–500 GWh/yr each, estimated)
Tier 3 — Small industrial (<100 GWh/yr each, estimated)
Structural Features of Nepal's Industrial Electricity Demand
1. It's a few large consumers, not a broad base
2. Industry is underelectrified compared to peers
3. Captive diesel generation is a hidden consumption layer
4. Manufacturing has been shrinking, not growing
pages.excerpt: Which industries consume Nepal's electricity — and which ones burn coal and biomass instead. The companion page to data-domestic-demand and the industrial-demand half of energy-substitution-pathway.
pages.body_text: Industrial Electricity Consumers in Nepal Which industries consume Nepal's electricity — and which ones burn coal and biomass instead. The companion page to data-domestic-demand and the industrial-demand half of energy-substitution-pathway. # Headline Numbers Metric | Value | Source | --------|-------|--------| Industrial electricity sales (FY 2024/25) | ~4,136 GWh | NEA Annual Report | Share of total electricity sales | 36.64% | NEA DCSD | Industrial consumer count | ~70,000 (1.23% of 5.7M) | NEA DCSD | Average per-industrial-consumer | ~59,000 kWh/yr | Calculated | Industrial total energy (all fuels, FY 2079/80) | 111,306 TJ (~30,918 GWh) | WECS 2024 | Electricity share of industrial total energy | 11.6% (12,874 TJ) | WECS 2024 | Industrial revenue from electricity (FY 2024/25) | NPR 39.88 billion (36.52% of total) | NEA DCSD | Outstanding industrial dues (FY 2024/25) | NPR 29.42 billion | NEA finance section | [!finding] Industry uses 11× more energy from non-electric sources than from the grid The 4,136 GWh of industrial electricity (~12,874 TJ) sits inside a total industrial energy consumption of 111,306 TJ. Coal + biomass + petroleum supply ~88.4% of industry's energy. The electrification of Nepal's industry — brick kilns, cement clinker, process heat — is still largely ahead of the country. See energy-substitution-pathway for the displacement strategy. # Industrial Energy Mix — All Fuels (FY 2079/80, WECS) Fuel | TJ | Share of Industrial Total | Typical Users | ------|------:|------:|--------------| Coal | 33,622 | 30.2% | Brick kilns, cement clinker, steel rerolling | Fuelwood | 31,894 | 28.7% | Tea dryers, food processing, textiles | Agricultural residue (bagasse) | 16,337 | 14.7% | Sugar mills, small agro-processing | Diesel + furnace oil + other petroleum | 16,582 | 14.9% | Captive generators, process heat, kilns | Electricity | 12,874 | 11.6% | Motors, lighting, grinding, induction, electrolysis | LPG + petrol | 477 | 0.4% | Forklifts, small equipment | Total | 111,306 | 100% | | [!note] The coal figure (~33,600 TJ) represents ~99% of all coal consumed in Nepal. Brick kilns alone are estimated at ~1,500+ operational (mostly fixed-chimney Bull's-trench type), concentrated in the Kathmandu Valley airshed and Terai districts. # Electricity-Consuming Industries — Estimated Profile NEA does not publish an industry-level sub-sector breakdown of electricity sales. The following profile is constructed from the total-energy fuel × sector table (WECS 2024), the WB CEM manufacturing analysis, and known industrial structure. GWh figures are indicative estimates only. ## Tier 1 — Large electro-intensive (>500 GWh/yr each, estimated) Industry | Estimated Electricity (GWh) | Basis | Notes | ----------|------:|-------|-------| Cement grinding & clinker plants | ~800–1,200 | ~40+ registered grinding units; grinding is electric-intensive (~110 kWh/tonne), clinker firing uses coal | Nepal produces ~5–6 million tonnes/yr; per-tonne electricity for grinding is well-documented | Iron/steel rerolling & induction furnaces | ~700–1,000 | ~30+ rerolling mills using electric induction/arc | Produces ~500,000+ tonnes/yr construction steel; induction is ~400–700 kWh/tonne | Cold storage & refrigeration | ~400–600 | ~200+ cold stores, mostly for potato/vegetable/fruit | High baseline load, significant in Terai where potato farming is concentrated | ## Tier 2 — Medium industrial (100–500 GWh/yr each, estimated) Industry | Estimated Electricity (GWh) | Basis | Notes | ----------|------:|-------|-------| Food & beverage processing | ~300–500 | Flour mills, dairy plants, bottling, edible oil, noodles, biscuits | Highly distributed; flour mills (~3,000+ small/medium) alone consume significant power | Textiles, carpets & garments | ~200–400 | Carpet weaving, garment factories, spinning mills | Nepal's single largest manufacturing export category; concentrated in Kathmandu Valley | Ferroalloys / ferrosilicon | ~200–350 | Several plants operating or proposed | Extremely electro-intensive (~4,000–8,000 kWh/tonne); yields 8–10× domestic value vs export sale. See domestic-led-hydro-strategy | Chemical / pharmaceutical / plastic | ~100–250 | Pharmaceuticals, PVC pipes, detergents, paints | Growing sector, ~50+ pharmaceutical manufacturers | ## Tier 3 — Small industrial ( [!gap] NEA sub-sector data NEA does not disaggregate electricity sales by industrial sub-sector in any public document examined. The table above is constructed from (a) the WECS total-energy cross-tab showing which fuels go to which industrial activities; (b) known process-energy ratios for each industry; (c) rough plant counts and output volumes. This is the single biggest data gap in the consumption profile. Obtaining actual sub-sector GWh data from NEA Load Dispatch Centre or Department of Industry would significantly sharpen the industrial-demand argument. # Structural Features of Nepal's Industrial Electricity Demand ## 1. It's a few large consumers, not a broad base From the NEA consumer data: industrial consumers are 1.23% of all customers but consume 36.64% of all electricity. This means approximately 70,000 industrial connections serve an average of ~59,000 kWh/yr each — but the distribution is heavily skewed. The top ~50-100 consumers (cement plants, steel mills, large cold stores) likely account for 30-50% of total industrial consumption. This concentration makes industrial demand the highest-leverage policy target: converting a single major brick kiln cluster or cement plant to grid electricity can shift the demand curve more than adding thousands of new household connections. NEA explicitly recognizes this by categorizing top consumers as "Most Important Consumers" (MIC), "Very Important Consumers" (VIC), and "Important Consumers" (IC) with dedicated customer care. ## 2. Industry is underelectrified compared to peers From the WB CEM: Nepal's industry consumed ~12,800 TJ of electricity in 2022 (IEA data), which was only 9% of final industry energy consumption. By comparison: Country | Industry Electricity Share | Electricity Consumed (TJ) | ---------|------:|------:| Bangladesh | 35% | ~147,000 | Nepal | 9% | ~12,800 | Bangladesh's industry consumes ~11× more electricity despite a somewhat larger economy. The gap is not primarily size — it is electrification depth. Nepal's industry burns coal and biomass because the grid was historically unreliable (load shedding 2007-2017, 14 hours/day) and industrial tariffs didn't incentivize fuel-switching. ## 3. Captive diesel generation is a hidden consumption layer The WECS cross-tab shows ~11,646 TJ of diesel consumed in the industrial sector — equivalent to ~3,235 GWh of potential electricity demand if the grid were reliable. At NPR 130/L diesel and NPR 9.44/kWh average grid tariff, the per-kWh cost of captive diesel generation (~NPR 25–35/kWh) is 3–4× the grid price. Yet firms pay it because outages still affect 76% of firms (WB CEM 2022 data). This captive diesel load is the low-hanging fruit of industrial electrification. Each GWh displaced from diesel to grid electricity saves the firm ~NPR 15–25 million in fuel costs and saves Nepal the foreign exchange cost of importing that diesel. ## 4. Manufacturing has been shrinking, not growing Manufacturing fell from 7.3% of GDP (conflict era) to 5.7% (repeated-shocks era). Construction overtook manufacturing as the largest industry subsector. This means: Industrial electricity growth (~10% CAGR) is outpacing manufacturing GDP growth — suggesting the growth comes from electrification of existing processes (diesel → electric switching, electric grinding replacing coal-based processes) rather than from new factories. If manufacturing recovers, industrial electricity demand could accelerate sharply — a positive-risk scenario for the demand-creation strategy. If manufacturing continues to shrink, industrial electricity growth may slow — a negative-risk scenario. # Growth Trends Metric | FY 2020/21 | FY 2024/25 | CAGR | --------|------:|------:|------:| Industrial electricity sales | ~2,816 GWh | ~4,136 GWh | ~10.1% | Share of total sales | 38.69% | 36.64% | slight decline | Industrial revenue | — | NPR 39.88B | — | Outstanding industrial dues | — | NPR 29.42B | — | [!warning] Dues are a structural concern Outstanding industrial dues (NPR 29.42 billion) represent ~74% of annual industrial revenue (NPR 39.88 billion). This suggests either legacy arrears from the load-shedding era or ongoing payment difficulties in certain industrial segments. Either way, it complicates the "build demand to absorb surplus" thesis — the customers you need most may not pay reliably. # Industrial Consumption and Hydro Hydro is 94.4% of installed capacity but only ~89% of available energy (imports supply 11% in FY 2024/25, mostly in dry season). For industrial consumers this means: Monsoon period: abundant hydro, low marginal cost, reliable supply — the ideal time for industrial production Dry season: hydro output drops, imports fill the gap at higher cost, and evening peaks strain the system — the reliability gap that caused 76% of firms to report outages in 2022 Industrial consumers that can shift production seasonally (e.g. food processing timed to harvest, construction materials for building season) benefit from the monsoon abundance. Industries that need year-round continuous power (cold storage, continuous-process cement, 24/7 steel mills) face the dry-season reliability gap and are the economic case for storage-deficit and solar-hydro-complementarity build-out. # Chart Specification 1. Sankey for industry — fuel sources (coal 30%, biomass 43%, petroleum 15%, electricity 12%) → sub-sectors (brick, cement, steel, food, textiles, other) 2. Stacked bar — industrial electricity consumption estimates by sub-sector (estimated GWh) 3. Comparison panel — Nepal industrial electricity share (9%) vs Bangladesh (35%) and other peers # Related data-domestic-demand — the full sectoral demand picture data-final-energy-mix — the total-energy denominator with sector × fuel cross-tab data-consumption-geographic-distribution — where industrial clusters are located energy-substitution-pathway — strategy for displacing industrial coal and diesel with electricity domestic-led-hydro-strategy — why domestic industrial load matters more than exports green-hydrogen-nepal — the pathway to displace coal in high-temperature industrial processes storage-deficit — what industrial consumers need for 24/7 reliability stranded-generation — monsoon surplus that could power additional industrial shift data-agricultural-energy-consumption — agro-processing and irrigation

pages.title: IPSDP Milestone Ladder 2022-2040
headings: Era buckets shown on the ladder
What this page does and does not claim
pages.excerpt: Figure 3.1-1 Development Milestones and Pathways in IPSDP.
pages.body_text: IPSDP Milestone Ladder 2022-2040 Figure 3.1-1 Development Milestones and Pathways in IPSDP. Figure 3.1-1 in moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 is the most compact official statement of Nepal's state-planning timeline from the current IPSDP. Its value to the wiki is not the exact plotted curve values, which are too compressed in the executive-summary figure to transcribe year by year with confidence, but the dated milestones and the named project buckets attached to each era. # Milestones the figure fixes explicitly Year | IPSDP statement | Why it matters | ------|-----------------|----------------| 2025 | Power exports surpass imports | Marks the crossover from historic importer framing to a net-export posture | 2033 | Self-sufficiency in electricity supply is achieved | The domestic-supply milestone arrives before the later regional-export framing | 2035 | The government's 28,000 MW development target is achieved | This is the core state capacity target the ladder keeps returning to | 2040 | Contribution to South Asian region by clean export | Export is framed as a later regional role, not the first milestone | # Era buckets shown on the ladder Window | Named generation projects called out in the figure | --------|----------------------------------------------------| ~2025 | Rasuwagadhi, Sanjen, Upper Modi A, Rahughat, Upper Trishuli 3B, Madhya Bhotekoshi, Upper Trishuli-1, Arun 3 | 2026-2030 | Tanahu HEP, Tamor Storage, Madi Storage HEP, Manang Marsyangdi, Budhi Gandaki Ka, Bheri 4 HEP, Tila-1, Tila-2 | 2031-2035 | Lower Seti, Uttarganga STO, Phukot Karnali, Nalsyau Gad STO, Upper Arun, Chainpur Seti, Dudhkoshi STO, Sunkoshi 3 STO, Lower Badigad STO, Budhi Gandaki, Upper Marsyangdi-2, Dudh Koshi-IV, Upper Tamor, Bheri-2, West Seti STO, SR-06 STO | 2036-2040 | Betan Karnali, Kimathanka Arun, Bharbung, Mugu Karnali STO, Tamakoshi 3, Khimti Shivalaya STO, Bajhang Upper Seti, Naumure STO, Upper Mugu Karnali, Bheri-1, Humla Karnali Cascade | # What this page does and does not claim This page captures the dated milestones and named project groupings with high confidence from the figure itself. It does not yet claim a machine-ready year-by-year table of installed capacity, demand, and export values. Those traces need a higher-resolution source or manual chart digitization before they should be reused as numeric data. # Related moewri-ipsdp-exec-summary-2025 nepal-power-trade-schemes domestic-led-hydro-strategy twenty-year-strategy

pages.title: Layer: Basin Seasonality
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Wet/dry season flow Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8
pages.body_text: Layer: Basin Seasonality Explorer label: Wet/dry season flow Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8 Basin annotations focused on monsoon concentration, winter weakness, and dry-season implications. # What It Represents This layer makes seasonality explicit on the map. Nepal's hydro advantage is physically real, but its value depends heavily on when water arrives. The annotations help separate annual volume from winter firmness. # How To Read It Use it with storage shortlist, operating hydropower, and downstream markers. The key question is how much of the basin's energy or flow is wet-season heavy and what that means for dry-season deficits. # Caveats Seasonality annotations summarize complex hydrology. They should be read as planning context, not a replacement for plant-level generation modeling or climate-adjusted hydrological studies. # Related seasonal-mismatch seasonal-arbitrage-trap storage-deficit firm-power data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Carto Positron Basemap
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Carto Positron Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap
pages.body_text: Layer: Carto Positron Basemap Explorer label: Carto Positron Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap Neutral light basemap for reading dense hydrology, project, and transmission overlays without heavy terrain shading. # What It Represents Carto Positron is the default visual ground plane for the explorer. It is intentionally quiet: roads, settlements, borders, water bodies, and labels remain visible, but the style avoids strong terrain color and imagery texture that would compete with project markers or basin polygons. # How To Read It Use this basemap when comparing several overlays at once. It is best for dense views such as operating plus construction hydropower, basin polygons with tributaries, or transmission corridors with node layers. If the question is about layer relationships rather than landform interpretation, this should usually be the first choice. # Caveats Because the basemap is visually restrained, it is not the right layer for judging slopes, ridges, road access, reservoir surfaces, or land-cover constraints. Switch to Topographic or Satellite when geography itself is the evidence. # Related data-map-inventory buildability data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Cross-Border Links
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Cross-border lines Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10
pages.body_text: Layer: Cross-Border Links Explorer label: Cross-border lines Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10 Conservative line/stub geometries for operational, under-construction, implementation-stage, and planned cross-border links. # What It Represents This layer draws cross-border links as conservative endpoint connectors or gateway stubs rather than only points. It makes visible the fact that trade is a physical network problem before it is a market story. # How To Read It Use it to compare operational, under-construction, implementation, and planned links. It helps explain why eastern, central, and western Nepal have different export and import possibilities. # Caveats Planned or implementation-stage lines are not bankable capacity until completed and operational. Gateway stubs are intentionally conservative and do not claim the full India-side route. # Related india-energy-relationship data-trade-time-series nepal-power-trade-schemes claim-transmission-immediate-blocker data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Cross-Border Gateways
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: International gateways Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10
pages.body_text: Layer: Cross-Border Gateways Explorer label: International gateways Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 10 Nepal-side gateway points for cross-border interconnections with status and timeline context. # What It Represents This layer maps the Nepal-side gateway points between Nepal's grid and India's grid. It is central to export strategy, import reliability, and the politics of monetizing hydropower beyond Nepal's domestic demand. # How To Read It Use it with trade, transmission, and hydropower layers. Interconnections explain why generation capacity alone does not guarantee export revenue: the system needs cross-border capacity, domestic corridors feeding those nodes, and political clearance. # Caveats An interconnection marker does not imply unrestricted commercial access. Trade rules, line capacity, synchronization, scheduling, and approvals still determine what can flow. # Related india-energy-relationship dhalkebar-muzaffarpur gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection nepal-power-trade-schemes data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Downstream Dependency Zones
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Downstream dependency zones Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 5
pages.body_text: Layer: Downstream Dependency Zones Explorer label: Downstream dependency zones Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 5 Strategic downstream exposure zones for agriculture, population, floods, dry-season flow, sediment, and delta sensitivity. # What It Represents This layer turns the route-to-ocean trace into a downstream stakes map. It marks broad regions where Nepal-linked river routes pass through dense agricultural plains, flood-sensitive corridors, and the Bangladesh delta system. For the non-technical reading sequence, start with how-to-read-geopolitics-map. For the main synthesis, use downstream-river-geopolitics. The five v1 zones are: Zone | Main read | ------|-----------| Eastern Uttar Pradesh Ghaghara-Gandak belt | Western and central Nepal-linked routes entering dense agricultural plains. | North Bihar Kosi-Gandak floodplain | High exposure to flood timing, sediment, agriculture, and dense settlement. | Bihar Ganges trunk zone | Shared Ganges corridor where separate Nepal-linked routes become a common downstream geography. | Padma-Meghna delta dependency zone | Bangladesh-scale delta exposure to upstream timing, sediment, and flood pulses. | Lower delta and Bay interface | Coastal-delta context for sediment, salinity, storm surge exposure, and delta stability. | # How To Read It Use these polygons as strategic context around the river route, not as precise service areas. Important fields: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| | Links each zone to the Nepal-origin route traces that pass into or through it. | | Qualitative pressure class for map interpretation. | | Qualitative agriculture relevance. | | Whether timing and dry-season support are strategically relevant. | | Whether monsoon flood timing and attenuation matter. | | Whether sediment behavior should be part of the strategic read. | | Whether the zone is part of lower-delta vulnerability. | | Plain-language interpretation for popups. | # Caveats This is not a hydrodynamic model, irrigation command map, population raster, crop-intensity map, or flood hazard model. It does not claim that Nepal controls total water availability in these zones. The correct reading is: these are downstream exposure zones touched by Nepal-linked river routes where timing, sediment, flood behavior, and dry-season flow can become politically meaningful. # Linked Data downstream_dependency_zones.geojson downstream_dependency_layers_report.json # Related how-to-read-geopolitics-map eastern-up-ghaghara-gandak-belt north-bihar-kosi-gandak-floodplain bihar-ganges-trunk-zone padma-meghna-delta-dependency lower-delta-bay-interface data-layer-nepal-origin-ocean-routes data-layer-nepal-origin-route-callouts data-layer-downstream-population-anchors downstream-river-geopolitics ganges-contribution storage-deficit

pages.title: Layer: Downstream Impact Markers
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Flow measurements Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
pages.body_text: Layer: Downstream Impact Markers Explorer label: Flow measurements Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Point markers carrying discharge, annual runoff, monsoon share, and impact notes for major systems. # What It Represents These markers summarize basin-scale hydrology at strategic downstream reference points. They make the core numbers easier to inspect than a polygon alone: discharge, runoff, monsoon share, and short interpretive notes. # How To Read It Use the markers to compare basins by flow scale and seasonality. They work best with basin polygons and downstream-system lines, because the marker gives a data point while the lines and polygons show spatial extent. # Caveats A single marker cannot represent all seasonal, annual, or sub-basin variation. Treat values as context for planning arguments, not as a substitute for station-level hydrological analysis. # Related data-basin-discharge seasonal-mismatch storage-deficit ganges-contribution data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Downstream Population Anchors
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Downstream population anchors Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 10
pages.body_text: Layer: Downstream Population Anchors Explorer label: Downstream population anchors Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 10 City and delta anchors that locate the population geography along Nepal-linked downstream routes. # What It Represents This layer adds named places to the downstream river story so the route and dependency zones are easier to orient. The anchors include major cities, delta nodes, and river-port style points along the Ganges, Kosi/Gandak/Ghaghara plains, Padma, and lower delta. All population-anchor popups route here because the project needs context, not a general-purpose city encyclopedia. The points are orientation marks for how-to-read-geopolitics-map and downstream-river-geopolitics. # How To Read It Use the points to locate the human geography around the river system: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| | The river system that gives the anchor its map relevance. | | Nepal-linked routes associated with the downstream geography. | | Qualitative city/metro/delta class. | | Plain-language reason the point is included. | | Caveat against over-attribution. | # Caveats These anchors do not imply that a city is supplied only by Nepal-origin rivers. They are context markers for downstream geography, population concentration, floodplain exposure, and route legibility. Coordinates are city/context anchors, not water-intake points, flood gauge locations, or infrastructure nodes. # Linked Data downstream_population_anchors.geojson downstream_dependency_layers_report.json # Related how-to-read-geopolitics-map data-layer-downstream-dependency-zones data-layer-nepal-origin-ocean-routes downstream-river-geopolitics ganges-contribution india-energy-relationship

pages.title: Layer: Floating PV Candidates
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Floating solar Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 5
pages.body_text: Layer: Floating PV Candidates Explorer label: Floating solar Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 5 Reservoir or future-reservoir floating PV candidates with confidence and capacity-band fields. # What It Represents This layer shows reservoirs and future-reservoir contexts where floating PV could become a leverage play. The idea is not that floating PV replaces hydro, but that it can use water surfaces and grid-adjacent hydro infrastructure to add dry-season or daytime value. # How To Read It Use it with storage shortlist and hybrid siting logic. A candidate is most interesting when it overlaps with storage, transmission access, and a credible reservoir surface. # Caveats Floating PV candidates are early-stage and confidence-tagged. Water-level variation, reservoir operations, environmental constraints, anchoring, grid connection, and institutional ownership all matter. # Related claim-floating-pv-leverage hybrid-siting-logic storage-deficit data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Future Regulation Scenario
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Flow regulation Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 11
pages.body_text: Layer: Future Regulation Scenario Explorer label: Flow regulation Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 11 Storage-first scenario markers showing which projects could alter river timing, dry-season support, downstream sensitivity, and cooperation risk. # What It Represents This layer reframes the data-layer-storage-shortlist for the geopolitics preset. The question is not only how many MW each project adds. The question is whether a project can change the timing of water and electricity enough to matter downstream. The layer includes mapped storage or storage-like candidates from the existing shortlist: Scenario role | Meaning | ---------------|---------| | Existing storage that anchors the present system. | | Storage under construction or close enough to matter for near-term planning. | | Planned storage with strong Nepal-side documentation and quantified dry-season value. | | JICA/NEA promising-storage candidates with dry-energy or storage figures. | # How To Read It Use this layer with Nepal-origin downstream systems, basin polygons, downstream impact markers, and origin/control callouts. A marker should answer five questions: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| Timing ( ) | Separates Already operating, Under construction, Planned, and Long-term candidate projects. | Flow impact ( ) | Indicates whether the project has baseline (no storage), low, medium, high, or very-high timing-regulation signal. | | Names the downstream river system affected by the basin. | Downstream risk ( ) | Flags whether the linked basin is geopolitically sensitive downstream. | Cooperation needed ( ) | Identifies where regulation could create cooperative surplus rather than only unilateral leverage. | The deterministic regulation classes are based on dry-season energy, total storage, and effective storage where available. They are scenario labels, not hydrodynamic model results. # Caveats This is a strategic interpretation layer. It does not model reservoir operations, treaty entitlements, flood-routing hydraulics, sediment trapping, irrigation withdrawals, or environmental-flow compliance. Several projects are planning candidates rather than committed assets. Location precision varies by marker; use the Location source ( ) and fields before treating a point as a site-grade location. data-storage-comparison includes Chera-1 in the tabular research workspace, but Chera-1 is not exposed here because it does not currently have a reliable map anchor. # Linked Data future_regulation_scenario.geojson - generated scenario layer. storage_shortlist_annotations.geojson - source annotation layer. nepal_storage_dry_energy_shortlist.csv - storage and dry-energy source table. # Related downstream-river-geopolitics ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics storage-deficit firm-power environmental-flow-policy data-layer-storage-shortlist data-map-layer-labels

pages.title: Layer: Grid Hubs and Substations
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Power hubs & substations Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 39
pages.body_text: Layer: Grid Hubs and Substations Explorer label: Power hubs & substations Layer group: Transmission and trade layers Feature count: 39 Named grid hubs and substation/place anchors used to orient the network. # What It Represents This public point layer gives the reader named grid geography: major substations, hubs, and anchor places used to interpret the transmission network. # How To Read It Use this layer with the major transmission network and cross-border links. It helps orient the reader and gives corridors recognizable endpoints. # Caveats Not every anchor is a surveyed substation coordinate. Some are towns, hubs, or geocoding references, so use the popup basis when precision matters. # Related data-map-inventory project-roadmap nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Hydropower - Construction
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Hydro — building Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 180
pages.body_text: Layer: Hydropower - Construction Explorer label: Hydro — building Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 180 Generation/construction-license projects, useful for seeing near-term capacity pressure on basins and transmission corridors. # What It Represents This layer maps projects licensed to build or generate, which makes it a pipeline layer rather than a current-supply layer. It is useful for seeing where future MW claims cluster geographically. # How To Read It Use it beside transmission corridors and basin seasonality. Dense construction clusters can indicate where evacuation pressure, financing risk, and winter-output limitations will matter before the projects become useful system capacity. # Caveats A generation license does not guarantee timely commissioning, financing closure, transmission availability, or winter firm energy. Treat this layer as near-term pressure and option value, not delivered electricity. # Related stranded-generation claim-transmission-immediate-blocker claim-mw-not-equal-value twenty-year-strategy data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Hydropower - Operating
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Hydro — operating Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 81
pages.body_text: Layer: Hydropower - Operating Explorer label: Hydro — operating Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 81 Operating hydropower projects from the project registry. Marker area scales by capacity, and wiki counts show page coverage. # What It Represents This layer shows projects that are in operation in the display registry. It is the closest map layer to Nepal's current hydropower asset base, separating built supply from future promises. # How To Read It Use it to understand the geography of existing generation. Marker size scales with capacity, so large stations stand out visually, but the map should be read together with technology type, river basin, seasonality, and transmission access. # Caveats Operating MW is not the same as firm winter power or delivered value. Run-of-river output can be highly seasonal, and a project can be technically operating while still facing evacuation or hydrology constraints. # Related run-of-river-hydropower nepal-energy-profile nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 claim-mw-not-equal-value data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Hydropower - Survey/Study
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Hydro — planned Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 311
pages.body_text: Layer: Hydropower - Survey/Study Explorer label: Hydro — planned Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 311 Survey-stage hydropower licenses: early pipeline signals, not built capacity. # What It Represents This layer maps projects still at survey or study stage. It is useful for understanding where developers see resource potential, but it sits far upstream from actual generation. # How To Read It Use it for option density, basin crowding, and long-run project geography. It is especially helpful when comparing potential-heavy regions with the actual operating fleet. # Caveats Survey-stage projects should not be counted as supply. Many will change size, ownership, design, timeline, or disappear entirely. This layer can easily exaggerate Nepal's practical build pipeline if read without status discipline. # Related hydropower-potential-categories project-roadmap claim-systems-conversion-failure data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: India Comparison Basins
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: India basins Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
pages.body_text: Layer: India Comparison Basins Explorer label: India basins Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Comparison basins outside Nepal-origin control, included to keep Nepal-origin leverage in proportion. # What It Represents This layer adds non-Nepal comparison basins in northern India. It is a guardrail against making the map too Nepal-centric. The Ganges system has many contributors, and not every Himalayan or plains tributary is controlled by Nepal-origin headwaters. # How To Read It Use this layer when explaining scale. Compare Nepal-linked basins to India-origin basins to see where Nepal is central and where it is only one part of a much larger downstream system. It is especially useful beside downstream systems and origin/control callouts. # Caveats These are comparison geometries, not the main evidence base for Nepal energy planning. They should not be interpreted as a full inventory of Indian hydrology or water-management infrastructure. # Related ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics india-energy-relationship data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: India Comparison Rivers
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: India comparison rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 11
pages.body_text: Layer: India Comparison Rivers Explorer label: India comparison rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 11 Reference rivers outside Nepal-origin control, included so the map does not overstate Nepal's share of northern India hydrology. # What It Represents This reference layer shows Indian rivers that help calibrate the Nepal-origin network. It adds visible context for nearby systems that are hydrologically important but not controlled by Nepal-origin basins. # How To Read It Use it when telling the difference between a Nepal-linked downstream path and the wider northern Indian river network. It is also useful for explaining why downstream influence is real but bounded. # Caveats The layer is intentionally selective. It is not a complete Indian river map, and it should not be used as the authoritative source for Indian basin planning. # Related ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics india-energy-relationship data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Map Text Labels
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: River name labels Layer group: Text label overlays Feature count: text overlay
pages.body_text: Layer: Map Text Labels Explorer label: River name labels Layer group: Text label overlays Feature count: text overlay Permanent river and basin text labels. Turn these on when geography matters more than raw features. # What It Represents This overlay adds permanent text labels for rivers and basins. It is a readability layer: it helps the user keep the geography in view while inspecting features that may otherwise be visually abstract. # How To Read It Use it during presentations, screenshots, or when comparing basins and rivers without constantly opening popups. It is especially useful when the map is zoomed to regional scale. # Caveats Labels can clutter dense views and may overlap at some zoom levels. Turn them off when project markers or linework need to be inspected closely. # Related data-map-inventory ganges-contribution data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Nepal-Linked Basin Polygons
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Basin areas Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
pages.body_text: Layer: Nepal-Linked Basin Polygons Explorer label: Basin areas Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Koshi, Gandaki, Karnali, and Mahakali upstream basin polygons, with Nepal share and monsoon context. # What It Represents This layer frames Nepal as an upstream hydrological actor. The polygons show the major Himalayan systems whose water, sediment, and seasonal flows pass through or originate in Nepal before entering the wider Ganges plain. The layer is not just a geography overlay; it is the base for understanding where Nepal has physical leverage and where that leverage is limited. # How To Read It Read basin area together with Nepal share and monsoon share. A large basin with high Nepal share indicates stronger upstream relevance for Nepal planning; a shared or border basin requires more geopolitical caution. The layer is especially useful before turning on project or downstream-impact overlays. # Caveats Basin polygons simplify complex hydrology. They do not resolve treaty rights, engineering control, reservoir operations, environmental flows, or sub-basin heterogeneity. The polygons should be read as analytical boundaries, not as claims of unilateral control. # Related koshi-basin gandaki-basin karnali-basin mahakali-basin ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Nepal-Origin Downstream Systems
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Downstream rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
pages.body_text: Layer: Nepal-Origin Downstream Systems Explorer label: Downstream rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 Main Nepal-origin systems traced downstream toward the Ganges plain to show where upstream control becomes regional influence. # What It Represents This layer follows the major Nepal-linked river systems beyond the national boundary. It makes explicit that hydropower planning, sediment, flood timing, and dry-season flow questions do not stop at Nepal border lines. # How To Read It Use it with basin polygons and downstream impact markers. The layer is strongest when the question is geopolitical or system-scale: where Nepal-origin water goes, which downstream names correspond to Nepal basins, and why India-facing transmission and water relationships matter. # Caveats The traced systems are schematic enough for strategic interpretation, not hydrodynamic modeling. They do not encode barrages, diversions, irrigation withdrawals, or local channel complexity in the plains. # Related ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics india-energy-relationship seasonal-mismatch data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Nepal-Origin Routes To Ocean
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Ocean route Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4
pages.body_text: Layer: Nepal-Origin Routes To Ocean Explorer label: Ocean route Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 4 HydroRIVERS downstream traces continuing Nepal-linked systems through the Ganges/Padma/Meghna route to the Bay of Bengal. # What It Represents This layer extends the existing data-layer-nepal-origin-downstream-systems beyond the northern plains. It lets the reader follow each Nepal-linked system through the larger Ganges network toward the Bay of Bengal. The four route features are: Route | Origin system | Merge system | -------|---------------|--------------| Koshi route to Bay of Bengal | Koshi-Kosi | Kosi-Ganges-Padma-Meghna | Gandaki route to Bay of Bengal | Gandaki-Narayani-Gandak | Gandak-Ganges-Padma-Meghna | Karnali route to Bay of Bengal | Karnali-Ghaghara | Ghaghara-Ganges-Padma-Meghna | Mahakali route to Bay of Bengal | Mahakali-Sharda | Sharda-Ghaghara-Ganges-Padma-Meghna | # How To Read It Use this as a strategic continuity layer. The existing downstream-system lines show Nepal-to-plains routing; this layer shows how those systems enter the shared lower Ganges route and ultimately reach the Bay of Bengal. Important fields: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| | Names the downstream trunk each Nepal-linked route joins. | | Indicates how confidently the current downstream endpoint snapped to HydroRIVERS. | | Shows the distance between the existing mapped endpoint and the HydroRIVERS route start. | | Approximate downstream route length in the generated trace. | | Records the HydroRIVERS tracing method. | # Caveats This is a topology and communication layer, not a hydrodynamic model. It does not represent reservoir operations, flow volumes after withdrawals, distributary splits, tidal dynamics, embankments, barrages, irrigation diversions, or floodplain storage. Dense delta distributaries are intentionally out of scope for v1. The layer preserves one strategic trunk per Nepal-linked system so the reader can follow the basin story without turning the map into a complete delta-network inventory. # Linked Data nepal_origin_ocean_routes.geojson - generated route layer. downstream_ocean_route_report.json - route QA report. # Related downstream-river-geopolitics ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics data-layer-nepal-origin-downstream-systems data-layer-downstream-impact-markers

pages.title: Layer: Nepal-Origin Route Callouts
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Route merge / delta callouts Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 7
pages.body_text: Layer: Nepal-Origin Route Callouts Explorer label: Route merge / delta callouts Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 7 Merge, transition, and delta markers that make the Nepal-origin route-to-ocean traces readable. # What It Represents This layer adds strategic reading points to data-layer-nepal-origin-ocean-routes. It marks where individual Nepal-linked systems enter larger downstream trunks, where the Ganges becomes the Padma system, and where the route reaches the lower delta / Bay of Bengal terminal reach. The layer is designed for map comprehension. It does not replace surveyed confluence geometry or a delta distributary model. For the non-technical reading sequence, start with how-to-read-geopolitics-map. # How To Read It Use the callouts as handoff markers: Callout type | Meaning | --------------|---------| | A Nepal-linked route enters the wider downstream plains system. | | A Nepal-origin route joins the Ganges trunk. | | The route crosses into a different downstream naming or political geography. | | The shared trunk reaches lower delta logic. | | The generated route terminal reach. | Important fields: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| | Links the marker back to the route traces it helps explain. | | Names the downstream trunk or transition point. | | Explains why this marker matters to the geopolitics story. | | States the precision caveat for the point. | | Flags how strongly to read the marker position. | # Caveats These are strategic callouts, not engineering-grade confluence points. Coordinates are intentionally approximate and are used to make the route-to-ocean layer legible at explorer scale. Dense distributaries, tidal channels, barrages, embankments, irrigation withdrawals, and local floodplain storage are out of scope for this v1 layer. # Linked Data nepal_origin_route_callouts.geojson downstream_dependency_layers_report.json # Related how-to-read-geopolitics-map mahakali-sharda-handoff ghaghara-ganges-merge gandak-ganges-merge kosi-ganges-merge ganges-padma-transition padma-meghna-delta bay-of-bengal-terminal data-layer-nepal-origin-ocean-routes data-layer-downstream-dependency-zones downstream-river-geopolitics ganges-contribution hydro-geopolitics

pages.title: Layer: Nepal Tributaries (HydroRIVERS)
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Nepal rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 22
pages.body_text: Layer: Nepal Tributaries (HydroRIVERS) Explorer label: Nepal rivers Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 22 HydroRIVERS-derived Nepal tributaries relevant to the power and basin story, including WECS potential where available. # What It Represents This layer turns the basin story into named river corridors. It uses HydroRIVERS-derived linework curated around hydropower-relevant Nepal tributaries, so the map can connect projects, basins, and downstream systems to specific rivers rather than only broad polygons. # How To Read It Use it to orient project clusters by river, to see which tributaries feed each basin, and to connect wiki pages about projects to the hydrological system they depend on. It is most useful with hydropower or basin-seasonality layers turned on. # Caveats HydroRIVERS linework is good for network context, but it is not a survey-grade river centerline. Some smaller or locally important streams may be absent, simplified, or named differently across sources. # Related wecs-river-basin-plan-2024 wecs-hydropower-potential-2019 run-of-river-hydropower data-basin-discharge data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Origin / Control Callouts
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: River influence Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8
pages.body_text: Layer: Origin / Control Callouts Explorer label: River influence Layer group: Hydrology and basin layers Feature count: 8 Interpretive callouts for origin, control class, Nepal share, and downstream identity across the basin stack. # What It Represents This layer provides short interpretive labels for the hydrology map. It explains whether a system is Nepal-linked, shared, or outside Nepal-origin control, and it ties that classification to downstream names and basin shares. # How To Read It Use callouts when presenting the map to someone who does not already know the river systems. They are designed to make the control logic visible: where Nepal is upstream, where it is only partly upstream, and where it is not upstream at all. # Caveats Control class is analytical shorthand. It should not be read as legal authority, treaty entitlement, or operational control over downstream water infrastructure. # Related hydro-geopolitics ganges-contribution india-energy-relationship data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Priority Watchlist
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Priority projects Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 12
pages.body_text: Layer: Priority Watchlist Explorer label: Priority projects Layer group: Hydropower layers Feature count: 12 Curated strategically important projects by size, storage value, basin leverage, financing, or transmission dependence. # What It Represents The priority watchlist is a hand-picked set of hydropower projects that should stay visible even when the wider operating, generation-license, and survey clouds are switched off. It is not meant to be exhaustive. It is a reading layer for projects that carry disproportionate analytical weight in the wiki's core argument about seasonal value, grid constraints, and delivery risk. The layer deliberately mixes two kinds of records: Group | Projects | Why They Stay Visible | -------|----------|-----------------------| Priority operating / buildout | Upper Bhotekoshi, Kali Gandaki A, Andhi Khola, Upper Tamakoshi, Tanahu, Upper Trishuli-1 | Operating or generation-stage anchors that make the abstract system argument concrete: visible sites, central corridors, storage relevance, or large commissioned capacity. | Radar survey | Nalsyau Gad, Mugu Karnali Storage, Arun 3, Upper Karnali, Betan Karnali, Phukot Karnali | Survey-stage projects that shape the long-run strategic map because of size, basin position, storage value, export relevance, or Karnali/Koshi corridor leverage. | This is the layer to use when the map needs a strategic reading list rather than a licensing inventory. It links the project geography to articles such as storage-deficit, claim-mw-not-equal-value, claim-transmission-immediate-blocker, and twenty-year-strategy. # Included Projects Project | MW | River / Basin Signal | Status | Why It Is On The Watchlist | ---------|---:|----------------------|--------|----------------------------| Upper Bhotekoshi | 45 | Bhote Koshi | Operation | Existing valley plant with legible site geometry; useful for checking whether mapped hydro points behave like real assets. | Kali Gandaki A | 144 | Kali Gandaki | Operation | Major operating Gandaki anchor and benchmark for the older public hydro fleet. | Andhi Khola | 9.4 | Andhi Khola / Gandaki | Operation | Small operating plant, but analytically important because Andhi Khola also appears in storage-comparison work. | Upper Tamakoshi | 456 | Tama Koshi | Generation | Largest commissioned domestic project in the stack; a required reference point for capacity versus delivered value. | Tanahu | 140 | Seti Khola / Gandaki | Generation | Most practical near-term new storage addition in the checked official set. | Upper Trishuli-1 | 216 | Trishuli | Generation | Central-north project tied to the Trishuli evacuation corridor and grid-first reading of project value. | Nalsyau Gad | 410 | Nalsyau / Karnali | Survey | Best dry-energy performer in the JICA promising-storage set. | Mugu Karnali Storage | 1,902 | Karnali | Survey | Largest survey-stage project in the registry and a headline test case for capacity, storage, and remoteness. | Arun 3 | 900 | Arun / Koshi | Survey | National-scale eastern corridor project with export and construction-system implications. | Upper Karnali | 900 | Karnali | Survey | Strategic Karnali project whose value depends as much on politics, financing, and evacuation as on MW. | Betan Karnali | 688 | Karnali | Survey | Large Karnali-basin candidate relevant to the storage-and-seasonality story. | Phukot Karnali | 426 | Karnali | Survey | Major Karnali project with a stronger document-backed project-area anchor than many survey records. | # How To Read It Turn this layer on first when orienting around the power-system map. Then add operating hydropower, survey-stage hydropower, storage shortlist, transmission corridors, and cross-border interconnections to see what each priority marker depends on. The most important fields are: Field | How To Use It | -------|---------------| / | Separates current-system anchors from long-run radar projects. | Capacity ( ) | Shows scale, but should be read against storage, dry energy, and evacuation context. | Status ( ) | Distinguishes operating or generation-stage assets from survey-stage possibilities. | Why it matters ( ) | Gives the editorial reason the project was promoted into the watchlist. | Location source ( ) | Explains whether the marker comes from a registry point, a document-backed anchor, or a river-aligned reference. | | Tells the reader how much locational confidence to attach to the marker. | The watchlist is also a navigation device. If a marker raises a question, the next step is usually an entity page, a corridor page, or a concept page rather than another marker layer. # Caveats The watchlist is editorial. It is not a ranking by commercial readiness, social acceptability, government priority, benefit-cost ratio, or environmental acceptability. It reflects the analytical frame of this wiki: winter value, system bottlenecks, geopolitical leverage, and delivery risk. Survey-stage markers should not be read as precise dam coordinates unless the Location source ( ) says so. Several records are river-aligned references built from public project data, not site-grade engineering linework. That is intentional: the layer is for strategic map reading, while project-level due diligence still requires the underlying feasibility, EIA, RAP, licensing, and transmission documents. # Linked Data priority_project_watchlist.geojson - 12-feature watchlist layer used by the explorer. data-layer-storage-shortlist - overlapping storage and storage-like candidates. data-layer-top-10-capacity-projects - pure capacity-rank comparator. # Related twenty-year-strategy storage-deficit claim-mw-not-equal-value claim-transmission-immediate-blocker project-roadmap data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Satellite Basemap
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Satellite Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap
pages.body_text: Layer: Satellite Basemap Explorer label: Satellite Layer group: Basemaps Feature count: basemap Imagery basemap for visual checking of settlements, reservoirs, roads, river valleys, and corridor anchors. # What It Represents The satellite basemap provides imagery behind the explorer overlays. It is useful when a map feature needs to be checked against visible land cover, settlements, river channels, reservoirs, road access, or grid-hub surroundings. # How To Read It Use it for visual sanity checks: whether a point appears to sit on a plausible river reach, whether a corridor anchor is near a settlement or substation area, or whether a floating-PV candidate corresponds to a visible water body. It is the best basemap for visual inspection rather than schematic comparison. # Caveats Imagery date, cloud cover, georeferencing, and resolution vary. A visible object is not the same thing as verified infrastructure, and an invisible object is not proof that a planned or underground feature does not exist. # Related buildability hybrid-siting-logic data-map-inventory data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Solar GHI Zones
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Solar resource zones Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 6
pages.body_text: Layer: Solar GHI Zones Explorer label: Solar resource zones Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 6 Coarse solar resource zones. These are strategic zones, not parcel-level siting decisions. # What It Represents This layer maps broad solar resource geography using GHI interpretation. It places solar in Nepal's spatial energy story, especially the Terai and high-insolation zones that can complement hydro seasonality. # How To Read It Use it with solar plants, solar suitability, and hydropower seasonality. The question is not simply where sun exists, but where solar can be built quickly enough to help winter deficits and reduce over-dependence on hydro timing. # Caveats GHI zones are not project-ready siting. Land access, grid connection, slope, social constraints, and procurement design matter as much as resource quality. # Related solar-resource-geography-nepal data-nepal-solar-resource-zones solar-hydro-complementarity data-map-layer-labels data-map-inventory

pages.title: Layer: Solar Plants + NEA 960 MW Awards
headings: Layer: Solar Plants + NEA 960 MW Awards
pages.excerpt: Explorer label: Solar plants Layer group: Solar layers Feature count: 89
