The export / demand-creation problem (seasonal-arbitrage-trap) becomes more pressing, not less, as solar scales. # Diurnal complementarity — a typical winter weekday A February Friday in Kathmandu region, demand shape from NEA Load Dispatch Center. Values normalized; evening peak = 100. Hour | Demand | RoR hydro | Solar (1 GW installed, clear sky) | Residual (Demand − Hydro − Solar) | ---:|---:|---:|---:|---:| 00:00 | 55 | 30 | 0 | 25 | 03:00 | 45 | 30 | 0 | 15 | 06:00 | 60 | 30 | 5 | 25 | 08:00 | 72 | 30 | 30 | 12 | 10:00 | 78 | 30 | 70 | (22) surplus | 12:00 | 80 | 30 | 90 | (40) surplus | 14:00 | 82 | 30 | 85 | (33) surplus | 16:00 | 85 | 30 | 50 | 5 | 17:30 | 92 | 30 | 15 | 47 | 18:30 | 100 | 30 | 0 | 70 | 20:00 | 95 | 30 | 0 | 65 | 22:00 | 75 | 30 | 0 | 45 | The reason BESS is the obvious next thing, in one chart: Mid-day (10:00–15:00) has ~30 units of surplus capacity under solar-forward operation. Evening (17:30–21:00) has ~50–70 units of deficit. A 4-hour battery charged 10:00–14:00 and discharged 17:30–21:30 bridges the gap exactly. A reservoir-hydro peaker (Kulekhani-cascade style, kulekhani-cascade) held through midday and released in evening does the same thing using water as the battery. This is the cleanest argument in the wiki for why Nepal's next priority after Terai solar is 4-hour BESS + reservoir-hydro peaking, not more RoR. # Rainy-day / partial-monsoon day profile Solar variance is the fair critique. An overcast August day in Kathmandu: Hour | Demand | RoR hydro (monsoon) | Solar (clear-sky 100) | Solar (overcast 20–30) | ---:|---:|---:|---:|---:| 12:00 | 85 | 100 | 80 | 20 | 15:00 | 80 | 100 | 75 | 15 | 18:00 | 95 | 100 | 10 | 5 | On a monsoon overcast day, hydro covers. On a winter clear-sky day, solar + BESS covers. On a winter cloudy day (rare but real), the system falls back to stored water. The three resources covering each other is the system. No single one carries the load. # Variance and forecast error Solar day-ahead forecast error (typical, Nepal Terai, current atmospheric model skill): ~8–12% RMS. Hydro day-ahead forecast error: ~3–5% (inflow is highly persistent). Combined system forecast error with 30% solar penetration: ~5–7% — manageable with existing dispatch. System-operator burden grows non-linearly past ~35% solar penetration. Nepal's current ~4% solar share has enormous headroom before forecast-management becomes expensive. By the time it does, BESS deployment will have caught up. # Capacity credit A widely misquoted number: "solar has zero capacity credit." This is misleading for Nepal specifically. Technology | Capacity credit for winter-day peak | Capacity credit for winter-evening peak | ---|---:|---:| Solar (standalone) | ~60% (daytime clear-sky reliable) | ~0% (sun is down) | Solar + 4h BESS | ~60% | ~45–55% (BESS discharges into evening) | RoR hydro | ~35% (dry-season floor) | ~35% (flat output) | Reservoir hydro | 80–95% (dispatchable) | 80–95% | Pumped hydro | >95% | >95% | The honest read: standalone solar does not replace reservoir hydro on capacity. Solar + BESS does, roughly half, at current BESS costs. By 2030 (data-solar-hydro-lcoe) the replacement is near-complete for the 4-hour evening peak window. # Chart specifications Three figures, in order of priority: 1. Monthly dual-curve — normalized hydro output vs solar (Terai) output, bar chart of demand. The core complementarity image. 2. Winter-day stacked hourly — demand (line) with hydro (filled area), solar (filled area), and BESS discharge (filled area) on a sample February day. Shows where the pieces plug in. 3. Annual volume swap — two Sankey panels showing current (hydro dominant, monsoon-surplus exported) vs 2030 (hydro + solar complementary, smaller seasonal swings). # Related solar-hydro-complementarity — the concept page this operationalises data-nepal-peak-load-curve-fy2024-25 — the demand-side pattern firm-power — the capacity-credit frame seasonal-mismatch — the problem this profile solves data-solar-hydro-lcoe — the price driving deployment data-solar-fleet-inventory — the MW this profile applies to

pages.title: Solar and Hydro LCOE
headings: Global utility-scale solar LCOE (IRENA Renewable Cost Database)
pages.excerpt: The numerical table behind solar-lcoe-crossover. Three time series — global solar LCOE, Nepal solar auction tariff, Nepal hydro PPA — plus a value-weighted adjustment.
pages.body_text: Solar and Hydro LCOE The numerical table behind solar-lcoe-crossover. Three time series — global solar LCOE, Nepal solar auction tariff, Nepal hydro PPA — plus a value-weighted adjustment. # Global utility-scale solar LCOE (IRENA Renewable Cost Database) Weighted global average, levelized, 2024 US dollars. Year | Global LCOE ($/MWh) | Module price ($/W) | Capacity factor (weighted) | Notes | ---:|---:|---:|---:|---| 2010 | 359 | ~2.00 | 15% | Crystalline Si, pre-scale | 2012 | 240 | 1.20 | 16% | | 2014 | 165 | 0.76 | 17% | First Chinese scale | 2016 | 110 | 0.55 | 17% | | 2018 | 84 | 0.37 | 18% | Auction-discovery era | 2020 | 59 | 0.22 | 18% | COVID module glut | 2022 | 49 | 0.25 | 19% | Tracker + bifacial standard | 2024 | ~44 | ~0.10 | 19–20% | Record-low GCC & India auctions | 2025 (est.) | ~40 | ~0.09 | 20% | Continuing oversupply | 2030 (projected) | ~30–35 | ~0.08 | 20–21% | IRENA midpoint scenario | Module price decline 2010 → 2024: −95%. System LCOE decline 2010 → 2024: −88%. # Nepal utility-scale solar auction tariffs Auction round | Year | MW awarded | Tariff band (NPR/kWh) | USD equivalent (at period FX) | Notes | ---|---|---:|---|---:|---| First utility round | 2016–17 | 24 | 7.30–8.40 | $0.065–0.075 | 5 developers; 3 × COD 2019–20 (Butwal, Mithila, Bishnu Priya, etc.) | NEA Grid Solar & EE Project EPC | 2019–20 | 25 | NEA self-build (capex-based) | — | Bidur / Trishuli 25 MWp | IPP rounds FY 2080/81 | 2023 | 45 | 6.40–6.50 | $0.048–$0.049 | — | IPP rounds FY 2081/82 | 2024 | ~170 (8 plants) | 5.94–6.50 | $0.044–$0.049 | PPA signed, COD 2026–27 | 960 MW national tender | 2024–25 | 960 (63 projects) | 5.70–6.20 | $0.043–$0.047 | Multi-year staged PPA | Nepal's price discovery is following the global auction curve with a ~3–5 year lag; the 960 MW tender tariff is ~40% higher than Gulf-region 2024 lows ($0.030–$0.035) and ~20% above India 2024 lows (₹2.4–2.6/kWh ≈ $0.029–$0.031). # Nepal hydropower PPA tariffs NEA-standard PPA structure for IPPs (all figures NPR/kWh; effective since 2016 structure, with periodic revisions): Project class | Dry-season (Dec–May) | Wet-season (Jun–Nov) | Blended | Escalation | ---|---:|---:|---:|---| Small RoR (≤ 25 MW) | 8.40 | 4.80 | ~6.20 | 3%/yr for first 8 yrs | Small RoR (>25 MW to 100 MW) | ~7.90–8.10 | 4.55–4.75 | ~5.90–6.05 | Case-by-case | PRoR (peaking run-of-river) | 9.30 | 5.15 | ~6.80 | 3%/yr | Large hydro (cost-plus) | 7.50–9.50 | 4.25–5.40 | ~5.80–7.20 | Case-by-case | Reservoir storage (new) | 9.50–12.00 | 5.50–7.00 | 7.50–9.50 | Case-by-case | Arun-3 / Upper Karnali (export) | export-priced, ~USD 0.043–0.055 | same | same | in USD terms | # The crossover table Technology × year | LCOE / PPA (NPR/kWh) | USD equivalent | Notes | ---|---:|---:|---| Small RoR hydro PPA (blended, current) | 6.20 | $0.047 | Time-un-weighted | PRoR hydro PPA (blended, current) | 6.80 | $0.052 | | Solar 2024/25 NEA tender (blended) | 5.94 | $0.045 | 960 MW batch avg | Solar 2018 NEA auction (blended) | 7.80 | $0.070 | First round | Solar 2030 projected (Nepal) | ~4.50–5.20 | $0.034–$0.039 | With 4h BESS: ~6.50–7.50 | Global solar LCOE 2024 | 3.8 (NPR eq) | $0.044 | IRENA weighted | Gulf 2024 record-low | 2.6 | $0.020 | AEDNRM, UAE | The Nepal hydro-solar crossover happened between the 2018 (7.80) and 2023 (6.40) solar auction rounds. By 2024, solar is 4–6% cheaper than small RoR on a blended tariff basis and ~22% cheaper on a time-weighted-value basis (see next section). # Time-weighted value adjustment A kWh does not have a single price. Dry-season kWh value in the Indian cross-border market is ~2× a monsoon kWh (seasonal-arbitrage-trap). Adjusted for when each technology delivers: Technology | Annual kWh | % in dry-season (Dec–May) | % in wet-season (Jun–Nov) | Value-weighted price ($/MWh) | ---|---|---:|---:|---:| Small RoR hydro | 1,000 | ~30% | ~70% | ~51 | Solar (Terai) | 1,000 | ~55% | ~45% | ~40 | Storage hydro (Kulekhani-style) | 1,000 | ~60% | ~40% | ~68 | Coal import (proxy benchmark) | 1,000 | 100% dispatchable | — | ~85 | Solar's ~55% dry-season share (resulting from clear-sky season + low monsoon penalty in Terai) pushes its value-weighted price ~20% below its nominal tariff; RoR's ~30% dry-season share pushes its value-weighted price ~20% above. The two effects compound: value-weighted, solar is ~22% cheaper than RoR, not 4%. This is the single most important economic re-framing for the claim-solar-cheaper-than-small-hydro claim. # Solar + BESS crossover trajectory Utility-scale 4-hour battery system LCOE (Wood Mackenzie / BloombergNEF data): Year | BESS LCOE ($/MWh, 4h) | Combined solar+4h-BESS ($/MWh) | Matches reservoir-hydro at (NPR/kWh) | ---:|---:|---:|---| 2020 | 190 | 240 | — (out of range) | 2022 | 140 | 185 | — | 2024 | 95 | 135–150 | — | 2026 (proj.) | 75 | 115 | ~NPR 15 (not yet) | 2028 (proj.) | 55 | 95 | ~NPR 12 (matches high-end storage hydro) | 2030 (proj.) | 40 | ~80 | ~NPR 10 (matches mid-range storage hydro) | Solar + 4h-BESS reaches NPR 10/kWh equivalence with new reservoir-hydro around 2030, at current price-decline trajectories. This is the point at which the firm capacity argument for new large reservoir hydro (vs solar + BESS) becomes unclear on pure economics. # Chart specifications Three public-facing chart options: 1. Time-series line chart — global solar LCOE 2010–2030 (solid), Nepal solar auction tariffs 2017–2025 (dashed with points), Nepal small-RoR PPA (horizontal band). Crossover point annotated. 2. Stacked-bar per-technology — current LCOE bars for RoR, PRoR, new storage hydro, solar (auction), solar (LCOE), solar+4h BESS. With time-weighted-value adjustment as a second panel. 3. Projection fan — solar LCOE + solar+BESS 2025–2035, with hydro-storage benchmark horizontal. The crossover year for firm-capacity equivalence is the climax. # Caveats and data hygiene Nepal tariffs are NPR-denominated and escalate 3%/yr for first 8 yrs in standard PPA; USD conversions use period FX and are approximate. Gulf-region solar auction lows ($20–25/MWh) reflect different capital costs, grid conditions, land prices, and tax structures — they are not directly apples-to-apples with Nepal. Reservoir-hydro LCOE has wide error bars because sediment / GLOF / cost-overrun risk is case-by-case. Solar + BESS capacity-credit comparison assumes 4 hours of storage; longer-duration storage (8h+) is not yet cost-competitive. PR (performance ratio) assumed 17–19% for fixed-tilt Terai; could rise to 20–21% with tracker + bifacial. # Related solar-lcoe-crossover — the concept page claim-solar-cheaper-than-small-hydro — the tracked claim seasonal-arbitrage-trap — the time-weighted valuation frame data-solar-fleet-inventory — the MW this pricing is being applied to data-solar-hydro-complementarity-profile — the profile that drives the time-weighting irena-remap-nepal — IRENA's Nepal-specific cost assessment

pages.title: Storage Capacity Comparison
headings: Dry-Energy Shortlist From The Merged Research Workspace
Off-River Pumped Hydro: The Bypass Route
pages.excerpt: Nepal's water storage infrastructure compared to regional and global peers. This is the single most dramatic infrastructure gap in the entire analysis.
pages.body_text: Storage Capacity Comparison Nepal's water storage infrastructure compared to regional and global peers. This is the single most dramatic infrastructure gap in the entire analysis. # Per-Capita Dam Storage Country | Per-Capita Storage (m³/person) | Notes | Source | ---------|-------------------------------|-------|--------| Norway | ~15,100 | Wide glacial valleys, stable geology, hydro model | Source review | China | ~664 | Massive dam-building programme | Source review | Global average | ~900 | | Source review | India | ~180 | Itself criticized as inadequate | Source review | Nepal | ~5 | Almost entirely kulekhani-cascade | Source review | Nepal has 1/36th of India's per-capita storage, which is itself well below global average. # Nepal's Current Storage Facility | Capacity (MW) | Storage Volume | Generation (FY 2024/25) | ----------|--------------|----------------|------------------------| Kulekhani I | 60 | ~85 MCM active | | Kulekhani II | 32 | (cascade) | | Kulekhani III | 14 | (cascade) | | Total | 106 | ~85–150 MCM | 279.8 GWh | [!contradiction] World Bank Water Diagnostic cites 85 MCM active storage. Other source-review summaries cite 130–150 MCM total. Likely different boundaries (active vs gross reservoir volume). Both are negligible relative to need. # Storage Need Seasonal storage requirement: 29.86 km³ (wb-water-sector-diagnostic) Current storage: ~0.085–0.15 km³ Gap: ~200x shortfall JICA estimate: Nepal needs 1,993–3,154 MW of storage hydropower by 2032 # Dry-Energy Shortlist From The Merged Research Workspace The code repo added a machine-readable shortlist that is more useful than headline MW alone because it ranks projects by dry-energy contribution: Project | Basin | Installed MW | Dry Energy (GWh) | Dry Share | Why It Matters | ---------|-------|-------------:|-----------------:|----------:|----------------| dudhkoshi-storage | koshi-basin | 670 | 1,252.0 | 37.1% | Strongest Nepal-side dry-season block in the checked official set | Nalsyau Gad | karnali-basin | 410 | 581.8 | 41.4% | Best dry-energy share in the JICA promising-project shortlist | Lower Badigad | gandaki-basin | 380.3 | 354.7 | 26.0% | Large Gandaki carryover storage with meaningful winter value | Naumure (W. Rapti) | West Rapti | 245 | 309.9 | 26.8% | Medium-basin storage with unusually high seasonal-regulation value | Sun Koshi No.3 | koshi-basin | 536 | 335.9 | 17.8% | Large reservoir block with meaningful dry support even if dry share is lower | # Valley-Shape Paradox Dam | Height (m) | Storage Volume | Terrain | -----|-----------|----------------|---------| Aswan (Egypt) | 111 | 162 BCM | Flat desert | Andhi Khola (Nepal, proposed) | 110 | 0.9 BCM | V-shaped Himalayan valley | Same dam height, 180x less storage. Nepal's topography simultaneously enables hydropower (steep head) and prevents easy storage (narrow valleys). # Off-River Pumped Hydro: The Bypass Route A 2021 Oxford University study (Blakers et al., Clean Energy) identified ~2,800 off-river pumped hydro energy storage sites in Nepal with combined storage capacity of ~50 TWh. To balance a 100% renewable Nepal at advanced-economy consumption levels (~500 TWh/yr), only ~1.5 TWh of storage is needed. Nepal has 17x more pumped-hydro potential than it would ever need — entirely off-river, avoiding the valley-shape paradox. This reframes the storage deficit: Nepal is not storage-poor in potential — it is storage-poor in executed infrastructure. The bottleneck is capital, governance, and institutional will, not geology. See also data-mountain-hydro-comparison. # Linked Data nepal_storage_dry_energy_shortlist.csv — 12-project storage shortlist with total storage, effective storage, annual energy, dry energy, and editorial priority notes. # Linked Figures storage_gap.png — current storage versus JICA-estimated storage need. # Chart Specification Bar chart of per-capita storage across countries (log scale recommended given the range from 5 to 15,100). Nepal's bar should be visually striking. Second panel: the valley-shape paradox as a simple side-by-side cross-section diagram. Third panel: Nepal's 50 TWh pumped-hydro potential vs 1.5 TWh need — a simple ratio bar.

pages.title: Electricity Trade Time Series
pages.excerpt: Nepal's electricity trade transformation from net importer to seasonal exporter. Central evidence for seasonal-arbitrage-trap and seasonal-mismatch.
pages.body_text: Electricity Trade Time Series Nepal's electricity trade transformation from net importer to seasonal exporter. Central evidence for seasonal-arbitrage-trap and seasonal-mismatch. # Annual Import/Export Volumes Year | Import (GWh) | Export (GWh) | Net Position | Source | ------|-------------|-------------|--------------|--------| 2018 | ~2,582 | ~3 | Heavy importer | wb-country-economic-memo-2025 | FY 2020/21 | ~2,330 | — | Heavy importer; imports 31.83% of supply | NEA annual report (Part 7) | FY 2079/80 (~2022/23) | 1,854.5 | 1,333.1 | Net importer (~521 GWh) | NEA System Operation Dept (Part 7) | FY 2023/24 | 1,845 | 1,946 | First full net exporter year | NEA summary | FY 2024/25 | 1,681 | 2,380 | Net exporter (699 GWh) | nea-annual-report-fy2024-25 | # Data Reconciliation Notes The merged code workspace surfaced an NEA internal mismatch that should be preserved, not hidden: NEA view of FY 2024/25 trade | Import (GWh) | Export (GWh) | Best use | ------------------------------|-------------:|-------------:|----------| Narrative summary | 1,681 | 2,380 | Headline annual trade framing | Monthly energy-balance table | 1,712 | 2,380 | Monthly system-shape analysis | Multi-year trade comparison chart | 1,711.5272 | 2,331.7360 | Multi-year monthly trade comparison | [!warning] The wiki now keeps all three totals because they do different jobs. Picking one silently would create false precision. # Monthly GWh Breakdown — FY 2079/80 (~2022/23) The first month-by-month import/export resolution in the entire research corpus. Source: NEA System Operation Department operational report. Nepali Month | Gregorian Approx. | Import (GWh) | Export (GWh) | Net (Import–Export) | ---|---|---:|---:|---:| Shrawan | mid-Jul–mid-Aug | 25.5 | 251.6 | −226.1 (net export) | Bhadra | mid-Aug–mid-Sep | 20.9 | 255.7 | −234.8 (net export) | Ashwin | mid-Sep–mid-Oct | 6.1 | 281.0 | −274.9 (net export) | Kartik | mid-Oct–mid-Nov | 0.5 | 202.3 | −201.7 (net export) | Mangsir | mid-Nov–mid-Dec | 18.7 | 87.8 | −69.1 (net export) | Poush | mid-Dec–mid-Jan | 202.9 | 0.6 | +202.3 (net import) | Magh | mid-Jan–mid-Feb | 311.0 | 0.0 | +311.0 (net import) | Falgun | mid-Feb–mid-Mar | 318.5 | 0.0 | +318.5 (net import) | Chaitra | mid-Mar–mid-Apr | 335.5 | 0.0 | +335.5 (net import) | Baisakh | mid-Apr–mid-May | 395.7 | 0.0 | +395.7 (net import — peak dry) | Jestha | mid-May–mid-Jun | 184.4 | 24.2 | +160.2 (net import) | Ashar | mid-Jun–mid-Jul | 34.9 | 230.1 | −195.2 (net export) | [!finding] This is the structural proof The table makes the seasonal-arbitrage-trap undeniable at monthly resolution. Nepal exports zero in Magh, Falgun, Chaitra, Baisakh — its four biggest import months. Baisakh alone (395.7 GWh import, 0 export) represents more import than the entire Ashwin monsoon export (281 GWh). A single peak winter month erases a full monsoon month of export earnings. Dry season total (Poush–Jestha, Dec–Jun): ~1,748 GWh imported, ~25 GWh exported — net import 1,723 GWh. Monsoon total (Shrawan–Mangsir + Ashar, Jul–Nov + Jun): ~106 GWh imported, ~1,308 GWh exported — net export 1,202 GWh. Confirming: Nepal earns heavily for 5 months and pays heavily for 7 months. The annual net depends entirely on the price differential between those two bands. ## Specific dry-season peaks Falgun 2080 (mid-Feb to mid-Mar 2024): 406.3 GWh imported, zero exported in a single month. Import-at-peak hit 643 MW in one day. Evening peak load (18:30–18:55) was when the import dependency was most acute. (NEA monthly operational report) Winter import approval: Nepal received approval to import up to 654 MW through key cross-border links for Jan–Mar 2026 — confirming that structural winter deficit persists even with 3,591 MW installed. # Linked Data nea_cross_border_trade_monthly_gwh_fy2079_80_to_2081_82.csv — 3-year monthly import/export chart series from NEA annual-report trade tables. lead1_monthly_import_export_storage_fy2081_82.csv — monthly panel joining imports, exports, storage generation, and system demand for FY 2081/82. nea_trade_chart_monthly_long.csv — reshaped long-form trade table for charting. nea_daily_archive_coverage.json — archive-depth scan proving the NEA daily-report stack runs across 64 archive pages and 639 unique BS dates from 2080-01-01 to 2081-09-27. nea_daily_trade_parsed.csv — currently checked-in parsed subset from the first 40 daily reports. nea_daily_report_manifest.csv — PDF provenance list for the parsed daily subset. # Daily Pipeline Coverage Archive depth known: 64 archive pages, 703 raw detail links, 639 unique daily dates. Known coverage window: BS 2080-01-01 to 2081-09-27. Checked-in parsed slice here: 40 reports covering BS 2081/08/18 to 2081/09/27. Implication: the archive itself is much deeper than the currently parsed local subset. For early FY 2079/80 months, the annual report trade chart still remains the authoritative monthly source inside this workspace. # IEX Price Evolution (Exchange Channel) Source: India Central Electricity Authority / IEX market monitoring report. Trade on India's power exchange (Day-Ahead + Real-Time markets, cross-border transactions with Nepal). Year | Nepal buy at IEX (₹/kWh) | Nepal sell at IEX (₹/kWh) | Spread | Implication | ------|--------------------------|--------------------------|--------|-------------| 2021-22 | 3.59 | 3.20 | −0.39 | Buy more expensive than sell | 2022-23 | 5.95 | 5.14 | −0.81 | Spread widens against Nepal | 2023-24 | 4.43 | 5.61 | +1.18 | Spread flips positive | [!finding] The spread flipped in 2023-24 For the first time, Nepal sold electricity on the IEX at a higher weighted average price than it bought. This is progress — but see the caveat below. Caveat: whole-system average still negative in FY 2023/24. Even with a positive IEX spread, Nepal's system-wide average for FY 2023/24 was: sold at Rs 8.72/unit, purchased at Rs 9.17/unit — a negative spread of ~Rs 0.45/unit across the full portfolio. This gap reveals that non-IEX procurement (winter peak imports outside the exchange, bilateral arrangements, constrained hours) drives overall costs above what the market data alone would suggest. Nepal is improving its exchange-linked value capture, but the broader portfolio problem persists. # Pricing Data Direction | Channel/Season | Price | Source | -----------|----------------|-------|--------| Export to India | IEX (2023-24 weighted avg) | ₹5.61/kWh (~NPR 7.11) | IEX market monitoring | Export to Bangladesh | Bilateral (fixed) | 6.40 US cents/unit | Tripartite PSA | Import from India | IEX (2023-24 weighted avg) | ₹4.43/kWh | IEX market monitoring | Import from India | Dry season average (broader) | ~NPR 9.17–11.50/unit | NEA / source review | Import from India | Peak hour (dry) | up to NPR 16.00/unit | Source review | Domestic PPA (storage, winter) | Regulated | capped at NPR 14.80/unit | ERC/NEA | Domestic PPA (storage, monsoon) | Regulated | capped at NPR 8.45/unit | ERC/NEA | Industrial ToD tariff (domestic) | Peak (17:00–23:00) | ~NPR 10.00–10.20/unit | NEA tariff schedule | Industrial ToD tariff (domestic) | Off-peak | ~NPR 4.65–5.25/unit | NEA tariff schedule | # Revenue Data (FY 2023/24) Export revenue: NPR 17.1 billion (avg Rs 8.72/unit) Import cost: NPR 16.9 billion (avg Rs 9.17/unit) Net in GWh: +101 GWh (net exporter) Net in cash: ~NPR 200 million — barely positive on revenue despite being a "net exporter" This is the seasonal-arbitrage-trap expressed in NPR: more kWh sold than bought, less money earned than spent # FY 2024/25 Revenue Export to India: NPR 17.19 billion (2,340 GWh) Export to Bangladesh: NPR 266.98 million (30.39 GWh) Total export revenue: NPR 17.46 billion Import cost from India: NPR 12.92 billion (~1,300 GWh) Net trade surplus: ~NPR 4.5 billion (699 GWh net exports) # FY 2025/26 First Five Months (Jul–Dec 2025) Month (Nepali) | Period | Export Revenue (NPR B) | ----------------|--------|----------------------| Shrawan | Jul 17–Aug 16 | 3.87 | Bhadra | Aug 17–Sep 16 | 4.49 | Ashoj | Sep 17–Oct 17 | 5.03 (peak) | Kartik | Oct 18–Nov 17 | 3.76 | Mangsir | Nov 17–Dec 15 | 1.10 (trough) | Total | | 18.26 | 2,714 GWh exported in 5 months — 38% increase over same period in FY 2024/25 Bangladesh exports: 147.43 GWh, earning USD 9.436 million (Jun–Nov 2025) By mid-December, exports stopped and imports began as dry season set in [!finding] Monthly Pattern Now Visible The monthly revenue data above resolves the previous gap. The 4.6x ratio between peak month (Ashoj, NPR 5.03B) and trough month (Mangsir, NPR 1.10B) quantifies the seasonal swing in a single fiscal year. Exports halt entirely by December. # System Peak and Capacity (FY 2024/25) Total installed capacity: 3,591 MW System peak demand: 2,901 MW (July 1, 2025) National peak demand: 2,409 MW Monsoon peak production: ~3,000 MW, with ~850 MW surplus exported Approved export capacity: 941 MW (28 projects approved for Indian market) Approved breakdown: 400 MW medium-term bilateral + 540 MW via IEX # Linked Figures electricity_trade_shift.png — pre/post trade-regime crossover graphic. lead1_monthly_trade_3year.png — three-year monthly import/export seasonality. lead1_fy2081_82_trade_vs_storage.png — imports, exports, and storage in the same FY 2081/82 frame. # Chart Specification The three linked figures above now cover most of this page's graphic needs. The missing chart is a clean monthly revenue curve for FY 2025/26 plotted directly against import resumption, which would make the winter scarcity tax legible in one line. # Bangladesh Trade Detail See bangladesh-trade-route.

pages.title: Electric Cooking Transition
pages.excerpt: Design and implement a coordinated cooking electrification programme with four integrated components:
pages.body_text: Electric Cooking Transition # The Intervention Design and implement a coordinated cooking electrification programme with four integrated components: 1. Appliance financing for induction cookers. A public or blended-finance facility providing concessional loans or hire-purchase for induction cookers targeting peri-urban and grid-connected rural households. Scale: aiming to raise electric cooking penetration from the current ~0.5% of households toward 20–30% over a decade. Target areas: municipalities and grid-connected corridors where distribution infrastructure is adequate. 2. NEA time-of-day tariff redesign for cooking load. Restructure residential tariffs to create an off-peak window (e.g., 11:00–16:00) with a discounted rate that makes induction cooking cost-competitive with subsidised LPG. The current industrial ToD tariff splits peak (NPR 10.00–10.20/kWh, 17:00–23:00) from off-peak (NPR 4.65–5.25/kWh). A similar residential cooking tariff tier could target the midday window, avoiding the 18:30–19:25 evening peak where the system is already capacity-constrained. 3. LPG subsidy reform. Nepal's LPG subsidy makes imported gas artificially cheap — undercutting electric cooking on marginal-cost terms despite hydropower being domestically produced. A phased rebalancing: gradually reduce the LPG subsidy while commensurately reducing the effective cost of induction cooking through the tariff and financing mechanisms above. The freed fiscal resources can partially fund the appliance financing facility. 4. Grid reliability improvement in peri-urban areas. Electric cooking is not adopted when the grid is unreliable. Feeder-level upgrades, transformer deployment, and reduced SAIDI/SAIFI in the target areas must precede or accompany cooking electrification — or the programme will fail on first implementation. # Theory of Change Nepal's energy problem is not that it lacks electricity — it's that it produces enormous monsoon surplus (700–800 MW of daily spill during wet season, stranded-generation) while importing expensive winter power and burning imported LPG for cooking. The energy-substitution-pathway identifies cooking electrification as the single largest demand lever: biomass (fuelwood) at 257 PJ and LPG at 23.7 PJ together dominate residential energy. Shifting 20–30% of cooking load to electricity would create ~50–75 PJ of new electric demand — roughly doubling current electricity consumption. This is bigger than any plausible export contract. The intervention creates a virtuous cycle: Absorbs monsoon surplus domestically. Every induction cooker that operates during the day or early evening uses electricity that would otherwise be spilled, curtailed, or exported at NPR ~7/kWh. Domestic absorption builds productive infrastructure rather than exiting the economy as a commodity transaction. Displaces imported LPG. LPG (23.7 PJ, concentrated in urban/peri-urban kitchens) is almost entirely imported. Every kWh that displaces LPG saves foreign exchange — the import-substitution value far exceeds the per-kWh export realisation (seasonal-arbitrage-trap). Creates baseload for the hydro fleet. Cooking load is predictable, schedulable, and partially shiftable. Evening cooking (18:30–19:25) coincides with the daily peak, which is a problem. But midday cooking (a significant share in Nepali household patterns) aligns with solar generation and off-peak hydro availability. A time-of-day tariff can steer load toward system-surplus hours. Enables the domestic-led strategy. The domestic-led-hydro-strategy argues that Nepal should build generation for domestic dry-season reliability first. Cooking electrification is the largest single domestic demand pool that makes that strategy numerically credible — without it, "domestic demand" remains a fraction of the export-ambition scale. # Current Status VERIFIED: Electric cooking penetration: ~0.5% of households (2021 WB estimate, up from 0.1% in 2011). 92.2% of households use electricity for lighting, but cooking remains dominated by biomass and imported LPG. See data-domestic-demand. Residential electricity is 42% of NEA sales (~4,742 GWh in FY 2024/25), and the sector grew at 10.8% CAGR. But this is overwhelmingly lighting and appliances, not cooking. NEA's FY 2024/25 report explicitly flags government policy to "encourage the use of electricity for cooking, transportation, and industry to reduce reliance on fossil fuels." Per-capita consumption: 465 kWh/year (FY 2024/25), 5–7× below middle-income norms of 2,000–3,000 kWh. Seasonal monsoon surplus: 700–800 MW spilled/curtailed daily during peak wet season. Nepal exported NPR 17.46 billion in FY 2024/25 against imports of NPR 12.92 billion (net surplus ~NPR 4.5 billion). LPG: ~23.7 PJ of imported cooking fuel, concentrated in urban/peri-urban households, undercutting electric cooking on marginal cost. PARTIALLY VERIFIED: NEA has a ToD tariff structure for industrial consumers. Whether any residential ToD tariff for cooking has been formally proposed or piloted is not documented in the wiki. UNVERIFIED: No Nepal-specific cooking load timing profile study exists in the wiki. Understanding the temporal distribution of Nepali cooking energy demand (breakfast, midday, evening shares; seasonal variation; urban vs. rural patterns) is critical to sizing the tariff, financing, and grid impact of this intervention. The fiscal cost of LPG subsidy and the precise per-unit LPG subsidy rate are not documented. The current market price range of induction cookers in Nepal, distribution channels, and consumer financing availability. Grid reliability metrics (SAIDI/SAIFI) for the peri-urban areas that would be targeted for cooking electrification. # What This Unlocks Domestic demand at export-relevant scale. Shifting 20–30% of cooking to electricity creates ~50–75 PJ of new demand — roughly doubling current electricity consumption. For comparison, Nepal's total electricity export was 2,380 GWh (~8.6 PJ) in FY 2024/25. Cooking electrification at 20% penetration creates 6–9× more domestic demand than the entire current export volume. Foreign exchange savings. LPG is 100% imported. At the energy substitution rate implied by induction efficiency (>80% vs. LPG ~40–50%), each GWh of electric cooking displaces roughly 2–2.5 GWh of LPG primary energy, saving NPR tens of billions in annual petroleum imports. Monsoon surplus absorption. 50–75 PJ of annual cooking demand, even if used year-round, provides a permanent domestic offtake for energy that currently faces curtailment or low-priced export. The load is inherently distributed — every connected household is a potential demand node — reducing the transmission dependency that constrains industrial offtake. Health and environment co-benefits. Indoor air pollution from biomass cooking kills an estimated 20,000+ Nepalis annually (WHO estimate). Electrification eliminates this entirely for converted households. Forest degradation from fuelwood collection is reduced. Women's time and labour. Fuelwood collection and biomass cooking are overwhelmingly performed by women. Electrification directly reduces this unpaid labour burden. # Research Gaps Nepal-specific cooking load timing profile. No study exists in the wiki documenting when Nepali households cook, for how long, and with what seasonal variation. Without this, the tariff design and grid impact assessment are speculative. This is the most important research gap for this intervention. Induction cooker cost and distribution. What is the current market price range? What financing products exist (if any)? What is the supply chain from import to rural retailer? LPG subsidy quantum. The exact per-cylinder subsidy rate and total fiscal cost are needed to model the fiscal space for reallocation to electric cooking incentives. Peri-urban grid reliability. SAIDI/SAIFI data for target municipalities would determine the minimum reliability investment envelope before cooking electrification can succeed. Consumer behaviour research. Why have Nepali households not adopted electric cooking when 92% already have electricity for lighting? Is it cost, reliability, appliance access, cooking culture (induction vs. gas flame for traditional foods), or information? Primary survey data would resolve this. Peak load impact modelling. What happens to the 18:30–19:25 evening peak if even 10% of households shift cooking to electricity? Does the grid have the headroom, or does cooking electrification require concurrent storage/BESS deployment? # Political Feasibility Who benefits: Urban and peri-urban households (reduced cooking fuel costs over time); the national treasury (reduced LPG import bill, improved balance of payments); NEA (a new, large, domestic customer base that absorbs surplus); the Ministry of Energy (visible domestic electrification achievement); women (reduced indoor air pollution and fuelwood labour). Who resists: LPG importers and distributors (whose business model is threatened); the Ministry of Finance (who must find budget for appliance subsidies and may resist LPG subsidy reform that is politically sensitive); potentially NEA's distribution function (added load on already-stressed urban feeders). Realistic path: This intervention has medium political feasibility — higher than structural separation or SEBON reform because it offers visible benefits to voters (cheaper cooking, cleaner kitchens) rather than abstract institutional restructuring. But obstacles are real: 1. LPG subsidy is politically entrenched. Any attempt to reduce it triggers consumer protests. The reform must be carefully sequenced: electric cooking must be demonstrably cheaper and more reliable before the LPG subsidy is cut, not after. 2. Appliance cost is a real barrier. An induction cooker costs NPR 3,000–8,000 [UNVERIFIED: market survey needed] — significant for low-income households. Financing mechanisms need subsidy support in early years. 3. Grid reliability is the silent blocker. If households buy induction cookers and the power cuts out at dinner time, the programme loses all credibility. Reliability improvement must come first, which requires NEA distribution investment. 4. Time-of-day tariff complexity. Residential ToD metering requires smart meters or at minimum time-segmented billing — infrastructure NEA does not currently have at residential scale. The most realistic near-term path: a geographically-targeted pilot in 2–3 municipalities with adequate distribution infrastructure, combining appliance financing + promotional tariff + reliability investment, generating data that can inform national scale-up. The pilot approach can survive political churn by being municipally rather than centrally anchored. This page flags the specific evidence gap — no Nepal cooking load timing profile study exists — that should inform pilot design. # Related Pages energy-substitution-pathway — the substitution logic ranking cooking as the #1 demand lever domestic-led-hydro-strategy — the strategic framing this intervention operationalises data-domestic-demand — the 0.5% electric cooking statistic and sectoral demand distribution seasonal-arbitrage-trap — the export pattern cooking electrification helps escape green-hydrogen-nepal — the industrial demand complement to cooking electrification seasonal-mismatch — why absorbing monsoon surplus requires domestic load creation stranded-generation — the surplus energy cooking could absorb twenty-year-strategy — Phase 2 domestic demand building storage-deficit — the physical complement to demand-side management

pages.title: NEA Structural Separation
pages.excerpt: Separate the Nepal Electricity Authority's three conflicting roles into independent institutions through three specific legal/regulatory actions:
pages.body_text: NEA Structural Separation # The Intervention Separate the Nepal Electricity Authority's three conflicting roles into independent institutions through three specific legal/regulatory actions: 1. Carve NLDC into an Independent System Operator (ISO). Amend the Electricity Act to transfer the National Load Dispatch Centre out of NEA and into a legally independent, board-governed ISO. The ISO would control dispatch decisions free of NEA's commercial interest in its own generation fleet. Until legislative amendment passes, executive action could establish a ring-fenced dispatch unit with transparent, published merit-order criteria. 2. Transfer AD penalty authority to the Electricity Regulatory Commission (ERC). Amend the ERC Act or relevant bylaws to make the ERC — not NEA — the entity that calculates, levies, and adjudicates Availability Declaration penalties. NEA would retain the role of off-taker but would no longer be the simultaneous prosecutor and judge of its own counterparties. 3. Create an open-access framework. Promulgate the draft Open Access policy (anticipated since at least 2022) allowing IPPs to sell to third parties: industrial consumers, export markets, and trading companies. This would end the single-buyer monopoly that makes every IPP a captive seller to NEA. # Theory of Change The NEA Triple-Authority Conflict (nea-triple-authority) is not an edge-case governance flaw — it is the institutional mechanism that converts Nepal's hydrological advantage into stranded assets and retail investor losses. When NLDC curtails a project for "grid security," NEA simultaneously stops the project's revenue as buyer and can penalise it for not generating as penalty administrator. The Barahi Hydropower case (barahi-hydropower) documents this double-bind operating even against an ERC statutory exemption. Structural separation unlocks value by: Removing the built-in incentive to favour NEA-subsidiary generation (34% of national output) over IPP generation (55%) in dispatch decisions Creating a neutral penalty adjudicator, ending the metrological impossibility of penalising RoR projects for failing to predict monsoon hydrology 30 days ahead Enabling corporate PPAs and third-party sales, which would allow industrial consumers to contract directly with IPPs — creating a price-discovery mechanism that the single-buyer model suppresses Without separation, every additional MW of IPP capacity faces the same structural risk: NEA can order it off-line and fine it for being off-line in the same transaction. The claimed 16% of project revenue consumed by AD penalties [UNVERIFIED: sector aggregate only] is a direct institutional output, not a natural cost of doing business. # Current Status PARTIALLY VERIFIED: The 2003 World Bank Nepal power sector review recommended eliminating conflicts of interest in NEA's structure. No legislation has been enacted in the 23 years since. A draft Open Access policy exists and has been discussed since at least 2022. No promulgation date has been announced. The ERC has demonstrated willingness to exercise regulatory authority — ERC Bylaws 2076 created an AD penalty exemption for sub-10 MW projects. But the Barahi case proves the ERC cannot enforce its own bylaws against NEA's control of cash disbursement. UNVERIFIED: Whether any parliamentary committee has formally investigated dispatch discrimination Whether any PPA contains an explicit clause exempting IPPs from AD penalties when curtailment is ordered by NEA/NLDC The exact statutory mechanism by which AD penalties are retained by NEA (rather than remitted to an independent fund) No legislation implementing any of the three separation actions has been enacted. The Fenner School's independent academic audit (ANU) has verified the institutional bias: "The major complaint of the rising IPPs is that the playing field is not level, that NEA as generator, transmitter and distributor gives preferential terms to its own projects compared to IPPs." # What This Unlocks End of uncompensated curtailment. An independent ISO would make dispatch decisions on technical merit, not commercial self-interest. NLDC curtailment decisions affecting billions of rupees in annual IPP revenue would be subject to published criteria and regulatory oversight. Fair penalty enforcement. Transferring AD authority to the ERC would create a neutral forum where projects can challenge penalties. The Barahi case — where a statutory exemption was overridden because NEA controls the cash — would become structurally impossible. Price discovery through open access. Industrial consumers paying NPR 10.00–10.20/kWh at peak could contract directly with IPPs whose blended PPA rate is NPR ~5.52–6.20/kWh, creating margin for both parties and reducing NEA's near-monopoly pricing power. IPP bankability. Removing the structural risk of uncompensated curtailment and unaccountable penalties would reduce the risk premium priced into hydro project finance. At the DSCR break-point of ~82–84% of contracted energy (q-design-discharge), every percentage point of revenue protected from penalty clawback directly improves bankability. Quantified impact: UNVERIFIED. No independent economic modelling of structural separation's NPV for Nepal's power sector exists in the wiki. The 37.1% revenue advantage Chilime enjoys over comparable private IPPs through its subsidiary PPA architecture (chilime) provides a directional benchmark: a 20-25% reduction in the effective revenue gap between NEA-subsidiary and IPP generation would represent tens of billions of NPR in redistributed value. # Research Gaps PPA clause text. The most important unresolved legal question: does any standard PPA contain an exemption for curtailment-ordered shortfalls? Primary PPA text has not been accessed. Resolving this would confirm or refute the double-bind's legal basis. NLDC dispatch data. No public, project-level dispatch logs exist. Without them, claims of dispatch discrimination against IPPs remain inferential (from the Fenner School audit and the Chilime PPA rate comparison), not directly data-proven. ISO legislative pathway. Which specific clauses of the Electricity Act 1992 require amendment? Has any draft bill been tabled in parliament? These questions are unanswered in the wiki. AD penalty aggregate volume. The 16% of revenue claim is UNVERIFIED. NEA's own records of penalties deducted, by project and fiscal year, would resolve this. # Political Feasibility Who benefits: Private IPPs, their lenders (NRB-regulated BFIs carrying ~4-5% hydro exposure), industrial consumers seeking lower-cost power, retail investors in hydro IPOs/rights shares who currently bear penalty risk without disclosure. Who resists: NEA management and the NEA employee union — the triple-authority is NEA's institutional power. NEA staff are also significant shareholders in Chilime and its subsidiary cascade, benefiting directly from the PPA rate advantage documented in chilime. The Ministry of Energy may resist loss of direct control over a major state-owned enterprise. Political patrons of NEA procurement may resist transparency in dispatch decisions. Realistic path: Nepal has had 18+ governments in 30 years. A comprehensive legislative overhaul (Electricity Act amendment + ERC Act amendment + open-access promulgation) is improbable in a single government's tenure. A more realistic sequence: 1. Executive action first: Ring-fence NLDC within NEA with published merit-order criteria — achievable without legislation 2. ERC capacity-building: Fund and staff ERC to prepare for penalty adjudication authority — requires budget commitment, not law reform 3. Legislative sequencing: Open-access regulation (simplest, most precedent internationally) → ISO legislation → penalty transfer The window of a majority government (current post-2024) is the most favourable in a decade for structural reform. The risk is that the political cycle turns before any component is enacted. # Related Pages nea-triple-authority — the institutional conflict this intervention resolves claim-governance-binding — governance as the meta-bottleneck barahi-hydropower — primary audited case study of the double-bind chilime — the subsidiary PPA rate advantage that structural separation would level ad-penalties — the penalty mechanism transferred to ERC stranded-generation — the curtailment outcome structural separation prevents ipo-hydropower-bailout — why retail investors are exposed to unresolvable disputes without structural separation claim-transmission-immediate-blocker — the transmission bottleneck that compounds governance failures

pages.title: Q-Design Climate Adjustment
pages.excerpt: Issue a formal directive from the Department of Electricity Development (DoED) mandating that all new hydropower project feasibility studies submitted for licensing must incorporate climate-adjusted hydrological decrement factors based on the ICIMOD "Guideline for Climate Resilie
pages.body_text: Q-Design Climate Adjustment # The Intervention Issue a formal directive from the Department of Electricity Development (DoED) mandating that all new hydropower project feasibility studies submitted for licensing must incorporate climate-adjusted hydrological decrement factors based on the ICIMOD "Guideline for Climate Resilient Hydropower in Nepal" (published March 11, 2024). Specifically: 1. Revise Article 9 of the "Guidelines for Study of Hydropower Projects 2018" to require — not merely permit — the use of bias-corrected General Circulation Model (GCM) projections, multi-pathway (SSP245, SSP585) climate scenarios, and SWAT/SWAT+ hydrological routing in all feasibility studies for projects above a specified threshold (e.g., 10 MW). 2. Retire the WECS/DHM 1990 methodology as the default for ungauged tributary hydrology estimation — ending the continued reliance on empirical regression equations derived from 1970s and 1980s data that assume hydrological stationarity. 3. Require a climate-adjusted Q-design parameter that applies a forward-looking decrement factor to the historical flow-duration curve, producing a design discharge that reflects projected dry-season flow declines — not the historical average. 4. Align licensing rules with multilateral lender requirements — all three major MDBs (World Bank, ADB, IFC) already mandate climate-adjusted hydrology as a non-negotiable condition for project financing. DoED's failure to mandate equivalent standards creates a two-tier system where mega-projects (Upper Arun, Dudh Koshi) use modern methods but domestically-financed mid-tier projects use 1990 methods. # Theory of Change The Q-design discharge parameter (q-design-discharge) mechanically determines both turbine sizing and the PPA contracted energy obligation. When Q-design is set using the WECS/DHM 1990 methodology — which assumes stationarity — the PPA promises the river will deliver what historical data projects, not what a warming climate will actually provide. This is not a theoretical risk. The Urja Khabar audit of 100+ projects found zero projects exceeded 80% of contracted energy except Chilime. The DSCR break-point analysis shows projects fall below the 1.0x covenant threshold when actual generation drops below ~82–84% of contracted energy (q-design-discharge). The regulatory system is currently licensing projects into structural financial default — and retail investors buying IPO and rights shares are not told this. Climate adjustment fixes the root cause, not the symptom: Upstream fix: Correct the Q-design parameter before the PPA is signed, rather than managing the consequences (AD penalties, DSCR breaches, loan restructuring) after the project inevitably underperforms. Downstream unlock: A climate-adjusted Q-design → realistic contracted energy → bankable project finance → fewer distressed IPOs and rights share bailouts → a healthier hydro sector that attracts rather than traps capital. Systemic unlock: If all new projects use climate-adjusted design, the aggregate contracted energy of the fleet becomes a realistic planning target rather than a fiction, enabling credible national energy planning. The intervention also captures the full value of the ICIMOD guideline — which extends beyond hydrology to GLOF risk, sediment loading, and permafrost degradation — making project-level hazard design climate-rational rather than historically anchored. # Current Status VERIFIED: ICIMOD's "Guideline for Climate Resilient Hydropower in Nepal" was formally published on March 11, 2024, following basin-level modelling by NDRI in collaboration with ICIMOD and WECS (September 2023 – March 2024). The 2024 guideline explicitly assigns a policy target to DoED: "Revise and implement the DoED Guidelines for Study of Hydropower Projects by incorporating additions proposed in this Guideline." This revision remains unexecuted as of mid-2026. DoED's governing document remains the "Guidelines for Study of Hydropower Projects 2018" with Q40–Q45 design requirements under Article 9. The 4th amendment added active storage requirements (15 days of design discharge, 35% dry-season energy minimum) but did not mandate forward-looking climate modelling. The World Bank (Decision Tree Framework), ADB (Climate Risk and Vulnerability Assessment at concept stage), and IFC (life-cycle climate risk screening per revised Performance Standards) all mandate climate-adjusted hydrology for financed projects. Post-2022 mega-projects — Upper Arun 1,040 MW ESIA (January 2024), Dudh Koshi 635 MW assessments — demonstrate clear integration of climate-adjusted hydrology, abandoning static Q40 methods for dynamic risk models. NRB has amended unified directives requiring BFIs to report on climate exposures of borrowers (early-stage ESG integration). PARTIALLY VERIFIED: The DoED 5th amendment (Ashadh 2082 / mid-2025) has been published but does not include climate-decrement factors. UNVERIFIED: Whether any purely domestically-financed mid-tier project (10–50 MW, using local bank capital without MDB involvement) has voluntarily adopted the ICIMOD 2024 guideline. Current evidence strongly suggests they are adhering to minimum Q40/Q45 DoED standards to reduce upfront CAPEX and expedite licensing. The exact internal bureaucratic timeline for DoED revision of Article 9, including any draft circulars or working group minutes. The specific political-economy dynamics blocking adoption — domestic IPP lobby resistance to higher upfront feasibility costs is the most likely factor but has not been directly sourced. # What This Unlocks More accurate PPA contracting. If Q-design is set at climate-realistic levels, contracted energy targets become achievable — reducing the systemic DSCR breach currently affecting the IPP sector. More resilient system planning. With realistic fleet-level generation projections, the twenty-year strategy's Phase 2-3 buildout assumptions (twenty-year-strategy) become grounded in achievable output rather than stationary-hydrology projections. Reduced stranded asset creation. Every project licensed today on 1990 hydrology assumptions that underperforms by 20–40% against contract creates a future distressed asset. Climate adjustment prevents this at the licensing stage. Alignment with international finance. Formal adoption would allow domestically-financed projects to meet MDB due diligence standards, reducing the current two-tier system where only mega-projects get climate-resilient design. Dry-season output more predictable. With GCM-driven estimates of declining dry-season baseflow (see peak-water and the Karnali decomposition showing snowmelt at 24% of annual flow), grid operators can plan winter import requirements more accurately. # Research Gaps Exact timeline for DoED legislative integration. While the absence of a mandate is VERIFIED, the internal roadmap, draft amendments, or specific bureaucratic obstacles within DoED remain unknown. Private-sector compliance outside MDBs. No empirical statistical breakdown of post-2024 domestic IPP feasibility study methodologies exists. Are any mid-tier projects adopting ICIMOD voluntarily? How many post-2022 licenses rely on WECS/DHM 1990? WECS political economy. WECS partnered with ICIMOD and NDRI on the Narayani Basin pilot. What is WECS's current position on mandating the guideline? What inter-ministerial coordination barriers exist between WECS and DoED? NRB enforcement mechanism. NRB's unified directive on climate exposure reporting is a potential financial bypass of DoED stagnation — but is it being enforced? Are domestic banks independently demanding climate-adjusted hydrology for loan approvals? # Pending Research A research agent has been tasked with investigating: 1. The current publication status of the ICIMOD climate-resilient hydropower guideline — RESOLVED: Published March 11, 2024. VERIFIED. 2. Whether DoED has taken any formal regulatory move toward adoption since 2022 — RESOLVED: No formal adoption as of mid-2026. VERIFIED. 3. Whether any projects licensed post-2022 show climate-adjusted methodology — PARTIALLY RESOLVED: Mega-projects (Upper Arun, Dudh Koshi) do, under MDB mandates. Mid-tier domestically-financed projects: UNVERIFIED. 4. Whether multilateral lenders have mandated climate-adjusted hydrology — RESOLVED: World Bank, ADB, and IFC all mandate it. VERIFIED. Outstanding questions the research agent flagged for further investigation: DoED internal memos, draft circulars, or working group minutes regarding revision of Article 9 — file formal requests or utilise stakeholder networks WECS officials' perspective on political economy barriers to adoption — engage directly with WECS leadership who co-developed the pilot NRB audit enforcement of climate exposure directives — whether domestic banks are autonomously demanding climate-adjusted hydrology for local loan approvals Whether any mid-tier privately-financed projects (10–50 MW) have voluntarily adopted the ICIMOD methodology THIS PAGE SHOULD BE UPDATED WHEN THESE FINDINGS ARRIVE. # Political Feasibility Who benefits: The national planning apparatus (WECS, NRB, Ministry of Finance — who underwrite the systemic risk of a distressed hydro portfolio); MDBs (whose standards would be aligned with domestic regulation, reducing transaction costs); future IPP investors whose capital would be deployed against realistic generation targets rather than regulatory fictions; retail investors who would not be sold shares in projects structurally incapable of meeting PPA targets. Who resists: The domestic IPP lobby and their engineering consultancy ecosystem. Climate-adjusted feasibility studies require: Upfront capital expenditure for advanced climate modelling (~3–5× current feasibility study costs, based on MDB project experience) Potentially smaller authorised mechanical capacities (lower Q-design → smaller turbines → lower headline MW → less attractive to retail IPO marketing) Specialised geospatial modelling capability that concentrated domestic consultancies may not possess The political economy is a classic short-term cost vs. long-term benefit trade-off, but the costs are concentrated (on IPPs and consultants developing projects now) while the benefits are diffuse (financial system stability, realistic national planning). The domestic banking sector, carrying 4–5% hydro exposure with rising NPLs, should in theory support the reform — but NRB forbearance (q-design-discharge) means banks have not yet been forced to recognise their hydro book's true distress. Realistic path: The reform does not require legislation — a DoED directive amending Article 9 of the 2018 guidelines is administratively executable. The blocking force is political will, not legal pathway. Two near-term levers: 1. NRB can force the issue from the financial side. If NRB mandates climate-adjusted hydrology as a condition of hydro project lending (extending its existing climate-exposure directive), the banking system becomes the enforcement mechanism for what DoED will not mandate. 2. MDB conditionalities already create a two-tier system. As mega-projects backed by MDBs increasingly use ICIMOD methods, the regulatory disparity becomes politically visible — and domestically-financed projects' physical underperformance becomes harder to attribute to "bad luck" rather than obsolete methodology. The 18+ governments in 30 years reality applies: administrative action under the current government is more probable than legislative embedding. But the DoED Directive pathway is administratively achievable within a single ministerial term if the political will exists. # Related Pages q-design-discharge — the Q-design mechanics, DSCR break-point, and generation performance data peak-water — the glacial-hydrological window that stationarity assumptions ignore seasonal-mismatch — the structural monsoon-winter asymmetry that climate change amplifies ipo-hydropower-bailout — why investors are not told their shares are PPA'd against obsolete hydrology nea-triple-authority — the institutional framework that enforces unrealistic contracted energy data-storage-comparison — why storage is the physical complement to climate-adjusted hydro design twenty-year-strategy — where climate resilience fits in the phased strategy (Phase 2-3) icimod-koshi-basin — ICIMOD source documentation

pages.title: SEBON Data Transparency
headings: Track B — Civic data project (independently executable)
pages.excerpt: This intervention has two parallel tracks:
pages.body_text: SEBON Data Transparency # The Intervention This intervention has two parallel tracks: ## Track A — Regulatory (SEBON mandate) Amend SEBON's disclosure regulations to require that IPO prospectuses, rights share issuance documents, and annual financial statements of listed hydropower companies be filed in machine-readable structured formats (XBRL or equivalent, with tagged data fields), not as scanned PDFs. Specific mandated fields would include: IPO/rights proceeds: actual use-of-funds breakdown (bank debt retirement vs. promoter bridge loan repayment vs. new project investment) PPA terms: contracted energy, tariff structure (seasonal split), and the existence of any AD penalty clause references Risk disclosures: explicit modelling of generation shortfall scenarios (80%, 65% of contracted energy) and their impact on debt service AD penalty exposure: penalties paid, by fiscal year, with reconciliation to ERC protections ## Track B — Civic data project (independently executable) Build and publish a machine-readable dataset of Nepal's hydropower IPO and rights share history, cross-referenced against: AD penalty exposure (from audited financial statements and the ad-penalties mechanism) Hydrological performance (contracted energy vs. actual generation, from project-level data in q-design-discharge) PPA architecture (subsidiary rate advantage documented in chilime, standard IPP rates from ppa-pricing) NEPSE trading suspensions and regulatory actions (e.g. Upper Syange, Khani Khola) This dataset would be built using AI/data engineering tools and published as an open civic resource. It is independently executable without waiting for SEBON regulatory change. # Theory of Change The entire structure of Nepal's hydro IPO market — pre-revenue listing, rights share bailouts, undisclosed AD penalty exposure — operates in a data vacuum. The ipo-hydropower-bailout page documents what is known: six companies, NPR billions raised, many for debt retirement. But the full picture is unresolvable because SEBON's systemic use of non-machine-readable scanned PDFs blocks automated analysis. Making disclosures machine-readable would: Enable automated accountability. Researchers, journalists, and analysts could programmatically compare prospectus promises against audited outcomes — revealing whether IPO funds were used as stated. Force risk disclosure. If SEBON mandates structured fields for PPA terms and AD penalty exposure, prospectuses would have to state, in machine-readable form, what retail investors are buying. Currently, investors in rights shares of distressed hydro companies are not informed that the off-taker can curtail the project and then penalise it. Improve market efficiency. Machine-readable disclosures would enable comparative analysis across all listed hydro companies, allowing retail investors to distinguish between Chilime (pre-IPO profitability, conservative Q-design, subsidiary rate advantage) and pre-revenue IPOs with unrealistic contracted energy targets. The civic data track (Track B) creates value immediately by building the dataset that should exist but doesn't. Even before SEBON reforms, publishing a cross-referenced dataset would: Make the ipo-hydropower-bailout analysis fully data-backed rather than reliant on six partially-verified company entries Create a public resource usable by journalists, parliamentarians, and NRB regulators Demonstrate the feasibility of machine-readable energy-sector transparency, creating pressure for official adoption # Current Status PARTIALLY VERIFIED: SEBON's IPO disclosure system is confirmed to use non-machine-readable scanned PDFs. The ipo-hydropower-bailout page's entire "What is missing" section exists because SEBON's filing format blocks automated extraction. Six hydro companies have issued IPOs and rights shares, documented from media and secondary rating agency sources — but SEBON prospectuses have not been independently audited. UNVERIFIED: What percentage of IPO/rights proceeds was actually used to retire promoter bridge loans vs. bank debt vs. new project investment What risk disclosures about hydrological underperformance, PPA rate risk, and AD penalty exposure actually appear in hydro IPO prospectuses The full list of NEPSE-listed hydro companies that issued rights shares within 3 years of IPO NRB's specialised hydro loan book — the true extent of off-balance-sheet restructuring and informal forbearance Whether any hydro IPO prospectus models the scenario where AD penalties consume a portion of revenue (ad-penalties estimates up to 16%) NEPSE has suspended trading on multiple hydro companies (Upper Syange for insider trading and delayed rights share disclosure; Khani Khola for controversial Q4 reporting and promoter share dumping) — indicating market-level problems but without transparent data infrastructure to diagnose their systemic nature. # What This Unlocks Retail investor protection. When a pre-revenue hydro project floats an IPO at Rs 100/share and six months later issues rights shares at Rs 50 to pay off bank loans, machine-readable disclosures would make this pattern visible across the sector. Systemic risk monitoring. NRB and the Ministry of Finance cannot currently see the aggregate exposure of retail investors to distressed hydro debt because the data is PDF-locked. Machine-readable disclosures would enable portfolio-level stress testing. Market integrity. Structured disclosure requirements would make it harder for promoters to dump shares before announcing rights share bailouts — the kind of behaviour that triggered the Upper Syange NEPSE suspension. The civic dataset (Track B) specifically unlocks: journalists can write data-backed stories; parliamentarians can ask quantified questions; NRB can cross-reference its loan book against public data. This is the fastest route to visibility because it requires no regulatory change. # Research Gaps Full list of listed hydro companies. The wiki currently tracks six companies. NEPSE likely has 15-20 listed hydro entities. A complete roster with listing dates, IPO amounts, rights share history, and trading status is needed. SEBON's current disclosure format policy. Has SEBON ever considered machine-readable filing? Has any pilot been attempted? What is the technical specification of SEBON's current filing system? NRB's specialised hydro loan book. The extent of off-balance-sheet restructuring and informal forbearance is the hidden counterpart to the public equity story. NRB data would complete the picture. International precedent. India's SEBI mandated XBRL for listed companies in phases from 2008-2011. Bangladesh's BSEC adopted machine-readable disclosure in 2019. What is the nearest applicable regulatory model for Nepal? # Political Feasibility Track A (regulatory): Medium-low in near term, medium in medium term. SEBON is not the most resistant regulator in Nepal's institutional landscape, but mandating structured data filing requires technical capacity SEBON may not possess. The cost of implementing XBRL taxonomy for hydro-specific disclosures would need to be borne by either SEBON or the listed companies. The hydro company lobby — which benefits from opacity about PPA risk and AD penalty exposure — would resist mandatory structured risk disclosures. Countervailing force: NRB's recent climate-exposure directive and growing NPL pressure in the hydro book create financial-sector demand for better data. NRB could push SEBON from the systemic risk angle. Track B (civic data): Immediately executable. High feasibility. The dataset can be built using publicly available SEBON/NEPSE filings, company annual reports, media archives, and AI-assisted text extraction from scanned PDFs. Publication as an open dataset (CSV/JSON with documentation) under a permissive license creates a public good that requires no government permission. The dataset would serve as both an accountability tool and a proof-of-concept for machine-readable energy-sector transparency — demonstrating to regulators what they could mandate. This track is a near-term action item for the wiki's author, leveraging AI/data engineering capability to produce a resource that currently exists nowhere in Nepal's public domain. # Related Pages ipo-hydropower-bailout — the IPO-to-bailout pattern this data intervention targets nea-triple-authority — the institutional structure that AD penalty exposure reflects q-design-discharge — the generation underperformance that makes hydro IPOs risky ad-penalties — the penalty mechanism whose aggregate impact remains hidden without data chilime — the irreplicable success case that prospectuses invoke without context barahi-hydropower — the case that proves penalties can override their own exemptions ppa-pricing — the rate structure whose differential impact on IPPs is invisible without data

pages.title: Transmission Completion
pages.excerpt: Complete two transmission infrastructure priorities with specific mechanisms:
pages.body_text: Transmission Completion # The Intervention Complete two transmission infrastructure priorities with specific mechanisms: 1. Hetauda–Dhalkebar 400 kV (134 km, 795 towers). As of late April 2026, the line is 98.24% complete — only 14 towers remain unbuilt, all in Wards 15, 16, and 17 of Hetauda Sub-metropolitan City (the Hatiya area). The intervention is to execute the mediated compensation agreement (brokered by the Energy Minister, late April 2026), complete the remaining 14 towers under the security arrangement already deployed, and energise the line. Target completion: mid-July 2026. 2. New Butwal–Gorakhpur 400 kV cross-border. Complete the second 400 kV cross-border interconnection to India. This corridor creates the physical capacity for Nepal's export ambitions to be realised — the current ~1,141 MW of approved cross-border capacity (primarily through the single Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur 400 kV link) lags far behind installed generation of 3,591 MW and growing. Supporting mechanism: apply the December 2025 land-use regulation amendment (permitting subdivision of land under transmission RoW with limited compensation) systematically to all future transmission corridor conflicts, reducing the reoccurrence of Hetauda-style blockades. # Theory of Change Transmission is the #1 immediate monetisation bottleneck in Nepal's energy system (claim-transmission-immediate-blocker). The 400 kV east-west backbone — essential for moving power from western basins to eastern load centres and cross-border points — has been incomplete for over a decade. The consequence is not abstract: 700–800 MW of zero-carbon monsoon electricity is spilled or curtailed daily because the grid cannot move it. The Hetauda–Dhalkebar section is the most dramatic bottleneck in the entire system: Only 14 towers remain out of 795. This is not a construction or financing failure — it is a pure community-resistance failure. The project has been under construction since 2013 (original target: 2016). The cost of this single bottleneck is staggering: NEA estimates NPR 5–6 billion per year in direct losses from power that cannot be evacuated. Over the multi-year delay, the cumulative loss runs into tens of billions of NPR — more than the cost of the line itself. Completion would be transformative. The Hetauda–Dhalkebar segment is the keystone of the 400 kV east-west backbone. When operational (combined with the already-completed Dhalkebar–Inaruwa 154 km eastern section), the full 288 km corridor provides the domestic grid architecture to move power from generation-surplus to demand-surplus regions. The Butwal–Gorakhpur cross-border line addresses the export capacity dimension. Nepal's single 400 kV cross-border link (Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur) carries ~1,141 MW of approved export. With installed capacity at 3,591 MW and growing, export ambition is limited not by generation but by corridor hardware. The second 400 kV cross-border line roughly doubles export capacity. Without these corridors, the logic of stranded-generation continues: more RoR MW commissioned → more monsoon surplus → more curtailment → more IPP financial distress → more retail investor losses. Transmission completion breaks this cycle at its physical root. # Current Status VERIFIED: Hetauda–Dhalkebar: 134 km section is 98.24% complete. Of 795 towers, 781 are built. Exactly 14 towers remain unbuilt: 2 in Ward 15, 10 in Ward 16, 2 in Ward 17 (Hatiya area, Hetauda Sub-metropolitan City, Makwanpur). Construction resumed April 23, 2026 under heavy police security, following a high-level mediation agreement orchestrated by the Energy Minister. Government target: mid-July 2026 completion. Community resistance nature: Demands for rerouting away from dense settlements, fair land compensation for RoW, and unaddressed electromagnetic field (EMF) health risk concerns. No major political group identified — appears community-driven. A district-level compensation evaluation committee has been formed under the Chief District Officer (CDO). Initial compensation payments were made in 2018 but some residents refused them. Delay cost: NEA estimates NPR 5–6 billion per year in operational losses. 700–800 MW of monsoon surplus cannot be evacuated. Institutional reform: December 2025 land-use regulation amendment permits subdivision of land under transmission RoW with limited compensation — specifically designed to ease future such conflicts. VERIFIED (from claim-transmission-immediate-blocker): Dhalkebar–Inaruwa: 400 kV, 154 km — Operational (June 2024). Eastern backbone complete. Dhalkebar–Bara: 400 kV, 100 km — Completed (March 2025). Operating at 132 kV via tapping; ~200 MW, upgradeable to 400 MW. New Butwal–Bardaghat: 220 kV, 21.5 km — Operational (December 2024). Creates 220 kV backbone from Butwal to Hetauda (~1,200 MW capacity). Marsyangdi Corridor: 220 kV, 68 km — 65% complete (target was May 2025; delayed). Damauli–Bharatpur: 220 kV, 33 km — 76% complete (March 2026). VERIFIED (comparable case): The Bharatpur–Bardaghat 220 kV line (World Bank-funded) faced similar community resistance and was resolved in 2023 through the World Bank's Dispute Resolution Service — a mediated settlement between community and government enabling line completion. This is a proven resolution model for Nepal's transmission conflicts. # What This Unlocks Hetauda–Dhalkebar completion (~July 2026): - Operational 400 kV east-west backbone, connecting western generation to eastern load centres and the Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur cross-border point - End of the NPR 5–6 billion/year operational loss from transmission-constrained curtailment - 700–800 MW of currently stranded monsoon generation becomes evacuable — directly addressing stranded-generation - The line's completion enables additional generation in western basins (Karnali, Gandaki) to reach markets, removing a binding constraint on the Phase 2 pipeline in twenty-year-strategy Butwal–Gorakhpur completion: - Roughly doubles Nepal's 400 kV cross-border export capacity - Creates a second independent India interconnection, reducing single-point-of-failure risk on Dhalkebar–Muzaffarpur - Enables the export volumes contemplated in Phase 2 (2030–2037) of the twenty-year-strategy - Strengthens Nepal's negotiating position in bilateral electricity trade by providing physical alternatives Systemic unlock: - The December 2025 land-use regulation amendment, if systematically applied, provides a legal template for resolving future community-resistance conflicts before they become multi-year blockades - The Bharatpur–Bardaghat resolution (World Bank DRS, 2023) provides a dispute-resolution mechanism model that can be institutionalised Quantified: NPR 5–6 billion/year in direct avoided losses from Hetauda–Dhalkebar completion alone 700–800 MW of additional monsoon generation evacuation capacity ~500–700 GWh of additional annual export potential (based on 700 MW × ~3 monsoon months × 24 hours × ~40% effective utilisation — rough directional estimate; seasonal and operational factors determine the precise figure) # Research Gaps The following are PARTIALLY RESOLVED by recent research (April 2026): Current construction status: 98.24% complete, 14 towers remain. Outstanding questions from the research agent's brief that require further investigation: Obtain NEA's revised detailed completion schedule for Hetauda–Dhalkebar with milestone dates Verify the specific compensation amounts and terms of the April 2026 agreement (CDO committee records, local media follow-up) Calculate the cumulative financial cost over the full 10-year delay period (2016 target → 2026 actual), distinguishing between curtailment losses and other costs Verify Butwal–Gorakhpur construction status, completion percentage, and expected commissioning date Document the operational application of the December 2025 land-use regulation amendment to active corridor projects Review World Bank DRS notices for Bharatpur–Bardaghat to extract the resolution mechanism specifics that could be replicated THIS PAGE SHOULD BE UPDATED WHEN THESE FINDINGS ARRIVE. # Political Feasibility Who benefits: NEA (NPR 5–6 billion/year in avoided operational losses); IPPs (end of curtailment for projects whose output the completed backbone can now evacuate); industrial consumers (improved grid reliability for east-west power flows); export revenue (additional cross-border capacity for monsoon surplus); the national treasury (reduced petroleum imports if additional domestic electricity displaces diesel/LPG). Who resists: The 14-tower communities in Hetauda Wards 15–17 have already been compensated (2018) and the April 2026 agreement appears to have resolved remaining objections — but community resistance could reignite if the compensation evaluation committee's work is perceived as inadequate. Future corridor projects will face similar dynamics in new geographies. Realistic path: HIGH feasibility for Hetauda–Dhalkebar, MEDIUM for Butwal–Gorakhpur. Hetauda–Dhalkebar: The intervention is already in execution. With only 14 towers remaining, construction resumed under police security, and a mediated compensation agreement in place, the mid-July 2026 target is physically achievable. The primary risk is re-escalation of community resistance if compensation payments stall or if the EMF health concerns are not addressed transparently. This is the closest thing to a "sure win" in Nepal's energy infrastructure landscape — the cost of failure is immense (NPR 5–6 billion/year), the remaining work is tiny (14 towers), and the political will is demonstrated (Energy Minister personal mediation, police deployment). Butwal–Gorakhpur: Cross-border corridors face dual approval requirements (Nepali and Indian regulatory processes). The construction status is "under construction" but the timeline to completion is less certain than Hetauda–Dhalkebar. Indian regulatory coordination and any emergent community resistance on the Nepali side are the primary risks. Institutional lesson: The Bharatpur–Bardaghat resolution (World Bank DRS, 2023) and the December 2025 land-use regulation amendment represent institutional learning. Nepal now has both a dispute resolution model (third-party mediation) and a legal tool (land subdivision under RoW) that should prevent future transmission conflicts from becoming decade-long blockades. The key question is whether these tools are systematically applied to the Marsyangdi corridor (65% complete) and Damauli–Bharatpur (76% complete) before they encounter Hetauda-style resistance. # Related Pages claim-transmission-immediate-blocker — the bottleneck this intervention directly resolves stranded-generation — the 700–800 MW of daily curtailment that transmission completion eliminates seasonal-mismatch — the generation pattern that makes transmission the binding monetisation constraint data-trade-time-series — the trade volumes and pricing that depend on corridor capacity seasonal-arbitrage-trap — why inadequate transmission worsens the value-destruction pattern storage-deficit — the deeper structural fix that transmission complements twenty-year-strategy — Phase 1 priority: fix stranded generation by building grid bottleneck-hierarchy — transmission as the #1 immediate binding constraint hetauda-dhalkebar-inaruwa-backbone — the corridor entity page gorakhpur-butwal-interconnection — the cross-border entity page nepal-transmission-landscape-2025 — the full transmission source compilation
